app_name: Fallout 3 - Game of the Year Edition
review_text: The story in Fallout 3 is probably its worst detriment. All of the characters, ranging from Liam Neeson, to Three Dog (who shouldn't exist as Fallout is a parody of the 1930's through the 1950's and not the sixties and seventies), to the Lone Wanderer his/herself, are under-developed and stereotypical, compounded by the fact that the dialogue is uninteresting and unnatural. With poor characters, we don't care for the events of the game as presented in the plot, which is unoriginal and irrelevant to the Fallout universe as a whole. Fallout is a parody of civilization itself, not just modern civilization, but civilization since the beginning. The original Fallout marked the beginning for post-modern society as the Vault Dweller paved the way for the Brotherhood of Steel and the NCR to prosper and spread, Fallout 2 marked the golden age of postmodern civilization, and New Vegas marked the fall of post-modern civilization into the typical corrupt and unjust ways of life as the battle for expansion and resources takes precedence over the well-being of the people. Fallout 3 is about giving people stuff to drink and little beyond that.    Gameplay and story go hand and hand and this is also true especially in Fallout in the form of player choice. The ability to become a porn star, join the mafia, or marry someone and divorce them the next day are just a few examples of how Fallout's trademark sleaze presented itself (going into the branching dialogue would take too long, it's that detailed). Of course, you also have the ability to choose how you wish to progress with the plot (pick a faction to side with, be independent, or just sit and let things resolve themselves, etc.) and through this you would receive a different experience every time you played. None of this is present in Fallout 3. The plot forces you to eventually side with the Brotherhood of Steel (a group of greedy and oppressive religious zealots) without regard for the Enclave (who shouldn't be here as they were destroyed in Fallout 2 and their being the antagonists is unoriginal anyway) or any other faction. Regarding populated areas, I understand that D.C. is supposed to be desolate, however there are two relatively large towns that immediately come to mind in Megaton and Rivet City, so why isn't there anything to do in these towns? With fewer urban environments, Bethesda should've compensated by providing an immense amount of varying activates to participate in, so why didn't they? It's not like it'd be difficult to program at all; it's because Bethesda wasn't willing to put the effort into adding to their product and Fallout 3 suffers due to their laziness.   To be honest, it's rather surprising that Obsidian Entertainment hasn't been handed control of Fallout. They developed New Vegas, which was better received by fans than Fallout 3 and more importantly (to Bethesda, anyway), New Vegas sold better than Fallout 3. Even more importantly, Obsidian consists almost entirely of members from Black Isle Studios, the same team that created the first two Fallout installments. This is a match made in heaven, so why is Bethesda still developing Fallout 4? It's completely and utterly foolish on Bethesda's part and with their track record of games as of late like Rage (a Fallout rip-off published by the same company who makes the product being ripped off?), Skyrim (one of the most over-rated games in the history of the medium), and Dishonored (another barebones, generic, and over-rated title) it seems we'll all suffer due to Bethesda's ego.

My opinion on Fallout New Vegas  Pros -Great Story -Open World -Amazing DLC experiences -Can be played through multiple times and each time can be different. -Plenty of mods to choose from -Character Customization is quite extensive -Plenty of freedom and great decisions that can be made  Cons  -Can be glitchy at times (Break dancing dead bodies as an example) -AI can be bad sometimes -Some parts of the game lacking from cut content.    Fallout New Vegas is an amazing game. Personally I found myself unable to stop playing when I first got it and had a great time, the story and world is amazing even more so than Fallout 3 in my opinion. There was just something really cool at first when following the trail to New Vegas and going on all kinds of crazy adventures on my way there, it was especially a memorable moment when I came over the hill to see New Vegas shining in the darkness after many hours leading up to it.   The main story leading up to the battle for hoover damn was great and I felt really immersed, however the battle itself was a fairly large let-down in my opinion and just did not feel right. One thing that I think the game did right is that there where some very tough decisions for me, some decisions took awhile to think about which I really liked, especially the end of the Lonesome Road DLC which I wont spoil.   The DLC's are damn good. Dead Money was an experience that took me out of Fallout and made me feel like I was playing a completely different game, whilst playing it I was infuriated and frustrated at times and wishing for it to end sooner, whilst at the same time It gave me such a wild experience that I also loved and left me feeling good once I was done.  Old World Blues was also a great DLC, it goes great to play after Dead Money because it ties into a somewhat overall story, whilst very loosely tied together the references to Dead Money and other things are great to find. Old World Blues is wacky and stays much closer to the standard Fallout experience whilst also staying unique, it left me wanting to find more of the crazy things that are hidden within the Big Empty. If having a conversation with your brain, talking to light switches, and fighting Roboscorpions sound fun then Old World Blues will be a blast.  Honest Hearts is probably the weakest DLC for me, the story and location did not grasp me nor really make me want to play more at all, however it was still okay.  Finally the last major DLC is Lonesome Road. I really did enjoy Lonesome Road, it is the highest level DLC in the game and truly feels like the final trial at the end of the game. Lonesome Road throws you into a harsh environment known as the Divide, it truly feels like a post apocalyptic world and feels closer to Fallout 3 in terms of its darker atmosphere in a more urban environment. The final battle and decision in Lonesome Road after going through the atmospheric Divide was truly a great moment, unlike the final fight of the main game it is tough, it feels like the culminating of everything you have done and then given a very difficult final decision.   Overall Fallout New Vegas is a great game and also one of my favorites, it is a game that if you can truly immerse yourself into its diverse world can be one astonishing experience. It has a great story, a huge world to explore, many ways to build your characters, and more, I recommend this game to anyone who enjoys open world RPG games like Skyrim.

You're a ninja. In space. With superpowers.  Best free game I've found - put 585 hrs into it at the time of this review and just now running out of things I want to do in the game. (review update: have 1,300 hrs in the game so apparently I've found more things to do - they regularly produce new content)  PROS: Excellent parkour system, nice weapons, highly customizable character/weapon loadouts, top-notch graphics. On one level it's a pretty simple 'kill 'em all' style fps, but on another level you'll dig into the complex mechanics of damage types and modifying gear and combining player's superpowers (abilities) to complete missions. The unique abilities for each character are pretty badass. Many weapons have unique mechanics, too. You can solo most of the game if you choose, but I'd recommend joining a clan to make things easier when you do the missions that just can't be done solo, and there are many veterans like myself around to help out newer clan members. It's free-to-play, but not pay-to-win. You can literally get everything in the game without spending real money due to the trading system, but that depends on your patience with farming.  CONS: I've played through a few major updates and have come to the conclusion that the developers are a highly talented team of visual artists who lack the skill to fix the persistent bugs and there's nobody in the company that'll speak up and say that an idea is akin to going full ♥♥♥♥♥♥. They do listen to their community and ultimately fix most of their blunders though. The game involves a lot of farming/grinding which can get tedious.

This game offers a bit of history on printing and fonts if you are interested in reading it, but it is more a passive history offering rather than a must for you to go through. You are a colon, rather than a marble, and must not get disconnected from each other. You must go through the platforming levels by collecting the alphabet, 6 asterisks and an ampersand. There are four levels to each world and eight total worlds giving you a total of 32 levels to go through.  If you are quick, this game shouldn't take much longer than 2 hours, but if you want to obtain all achievements it will take a bit longer. The graphics aren't much to talk about, but the music is rather calming during the course of this game.  Overall, not a bad game for the price. I'd definitely recommend it. This game reminds me a lot of Nightsky.

review_text: I'd really like to reccomend this game - But the problems it has and the cut scenes / frames of animation missing make this game tough to play on the PC. This is not the original disk version of the game that was released by other publishers instead it's been optimized for tablets and ported over to the PC. The mouse controls run too fast and there is no way to adjust this. The way you advance through some scenes makes it impossible or next to impossible to advance to other areas of the game. It's like you almost have to click through everything in the scene your in just to see if something happens where your character can advance. Some NPC characters in game you interact with have limited amounts of animation, but mostly have speech bubbles with audio voice overs. At least most of the FMV is here. Shame since this is a great game that you may want to play through once more - but only if all the bugs present in this game are gone.

Game is rather poorly designed. Pretending it was marketed as a roguelike (which it isn't) it hardly gives the player a chance to, well, play. The fact that within the first 30 minutes I have died 4 times is annoying, but the fact is it really is a card game in that most of what you do is determined by luck. In a respectable roguelike, continual RNG abuse can provide an extreme challenge and defeat most players, whereas there is simply very little skill actually involved in this game. As a player you can slightly alter your deck, but again the deck can just screw you. It's frustrating and random and all based on the luck of some simulated draw, and this is coming from somebody who enjoys Roguelikes like FTL and Gods Will Be Watching. It just feels like the entire game is contrtolled by the deck, and early game there should be a fair amount of actual skill involved. Even buying it on sale with a gift card feels like a huge waste of my money, and I sincerely regret buying the title.

Got this game in a bundle, probably would never have picked it up on my own, I rarely play the genre nowadays.   But I would have missed out on a lot! I don't know how far I'll ever get in the game (got to the part where you go deeper and deeper into the chasm, but got stuck somehow and couldnt progress, must have missed something) but it hardly matters. I had a blast playing it.   There are about a dozen different weapon types, and you can alternate between 2 of them at a time, so you can make very different builds. I mean it's not quite Titan Quest since everything is just damage but it's pretty cool. Also you have deck cards which buff stats and give procs and stuff like that.   I imagine there are more powerful builds but I mostly went shotgun + scythe, just because, it was pretty dam fun, that's why. Tried everything though. I liked the Books as a ranged weap too.   Combat was really fun. I found it to be kinda easy on Normal but then to complete the challenges could be a bit tough. Actually the difficulty felt perfect to me. EXCEPT THAT ONE STUPID PART WITH THE ESSENCE THAT KEEPS SPLITTING!! that was TOTALLY out of place, and was pretty much the only time I ever died. and died and died and died - the difficulty is completely and utterly out of place for that area - might want to give some kind of indication about that .. lost my hardcore char bc of that. And the devs even said they originally meant to make it way harder. What the ♥♥♥♥? it's a good challenge but this is basically 2 steps up from the beginning area... terrible scaling.   But, i gotta give them a pass bc that's practically the only thing negative I can think of to say. It's a pretty casual game actually, unless you do pvp and stuff, i guess. And that's what I really liked about it- when I wanted a challenge, I did the 5 star challenges, otherwise, i just kept moving along, kilin stuff & leveling up.   Good stuff.

Dragons Dogma: Dark Arisen reminds me of some elements of Elder Scrolls games mashed together with The Witcher 2. I feel like this would be the sort of game I would imagine on the playground as a kid, arguing about which two games combined would make the best video game EVER.  I’ve had a mixed relationship with Dragons Dogma. I initially had a lot of problems with the first few hours and called it quits. I restarted a few weeks later, and then thought the game was kind of clunky but fun enough to continue. Following this, I had a 40ish hour period where I thought Dragons Dogma was a really under-appreciated experience and that it was one of the better games I’ve played recently. As I came closer to the end, I came to the conclusion that Dragons Dogma is a heavily mediocre game with a lot of half-baked ideas that come together in a strange package.  DD:DA gives you a surprising amount of freedom with how you play. There are a variety of classes with different playstyles, and you are encouraged to switch around often. You also get to customize a pawn, which is an AI companion, and hire two others from other players. Pawns have personalities and can be wildly different from each other. You're able to hire pawns that have quest knowledge to help guide you, or pay more for stronger pawns who steamroll through enemies. There are a huge number of quests, and a huge world to explore. The combat has a satisfying punch to it, and fighting certain beasts is crazy fun because you can do crazy things like climb up them, hack off tusks, break arms, and more.   Regarding the variety in monsters, you’ll quickly realize that there are not very many things you actually fight in this game. Not too many varieties exist and it’s kind of disappointing that there aren’t a larger variety of enemies to face. Cyclops and griffins are fun to fight, but once you’ve killed your 30th cyclops it’s just not that engaging anymore.  The world is sparsely populated and a good 40% of your playtime will be jogging from one side of the map to the other. The quest design is kind of crappy in that you won't have too many clusters of quests to do at once... you'll run for fifteen minutes to talk to someone for a minute. You then either have the joy of running all the way back, or hopefully having a consumable item to use. The fast travel teleporting is janky, and although you can set your own waypoints, it doesn't fix how grueling it can be to set them up. I've had tons of odd quest bugs as well, like freezing NPCs. The game has a distinct layer of Capcom jank all over it.  The pawn system is the coolest part of this game. You get to customize an AI companion and they learn the game along with you. You can rent other players pawns and bring them along, and they bring knowledge from their worlds. Pawns will also learn knowledge from you and bring them back to their masters. The system has a learning curve, but it is all pretty simple once you get the hang of it. Pawns are a really fun part of the game, however they annoyingly seem to never shut up, so you’ll constantly have between one and three companions constantly babbling about almost nothing. Occasionally a pawn will drop a good hint on how to defeat a monster or a shortcut to take in a quest, but most of the time they seem to just ramble about things they’re observing.  To add to all of this, you'll randomly get cut off from tons of quests or storylines just because you accidentally did a thing, and a mechanic that is never explained to you plays a massive role in the ending. I ended up having a wiki open all the time to hopefully not miss out on something, and constantly alt-tabbing to use a wiki gets annoying. Even then, I kept finding out I had just locked myself out of the best way to get discounts in the game or some really useful equipment. Even though these things would only be fleetingly useful and most of the quests you get locked out of aren't very engaging anyway, it sucks to talk to someone and then suddenly fail ten quests for no discernable reason.  I've really enjoyed playing this game at times, but sometimes the design decisions frustrate the hell out of me. Some people really love the endgame, but I’ve found it to be an obnoxious grind and it feels like I am punished for not following a min-max guide to beat the game, as I’ve missed a ton of quests and my characters stats aren’t ideal. I could run the two endgame dungeons over and over and over again hoping for rare (2% - 8%) drops from certain chests in order to gear up to do the dungeons even faster and maybe kill the endgame bosses which provide you better gear to kill themselves with… or I could just finish the game and call it good.  Notably, there is a NG+ mode, so if you find that you actually really love this game (which I know some do), you can keep the adventure rolling.  If I could, I'd give this a middle rating. I'd kind of recommend it, but some things really suck about it and might prevent some people from enjoying it. I dunno. Sure, I'll give it a thumbs up. I have had some good times with it… but a whole lot of bad ones, too.

'GET HIM!!! PUNCH!!! BACK! JAB!!! WATCH YOU FEET!! NOOOooooo!!!! GET UP!!! GET UP AND FIGHT!!!'  What an awesome game PUNCH CLUB is. Take the Rocky series, 16 bit retro graphics, a pinch of the Sims, a good storyline and great game mechanics and you have the best indie game I've played in 2016.  You control and manage the life of 'Johnny' boxer (Well at least that's how I call him). Feed him, get him a job, try and jungle between training sessions, love interests, learn new tricks, it's all about how you manage to choose your priorities. There's many possibilities and the skill tree offers tons of options, as well as specialities. Very interesting on a tactical level, well balanced and actually quite hard, the progression is quite a challenge.   You'll never get to control the boxer - this ain't Street Fighter.  When the fight starts, you just have to sit back and hope for the best. You can still change a couple of tactical moves between rounds but you'll find yourself in a situation pretty similar to the Football Manager series. It's only about having a good game plane and the best preparation you can afford because once the gloves are on, there's nothing you can do to prevent your guy going down.  Best moment in the game was after an hour play, I'm yelling at my screen and encouraging Johnny, totally into the match.  This game will wake up the Don King that is in you.

*The* scariest game I've ever played. Play through the whole thing alone in the dark with headphones on, and you're a braver man than I.  This title was the follow up to Frictional's Penumbra series, and continues the same style of play. However, unlike Penumbra where the darkness was your friend, it's an ever-present threat in Amnesia. Staying in the dark for too long will slowly scratch away at your sanity, causing you to see and hear things that aren't there. Insanity causes your character to behave erratically, essentially turning you into walking monster pheromone.  Seeing as lantern oil and tinderboxes are in limited supply, this serves to drive the player forward through the game.  The sound design is really fantastic, the graphics are admirable and the environment and monsters are diabolically nightmarish. The only thing keeping this from being a perfect horror title are some simple gameplay exploits.

If I continue to the next scenario the medal is there but if I shut things down and then come back later it is not there. It doesn't matter to me (I am not very good at the game but mostly just have fun 'playing with trains') unless later on it does not allow me to play more advanced scenarios due to 'not completing the earlier ones'. Another glitch (might be my memory too) - seems like when I had this game back when it was new I could (in the financial portions of the game) buy stock in the other AI companies and also that I could get a $500,000 loan from the bank - can not seem to do either of those things now. And now, for what it is worth, the last two games I've played the medals are left on the board.

'Would I recommend this game' is a difficult question. The game itself is good. It may be the weakest installment in the Bioshock franchise to date, but the weakest installment of an amazing series is still pretty great. The reason I decided to say 'no' is because of the amount of crap I had to go through just to get the game to play. I have about 16 hours of playtime logged, and I'm willing to bet a good half of it was spent troubleshooting, checking to see if various fixes worked, replaying segments and sometimes entire levels... it got old, fast. For a game that's been out for so long to not have a single fix for everything is pretty inexcusable, especially one that you're expected to pay for. I ended up looking up various guides on Steam and 2K's support forum to get the game to work, uninstalling and reinstalling the game twice, uninstalling and reinstalling programs on my computer, tinkering with files, settings, and compatibility modes, even editing the .ini file itself. The most prominent issues I had were it constantly crashing shortly after starting the game (just when you get the rivet gun), on the loading screen to Siren Alley, the loading screen for Fontaine Futuristics, and near the end of the Fontaine Futuristics level. All of these required multiple attempts on my part to fix and finally finish.  On the other hand, my closest friend also has the Steam version of this game and reports not having the same problems. But for any game to give anyone this much problem, I wouldn't recommend spending your money on a roll of the dice to see whether or not it'll work (for the record, I had no problems with the original Bioshock, so don't assume that just because you played through that with no problems, you won't encounter issues here). It's truly upsetting that what could, and should, have been a pleasant, fun gaming experience turned into a frustrating nightmare, and as soon as I got to the ending, I uninstalled and washed my hands of it. Multiplayer? Other endings? Achievements? Forget about it, not worth the grief.  If you do decide to give it a shot (in which case I recommend a console version), here's what I thought, issues getting the game to run aside: like I said, it may be the weakest link in Bioshock's Great Chain, but that doesn't mean it's a total waste of time. A lot of the gameplay was simplified from the first game (such as hacking, researching, even the Tonics all being in one area instead of being in four categories with different slots you need to open), some undoubtedly for the better (it's telling hacking and researching didn't appear in Infinite) and some for the worst. There is the mechanic of carrying around Little Sisters and guarding them as they gather, akin to the ending of the first game only much faster and less frustrating (with tonics to maximize ADAM and speed up the process becoming available fairly early). However, there's up to three Little Sisters per level, and each one can gather up to twice. It's really up to you how you decides to handle them, as in the original, but the fact remains you're still going to have to throw down with a Big Daddy and you're still going to have to deal with them somehow if you want ADAM.  The main point that this game got a lot of flack for was the story. The game was, admittedly, a cash grab/attempt to keep the Bioshock name alive while Ken Levine and Take-Two/Irrational were working on Infinite, farmed out to 2K Marin. I, personally, think they did a decent enough job with what they had. The story lacked the symbolism and social commentary that made the first one so powerful, and a lot of the mystique and creepiness that Rapture had during the first outing is absent here. I won't even claim the story made total sense in the end (you pretty much need all the supplemental materials, including the dubiously canon novel Bioshock: Rapture by John Shirley, which did have input from Ken Levine, to make it close to solid). And, to quote the venerable Yahtzee, the original Bioshock's story didn't really need a direct sequel. But I wouldn't say the whole thing is a wash, either. For one thing, there's at least two moments in this game (I won't give spoilers) that I personally felt were some of the most powerful in the entire series to date.  It really seems like when they were at the drawing board, whether intentionally or not, 2K Marin got it in their head that in making a game that wasn't basically an exact rehash of the first, they needed to make it as different as possible, and ended up creating the original on Opposite Day. WHAT FOLLOWS ARE SPOILERS FOR THE FIRST GAME!  Whereas in Bioshock, you play a man that starts out with nothing and gradually finds his purpose throughout the game, in Biooshock 2, the main character has lost everything, including his identity (he's a Big Daddy), and is on a desperate journey, whether out of compulsion or genuine concern, to reclaim the one thing he had left. Whereas Andrew Ryan was a staunch objectivist who constantly extolled the value of looking out for yourself and acting in your own self-interest, the main villain of this game, Sofia Lamb, is a collectivist, with a cult built around being utterly selfless and willing to lay down your life if it would better serve mankind as a whole. Throughout the first game, you're guided by a man who seems kind, helpful, and sincere, but ultimately betrays you and reveals you were an unwitting pawn in his grand plan all along. In Bioshock 2, you're largely guided by a man who is a confessed and mostly unrepentant sleezeball, who makes no secret of the fact that he's mostly using you as a means to get him out of Rapture as well so he can profit off of the technology there. As he knows his plans hinge on you achieving your goal, he's willing to play ball. And let me add that 2K Marin deserves some major props for making a character that is so slimy, yet ultimately charming and likable.  All in all, it's a fun game. You won't miss anything for skipping it, especially now that Infinite is out, and I personally wouldn't blame you if you decided to give it a pass and not bother with this slightly subpar installment in the franchise. But with 2K Marin now in charge of the franchise as a whole, it might be worth it if you're a fan of the series just to see how they've handled it in the past. Just, for the love of little yellow duckies, play it on a console and don't waste your time, money, and possibly hours of frustration trying to play the Steam version.

Fun at first, but as the difficulty increases as you progress the games flaws become glaring. Instinct spotting that fails to work randomly, point shooting failing to trigger, enemies that can shoot you while both of you are behind cover, really REALLY bad camera angles at times etc etc etc.  The thing that really killed it for me though was the fact that enimies wearing the same uniform as you looked at you more than others. For example, if you were at Blackwater and dressed as a security guard, you would get spotted nearly instantly by other security guards, but if dressed like a plumber, no one would notice you. Which was weird considering that the place was on lockdown and there were ONLY security guards walking around. Disguises seemed to not even work most of the time as you were spotted as soon as you walked around a corner. The absolute most frustrating part for me was the fact that the game FORCES you to kill certain non-target characters to proceed, but then penalizes you for killing non-target characters. It was like there were 3 or 4 teams of people developing this game and none of them were talking to each other.  The game started out fun, but the further I progressed the more disappointed I became. The story was not that great and the cutscenes failed to explain why you needed to kill the person DIRECTLY after the cutscene. I had no idea why I needed to kill 75% of the people I did. This game is just an awful mess. The one saving grace of this game is that it came bundled with the Hitman Sniper Challenge, which is FANTASTIC. Sad really, that the minigame meant to get you ready for the main game is better made, more cohesive and more fun than the actual game.  3/10 I do not recommend this game.

On the less positive side, hunting and the levelling system are kinda useless and barely noticable but it's better to have at least some kind of progression than none. Some people complain that 'there's too much shooting involved', although TR series was always like that, do people not remember killing a ♥♥♥♥♥on of bandits on snowmobiles in Tibet or the Venice level full of angry mobsters in TR2?  Besides, running around and annihilating everything that gets in your way has a very certain charm about it as you see Lara transform into this badass gun-wielding death machine as you blow people's heads off with your shotgun or take them out with flaming arrows.

This is a must have for any RTS gamer since it's really the one that brought the genre to the forefront. If you already own the game, then getting this is also recommended since it's optimized for modern systems and supports steam online play. The HD remake was unfortunately riddled with bugs at release but most of these seem to have been fixed. However even though online 'works' and there is a sizeable population, the HD remake still is plagued with many of the problems that affected the original AoE2 such as random out of syncs, atrocious lag every other game, and players dropping randomly. These are problems more to do with AoE2 itself and are not unique to the HD remake.  As for the expansions, thankfully you can filter multiplayer games according to which DLC is required. I would say that The Forgotten is easily forgotten (no pun intended) since the campaign scenarios lack any narration and really although the civs have new art styles, the only one that people seem to use are the Incas. The African Kingdoms on the other hand is a true solid expansion pack. The campaigns are very well polished and are narrated. It's also a blast to play as the Berbers, Portuguese and the Ethiopians. I just wish that both xpacks got the same treatment but apparently The Forgotten was already a fan made modification that was just modified further for commercial release whereas the African Kingdoms was a fresh new xpack. All in all though the online community for this game is still fairly active suprisingly after all these years and it's welcoming to all skill levels.

So far pretty fun, especially with a partner. The writing is excellent--often hilarious--and the setting manages to avoid being generic. The combat system plays well and as a spellcaster, let me just say: the spellcasting system is really, really cool. Through spells as well as physical interaction you can alter your environment and make use of it. There's the old D&D trick of using grease + fire, of course, but you can also do things like create steam or rainstorms, etc.  There's one pretty irritating bug with a workaround. You can't see most of the conversations your partner has except for a brief floating text over their head. If you open up the journal (L) and go to Dialogues, though, you're able to see the conversation as it happens. Not terrible but I hope they fix it.

Warframe is an extremely fun game, with loads of story, meaningful upgrades and progression, and a tantalizing lore that gets flushed out more with each update. Also a nice nostalgia piece for former players of Unreal Tournament, as it's clear that many of the guns Digital Extremes ported the concepts over into this new game. (Angstrum = Rocket Launcher, Flak Cannon = Drakgoon, Torrid = Bio-Rifle) This game only suffers from one crippling flaw, microtransactions. The platinum system makes it nearly impossible to progress with an insane amount of grinding, and is even used for something as ridiculous as weapon slots, and even worse, platinum is expensive (for an in game currency). All in all, a good game, if you don't mind falling behind a little if you're not willing to pay money.

Gone Home is a personal thing. If I were to answer the question 'Should I buy this?', it would depend on who you are.   1. Are you patient? 2. Do you like to drink in your environment, explore and discover a good story rather than being hand-fed it? 3. Do you like 'games as art'? 4. Do you enjoy 'walking simulators'? (No action or puzzles) 5. Is this game on sale at the time? 6. Are the 90's full of childhood nostalgia?  If you agree with all of those, then yes, it's worth your time. I don't recommend paying full price simply because it's far too short for what is asked for and there really isn't much content. It is a one-time experience that'll stick with you in memory, and you may not even consider it to be a video game. It is something indescribable. I found myself in tears more than once due to the introspection and empathy this little gem put me through.  Don't let the outpour of negative reviews deter you from at least giving it a look.

Time for a good, bad, and ugly review. (NON PVP player) Final verdict at the end.   **************** The Good things *********************  Character generation – IMHO more MMO's should adopt this style of both character creation, and customization. Your stats and abilities are almost totally independent of your characters appearance, and there are a LOT of appearance options to customize your hero. I personally Cosplay my DC character as Starlord from the Guardians of the Galaxy movie, and it looks and feels awesome to play!  Action/fighting/button mashing combat – If you are like me, you are sick of the action/hotbar style mmo mechanic. This game has a more traditional action game approach to controls, and works BEST with a controller, but also well with a mouse and keyboard. If you like Games that give you button combos, active dodging and countering, you will like this.   Story – It is clearly a “theme park” mmo in every sense of the word. IMHO it does this well, each story arc is mostly fun, and only a handful felt boring or like something I couldn't wait to finish. Nothing too deep, nothing too shallow. Nice bite sized plots that you can finish in a few hours at a time. Great for casual gaming.   Gotham – The city of Gotham is legit, and makes you feel like you are in the Batman animated series in many ways.   Sound and graphics – for an MMO, these look great with everything maxed out. The colors, the characters, and the environments (especially gotham) are full of eye candy.   Gameplay overall – The game play is very addictive as I mentioned before, even just flying/running/climbing around the cities is fun. The fighting action is great. But another great thing are the movement modes, I find acrobatics/grapplinghook to be the most fun to play, and flight to be the most useful.   Free to play – This is kind of both a good and bad – the free to play model being used here is what I prefer to call – the Extended preview model. The free elements are enough to level you to 30 and beyond, but you will be limited in a lot of ways, and won't get to experiment with all the possible power and weapon combos. As free to play games go, if you enjoy the game you will be getting a LOT out of the game even if you never buy anything.   ********************* The bad things ************************  Grind - The level range is only 1-30, and when it finishes, the game devolves into repeating content for marks which you use to buy gear that raises your level (similar to destiny's light level). This is fun for a little while, but once you see how long it takes to grind for marks, and how much content you have to repeat to do it... you will likely quit, most people I know get their full Tier 1, then either quit, or make a new toon experience another storyline up to thirty, then quit.   Lots of empty space – I can't tell you how often I'm traveling around, especially in Metropolis, and feel like the city is empty and unfinished. Huge sections of the map have almost no reason for going there, except exploring for collectible elements. No random enemies to fight, no quests... I'd say only about 1/3 of the map for each city has actual content in it, the rest is just travel and exploration fodder.   The hideout system – This is boring to me, it's got a lot going for it I suppose if you like these things, but I just can't get into the layouts or designs. However, unlike most other MMO's where a home is just a fun vanity collectible box, you NEED a hideout or else you won't get certain upgrades, buff and perks... My hideout is basically an empty warehouse looking place with a few key elements so I can get my buffs...  Low level instances – The balance on the low level 4 player instances can be frustrating. They can be very difficult to beat at the level they claim to be designed for, and often a high level player will be in it, which seems to make it harder for everyone else.   It's not really free to play – This depends on your perspective, for me, I don't expect games to be totally free... either I need to buy it, or buy things in microtransactions, or pay for monthly. Nothing is free. I like FTP that lets me try a game like this before committing to it with a monthly sub. The problem is, when I subbed, I had no idea what endgame was like, or that I'd basically be grinding for gear...   **************** The Ugly things – Not too many ugly things here... *******************  The Grind – Yes, I mentioned it before, but it really gets annoying past 30. Even though more and more content opens up as you increase your level tier... All it really amounts to, is new instances to grind for marks. There are no new levels, no new story arcs (not really). If DCUO had proper content expansions for post 30, something to give you a REAL reason to level up (new story arcs, new levels etc), it would really be something special and give you a reason to grind.   No cross platform between PC and PS4, but you CAN play PS4 with PS3. - This sucks for people like me who are master race PC users, but whose friends and family are all Console limited. My Son and Wife both wanted to play with me, but can't... but at least they can play together... without me! :(  ********************* Final Verdict *********************  Totally worth playing till 30, and a little beyond. Totally worth a short sub to unlock all the powers etc, or buy them outright one by one to save some money. I subbed for 3 months, and will cancel before it renews. I got my fun out of it, and will sub again only if any major additions or changes warrant it.

app_name: Injustice: Gods Among Us Ultimate Edition
review_text: I have an odd relationship with Injustice on PC. On console I got it when it came out, played the story 2-3 times, owned multiplayer lobbies. I got it for PC for the DLC and so I can play while talking with my friends. First reactions: graphics are good. Second reaction: Buggy as crap. The fight was slow motion because when the game was ported from console it was still frame dependent or independent or whatever, meaning they churned out every frame even if the pc couldn't handle it. Most games turn down the FPS if the console isn't good enough and yet thats not how Injustice rolled because hey it works on console, but on pc it made Batman walk slower then a turtle in molasses. This is bad but then kabambam found a fix online and it's good as new. The game is fun, unique to MK but has the same style as it, lots of well known heroes but also vague ones for hardcore fans. Costumes, good story. My one complaint, however?  Multiplayer. Multiplayer in this game is amazing, it gives the highest replay value to all fighting games and I:GAU does it greatly. You can find a single opponet, or you can join lobby-esque rooms where you can challenge people or chill and chat with people. You can find (my favorite) Survivor/KOTH matches, where there's like 5-6 people but there's 1 fight at a time. You lose? Back to the bottom of the list. You win? Fight the next guy. Survivor is just KOTH but when you win you keep the amount of health you had when you won, preventing good people from bein up there forever. You can even bet on who will win, chat with people in the lobby. It was awesome and fun on console when the game came out, yes. But the actual complaint? NOONE PLAYS MULTIPLAYER. I mean, literally not one person. I have sat searching for koth, survivor, single matches and not one, not even being dragged up into higher levels or down into lower level opponets. Find a room? Every single room has 0 people. So to fix my only complaint you guys should totally add me and play with me hahahah please.  So, in conclusion or TL;DR game is GOOD little buggy but worth it. Preferably buy it on sale because 30 bucks yeesh but it comes with a GOOD amount of DLC which some people paid 30 bucks for on its own. Most serious issues have fixes on google so find it and then when you're ready to face those people online to hone your skills there wont be any. Sucks. I give this 10/10 solely so more people will buy it and play online.

One of the most difficult tropico games, given you choose the right settings or workshop scenarios. Lots of fun but if you are easily frustrated play at lowest settings. Some of the dlc is not worth the price but some is. The reviews are pretty accurate and will let you pick which dlc you want. Tropico 5 expands on many areas other Tropico games have not delved into while getting rid of some of the old features (some which were pretty good). As a Tropico game goes this is without a doubt the most unique. In a good way or a bad way is up to the taste of the gamer in question.  1. Economy. The economy is much better and more realistic. There are now features like trade routes, which when used effectively can help a lot. Used without the foreign relations penalties in mind can now result in foreign invasions, and trade route embargos. The settings for sandbox maps are more generic in presentation but work just about as well as other Tropico games. Some building upgrades tend to work a bit differently than Tropico 4. I am sorry that i do not know how to adequately describe the changes, yet i find them welcome. If you like the challenge of making money you will enjoy this. There are also buliding managers that provide various bonuses and in some cases both bonuses and penalties to your buildings and even perhaps surrounding buildings. This means citizens now have roles. Some are good roles like supervisor. Some are bad like rebel or with the espionage expansion foreign spy.  2. Military. The military game of Tropico 5 has new features. Not only do your troops fight the rebels but ANYONE who attacks your island or your rule. There is still the potential for coups, although personally i find it easier to maintain the military factions' approval than previous Tropico's, but this does not mean the military portion of the game is easier. In fact even on the easiest settings it is more challenging than many other Tropico games. Something else that should be mentioned is there are a lot of military options even in vanilla Tropico 5. If you add dlc and the Espionage expansion you get even more. I think the military game is more realistic as well. YOu can also see the stats of you troops which now operate in squads. There are three stats, attack power, health and number of units in the squad. Troops also do not surrender as easily. Sometimes they will not surrender at all choosing to fight to the last man which i think occurs more often with high miltary loyalty.  3. Foreign Relations. This has been changed quite a bit as well. In the colonial era it is really hard to get invaded by the crown. Pirates can invade but are usually easily beaten. I think this is a good balance choice by Kalypso. Nothing is worse in a strategic game than jumping in and being outnumbered and outgunned from the very start, with no chance of preperation. However if you do not prepare for the next eras being invaded by superpowers is much more risky. In the colonial era, the main effect with having bad relations with the crown is lack of trade routes, which can hurt your income and lack of yearly funds sent from the crown, which is not vital but every little bit helps. Latter foreign relations get more tricky to manage, especially in the modern era. Much like Tropico 4 there are 5 factions. Only two can invade you that i know of. The dynamics of trade routes become much more complicated, although Tropico 5 gives you various ways around that, if you invest wisely. You can now import trade goods directly from foreign powers as well as minor powers, smugglers and in the colonial era pirates. With the waterborne expansion smugglers become a more viable option with the smugglers docks building. The Espionage expansion makes significant changes to foreign relations. You now have spies and more ways to detect the roles of citizens. Police stations now help detect the roles of citizens. As a sidenote police stations can now serve the additonal role of fire station with an upgrade. Spies can be used for various missions some of which i am still familiarizing myself with. The ones i am familiar with are steal money and steal technology. That's right Tropico 5 now has research and scientists. Frankly there's not enough of that. It is very reminiscent of a certain mission in Tropico 4 with the exception of the scientists producing the research points. Yet i digress. Nations will now demand more from you including more frequent survival from foreign invasions missions. Most of which occurs in the World Wars era. Embassies are much more functional than the foreign minitry building and provide more options for manging foreign relations. There is a cost for every foreign action, so in actuality foreign relations is harder in my opinion.  Island Factions. Throughout most of Tropico 5 i find the importance of island factions is not nearly as important. Actually with the exception of the military faction I ignore the others. Yet this is because the constitution you develop on your island influences them a lot. With the right options island factions can be a dominant force, or firmly under your control which is how i play. If you give a lot of power to the factions on your island i can imagine those factions being more important than in previous tropico games. Factions can now influnce protests.... well as far as i can tell. There are both faction protests and citizen protests.... as far as i can tell. Citizen protests you can buy off, negotiate to do certain tasks to appease them, or send in the troops. Yet this is all fuzzy to me because i never have protests the way i play. I may have a island wide uprising on occasion which my troops will usually put down, but never protests :). Also with the right constitution option it is now possible by all appearances that some military faction supporters can now fight as militia, which is handy during foreign invasions and those nasty island wide uprisings :). It is very possible to have an army of 200 without much effort by the end of the game. One other thing is edicts now can more directly affect miltary strength. Those militia from the milittary faction work almost as well as modern infantry when used with the right to arms edict. Problem is rebels are stronger and those citizens who join the uprisings. Anyone who invades your island and faces 5 squads of modern infantry, 3 tanks squads, 2 apc squads, fighter bombers and cruise missiles ( which is usally what i run with in the modern era, will be promptly, efficiently and quickly squashed with a minimum of effort and collateral damage. Watch out for those fighter bombers they can kill your citizens.  In summary Tropico 5 offers many ways to play. In fact more ways than previous incarnations with the addition of multiplayer and workshop content. I give the economic aspect a 9/10. The military aspect a 10/10, the Foreign relations aspect a 10/10, and the Island Factions aspect a 7/10.

'A quirky little game about a ghost girl using gravity to collect the spirits of her dead animal friends in her extremly long hair. One of the most relaxing games out there, but don't for a second get the misbelief that its boring.'   The story is lived out in reverse. As you are collecting the animal spirits and terraforming planets you will relive the backstory about the little girl and her animal friends. For this kind of game it's a really good way of telling the story, because the intresting plot does not lie in what happens next, but what happened earlier, before you wake up as a ghost. You will also meet some guardian animals thatr really stand out. The game deliver it's message while still managing to keep the humour.  There is basically not an options menu. There's resolution and subtitle settings and that's it. I'm not sure it needs a more detailed graphical menu because of the simple yet good looking artstyle, although it would be nice with some sound sliders. Any computer should be able to run this with no problem.  Gameplay wise there's not much to talk about. You use gravity as a tool to gain flowers to extend your hair (yes you read correctly) and collect stars aswell as animal spirits and planet parts. The atmosphere is very calm and serene and you can't die (obviously because you're already a ghost...), which makes the game very relaxing. It is the perfect game if you had a rough day and don't have the energy to play anything complicated.  I really reccomend that you give this game a try and also, if you like the game you can spread the love because you get a gift copy to give to a friend for free when you purchase the game.

The Vanishing of Ethan Carter, a narrative adventure game from The Astronauts, is a miracle of game storytelling. It redefines immersion and sets the bar for realistic simulation of the natural world astonishingly high—one could even say, excessively high.   It tells a story about a young boy who appears to have vanished. The gameplay consists of an exploration of his world, uncovering and solving a number of mysteries about what appears to be happening.  The game warns you that it will not be holding your hand, and, through the protagonist, that things are not at all what they seem. The task of the reviewer is impossibly difficult because no explanation of how true this turns out to be can be made without spoiling the experience.  All I will say is this: welcome to the mind of a young boy.  With most games, the game-play is what matters the most. With this kind of game, however, spoiling the story will spoil the game. It is similar to other story driven games like “Papo and Yo,” “Brothers, a Tale of Two Sons,” “Gone Home,” and possibly also the Amnesia games (particularly the second of those). Although the visual style has drawn comparisons to “Dear Esther,” the games are only superficially similar. In 'The Vanishing of Ethan Carter' one is very much a participant in events.  The game world is breathtakingly lavish and beautiful. For a small game, a massive effort at realism has been undertaken, and it succeeds wildly. If you've seen the game (not just screenshots) than you know that my praise is not excessive. Frankly, the story would have stood up well with a far less demanding art style. Yet they brought it off brilliantly.  Like many adventure-story games, there are puzzles to solve (several of these are quite entertaining and clever; have a pen and paper handy), items to locate and put in their proper places, narrative chronologies to determine, locations to discover. The process of uncovering clues and discovering meaning is not difficult, yet the tasks are varied and interesting. The game is not a long one (I stretched it to more than six hours).  However, this game integrates game-play and storytelling better than any game I’ve seen. Everything has a reason—even the fact that despite the realism of the world you’re exploring, the sun never moves in the sky. Everything is seamless and utterly engaging.  None of this would mean anything if it weren’t for the fact that, as the game progressed and the boy’s family situation became more and more clear, I found myself caring about him as if he were my own son. As Prospero the detective, I became dedicated to finding him, to helping him out of whatever desperate situation he found himself in. And the truth, revealed at last, is moving and satisfying as narrative in a way that few games have achieved. I've read novels that were less satisfying than the story in this game. Think about that a minute.   I’ve got to salute the developer team for performing a miracle: they have created a story that is told in a completely believable world that could not be told in another medium.  The game is not without its flaws, the principal one being the auto-save system. The game encourages you to explore, and when you uncover the solution to one of the many mysteries, the solved state of that mystery is saved. Each successive “scene” is saved along with the others as you discover them. However, when you quit the game and return later, any scene that you’ve begun but not finished will revert to its “undiscovered” status. This is annoying until you realize what is happening; if you don’t get it quickly—I didn’t—you can potentially lose hours of wandering about. The game is a luscious world that begs you to wander about. Just realize that once you discover a clue, that means (generally) that there are other clues nearby, and that you need to find them all and complete that story segment before you can safely leave the game. It is true that the clues are generally in the same locations, so this is not difficult, but it helps to be forewarned.  There are also minor inconsistencies (in the version that I finished, it is possible to have two trains at once in the mine…), buttons that don’t seem to work properly the first time you press them, and other minor graphic glitches.  But none of these, even the auto-save system, which the developer assures us would be impractical to change at this point, detract in the least bit from the game. Once you get into the rhythm of it, and have completed your first scene, things proceed very smoothly.  Gamers who liked the games I mentioned earlier, or other story-driven but non-passive games will enjoy “The Vanishing of Ethan Carter.” The art design is best-in-class. The original musical score is haunting and beautiful, and is still playing in my head.  Bravo, Astronauts. Thanks for the wonderful story. I’m looking forward to your next project.

A nice atmospheric story-rich simple game. It's not much game-play wise: you switch by just wandering around, balancing on tall structures, and jumping through rocks and platforms. There are a few very simple puzzles. The jumping part is fun, accompanied by low gravity.  The atmosphere is spot on. Nice dark ambient soundtrack and visuals are unique, and good looking, although there could be more detail in certain more monotonic geographical areas of the game. The story is very good, rich in mystery and even emotion. In addition to the story there are some collectibles, which surprised me as they were accompanied by some intelligent information. The game world is divided into many short levels, all of which are very varied and some are absolutely astonishing in terms of atmosphere and visuals.  The game is short and can be finished in five hours, unless you (like me) get drawn in by the atmosphere and want to explore every corner. Suggested to people who like post apocalyptic atmosphere and mystery.

Borderlands: TPS has taken a lot of flak for a variety of reasons, including game length, lack of DLC, sometimes-awkward humor, impressions that some game areas were rushed, and other complaints along those lines. Certainly there's truth to a lot of that, but the game is quite solid overall, and I've had a great time with it (played through the main storyline with three characters so far).  Mechanically, I think the combat in TPS is better than that in B2, mostly just because of a wider variety of weapons thanks to the lasers, and the added low-gravity boosting and butt-slam techniques. When I hop back into B2, I've found I miss those quite a bit. I've been finding the player characters a blast to use as well, with some fun skill trees to explore. Humor's subjective, of course, but to me the jokes land far more often than they miss, and I'm quite fond of the pervasive Australian influence.  Complaints about the amount of story content are difficult to shrug off when compared to B1 and B2, which were both longer and featured far more story DLC, so your hours-per-dollar ratio is going to be lower than with the other two. However, TPS really only suffers in comparison - there's plenty of story to be had here, and even though it seems likely we'll only ever get the Claptastic Voyage story DLC, there's a lot to keep you amused.  It's certainly a shame that we don't seem likely to get more story DLCs, though. Likewise, some areas do certainly feel like they were rushed. Vorago Solitude feels particularly empty, for one. The TPS narrative is a little underwhelming as well, partially because it's acting as a bridge between two already-established plots, and partially just 'cause the Vault Hunters seem to have less agency this time around than usual. (Not that you ever did anything but accept other people's missions in B1 and B2, but I felt more like a spectator this time around.)  Still, I can't imagine that anyone who enjoyed B2 wouldn't also enjoy TPS, so long as they're not mentally expecting something 'bigger' than B2. B2 is almost certainly the better game overall, due to the sheer amount of content and relative tightness of the plot, but TPS has been keeping me well amused for some time now, and I'm already looking forward to diving in as another character.

This is probably one of the best RPG games made on RPGMaker that I have ever played. Another one that I enjoyed as much is Eternal Senia. The storyline, characters, graphics, and music are all charming. Best yet, you can avoid the monsters so you don't have to have endless battles (unless you want to). There's also very little grinding needed to go from one boss to the next which is great. Now, is the game worth the full cost? In my view, no. The main reason I say this is not because I don't like the game but because the game can be beat in less than 20 hours. I bought it on sale which was completely worth it.

Real Time Strategy *Minor Cliches  Overall 4 out of 5 Complexity 4 Graphics 5 Immersive 4 Innovative 3 Game Pace 4  Yes, this is Homeworld, despite being in a planet it's not another Command & Conquer. The game felt much like playing Dune 2 again (after 20 years!) and it has two very fundamental differences from the C&C series: The resources are more scarce and you need to be more responsible about it and the specialisation of the units require more active control of them other then just throwing hordes on your enemy.  The main campaign has a well written and engaging plot, which makes the game far more immersive than RTSs normally are.  There are some basic things missing, which is hard to understand the reason since this is a quality game. Managing formations of the groups for example is a nightmare, you can manually organise the units while on stop, but if you order an advance all becomes a huge mess by the different speeds. Another things that annoys me is the Skirmish standard 'build quickly and attack quickly' strategy, I would expect a proper strategy game to do not reward this as when it works it flattens out the game, but that is recurrent in RTSs.

Tales from the borderlands takes the borderlands universe of pandora that we all know and love and adds the heavy story based gameplay that telletale have become known for lately with the walking dead seasons 1,2 and the wolf among us.  In this game you play 2 characters, Rhys and Fiona. Rhys is a hyperion employee and Fiona is a grifter.  rhys-smash-card-3 The game starts with Rhys telling a nice friendly individual about the events leading up to his and Fiona’s first encounter. The story telling switches between the 2 of them with the first part of Rhs’s story taking just under an hour if you play it slow.  fiona-smash-card  Speaking of playing it slow there are various random bits of lore which can be found using Rhys’s scanner which you are introduced to in the first few minutes. This can reveal information on skags weaknesses to a persons cause of death to someones favorite type of donuts and all this can be fairly funny.  Rhys has his scanner and Fiona is a grifter, she can find money throughout the world. Money can unlock different dialog options such as maybe a bribe or maybe you might want to buy something who knows. The money system seems interesting and encourages players to explore their surroundings so that all the little touches aren’t missed.  Tales from the borderlands takes away all of the combat that the borderlands games are known for or at least mostly as you are on pandora fighting is pretty much unavoidable. The combat is changed to the standard form of combat for the telletale games, it is all done through quick time events meaning no more running into raider camps shooting everyone full of holes.  The game introduces several characters in the first episode that all have unique personalities and some of them people who have played other borderlands games will know however there are some new guys. As with all of the telletale story focused games there are some characters that you like, some you don’t care about and some that you love.    As you do in the walking dead and wolf among us your choices may result in some characters living or dying or losing an arm. That character you love might just die or maybe you can save him or you might get to choose to leave the guy you always hated as skag food so be prepared for what is sure to be an emotional trip pandora.  Overall Telletale’s form of story telling works extremely well in the borderlands universe and keeps the humor that the borderlands series is well known for and combines them into a great success that is tales from the borderlands, you will laugh and you will grow attached to some characters instantly.    The thing that is different from the walking dead and the wolf among us is the story telling is told from 2 perspectives. This gives a really interesting experience as if something seems a bit strange from one side it might just be explained from the other point of view.  The tales from the borderlands episode 1 is a great success with unique characters, a very borderlands story line, awesome music and is definitely something you should play if you like borderlands, telletale or both.  9.6/10 a must buy for borderlands fans and telletale fans alike I cant wait for episode 2. original post link  https://gamingmalarkeyreviews.wordpress.com/2014/11/26/tales-from-the-borderlands-ep-1-zero-sum-review/

app_name: DARK SOULS™ II: Scholar of the First Sin
review_text: This is Basically a game of the Year version of Dark Souls 2. Is it worth buying if you already own the game and all DLC? Nope. It's only worth it if you don't own the game at all, or if you only have the base game and none of the DLC since upgrading is about the same price as buying them all plus you get all the shiny, pretty bells and whistles.  The graphical updates make a noticeable difference in some areas, but not all of them. Darker areas like No Man's Wharf aren't that different but the view when you first see Majula is absolutely breathtaking with DX11 Lighting.  The updated enemy placement isn't particularly important to new players however it will make new playthroughs somewhat refreshing for returning players.  If you've never played Dark Souls 2 before, or even the series as a whole, this is defienetly a great place to start for new players. If you want a challenging RPG with a solid combat system and a gratifying sense of reward and accomplishment then this is the game for you.

My feelings of Tyranny are very conflicted because it is a game that seems like an objectively superior game than Obsidian's past work, but they made one enormous error which almost ruins a terrific experience. If you've played Obsidian's last game, Pillars of Eternity, you will be fairly familiar with Tyranny's game mechanics, however there are many cool changes, updates, and ease of life adjustments in this new installment.   First off lets start with Conquest mode which is basically a way to forge your character's past few years as part of the invasion force before the game starts proper. This is something I've seen before notably in games like Mass Effect 2, but only if you did not play the first game. Mass Effect 2 had a system where you could basically run through a crash course of decisions that were possible in Mass Effect 1 so that your character was up to speed. Conquest mode takes that concept and expands it greatly while using it not as a stopgap, but as a way to further customize your experience.  You will find that choice was the major driving point of Tyranny. Obsidian wanted to make sure that you felt you had full control over your character's past, present, and future and for the most part they succeeded, with a few caveats. You can only break alliances at certain points and not, say, at a critical point in the mission. Not being able to abruptly turn on your allies to gain advantages seems against the spirit of the choice system and is something that was available in past Obsidian games. The overall story premise is very interesting, The bad guys won and are in total power. You are a part of an unstoppable force taking over the last free region in the world. You are able to gain loyalty and/or sow fear among your allies and various factions. What do you do?  Once the game starts you'll notice Tyranny has eschewed some of the DnD trappings of past Obsidian titles and gone with a more modern cooldown system for spells and abilities, no more will you have to ration spell uses. Which is not to say there are no powerful on rest abilities, Tyranny has those in spades with Chrono Trigger-esque combo abilities and later in the game powerful artifact equipment. Most impressive of all; Tyranny has a robust, engaging, and extremely satisfying fully custom spell creator. The feeling of finding new spell components and being able to experiment was extremely fulfilling and my character was not even a magic user! However, this spell creator highlights the one major flaw of the game, and it is a flaw that almost breaks a truly magnificient effort.  To understand the problem you need to take a look at the game Obsidian made before Tyranny: Pillars of Eternity. Pillars was a truly epic game with a complete story start to finish and very little seemed rushed, In fact, the game may have been TOO fleshed out. Tyranny's near fatal flaw is just as the game is picking up speed, just as your character has become a force to be reckoned with in the story, just as you have enough spell components to make some truly amazing combinations the game ends abruptly with a powerpoint presentation ending for your troubles. The promise Pillars of Eternity and past Obsidian games have made to give you a full on story start to finish was broken. It truly is the biggest gaming blue balls expeience of my 25+ years of gaming. And yet I rated it positive. Why? Because if I had read half of one of the best books of all time, or listened to half of one of the best albums of all time I would still be enriched for the experience. The nature of the modern video games industry is such that DLC is an inevitability. Perhaps Pillars avoided the production schedule axe due to it being crowdfunded and Tyranny did not. Perhaps I will have to pay for the game all over again in DLC to get the rest of the experience. Will I be willing to pay that? Perhaps.     TL;DR Tyranny is the best half of a game you can buy right now, be prepared to buy the second half through DLC

First of, I'd like to thank Steam for promtply refunding this for me. 10/10 for the Steam support team!  Now to the game itself...  When I go to a cheap restaurant and pay 6€ for a steak and it sucks, I suppose it's ok; you get what you pay for.  When I go to a luxurious restaurant with the best chef in town and pay 60€ for a steak and it sucks, I'm sorry but it's NOT ok.  Same applies with The Division. I paid 60€ for it and expected to get a top of the line product. What did I get?  QUEUES. Queues in a game? Really? My total playtime was about 3 hours. out of these 3, 2 hours were wasted waiting in queues for a simple quest of just pressing some buttons on a laptop. The dissapointment here was huge and while I was so excited and open minded to explore the game, this simply killed any enthousiasm and tolerance that I had.  LOUSY CONTROLS. Not sure if this was just me but most of the actions I was trying to execute were either lagging or not working sometimes. For example, left mouse click fire, NOT FIRING. V for medikit, opening up the '?consumables?' menu. They have seriously messed up the 'press and hold' and 'press' controls IMHO. And being an old school gamer, excuse me but where are the duck and prone controls? Are you kidding me?  BORING GAMEPLAY: So much for the storyline here. I jumped on the hype-wagon believing its a viable singleplayer game with great potential. Being a singleplayer type of gamer, I was even considering playing this with other random players online which normally I avoid. But all I saw was a repetitive 'run around an empty and boring city from A to B' and 'shot some hoodied trashmobs rinse-repeat' gamestyle. Sorry but that is not my idea of fun.   The positive  Great graphics. Though I've seen better graphics from smaller budget games, the graphics were actually pretty damn good  Great concept and potential. I was glad to see a game like this with no zombies ruining it. Too bad it got wasted like this.  CONCLUSION  I strogly recommend people NOT to buy this game especially if you are the hardcore singleplayer type. As I said above, paying 6€ for a lousy product is ok but 60€? 60€ to stand in queues in a AAA title?. No, this is UNACCEPTABLE.

(You can listen to my thoughts on this game here if you don't like reading -  http://pauseyourgame.com/2015/09/extra-extra-bit-the-s-s-horror-ship-stasis/)   Stasis is an amazing isometric point and click horror adventure game(damn thats a long description). As a horror game its hits on all the right notes, rather then focusing on jump scares which loose their value at an exponential rate and wouldn't really work for an isometric game, stasis rather focuses on the ever constant mood. The set pieces are what they are, bloody, goory, and just out of your vidision to get a complete picture of exactly you are looking at. As you might imagine, this lack of view is actually a boon to the game as it allows the player to fill in far more horrific items they may dream up to fill the holes. While you may have no problem sleeping, playing the game you will force yourself to consider ethics and morality not within the confines of the game (there is no choice) but your own as you explore this 'S.S Horror Ship'.  As an adventure game it manages to miss nearly every pitfall many recent point and click adventure games run into, game logic. Stasis, when it comes to puzzles are firmely placed in real world logic, meaning the puzzles are based off of knowledge you already posses, rather then say a game like Deponia(which I still enjoy), where the puzzles are over the top and becomes a exercise and understanding the game logic then finding the puzzles. Being from a Isometric standpoint there is a few very scarce issues of not being able to see what you need to see or interact with due to the darkness of the object or how it looks relation to everything else, but as i said, these are scarce.  Stasis has but one flaw and that is its menu system can be clunky or annoying to work through. The game menu is 'chunky' for lack of a better term and hates to remember your volume settings. In addition some puzzles that have a menu system lack a certain responsiveness making it confusing occasionally what you are suppose to be doing.  Stasis manages to pull off what I consider the best kind of horror that a horror game can provide, forcing the player to look inward as they progress through the situations presented to them through the game that they have no hope of fixing, only moving past.

Not your typical horror RPG game, Stray Cat Crossing is more of a Through the Looking Glass style tale about a girl named Cat and the disturbing world that surrounds the house that she has gone into while making off (unknowingly) with your scarf. Why you need this scarf so much and just why this house seems to hold so many nightmares within is soon explained in three parts.  Admittedly, I was waiting for Stray Cat Crossing since greenlight and now that it's out (and cheap) I was happy to play it. You'll be able to play this in one sitting. At the most, you'll do three hours if you're getting stuck. You probably won't.  There's no real combat in this game other than fleeing from a few monsters. Most of the time you'll be scratching your head over some moderate brain benders, but nothing is really too tricky in this game. The hardest puzzle is the red larvae passwords and that shouldn't be too hard if you listen to the other hints from the larva and try to draw connections to the storyline in that area. You'll figure it out eventually, I promise.  This game is unusually storyline-rich. There's less gore and less emphasis on the jumpscares (they are there, however, but tend to pop up only in relevance to what you're doing) and more on figuring out Cat's story. I find that some RPG horror games can overdo it on the scaring you out of your skin aspect. This game seeks to disturb you and keeps you involved by introducing these figures in Cat's life. You're immediately shown that Cat feels guilty over something, but why? What is wrong with this tiny little girl and why are you chasing her through this nightmare house that Tim Burton might rent for a summer vacation?  The music is rather beautiful in the game. I was disappointed to not hear the trailer music anywhere (I'll live!), but every level was delightful. Leaving the game on pause for a quick break wasn't a nightmare. I can see (and hear) the effort. The jumpscare cutscenes are animated very well and while not something that certainly scared me, but made me feel uncomfortable and with an impending sense of doom. I preferred that over screaming in horror. I loved the design of the characters and how other-worldly they felt. While the main character was the only 'normal' person there, supposedly, they also had a special look to them as well, which made me feel like there was something more.  Now that I've lavished praise, let me talk about my dislikes of the game. I was reduced to only using the arrow keys. A mild annoyance and once I was able to sprint, I didn't complain as much. It gave me a reason to rest my hands across the keyboard. However, after only using the jump function for one part of the game, it was relatively useless. I would have loved to see it again after I had mastered it. (Because that is one angry fetus monster and he did eat me a few times!)  Since I'm a handicapped gamer and I often make reference to it, there is quite a bit of flashing graphics in this game. Do not, by any means, play this game if you are prone to seizures. This game may cause severe migraines. Please take a break and do not play straight through. Otherwise, it wasn't too awful. It's not so much a negative as I was neutral on it. Not my favorite, but if I could have turn it off, I would have loved to.  What I loved the most from Stray Cat Crossing is that I didn't expect needing to grab a box of tissues by the end. I was sobbing. I was hugging my laptop and sobbing. I've already mentioned it, but allow me to do so again. The storyline really has a lovely build up. You're already piecing together the truth, but once everything is revealed and you have your confirmation of Cat's life, you will feel like your heart is being ripped out. With emotional lines and capturing the feelings of love between family members and friends, Stray Cat Crossing has a theme of love and never forgetting your past, even if it gives you some of the greatest pain you can ever endure.  Play it. It's a good way to blow off two hours and it really is cheap. You won't get your typical horror rocks off from it, but it's a much more artsy style of horror.  From what I saw of the ending? I hope that means the story really isn't over. Not just yet.
