Alright, time to get downvoted, reported, and have slurs hurled at me. Alternativement, perhaps non.  Undertale is an interactive computer video entertainment executable about how easily you can succeed financially by preying on the minds of the easily entertained. I ask you, before reading this review, don't give games sympathy because they're 'indie' games. The time of indie games being revolutionary has come and gone. With the introduction of crowdfunding to indie devs, the difference between a game funded by a studio and an indie game barely exists. Sure, the indie dev has more creative freedom (and less of an obligation to deliver a decent product) but both don't risk financial loss for the developers, either the studio funding the game, or those who pledged money to the devs. Now, let's start off with what went well. The combat is something fresh, new, and enjoyable. Each enemy feels unique, and the boss battles are fun. Even though each enemy is unique, there are only a handful of types in each area, meaning you'll fight the same enemies over and over, but apart from that, a lot of the ideas are unique and aren't repeated (such as the minigames like thundersnail, and mettaton's jetpack section). And now for the cons. The music is average, especially considering Toby Fox is primarily a musician. A couple of catchy jingles were all it had, the best being amalgam, which you usually only get to hear for all but 10 seconds. The characters are all 2-dimensional and their personalities can be summed up in 1 or 2 characteristics. The game was entirely dependent on people's nostalgia for the Mother series. Hell, the dialogue was barely original, a lot of the jokes were lifted straight from tumblr posts you can see on pages like 'the best of tumblr'. The game did nothing revolutionary or new, instead preying on a tried and tested formula evident in tumblr 'humour' posts and other fandoms that have existed over the years. Also, considering the time it took to develop and it's budget, Undertale was way too short and had a lot of missed potential.  The art style is inconsistent and the overworld sprites are just objectively horrible. The hitboxes are terrible and caused many of my frustrations. I also have a problem with the way the diagonal movement works, as it shows poor understanding of programming, but that's more of a personal quibble for me. As someone who has been using GameMaker for just 2 years but knows how to fix both of these problems, I'm suprised nobody has pointed out these problems yet. The boss combat mechanics are also underappreciated; when starting off with the game, I was hoping to see an area for each boss (as Papyrus is in Snowdin, and Undyne is in Waterfall, I hoped for a spooky world when fighting Muffet), and some of the enemies in said areas used attacks which changed the player's SOUL to the same colour as the boss did. While I'm not sure how this would have turned out, it would have made the game a little longer, and in my opinion, it would be preferable to the sometimes repetitive attacks each enemy has.  The final bosses are extremely easy and are a huge letdown. Asgore was a good boss, but was cut short due to the fact that the final hit you land takes the last 20-30% of his health. I completed Flowey's fight without dying once, due to the fact that as soon as you drop below 20% of your HP, it cuts to the other human SOUL scenes, which heal you very soon after starting. Asriel is ridiculously easy, as you cannot lose the fight. The game places you right back where you started with full HP whenever you die, and one of the ACT commands fills your inventory with items that max our your HP. Sans shows some challenge, but that's only because of the attacks in the GUI, and the janky platforming mechanics.  While Undertale isn't a bad game, it's hideously overrated, and I wish it hadn't been as hyped as it was so that I wouldn't have been so let down.  I can't recommend you play this game, nor avoid it. Buy it, play through an hour or so, if you don't like it, refund it. Letsplayers don't really do such a narrative-driven game justice.

2/10  ... I have NO idea how, but this game managed to be worse than its predecessor. Which is quite surprising, given how the predecessor is by far not a good game.  The Lost Souls is the sequel to White Mirror. Supposedly, this time you're playing with a different character, since the one from the first game seemingly managed to escape. Once again, the one good thing about the game is that it looks good. Everything else is just as bad: barely any challenge, barely any story, barely any sense, barely... Anything.  The puzzles in this game managed to be even WORSE than they were before. How? In this case, you don't read documents that give you clues. No, the game just straight out describes the puzzle for you on the screen. I'm not kidding - in the first puzzle the game just shows a sequence of 6,5,4,3,2,1 and tells you to type it backwards, so you type 1,2,3,4,5,6 and the 'secret door' (which is NOT secret, it's LITERALLY out in the open, right behind you) opens. And every other puzzle in the game? Just type words. The game shows you backwards words like 'nepo pu' and you type 'openup'. THAT'S IT.  Oh and if you somehow mistype one of the solutions - if you accidentally type another letter (which isn't hard to do, considering you use WASD to walk), for instance? You're locked out of the puzzle. I'm not even kidding. You can't retry the puzzle. You have to load a previously saved game or restart everything.  And the ending?   THE MIRROR IS BROKEN AND YOU'RE STUCK IN HELL. THAT'S IT. I'M NOT EVEN JOKING. You have no idea why the character from the first game went to that house in the first place, then you're playing with this character who's actually in hell trying to find the so called 'mirror' only to find it's BROKEN and because of that, there's no escape. It's honestly one of the worst endings I've EVER seen in games.    And last, but not least - the game crashes. A lot. I had to retry chapter 3 three times because the game would just constantly crash.  Now, I really, REALLY want to support small devs. I know there's time and effort invested into doing games and it's often not easy doing it alone or with a small team. And these games (The Lost Souls, White Mirror, Verge: Lost Chapter, My Bones) are super cheap, so I wanted to say that at least they're worth the price for a short experience. But despite all of these, I cannot recommend these games (except for My Bones which I haven't played yet). They're just bad and it's not like there aren't any good options I could recommend instead - for instance, Black Rose, which is FREE.  If I could send a message to this dev, I'd suggest that he stop making all these incredibly short games and instead just invest time into making one that's longer and better. Create a story that makes sense and explains things. Make creative puzzles that make the player have to think for at least a short while. Create some level of challenge rather than just putting monsters that are incredibly easy to avoid. Don't overdo cheap jump scares and constant sounds/voices that come from nothing, create a creepy atmosphere instead (which isn't actually too hard - a dark atmosphere with just the occasional disturbing moment would be more than enough). Find someone to correct your english. Find people to test your game and make suggestions. And only THEN put it up for sale. Or, alternatively, do the same that was done with Black Rose - make a good game and release for free, then make an even better game and then sell that one.

This scintillating adventure of Mankind vs. Aliens beautifully combines careful base management with tense turn-based combat in a concoction that always has you craving the next sip. With a pinch of randomisation, a few fully fledged DLC packs and some interesting difficulty modifiers up its sleeves, the single player alone has the potential to offer near limitless hours of gameplay.   Despite being given nothing but a name and some stats, you’ll soon find yourself getting attached to the soldiers you recruit, train and command on the front line. The plot isn’t anything revolutionary for a game about fighting the threat of aliens invading earth. Gratifyingly though, the game doesn’t waste any of your time explaining or embellishing that which could be ascertained through gameplay instead.  Before closing, I should mention these views come without any contextual experience of earlier games in the XCOM series. But regardless of how it compares to its predecessors, XCOM: Enemy Unknown is a masterpiece that has me entranced.

I'd recommend exploring the optional content for more fun, such as the new set of unbreakable armor, master assassin quests, hideout sieges, etc. Word of Advice: Don't worry about bomb materials, they are plentiful and you'll get to restock very often.

Demigod is just awesome, and although small in scale, a great option for fans of DoTA-style games. Select from 10 different heroes (Assassins [specialise in taking out enemy heroes] and Generals [specialise in supporting friendly forces and commanding your own units]) each with unique skill trees and abilities, and each with a different playing style. Go stealth/critical damage, ranged caster, close combat disruptor or otherwise, any character will offer you a challenging experience. Only a handful of maps and game modes to choose from, but don't be alarmed; it doesn't get boring if you play multiplayer. Optional community mods available to crank up the gameplay, too, if that's your thing. All around, just awesome *****

Very very well optimised and you can get very good performance on even less capable systems. To top it all off, there is a superb storyline and gameplay, which will leave you emotional and feeling accomplished. Everyone's favourite female protagonist's latest game is superb. You should buy this.  8 and a half voyages to distant lands out of 10

This is a solid game. Decent puzzles, fairly good ranged combat, and a good overall pacing, including the rate at which you acquire/upgrade your gear. The game is beautiful and the world is really well crafted, pretty big, but completely managable to traverse.  The story is okay, but you kill so many people so efficiently that Lara's disbelief at what she is doing wears thin a little quickly. The first kill is believably world-changing for her, but after that you pickoff bad guys as though she does this every day, with not a lot of remorse or reflection being meaningfully shown. I found that the best bits of the story are, interestingly enough, in the optional collectibles. You get a good sense of the crazy nature of the island from picking up journals and, to a lesser extent, the artifacts.  The game's biggest problem for me was its overreliance on quick time events. Get in close with an enemy? QTE. Hit a ledge after a long jump? QTE. Finish a boss? QTE. Any game that regularly instructs you to 'mash F to ____' should take a step back and reconsider some mechanics.   All in all though, a thoroughly enjoyable game and a great pick up off the Holiday Sale.

After playing a game like Alan Wake, its rather refreshing to find a game that appreciates the platform it was brought into, being the PC, which is notorious for subpar console ports. Forcing anti aliasing and having a very limited graphics options menu does not equate to a great port. Reason being why I should gush over how buttery smooth Max Payne 3 ran on my rig, with its rather extensive graphics menu and fantastic optimization. You never really appreciate a good port until you run across a ♥♥♥♥ one.  Anyway, lets get down to business…  Theres a lot of good in Max Payne, and quite a bit of bad to compensate. One is something the franchise has never really been able to accomplish, being, having an engaging narrative. Max, finding himself in the crosshairs of a New Jersey mob boss, travels to Brazil with a man named Passos, promising tons of cash and escape from his current predicament. What ensues is, from what I’m able to muster from my playthrough, was just a ♥♥♥♥ ton of noise and ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥t. It’s standard action movie fair, with little creative flare. You’ll end up forgetting this the entire thing, along with its unique setting.  Forgetting my two sentence limerick, I like Passos. He’s a funny guy and always has your back in the middle of a fire fight. But everyone else are simply cardboard cutouts, completely two dimensionally with zero personality. Whenever someone is killed off, you don’t really give a flying ♥♥♥♥. You just want you mate to stop crying so you can move onto the next shootout.  That’s another issue, as Max Payne 3 seems to fall into this cycle of moving from shooting gallery to shooting gallery. While, yes, that pretty much describes every single FPS in existence, they at least offer engaging combat mechanics to compensate and enemy variety. While bullet time remedies the issue slightly, a dude with a gun is virtually the only dude you're going to kill. Still, it retains tension and is engaging with the bullet time (effectively slowmo) mechanic, which is a godsend in hotter fire fights. Longer play sessions will tend to get grindy and feel as if Paul W. S. Anderson was behind development.  Going with the theme of combat, the gunplay is fan-♥♥♥♥ING-tastic. Whether it be magnum, pistol, shotgun or assault rifle, every round expended has kick and power. When you clear a room of enemies, you can’t help but feel like god. Contributing to this is the expertly balanced difficulty here. Powering through on Hard Mode, I couldn't help but feel a sort of Dark Soul’s type of challenge in which it is completely fair and challenging. If you die, you died because of you, only amplifying the euphoria of victory.  Now, what I learned from Alan Wake was animating a models lower jaw and hiring the most inept voice actors does not lead to a satisfactory audial or visual experience. So, I feel I should note Max Payne 3 has some pretty good talent with is animators and voice actors, feeling very close to real thing. And NOT the atrocities that walk Bright Falls, with cold, unfeeling eyes. Expressionless faces and barely moving mouths, you can understand why Alan Wake was a horror (as well as learning experience) for developers.  Moving away from the mechanical aspects, the game looks fantastic. Its has a widely variant color palette, with bright colors and high contrast for sunny Brazil. To the drab brown and greys of a snow blanketed Hoboken, Max Payne 3 is quite the visual treat. You’ll often be left in awe at the detail that was carved into the world. Bullet impacts, soaked fabric, creasing cloth, and trash on city streets all contribute to immersive side of the overall experience.  Despite its forgettable story, characters shooting gallery mentality, Max Payne 3 is still an enjoyable experience. With tight gunplay, bullet time mechanic, and decent voice acting, I’d dare to say that Max Payne 3 is a pretty good game overall. Though, don’t expect much to think about when you set it down.   Verdict : 7 -  Good

With the new update, blacklight revamps it's UI and adds some changes. The biggest change is Prime membership. All users who played the game pre-patch get this membership. Otherwise, I believe it's 5 bucks. It allows you to permanently buy weapons with GP. Not neccessary, but still an important feature. Additionally, everything is significantly cheaper (think 5000gp to 1000gp, 12000gp to 4000gp), although attachments are now locked to one loadout. You will have to buy something once for each loadout if you want to use it (such as a muzzle).   But it's the same fantastic first person shooter experience I have loved ever since playing it. Gunplay is tight, accurate, and rewarding Movement is fast Maps are varied and fun with no OP areas to camp It's easy to make GP if you are good Starting equipment is satisfactory for doing the job; you can easily get 200gp per round, around 2000-3000 gp per hour Blacklight is still the pioneer in customization; near infinite weapon setups and equipment combinations Equipment is fun to use and changes up the game significantly EVERYONE GETS TO USE THE HARDSUIT I'd say it's worth the 5 bucks for the Prime Membership. Permanently buying things is an important thing to do, although you can still just keep renting things over and over. Do at least try it out. You won't regret it.

I enjoyed this game back when my friends and I played it in 2014. But as it is now, I hate it.  -There really needs to be one more difficulty setting, between casual and survival. I don't mind, and in fact enjoy, having the need for food. But I absolutely loathe dropping items on death, especially when that can include key quest items. -The movement feels extremely slippery. The difference of 0.05 sec on a keyhold can be the difference between taking a small step and throwing yourself off a cliff to your death. This is only worsened by the abundance of deep pits both above and below ground. -The hotbar feels extremely restrictive in comparison with other similar games. -The intro feels like it skips explaining a lot. You learn to dig, you learn to fight, but not to craft. No explaination for hunger is given until you are already near starvation. -And you are almost immeadiately told to dig to the center of a planet after having just touched down for the first time. Intro quests should aid the player in learning the game before throwing something that ambitious at them.

app_name: Total War: ROME II - Emperor Edition
review_text: Firstly, I will establish that i have been a long term customer and fan of the Total War branch since the beginning of time and really wanted to love this game.   Con: It's really incredible that not only did this company push away alot of its mainstream consumers but also angered much of its Die Hard fans. I mean, it took so long just to make this game playable POST RELEASE. For its premium pricing it is obviously a unacceptable display. (bugs, glitches, freezes etc)   Pro: That being said at least they bothered to manage to save the game and bring back some consumers.   Con: Secondly, i was told Rome 2 would have a 40 percent budget increase on top of the Financial backing of a huge company yet they spent way too much money on graphics and half the kingdoms look like they are are going to a pyjamas party. They should of focused on cultural diversity so everything looks alien and rich in culture from lusitani to iceni to Parthia. Or how about inhancing the trade system, internal polticial system and diplomacy system? It's amazing that indie companies can absolutely whipe this franchise clean from quantity to quality of content. Yet Total War can not? how about taking the time to bring that into play via your expansions? no? well clearly they have not!  Pro: I really enjoyed how building/ improving the city effects the expansion of the city on the map mode and also how that is interconnected to the difference's in cultures. That was something really great that CA did.   Con: When Rome 1 Came out they had a option to actually visit your city and go into it, i clearly expected them to bring this into fruition and capitalise on the city builder Sims/ Cities kind of feel to target a grandure target market but not only did they not do this they removed the city visitor option all together so that we can no longer are rewarded with beautiful visual displays enjoying our incredible city from within.   Pro: Without a doubt the Battle system is Great adding more variaty with army legacy names give us a personal sence of belonging and meaning to the armies we use. The in depth battle system is what saves this franchise.   Con: I have to say that Sega has been suffering as a company for good reason it's apparent that profit has become the main goal and mentality/values of its company instead of having a pro fan, and pro accurate History loving values/ employees within CA, they are making the same mistake as Rome and will fall just as Rome.    I know it might be a tough review but your consumers only mean well and want to see this game and company become the best it can be so that we can throw more money at you guys! Hopefully Sega will learn from its mistake.

I've noticed that the reviews for Morrowind, Oblivion, and Skyrim tend to be about why that game is the best Elder Scrolls game and the others are terrible in comparison. I really enjoy all three of them (sorry, Arena and Daggerfall, you're just not very enjoyable by today's standards), so I figured I'll give reviews for each that focus on what's unique about each of them, and what kind of players that will appeal to.  Oblivion was my first Elder Scrolls game (and my first open-world RPG in general), so it has a special place in my heart. Anytime I hear that music come up when I launch the game, it brings back all sorts of emotions and wonderful feelings related to discovering a whole new type of game that I thoroughly enjoy. As good as the music in Morrowind was, Oblivion's music is even better for me (both are better than Skyrim's).  For positives, let's start with Oblivion being the most deliberate of the three games. What I mean by that is that things are the most contained and orderly in Oblivion. You don't 'accidentally' do anything in this game, as opposed to Morrowind and even Skyrim to a degree. Leveling up is slower in Oblivion, and so you don't race through anything. It gives you plenty of time to savor everything. You don't start a new game and find yourself pimped out with super powerful gear or spells a couple hours later. The landscape is also fairly contained, with each region and city being unique in its own right, while not having any drastic changes. The most extreme the landscape gets (besides when you are transported to the hellish realm of Oblivion or the DLC that sends you to the Shivering Isles) is some snowy mountains in the north. And for this game, that works perfectly. You are in Cyrodiil, the center of the empire, and therefore the most refined. The somewhat subdued landscape fits into that perfectly. Compared to Morrowind and Skyrim, this game world is neat, orderly, and prim and proper. It makes you form an emotional attachment to the game, since you focus less on the landscape and more on people and feelings, while still enjoying beautiful scenery. And when you get to the Shivering Isles DLC, you're well versed in forming those emotional attachments, while now having some very bizarre and magnificent scenery to add to your playing experience.  The biggest positive of Oblivion (for me) is one of the most pleasant surprises in the entire series. My single favorite thing in any TES game is what would seem to be some relatively minor DLC from this game. There are four distinct castles, lairs, caves, and towers available through the DLC. Each is geared towards a specific playstyle (warrior, assassin, thief, mage). There are all sorts of things available for each, and some questlines regarding them. I find myself spending a lot of time doing those quests and getting enough money to afford the upgrades, some of which are incredibly useful (alchemy ingredient gardens, a pirate crew that goes out and plunders for you every week, a dark shrine to Sithis that grants you potent powers based on your infamy, etc.). There are also unique items in each. Basically, they are the greatest player homes in any TES game, and they're official content, not mods.   For the negatives of this game, we will actually revisit some of the positives I mentioned. It's slower than the others, which can sometimes make you feel bogged down. It's polished, nice, and orderly, but sometimes you want something more wild and rugged. Think of it as the difference between living in a nice suburb and living in a cabin in the Rocky Mountains. There's not really anything wrong with this game, but it always stays in the safe zone. There are no glaring errors, but its peak is lower than the other games. Basically, Oblivion is content with always hitting singles or doubles, so that means that there are no strikeouts, but also no homeruns.   Oblivion will disappoint you the least often of any of the three games, but also won't take your breath away like Morrowind or Skyrim does occasionally.

Instead of boring 'cinematic' experiences and terribly overdone 'save the world' RPGs, we get a legitimate video game, which features challenge, innovation and is crafted with love by a development studio who care about the game itself and have made something they wanted to make, not something according to a checklist given to them by shareholders.  - Environmental interactions not seen in a RPG since the old Ultima games - Witty, humurous dialogue - Challenging and dynamic turn based combat - Charming and innovative co-op conversation system  This game makes me feel like the age of good video games never ended, it's rare when a game can just make a critical player happy, this one does, and it does it really well.

This game copies L4D in pretty much every aspect that made L4D fun, which is good, and the things it changed or added are competently executed and fit rather seamlessly together, which is good as well.  Melee combat isn't terribly complex, but it feels great, and timing your blocks right will take some time to master. The weapons and character classes actually feel and play very different from each other, and the loot system, while a bit frustrating when you roll like crap five times in a row, is very motivating. The special Skaven, particularly the Packmaster, still glitch out sometimes, but I haven't discovered anything gamebreaking so far.  If you like co-op, Warhammer Fantasy, loot, or any combination of those three, I can recommend this.

Elsword is sort of like a 3D Maple Story in it's own way. So if you enjoyed that, you might like this.  From the begining it can get qutie repititve really fast. It's not like most MMOs where you have an abundance of quests to do, rather, you only get a few quests per level. This can get pretty frustrating past level 40, as it forces you to either dungeon grind or field grind (Fields are areas with monsters that you can do repeat quests over and over. It's very stamina efficenet on weekdays, and gives a fair amount of xp.) Ereda Island is also a nice alternative if you're looking for PVE mixed with PVP. It's stamina efficent and it gives good exp.  This game does have a stamina system. When you run out of stamina, you can no longer dungeon or gain exp from monsters in field. While most would be turned off by this, I personally don't find myself ever running out of stamina. This might be because I enjoy playing plenty of characters though, so I switch often. On weekends, stamina is unlimited.   I can't really think of many things that are bad about Elsword, persay. Some would call it pay2win, due to the only way you can aquire costumes and such is through the cash shop. Plenty of players are able to play with no trouble and never spend a dime on the game. I have personally cashed, but I do remember what it was like not to cash as well. Not being able to cash limits some things, but I wouldn't say by much. You could also buy KC (The cash shop currency) from other players, but our economy is quite terrible right now so that may not be an option. Whether you cash or not does not limit your ability though, and those who think like that are silly.   I think PVP is the one reason why I think this game is great. The combo system and how everything works together is very interesting. Unfortunately, many people are quite rude in PVP and those who are considered the top players have a large ego. Prepare for the salt if you decide to PVP. (I do not recommend PVPing before at LEAST 45 if you decide to, due to level differences. If you choose to PVP before then is at your own risk.)   http://elwiki.net/  is a great place for game information if you're looking to have questions answered. You can always try the game's forums as well. People are usually kind there, just be careful on how you word things.

I heard a lot of good things about Shadow of Mordor. Now I have it I can write my mind about it.  The game reeks of console graphics. It doesn't look all that pretty on a PC. On the plus side, it runs very well on older rig such as mine although it won't recognize my game pad (it would only recognize XBOX gamepad I believe)  Now, I'm glad the developer decided to make the game with open world sandbox at its foundation. I always put replayability above all else. That being said, the game could feel repetitive after a while. Perhaps because it's quite a good game many people find it hard to stop playing thus they play for extended period of time with little break in between.  The nemesis system that makes the Orc captains dynamic in apperance, status and power is the gem of this game. You can't really appreciate it until you have a very high level orc captain that manages to kill you again and again and again and again and again without failure. I had such an Orc Captain that I never managed to defeat so he rose from being a nobody to a level 20 legendary warchief bodyguard whose level was much higher than the warchief he was protecting. His nickname was Urshak 'the immovable'. He was invulnerable to all melee attacks from men, orcs and beasts alike. He had a talent at killing monsters. His blows were devastating and hard to counter. However, he had a sole weakness that he was injurable to ranged attack (arrows) and this coupled with the fact that he never fled any fight because he was fearless opened the opportunity to take him down. All I needed was sufficient number of elf arrows. After 5 assassination attempts on the Orc captain (it took 5 attempts because I ran out of arrows), I finally managed to dispatch him from a safe distance. An extra magical elf arrow perched atop the rooftop where I was shooting helped me to that outcome.  So his reign of terror was ended without a scratch to me. I finally got rid of my biggest nemesis who was more powerful than any warchief. And that was the most rewarding feeling I got from a game in a long time.......  UPDATE: After playing more it's better than I thought. The branding ability that you unlock in the second chapter enables you to turn Orc or Uruk captains and warchiefs into your followers. It's a lot of fun turning a powerful(elite or legendary) captain or warchief into your follower. Their followers in turn too become friendly to you thus enabling you to amass an army or Orc and Uruks. It makes you feel even more badass to have Uruks and Orcs as your followers.  Perhaps I forgot to mention this but the game allows you to gain intel on any captains and warchiefs. Once you have obtained intel on them you will be privy to all their strengths and weaknesses and this information would help you in either dispatching them or turning them into your followers. Additionally each captain and warchief has their own personality. It really makes them to be perceived as a distinct individual. This in turn helps with the immersion in the nemesis system.  Coupled with the open sandbox world, this game is cool beyond belief. You can have your own army or Uruks and Orcs. Highly recommended.

Effin great. Up there with the likes of Chaos Theory, Theif and dishonoured for me. Suprise gem from an unknown dev. Well worth the praise its getting in the steam reviews.  The stealth mechanics checklist is well covered with suprisingly clever AI that follow object noise, last positions, searching hiding places, varying patrol patterns and degrees of alerts. You can hide in the shadows although not to the point blank invisible degree of Splinter Cell or Thief. There are many different ways to kill, some can be considered as accidents IE Hitman style (which won't count as kills on the statistics). Its mostly about enticing guards with noises and clones and getting the upper hand by traversing the complex terrain.  The main unique mechanic is the ability to make clones which closely mimic your abilities but can do things like crawl through certain gates and more importantly are completely expendable. Just remember to cancel them for complete return of their costs. Early on you won't really need to use your abilites. Keen observations, layout knowledge and a couple of key stealth kills will easily get you through most challenges. However towards the second half of the game you will find yourself relying way more on your clones or even just your own cunning ability to manipulate guards.  The levels are massive and complex with widely varying paths through them. Although you do revisit some locations (albeit with some additions to the layouts and of course way higher guard count) which did sour my opinion of an otherwise phenominal game.  I recommend playing it on hard and not to be afraid to kill lots of guards on your first playthrough because the animations for each of the different kills are worth seeing. Going for all the coins in each level with 0 alerts and completing all secondary objectives for a lengthy 20-25 hour first playthrough. (Remember that clones causing alerts or clones doing anything for that matter will not count towards any statistics except kills (unless accidental like cranes/poison/etc)

Great game.  Great story, great art, great characters, great voice acting. I was sad to see the game end. It's of proper length and it has a satisfying ending and I hope it will get a sequel because I got attached to the main characters and would love to see (and hear) them again.  It has varying forms of puzzling which never bored or frustrated me. I glanced at a walkthrough a few times to hasten a few things up which I didn't get right too soon, but I believe everything is solveable perfectly fine if one isn't in a hurry and maybe uses a pen and paper to sort some stuff. ´ A good thing: Throughout most parts of the game you can press a button to have things you can interact with in the world get highlighted. It helps those who don't want to look for 'hidden items'. It's not mandatory so those who love getting their full classic adventuring way can have it just as well. There is also a 'combination helper' that, when enabled, puts a glow on your cursor when you hover with one item over another or over a spot in the world which it interacts with (though it seems towards the end it is sometimes isn't working fully either, intentionally or by accident).  The story could have some logic loopholes, but I need to think some details over because things can get convoluted with various characters looking differently at events throughout different timelines and sharing their (un)knowing amongst each other.  A downer: The English text and English voice acting aren't always aligning. Sometimes something is said but something else is written. In a few instances I had text or voice lines appear from previous scenes that made absolutely no sense in the scenes I was in at the time. One line (well, it was just one word, but still) was skipped away quickly even though I didn't press any button at all. There is a background journey 'log' of sort you can look up and I noticed one text part not existing at all in the English version (it isn't just lacking translation - it's really simply missing, you get an empty subwindow when you try to read it in English language). These things don't happen too often, but they aren't exactly rare either.  However it's possible the English voice acting is better than than the native one. I played the game in English because I'm used to that but glanced at the German version because the developers are from Germany: It's by no length bad but in direct, short comparison I liked some important characters better English voiced. But I could be biased because, as I said, I played it mostly in English.  I noticed a specific scene that has significantly deviating English lines. The basic idea is still there, but there are some implications which are more direct in the German version. Could be that it's different in English because these are sexual implications. I hope the English version didn't shy away from the German original too often like this because as much as I like this game I don't think I'm gonna replay it in German anytime soon.  Also, that journey log - it looks kinda nice, but it's not very handy to use. It's a wall of images that get added over the course of your journey with some logical lines connecting to the images back and forth. You click on these images and lines and get some info on what happened (which is appreciated because the story can be confusing at times). However some parts of this image wall one may look over accidently because there aren't always clear cuts between the images and connecting lines.  It won't hurt to know the prequel 'Chains of Satinav'. The title 'Memoria' is unfortunate as it should be 'The Dark Eye 2: Memoria' to make clear that things happened before. This is an independent story, but one of the main characters and his female companion clearly had a long journey of their own before this, there are some references to events that happened back then one may understand and relate to better when having played the prequel first. I can't tell right now because I haven't played Chains of Satinav yet. The English voice acting is very great at showing that these two feel a lot for each other and went through a lot together, though, so it could be very well a positive aspect because it wants me to play Chains of Satinav soon.

tl:dr  2nd Review - 95 Play Hours (Aug 24, 2016) ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ So I am still having the same issues with the FPS (see below) even after a couple of updates to the game and Nvidia's drivers.  I have yet to leave my home, initial starting, system. At hour 70, I finally got the blueprint for warp drive, and at that time I also got blueprints for the Hazard Protection Exosuit, Life Support and Jetpack. None of these three items can be discarded, so I don't know why I have received them.  I have learnt all 412 Korvax words - the only language in my starting system. Yes, you will be learning multiples of the same words. I have maxed out my Exosuit and weapon slots, but my ship is at 37 slots.  There is lots of information coming in regarding Korvax history and their philosophy of existence, and now I am getting their story of the Gek - all very interesting stuff.  The game is still a great game!!! I am enjoying the exploring immensely. At this stage I have only explored the 4 planets and 2 moons in my system - and not even exhaustively.  If you have treated yourself to the supposed end-game spoilers, then you will get a kick outta knowing that what is happening is alluded to within the Korvax philosophy. I won't say anymore about that here, but I suspect there is more to come.  At the 100+ hours playing time, I will deem myself ready to warp away from my home system, whether or not I have a maxed out ship. I am in no hurry to get to the universe's centre, but one day I suppose I will look forward to getting there.    1st Review - 55 Play Hours (Aug 18, 2016) ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ Let's start with the problem that almost everyone it seems tends to be having: the current graphics issue (Aug 2016) is one where the game's frame rate plummets to 10 FPS (for me), which requires me to restart the game every 30 mins, almost like clockwork! Other than this it is a solid game. My best FPS has been 69, looking up at a blank sky, my worst FPS is 28 playing on a planet with grassy plains, all settings maximised, borderless window, 1920 x 1200 rez (Alienware Aurora R4 i7, GTX 690, almost 4 yrs old now). I rarely get FPS jitters, except at the start of the game when it is still loading resources or getting in/out of my ship.  The key to enjoying this game is to know that your preferred game play style is one of being an explorer/hunter/gatherer at heart. If you do not like doing any of this, and grindingly so, then give this game a miss, or get it when it is on special.  Is this game worth the full price? I feel it is worth every penny. So many complain of price these days, but never think of the price of going to the movies, or a couple of family take out meals, which last all of a couple of hours of enjoyment eating them, this game will last a virtaul life time longer.  This game provides ample opportunity to explore, which is my preferred style of game play. I have played now for over 45 hours and I have not gone beyond the starting planet. There is so much to find, gather, build - and rebuild when buying new equipment upgrades. Even in the last few hours I have been finding new technology, words, vistas ...  There has been too much expectation regarding the first-person shooter and space dogfighter qualities. This game does not excel at either of these, but rather provides opportunity to mix up your gameplay a little. For instance, if you are tired of mining titanium, take a break and shoot down drones for a while. Had enough of exploring, looking for that cave entrance? Pull out your grenade launcher and create your own tunnels to intersect with what is already there underground.  As for multiplayer (MP), this game is NOT multiplayer in the conventional sense. Hello Games has stated that No Man's Sky does include MP elements, but if you purchased it thinking it was fully MP, expecting to meet up with people and go on journeys together, then you were misinformed. The Steam page says the game is single player only.  The concept of MP that I believe the game alludes to is one of data sharing. I have yet to encounter this myself, having only spent all my gaming time on my starting planet, but I expect to see player-named planets and species as I explore the universe. While MP mode is a very contentious issue, Hello Games, I suspect, will improve the experience over time. At least, that is what I hope.  Currently there is no DLC, but this too is another hope of mine, in which additional assets for the procedural generators will help spice up the game further. Actually, player-made assets would be nice! Having hundreds of creators would certainly build up the asset library quickly.  Score: 9 out of 10 (-1 for initial graphics issues, that will be fixed in due time - I hope!)

Not sure why it says I've played so little -- I've played at least 4-5 hours. Anyway ...  I want to like this game so badly! But I don't!  It's so pretty, the music is nice, and the gameplay is weird and different.   It has all of the elements of being good! But isn't!  What it really boils down to is ... It's stressful.  Each day's goal is to: Find enough food to stay alive, and enough firewood to keep a fire going all night.  Sounds easy, right? Wrong.  The days are SO SHORT. THEY ARE SO SHORT!!! And each one is a crapshoot with an unfair weight on luck.  99% of days after the first 3-5, you will be barely keeping your health at 10-20% while scrabbling together enough twigs to keep a fire going that will burn out halfway through the night.  Then, if you manage to wake up, it's raining, and you waste the entire day trying to find a new location with even ONE berry or ANYTHING of use (and find nothing).  You can't make ANYTHING without recipes. You can't get recipes without food. You can't do ANYTHING, any TINY thing, without cutting into your stamina, and food's the only thing to bring that back. And 25% of the ♥♥♥♥ you find poisons you! Is it hot? Less stamina. Is it cold? Less stamina. Is the wind sort of blowing? Less stamina.   The main character has:      the most aggressive diabetes ever experienced  (people can live like a month without food you know!) ,      a severe head wound  (if you learn more than 12 things, you have to overwrite memories!!!!) ,     the least creativity of any human in humankind  (apparently even snapping twigs off trees by hand is beyond their scope) ,     the stamina of an obese geriatric patient with mono,     less personal item management ability than the memeguy who couldn't hold all those limes,     and the slowest legs to ever both exist and still be ambulatory.   If you do ANYTHING it takes a HUGE chunk of the day. Even if that thing was a mistake, or if there's nothing there to find. There are some days where you can literally try hard to survive, but find NOTHING useful, and do NOTHING to save yourself.   Honestly this is  Dreamlike Death Simulator 2015 .   I got so frustrated, so convinced I suck, that I tried to watch playthroughs to get an idea of what I'm doing wrong. I can't find a complete one! Everyone gives up!!! WTH?  In the story, part of the thing is that you're trying to do something which has been tried a million times, and every time, the character died trying. Which I believe. Because this ♥♥♥♥ is only for the coolest cucumber with the most luck in the world and the most time/patience to do whole chunks of time over and over and over to get it right.  If you die 3x, it's game over, and you start over. So all you want to do is reload. But you can barely ever save because food is so scarce.  Honestly this game would have been TOTALLY AWESOME for me,  if they had ONLY had a mode where it saves every night , so if I ♥♥♥♥ up a day, I can try over. And the mode is optional, so the people with tons of time on their hands can do it the hard way.   I realize games from my youth were like this -- game over meaning you start all over -- but I'm too old for that ♥♥♥♥ now, and it really shouldn't exist as anything but an option for the hardcore in the era of computer games with disk space to save ♥♥♥♥ to.  SO, this game will be awesome for you, if you like to whittle away many hours fighting the VERY UNFORGIVING clock to advance, only to be sent back to start if you're unlucky or make mistakes.  If you don't like that sort of thing, look elsewhere.  Which is a shame, again, because it really DOES have all the trappings of what makes indie games great.

The Final Fantasy series are just one of those things that you just have fond memories of and just don't want to say anything bad about. It's the sacred word that no one wants to question. I remember when this title first came out. I had such wonderful memories of FFII and FFIII (American version) that pretty much set the standard for RPGs..epic story lines, challenging boss fights and characters that you really connected with. When FFIII blew my mind with the destruction of the world halfway through I couldn't believe it and just stood in awe of it's brilliance. FFII with Cecil and the Red Barons along with the Kain, Lydia, Palom and Porom ...just legendary.  I don't know...I just could not for the life of me connect with this game. I got about halfway through and just didn't care anymore. I tried years later for nostalgia and still..nothing. Don't get me wrong, I still think this game is better than most RPGs out there and it deserves a place in gaming history, but just not all the overblown hype it's received. I remember when this originally came out on the PS1 and I thought the graphics were a bit odd because of the polygon craze at the time. This game would have been so much better with a 2d rendered artistcally done scenes. I personally would recommend playing FFII or FFIII (U.S. versions) as well as FFIX or FFX. You will have a much more satisfying experience in my opinion.   Still going to recommend because come on..it's Final Fantasy, but just average Final Fantasy.

It's very hard to review this game, so I'll just throw out the basics--  This game is fun. It's not amazing, but it's very solid. There are a ton of diverse characters to try out, so there's something for every playstyle. The obvious flaw is the failing playerbase-- it can be very hard to find matches, and if you're inexperienced, you have a good chance of being matched up against premade teams of very experienced players, which can be frustrating.  This game has serious potential, and although it's been fading fast, I'm still holding up hope that this game can be brought back to life. At this time (the fall sale) the game is only $15, which is a great deal even to just play this for single player/co-op story mode! So if this game intrigues you, I say take a chance on it! It's fun and you'll be contributing to bringing life into this community to make the game even better.

This is an excellent party or singleplayer game that is similar to Super Smash Bros in terms of gameplay style (fast paced platformer arena game against other locally or against AI). It's quite fun and fast paced no matter if you're playing against friends or AI.  Pros:  -$5 -Has level editor -Good quality/decent number of premade levels, you don't need to make levels to have good ones -Lots of weapon options/combinations make for different/interesting play styles -AI that I've played is pretty decent, i've only played easy so far and it is pretty good for a beginner and learning weapons better. I'm hoping harder AI presents a good challenge as I improve. -Quick to set up, easy to get into fun. -Customizable and simple controls (but see cons) -Simple, yet good looking artstyle.  Not bad, but could maybe be better:  -**No weapon descriptions**, some are self-explanitory, while some you have to try to find out what they do -It has frozen on me twice over 0.7 hours of gameplay, BUT it was fine after pausing/unpausing, so it was just annoying -Can only aim at 45degree angles, but this does make it slightly harder/you need to plan shots better in order to hit others. I could see it actually being better this way, but I havent decided for sure yet. -I would like independant movement and aiming (currently you aim where you're moving), but this does make it more challenging to line up a good shot, and makes it more skill based.  Cons:  -Only 4 player. I could see it being quite fun having more people, but ONLY ON BIGGER MAPS. -Control customization can be hard to interpret. All inputs are labled as a numbered input for controllers, and the function is a letter/number combination, so it can be hard to tell what you're assigning that button to. -LOCAL MULTIPLAYER ONLY, so no LAN, or over internet gaming. Dev has addressed this and explained that it didnt work well over the internet, but LAN gaming would be nice to have so crowding around a keyboard or having multiple controllers wouldnt be necessary.

Overall a very fun game and kept me hooked all on one play though. A great atmosphere and intriguing cases to solve, while making sure to keep your balance between karma and reputation, was quite engaging.  The characters you interact with, were quite good and were interesting, but nothing groundbreaking. Never the less, they helped move everything along and were hardly 1-dimensional. My one complaint about setting was the whole “ruthless leader” image they try to force. (why do I consider the counsel members “minions” again?). It's a bit eye-rolling, especially when you can be quite nice at times, and you are just trying to keep these dang students from burning the school down (quite literally at times).  The only real flaw is that the game could really use some sort of tutorial or guide for the more confusing parts of the game. It took me almost half the game to figure out how to properly detain a lot of troublemakers, and i lost a lot of reputation just because I knew who the troublemaker(s) were but had no idea on how to get them to stop. Another example was the boss of the game, the “fight” was just given to me, and having not EVER to deal with the new game mechanic, I had to intentionally lose, just to find out what everything did.  In the end tho, the game was a unique experience that I had fun playing, and ignoring a few bumps, always kept me on the edge of my seat.

It's pretty clear that The Park was inspired by P.T. in a lot of ways. It's basically an interactive teaser for The Secret World which is a fairly decent MMO from the limited time I've played it. The execution here is just far too sloppy and predictible to come close to what Kojima did with P.T.   Predictible being the key word here. This is a stratighforward, linear walk sim. This hurts for two reasons. One, it takes little to no logic to get you from start to finish. It's not a rails, but it might as well be as you let the game holds your hand until the credits roll.   The second reason the game suffers from it's format, is there is no sense of danger or urgency. Part of what makes the best horror games compelling is the survival aspect. With P.T. you had the ghost Lisa that was quite hostile towards you. In The Park you know that nothing will hurt you. Any of truly dangerously feeling elements are in-game cut scenes and the remaining horror elements are a series of jump scares that were all so predictable, I found myself bracing and calling out each. I won't rob you of any, but you're gonna know when they're coming.   When it comes to price, I try to avoid saying a game is too expensive. If I really love a film, I have no issues dropping $20 for the blu ray. Games are the same. Even if it's only an hour long, an absorbing and fanstatic journey can be worth the price in my opinion.   The Park, however, doesn't come close to meeting those kind of expectations.

But this game will also deliver sincere delight, if you can obtain victory. It's an experience so brutal it bestows a sense of achievement which is ancient. As old as the NES. Interesting concept at first glance, but it takes away the illusion of choice the moment the mafia inevitably kills you if you don't do everything they say (or, in retrospect, the instant you load a savegame and realise that all events repeat without the slightest variation).  Might as well remove any user interactivity and play it as a (not very good) movie (not very good going on bad because you can't even see the outcome of the cases; as with the rest of the game, they build up your expectations and then leave you hanging).   No replayability at all whatsoever  (even if you manage to survive the inevitable assassination - probably by editing the save file, which seems to be the only way, and which you also need to edit if you want even  the smallest  chance of not failing every asignment due to unvelievably incompetent officers -  all  events are scripted and repeat exactly the same way on every replay, so replayability would be nil anyway even if the  'game'  was playable).   Absolute waste of money  (I can find my jazz & blues, the only somewhat redeemable part of the  'game' , elsewhere for cheaper, thanks),  but designed so that most victims won't realise until they've played too long to ask for a refund , which makes it  clearly  an intentional scam, not mere incompetence (the developers clearly know what they're doing; Hanlon's razor is  not  applicable).  Developer blacklisted, won't be falling for it again.  (Just in case you fell for it and wasted money on this thing and are too late to refund and masochist enough to attempt to  'play'  it, you'll find the xml file you'll need to edit to see how the story ends in  %localappdata%\\..\\LocalLow\\Weappy\\This Is The Police\\ . Editing it should be pretty straightforward, just make sure all three loyalties stay at 150 and all proffessionalisms at over 1000, and add as needed to the amount of recruitable officers and your available money. Although it's quite probably not worth the effort if you have anything better and more enjoyable to do, like staring at a wall doing nothing whatsoever.)

I am a massive fan of the XCOM serries but I feel this game was extremely sub par and struggled to play the few hours I did. I have been a gamer a long time and hold things to higher standards probably more so than most. There are too many good games in this genre to waste time playing this.   Story - The voice diagloues are similar to a Mass Effect but have 0 consequence based on choice as far as I got. The mouth movements are very harsh and there are only 3 or 4 body animations the main character will do. Even in the few hours I played, this REALLY bothered me. I would of rather had still faces with text bubbles popping up like a SNES game versus what was done. The quality of the actual voice acting was decent but doesn't come close to making up for the rest. The story itself is dull, aliens have attacked and you scramble to get your defenses ready and find out why.   Combat - I find the Mass Effect serries combat to be MUCH more engaging than this, but they share a great deal of similarities. It is a 3rd person tactical shooter. You need to use cover and special abilities given to each class to win.   Graphics - Very poor character animations in dialogue. Combat animations are okay. Nothing to brag about.  Over all - I cannot recommend this game because it simply lacks polish. If you aren't good at shooters maybe this game will provide more in terms of tactical ability needed, but for it was put the troops on the flanks and shoot until the enemies are dead. I would absoutely recommend the Mass Effect serries over this because it is better in every aspect.

Runaway: A Road Adventure is a point-and-click (P&C) adventure game which, by design, it is no more than your typical P&C game from the ‘90s.  The story centres around Brian Basco, an initially shy and nerdy Physics graduate from New York whose life totally changes the moment he almost runs over an admittedly hot chick, Gina Timmins, with his car. Could this be faith? Taking her quickly to the hospital, Brian soon finds out that the mafia gang is after her as a result of possessing an enigmatic crucifix given by her father which she pledged to protect. From here on out, it is knowable what is about to happen and is already hinted in the title itself: become runaways chased by the mafia whilst trying to decipher the mystery behind the crucifix.  While the set up already sounds painfully cheesy, enough to put it next to your minty collection of B-movies from the ‘80s, the overall story is actually decent for an adventure game once you witness the conclusion at the end. In fact most of the story’s writing is corny, but feels shamelessly light-hearted. Split into six chapters, it becomes evident after the second chapter that the story itself is something which must not be taken too seriously – in a good way. It can be wacky (like some of the extravagant support characters) and ridiculous at times (I lost it at the “abduction” part), weirdly making it a very amusing, yet silly, experience. The story builds up well as the game progress, but loses some steam about the second last chapter. It provides several funny moments, but there will be a debate on whether this is because they are intelligently funny or because they are so bluntly stereotypical, corny and a bit sexist. Kind of like laughing at a joke which seemed good on paper but was abysmally delivered. When the story does get serious, it can feel like you are playing a low-budget Broken Sword game. And there is plenty inspiration taken from the first two Broken Sword games displayed in here.  Runaway: A Road Adventure is possibly one of the hardest adventure games you will ever play when it comes to solving puzzles. This can either turn into one of the most satisfying moments of the day when you singlehandedly discover the solution to a challenging puzzle, or the most induced rage you will ever exhibit. In general, the puzzles are mostly logical and varied especially in the first few chapters. Conversely, they are exceptionally unintuitive and require a lot of pixel-hunting. A lot. This will be the thing which will turn off most people, but if you are patient and like actual challenges, then this will be rewarding because the puzzles are truly enjoyable -- a bit absurd and scientifically inaccurate at times though -- once you realise their solutions. Obviously, there are still some awful puzzles which require a lot of backtracking for instance, mainly in the second last chapter. Honestly, that chapter was not well designed from a puzzle solving point of view. An advice is to have a walkthrough next to you just in case you get completely stuck at any point. Also, minor puzzle spoiler: getting high is a solution to one of the puzzles. Greatest puzzle ever?   The graphics are impressive in respect to the hand-drawn backgrounds, which might be the main reason why anyone would be attracted by this game in the first place. There is a variety of nicely drawn locations the player can visit, although the last three chapters are featured in the same dessert setting, so it can be argued that it loses some of its magic in the second half. Sadly, the animations in this game are subpar. The closer the camera is to a character model, the uglier they get. This ugliness can range from realising how bland the models are to their very static facial movements. Similar moments are also noticeable in many of the game’s CGI cutscenes which can be a bit horrific to watch. For instance, there is a particular animation of Gina crawling on the ground which looks dreadfully creepy. Thus, retrospectively, the animations did not age well. Rewind eleven years back, I would probably have been happy with them.  The voice acting is a mixed bag. It is average at best, but enough not to harm the playtime experience. On the other hand, the more hours you sink into the game the more you will get comfortable hearing the voices. Sadly, that is not applicable for some of the support characters who simply make you beg to turn off the audio completely in an instant. Inevitably there will be two or three characters which turn out to sound annoying, not necessarily because of the voice actor’s capabilities but the way some deliver their lines – intentionally over-the-top. The audio compression is also poor, making the dialogues sound fuzzy at times. And this, if you did not know, is not an adventure game from the mid-‘90s which all sounded as if the voices were recorded in a tin can back then. Frankly, the fuzziness is not distracting at all, but rather noticeable when characters talk. Lastly, the soundtrack is solid, without a disappointing track.  One personal annoyance is Gina’s portrayal and lack of involvement in the game. Most of time she is either unconscious, kept locked as a hostage or unable to move due to injury making her useless in helping Brian in any of his tasks. Honestly, she is a pathetic damsel in distress with some admirable “assets”. Thankfully, there are signs of personality and humanness to her character than it meets the eye by the end of the duo's adventure. Yet, she lacked any solid character development throughout most of the story, giving little reason to feel attached to her as a character.  In conclusion, Runaway: A Road Adventure is an adventure game which you will either love for superbly replicating that old-school and wacky feel of classic adventure games -- or hate for its unintuitive puzzles and low production values in the audio and animation departments in particular. Unlike more modern adventure games with damsels in distress, such as Deponia which shares almost the same critical flaws, Runaway: A Road Adventure has a lot more charm. Maybe because it does not take itself too seriously, just the way Monkey Island was more of a comedy-oriented adventure game (of course, the latter had top-notch production values which outshine Runaway in all aspects). Regardless, while there is a lot of room for improvement, the story alone provides enough inceptive to invest time into this adventure until the end if you like wacky fast-ride adventures.

Ah, Loadout. Never fails to amaze me. This cartoony, 3D-shooter has stolen my heart the instance I played it- not that I was good. Heck, no. I failed terribly and my first match was a complete disaster. But, the community is great. The gamemodes are fascinating. And you can go naked. Yes, you can dress up your character, the way you like.  Also there's full weapon customization. Do you want to beam them with a blazing burn, shoot them full of electric bullets? Or do you want to give your team members a boost or help them in a period of need by healing? Everything can be edited. The way you like. It's a great game and I recommend it to everyone who likes a bit of randomness, action and cartoony characters.

What can be said? It's Doom. Through and through, it's an ID Software Doom game. Over-the-top represenation of the UAC and what Hell looks like, right down to the name of the monsters. A word to any AAA developers, this is how you're supposed to make a very immersive FPS with scary elements. Yes, there are jump-scares, but a lot of the atmosphere is just as scary as well (the sounds are absolutely amazing).  My only grip with the game is the gun order is not the same as the original Doom. The original Doom was:  1 - Fist/Chainsaw 2 - Pistol 3 - Shotgun/Double-Barrel Shotgun 4 - Chaingun 5 - Rocket Launcher 6 - Plasma Rifle 7 - BFG 9000  I guess they were out-of-order since a few new weapons were introduced (hand-held grenades is one of them). Regardless of that small gripe, this game is totally worth every penny. A total steal when on sale. So turn down the lights, throw on some headphones, crank up the volume and prepare to enjoy the Doom universe through and through.

I played AOW 1&2 for over 10 years and I think it was one of the best of all time. AOW 3, aside from new graphics and user interface, is 99% EXACTLY the same game as AOW 1&2. Sure, the combat is different (and better) from 1 and 2, but it is an exact rip-off of dozens of other turn-based-strategic-combat systems that have been standard in this genre for around 7 years and brings nothing new at all to the table. Specifically, for example, the combat in AOW 3 is nearly identical to Elven Legacy.  What else is different? There are some new spells, some new research, some new units, and a few other minor things not worth remembering. The economy and resources are identical to AOW 1&2 (not that this is a bad thing, but it's 0% new). The productions are just about the same, the structures are the same (except for new graphics). I think AOW 3's best feature is the graphics...but...   The scenario maps are...not that great (or rather, nothing special). Yes the graphics look good, but you can tell that little imagination went into developing the maps for the scenarios. I say this because I played hundreds of scenarios created by the community for AOW 1&2 and they were hundreds of times better than the scenarios that came with AOW 1&2. I'm hoping this will be the case for AOW 3. I have full confidence that the AOW 3 graphics engine will really, and finally, be showcased when the AOW 3 community starts making scenarios. (I'm talking about people like Swolte and The Groll. Please, PLEASE work your magic for us.)  Now, am I saying not to buy AOW 3? No. I think any AOW 1&2 fan will really enjoy it. However, anyone who didn't play AOW 1&2, and has played other popular turn-based-strategy-rpg games, will have no reason to be impressed. But that doesn't mean the game won't be an enjoyable experience. Don't get me wrong, AOW 3 is a quality game, it's just there's nothing at all new about it for this genre.  I've now played for 40 hours on two maps and am sick of this game. It feels like I'm playing AOW2 with poorer maps. Despite the graphics, the maps in AOW3 are POORLY decorated, as if they were decorated by an AI--they look completely generic and more or less the same except for their shape. I've seen all the AOW3 maps, which are much smaller than AOW1&2 (even the XL size), and they look like they were decorated with a palette of maybe 15 tiles. Good maps in AOW2 are decorated with thousands of beautiful tiles and exquisite detail. The maps in AOW3 are plain, boring, and pedestrianly conceived. THERE IS NOTHING AT ALL WONDROUS ABOUT AGE OF WONDERS 3. The tiles are limited, plain, generic, and boring whereas the tilesets in AOW2 were hundreds of times more vast, and indeed, many of them were wondrous. There used to be all kinds of beautiful, cool swirling magically wondrous things in the AOW2 tilesets, but they are nowhere to be seen in AOW3.  I think the problem is that instead of attempting to break some new ground in the genre, all they did was take the easy way out and bring AOW2 into the 21st century (circa ~2007, mind you) by giving it a makeover. That's it, AOW3 (more like AOW2.5) is nothing but a makeover. And they thought they could actually make money doing it. I mean, they must be trying to target the undiscerning consumer who loves playing games in 2014 that were good enough to be out in 2007.  To wrap this up, finally, so I can put the bad taste behind me... I would have been 100 times more satisfied if, instead of taking the time and effort and cost to 'develop' AOW3, they simply put out a new pack of beautiful, wondrous maps for AOW2, which was a game that I can only describe as perfection.

A surprisingly good game. It's strengths rest first and foremost in its presentation. An engaging story built from a fresh and interesting premise, supported by one of the best musical scores in recent memory and great art design. I also enjoyed the gameplay itself. It was challenging and the mechanics, while simple and repetitive, managed to not be boring with an appropriate pacing in the introduction of new abilities.   The camapaign length was appropriately timed and paced as well. At no point did I feel as if the set pieces were dragging, nor did I feel it ended to quickly.  The only negatives, as reflected in other reviews, is that the version of Neo Paris presented in the game begs for a more open-ended exploration experience, instead of the railroaded set-pieces in the game. I did not feel this hurt the game at all, and it's possible that any change to the structure would have taken the story off the rails and perhaps weakened the game overall. But I can't help but feel a little disappointed that there weren't more opportunities to freely explore the well-built world of Remember Me.

The Banner Saga is such a refreshing game on so many levels, the influences are very noticable, but it all feels remarably fresh. The visuals are stunning at times and it's one of those games that really made me wish I was an artist. This fatasy world Stoic has created is something remarkable and, as far as I know, new to video games. There are no dwarves, elves or other things you might expect from the 'fantasy' genre, but rather something entirely new. Gameplay to me was the weakest part, it is engaging and fun to play, and weak for this game is still very strong, but I felt myself wanting to finish a battle quick just to see where the story went. It's very easy to sink many hours into this game. Be warned though while it clocks in at around a 10 hour or so play through, this is the first part of a larger series, so don't expect a full conclusion. There is only one major issue I had and that was that I wish more of it was voice acted, most of the cutscenes are, but the rest is just text. Even though I got over that very quickly, it would have added so much to the game in my personal opinion. If you are like me and have always wanted a hand drawn Disney style Viking themed dark fantasy RPG that plays like the love child of Fire Emblem and The Organ Trail, then this game is for you.

Had been looking into this game for a while, so I caved in when I saw it for sale. So I was somewhat aquaited with the game when I first started paying it, or so I thought. This game threw so many curveballs my way I felt like a major league baseball player at the plate. I found myself creating a routine to checking informaiton, weighting the pros and cons of letting a single person through, and worrying about my dying son (he died) all in one day. All of this can sound like an overwhelming experience, but it felt perfectly balanced throughout the days in-game by working towards more and more to check everyday. You truly feel like you are deciding the fate of Arstotzka with every person you let through and every decision you make. With there being 20 different endingd you are bound to at least get 3 or so of them through a single play through, and the game offeres you a chance to go back to a certian day to change the outcome.   TL:DR If you would like to have an impact on the fate of a single country in the form of an under paid and over worked border checkpoint inspector, then this is the game for you. Lots of fun.   Glory to Arstotzka! ~In loving memory of my son

Oh yeah, and they aren't moving. Meanwhile, I can be running and from a mile away a guy will spin around, have just BARELY pulled up his sights, and get me first shot. This is one of many times this has happened. I've beet less than 10 feet away, aimed as perfectly on someone as you can possibly be, and multiple misses, then they turn around and get me. I know this happens to other people because I've had stellar games reaching 1st place with high kills, low deaths, and with an always moving style of play (because ♥♥♥♥ you if you get online to take a rest and stay prone the whole game) and during some of those games I wondered how I survived so many encounters. They were probably raging about how they should have killed me, and I wouldn't blame them.   I have been telling people to play this game in hopes that the bugs would be gone quicker than this. Cons: Not a huge online player base, you'll get matches but not always instant or at your level, not too many new players online imo If you're uncomfortable with a hot mostly female anime-ish cast you'll feel weird playing it. Super hard to navigate menus initially, this will probably take your first 10-15 minutes.  Overall, Buy if you like the vibe of the artstyle and are ok with spending some time in training mode before you're online compettitive. But this game has been very fun with friends. If you're looking to pick up a first (or on and off) fighting game, I'd 100% recommend this one! Pretty cheap too.

But after a few minutes, I realized that they had managed to keep the level of complexity of this game at the same level, while making the UI work FOR you, not against. Buildings can now be found without having to remember exactly where you placed them, many building upgrades can be done universally, instead of clicking through each one, and the new Era system forces you to adapt as your island changes. Well done guys!

I think i've leveled from 1 - 30 about 40 times now and I just cant take it, you know the hole game just becomes so dull and meaning less it really just eats away at the fun and Co-op this game could have that you have to 'Die' all the time, its happend many times even when walking in groups of 4 people or more, 1 sniper i all it takes. and it just isnt funny at this point. Gauntlet doesnt have drop in play. The only way to get 4 people in one session is to sit in the lobby, but most leave after waiting a few secs making it very difficult to get a full party. In this version like the arcade there can only be one wizard or one fighter, that means often a player will leave if they cant be the class they want making it more difficult to form a full party. Its hard to make a full party now, let alone when it gets old noone will play it meaning that most people will be left playing it alone since without drop in it would mean sitting in the lobby for ages hoping even one person had the game installed to join. If someone quits mid level or if you have 4 good people but one quit and need 4, then you have to quit the match and start over, ugh.... Drop in during gameplay was the first question asked in the forum when it was posted on steam, no wonder it never got an answer :P  The camera is real weird in the game, it does not focus on your character, instead it shares the screen with all 4 players like an old game where people share the tv. This means often people can stuck in corners or cant move at all because of a wall.   The game works, the devs are active in the community. So there is that... Rating: 7/10 Value: $4.99

I sure hope they bring out modding tools for this, they may already have plans too. I haven't actually read into it. But the theme and everything from the combat to how the story plays out. I can see a pretty epic Game of Thrones mod coming out for this. Anyone with me?    But enough of that. About the actual game...  You know when a game exceeds every single on of your expectations? No? Well, you do now! The Witcher three had me from the beginning as soon as I saw how the combat was in the tutorial. Upon seeing a few gameplay video's and brief introductions to the combat I wasn't at all convinced. But the second the tutorial begun I was absolutely blown away. The swordplay is down to a T and the way you implement your powers in the game makes it even better. A little later in the game you'll even see a few beheadings and finishing moves you didn't realise were there at the start and my god are they brutal!    Side quests feel pretty amazing, you feel a part of the story and feel like you're making a difference in the game world and well, you really are! *SPOILER*- walking into an Inn a group of soldiers start making a deal out of you being there. You then get three options, Tell them to back off, tell them basically how dangerous you are (which scares them) or you can buy them a drink, (which works in your favor later in the game) I've chosen two so far, one is telling them how dangerous you are which doesn't really change how hard it's going to be seeing the baron later in the game, buying them a drink gets you straight in and killing them all makes the whole village scared of you and the guards refusal to allow you entry, forcing you to find alternative means to entering the castle. The Baron will also change up his dialog a bit depending on your previous actions -*SPOILER*    The Graphics are pretty damn amazing, considering I don't even have them maxed out. The detail on Geralts face, and when you're talking to different NPC's is just mindblowing. And real time beard growth? Well that just goes with out saying.   Every fight you find yourself in can be a fight for your life and I'm playing on the easiest difficulty! It has a nice balance between easy and hard which I find keeps me intrigued and on edge whilst playing the game without getting extremely frustrated because I can't get past that part.   It goes without saying though, whilst being overwhelmed with positivity the game has a few, although not many flaws.  It's still in the early stages yet, patches haven't been released, bugs haven't been reported so it goes without saying, the game has its fair share of bugs. The occasional (but suprisingly rare) FPS drop a few graphical glitches and one or two problems where the game keeps automatically alt tabbing out of the game on occasion. it can be annoying because during cut scenes it will crash and force you to have to restart. (Bare in mind, that might be a bug or just my computer)   Oh, and I forgot to mention the map size. Well, my god. Everywhere is different, a totally different atmosphere depending on where you are and the weather can drastically change that! you go from dense forests to wide open plains to murky horrible marshes and lightly populated villages. I can't praise the game enough in all honesty!    All in all, buy this game. Just, buy it! YOU NEED IT!
