Compared to Far Cry 3, the intro and story feel rushed. In Far Cry 3 you go through a pretty cool scene to be thrown into a bamboo cage and everything is screwed. In this you're just screwed from the get go. In Far Cry 3 you have a sense of feeling for your character. You know who he is, and what you're trying to accomplish. Save your friends. In Far Cry 4. You literally just came here cause your Mother wanted you too and well ♥♥♥♥ you're all up in a civil war and for NO REASON decide to participate.   Like seriously. You have no reason what so ever to help out the Golden Sun. You just get stuck doing it. When it comes to a story.. I'd NOT RECOMMEND THIS GAME.  As for the controls. They're a little sketchy. I do not recommend using a game pad because there's virtually no aim assist. You will miss constantly. Mouse for the win.   As for the SEASON PASS. I Paid 90 bucks for nothing. Literally I have nothing extra that other's do not for the 60 dollar version. I don't know if this is a bug or something but I see no extra missions or benefits for season pass... Don't even bother.  PvP Multiplayer. More like. Sit in the bell tower and let the enemy team win. Also, why is there no voice chat? There is no communication possible in your team. You have an option for a Friends tab but you can't add anyone through matches or invite them that way. At least, no way I have figured out.  Not like i'd want to invite people who literally spend all their time in 1 point on the map and don't even try to win anyway.   PvP is Golden Path vs Rakasha. And is not ever straight up PvP. It's objectives and you take turn as Golden Path in 1 round, and Rakasha in the next. Basically it's stupid because they are very biased. RPGs vs bow and arrows... RPGs win.

A fun FPS with a twist. Choose one of serveral characters, outfit them with different placeable blocks/weapons/etc., and start the match. You have about five minutes to build defenses on your side of the map, and then the separating wall comes down and you defend your cubes and bases, while trying to destroy those of the enemy team. Build up turrets and radar and walls for defense, charge straight for the enemy, or be a sneak and try to dig under enemy lines and place spawn points, bombs, and more on their side of the map. Nearly every block in the game is destructible, so you have a lot of freedom. Definitely worth trying.

This game can be enjoyable for visual novel fans , however it has some problems and I have to warn you about them .  First of all, the art is mediocre and it looks ugly in some scenes. It is quite clear that this wasn't drawn by a professional. Secondly , there are a lot of plot holes. Several questions are not answered even if you unlock the true ending. Of course this is a story about time travel so some plot holes are inevitable but I still feel that the author took it too far , combining science fiction and magical elements but without really explaining anything. I don't want to spoil the story to anyone who wants to play it, but whoever has already bought it knows what I'm talking about. Thirdly , this game feels a little short for the 10 euros you have to spend in order to buy it, but at least there are multiple endings so it has some replay value. That being said, I do not regret buying this game because as I mentioned before, it is enjoyable. Some of the characters are adorable and the story can be interesting if you ignore all the plot holes. I also liked the little puzzles they added every time you had to choose an option in the story, it is a nice, original idea for visual novels. In conclusion , I recommend this game if you're a hardcore visual novel fan , willing to overlook the few problems it has.

-- addition to review --  We just had the multithreading patch, the game has seen some improvements for sure. But IMHO, the core problems from my initial review still exist. And even though gridlocks have been noticable patched, they havent been fixed. They are still there, just less.   Graphics/Sound is all good. Simulation has its problem and illogical parts, which can become problematic at times.  -- original review -- I would have to say it was worth my money considering how many hours I have spent on it, on the other hand the value of that time was taken down drastically by the problems the game has on a simulation level and with its tools. And considering this game has little to no value for a quick 3h game, but is meant to played for many hours I would have to say it isn't worth it as once you reach a level where it starts to get interesting the nuissances become problems. Graphics/Sound/Music was fine. But the core of the game, its simulation is just bad.  Not to badmouth the game, cuz they could still fix things, but to support my negative review here goes a list of things I have encountered: Laying down roads or rails often results in 'collision' without clearly specifying what is colliding leaving the user to guess what to demolish. Using the 'Load All' option can actually cause your line to be ignored completely. The amount of traffic that buildings generate can bring the traffic in the city to a grinding halt, so that bus routes become so slow that they arent used, causing probably more cars to spawn. You can only draw rails over roads, but not roads over rails or rails crossing rails. You sometimes can build a ridiculous 45 degree slopes, but most other times a 5 degree slope will give some error that it's too steep. There are also some very unbalanced issues. I've seen situations where building a road on fairly flat land cost more to raise lower ground for road than it was to build a bridge over it. Loading bays can hold equal or less to the trucks you have ( in late game ). 2M to remove a football field with 2 bleachers, but 700k to remove a 14 story building. And more of these weird inbalances. I've seen a (in-game) months worth of cars get priority to cross an intersections, while the busses that were the first in their lane never moved an inch. The UI is really lacking in many ways and there is little feedback to the player. Something as basic as visual feedback on the signal states isn't even in the game. I could probably name a few more.

Chip's Challenge is a tale of the indomitable spirit that burns in the hearts of men. The long road stretches before all of us, obscured by great oceans, blazing wildfires, frozen wastelands, and one-way directional floor tiles. Our dreams are hidden behind closed doors, and only the most courageous among us seek out the appropriately-colored keys. Chip is one such courageous individual. He will not rest until his demons are conquered and the challenges set before him lie as blazing piles of ash in his wake.  He endures the pain and the sorrow, he fights through tears and blood, and he hits reset for the one-hundredth time after pushing a block into the wrong water tile. He does this for love, for the hand of Melinda the Mental Marvel, but he also does this for himself. In conquering the insects, the rocket ships, and the chattering teeth, in walking across the fires, sacrificing the technological relics, and traversing the transdimensional portals, he ascends beyond his own mortal shortcomings and into the realm of gods. His chains unfettered, he is unstoppable. He is the hunter, and block puzzles are his prey.  This is not merely a game. This is a rite of passage into your own potential. This is the mountain that stands in your way, the path you must build with your own two hands. This is your Andre the Giant. This is your King George III. This is your Apollo Creed. When the chips are down and it's your call, are you all talk or do you have something to show? Does the blood of the warriors pulse in your veins? Are you one star shining in the night sky brighter than all the other stars?  Just who the hell do you think you are?

I’ve been playing it for getting on for six hours now. I say playing, but what I mean is ‘watch numbers roll in and occasionally press a button when one lights up’. This is a game which solely concerns itself with the acquisition of money, which is then spent on buying things which mean more money. Speculate to accumulate and all that. An infinite snowball effect – buy a small industry, spend its profits on a larger industry, and then a larger one, and onwards and upwards until what began as a handful of dollars trickling in every second becomes billions.  All you have to do is click a button to buy/found/invest in/not sure but it doesn’t matter I guess a new company as and when you have the cash. So initially you’ve got just one lemonade stand, but its profits get spent on buying another one. Make it to 25 and the speed of its profits double. Same again and 50, 100 and 200 (and beyond, but I’m not there yet). Or you can wait a while longer and unlock a whole new industry – newspapers, pizza delivery, donut restaurants, all the way up to banks and oil companies. Each ‘new’ industry brings in vastly more sums of money, but at a slower rate, at least until you upgrade it. So you sit there waiting for these little money timers to reach their end, then you click again, repeat and repeat and repeat, spending everything you earn on making everything you own earn more.  With enough cash, you can hire managers to do the cash collection click for you. At that point, AdVenture Capitalist is supposed to become an idle game. It runs in the background, making lots and lots and lots of oh my God sickening amounts of money, and every so often you dip back in and unlock something more, in order that the next time you go back in you’re earning orders of magnitude more cash all over again. You can even exit the game and when you load it back up it’ll have calculated what you’ve earned since the last time it was open. It’s Fable III without the fighting, basically.  But I didn’t do that. I didn’t exit. I barely even alt-tabbed. I just sat there, watching, waiting, hungry for a button to turn orange so I could buy more of something. I was ravenous for more cash; cash which only had a function within this accursed game. Every line I write in this post, I flick out to see if I can buy anything else. And then I end up staring at these green rows, all refilling at varying speeds.  The lemonade stands on top left move like lightning, earning $762 a second, while the oil company at lower right will give me $89 billion every 10 hours. By tomorrow morning I can have upgraded the oil co enough that it’ll be making me something like $200 billion every five hours. By tomorrow night, I might see my first trillion. A few of them and I can triple profits across the board.  At some point, I’ll sell all my shares and start over, this time with a horde of angel investors impressed by my monstrous ability to make monstrous profits, their input meaning I’ll make money even faster, even bigger profits this time around. And it will go on and on and on and on and on and maybe that would be OK if only I could stop watching. If only I could tell myself I didn’t have to be there, watching, the second another button lit up, that it doesn’t matter if I don’t buy an upgrade as soon as I can afford it, that the world will not stop turning because I’m not at maximum revenue efficiency every second of every minute of every hour of every day.  It’s just numbers getting bigger. It’s the cynical serpent curled aroundthe heart of every social game, every action RPG, every MMO, every gambling machine, but at least it makes no pretence that it’s about anything more than that. There’s this thin veneer of Monopoylisms with a touch of sardonic Fallout nonchalance and lazy references to famous figures, but it’s just set-dressing and it knows it. This is an experiment in how venal we can be.  I know that, even by the standards of videogames, nothing I do in it has any meaning, that I could never begin to justify the time I spent with this. In my only defence, at least I’ve resisted the entirely optional in-app purchases which would make everything happen faster still. It feels like cheating, but I know that even so all I’m doing with this game is cheating myself of time and life.  Yet in an hour I can upgrade my Bank to 25, which will double the speed it cashes out, and that’ll mean something like $60 billion every half an hour. It would be wasteful for that bank to spend even a minute performing beneath this amazing potential.  A lifetime ago, I was making $2 every few seconds from lemonade sales. What a nobody I was.

October 30, 2016  Doodle God is weird in bad ways. I suppose it's technically a game. Put icons together to make other icons, with some flavor tech thrown in to add humor. JoyBits is at least honest in saying 'Mobile. Social. Casual.'  I can't really talk too much about the game, as it is not inspiring to any degree. New icons are added as the game progresses, but it's still simple combos. Puzzles and artifacts are also available, but they play out the same. It's not thoughtful enough to be a puzzle, but the amount of icons prevent brute-forcing strategies. It's almost an easy 100% with guides, but the Patience achievement can be finicky.  Doodle God is just not fun, and shows the flash/mobile game roots even after purchases have been removed. I feel like the game is aimed at younger kids, but the content doesn't seem appropriate. There are multiple icons related to sex and alcohol, and some flavor text with vulgar words, so I can't imagine many parents would be happy. If the game is for matured gamers, I can't imagine that they will be intellectually or mechanically stimulated. New content (more of the same) may be added, but bigger changes are reserved for mobile sequels (Doodle Kingdom, Devil, 8-bit, etc) available for purchase. I expect those games will be boring bundle fodder, which is where I got my copy. Don't bother at any price.

If you want a lego game by Tt that isn't glitchy and is playtested well, go get something else.  The problems started at the menu screen. For awhile, the game played just fine. Now however, the game loads and only half the screen is covered although full screen is toggled on. It can be fixed if you want to go into the options and change the screen resolution so the game refreshes.  Now, as a gamer for my entire life and an aspiring game dev, I can tell you this is the most glitchy game by a AAA company I have ever come across.  As the game goes along, more and more things were ignored within development. FPS drops become more frequent as the game progresses, and parts of the game refuse to register, leaving you stranded, annoyed, and having to restart a level. Then there are the random, even more fun glitches where it continuously changes your character on any button press, rather than just 'Y'. The raptor level, level 1 of Jurassic World, is especially glitchy and where I've become too frustrated to continue when hitting another level breaking glitch.  All in all, the people working on this game had something else going on that was more important. If you want a bug free game that is well polished and playtested, find something else. Show Tt they can't just let this crap slide.

There's a small progression system and limited, multiple class selection each game (so not everyone can have a sniper, bipod mounted MG, etc) but each class plays very differently and offers unique ways to attack or defent depending on the weapons available to it.   Rising Storm is a fun xpack but I've mostly just stuck to the base game. It plays pretty similarly to the base game but with different maps and some other features due to the factions represented and weapons available to them.

app_name: Forgotten Tales: Day of the Dead
review_text: If you know me at all, you know I LOVE solitaire. I’ve been playing it ever since I was a wee lass, but golf solitaire has recently become my favorite version of the game. Imagine my delight when yet another title released featuring the enjoyable gameplay I’ve come to love. Forgotten Tales: Day of the Dead had SO much promise: beautiful artwork, a fantastic theme, and interesting new power-ups and board goals I hadn’t seen done in other games — and yet against all odds it failed. As a lover of solitaire and supporter of indie game developers everywhere, it pains me greatly to have to write this review, but it is my sworn duty to tell the whole truth and nothing but the truth, so help me Dios.  Let’s start with what this game does well, its presentation. Everything about the way Forgotten Tales looks and sounds is an absolute joy. I love the Day of the Dead theme, especially the story snippets with cultural references that lovingly incorporate the mythology behind this Mexican day of celebration. The artwork is beautiful, colorful and uniquely stylized. And there’s the soundtrack, all royalty-free licensed pieces, but the compositions are wonderful, reminding me of several movie scores I hold dear. I simply cannot express enough how much I enjoyed the look and feel of this game. Then we come to the gameplay.   https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ogv6bqeLgg0  Forgotten Tales uses the golf rule set, which involves playing cards in ascending or descending order until all have been removed from the board. There's a bit more to it than that, but it's a very easy version of the game. One might wonder how you can possibly mess that up, and I’ll tell you how; you make the draw pile NOT random. Just let that sink in for a second. A solitaire game that does not have a random draw deck. This one point, this fatal flaw, is what ruined the gameplay for me. Luck of the draw has ALWAYS been a part of solitaire no matter what version you’re playing, and finding ways to overcome it with strategic gameplay and the smart use of power-ups and wild cards is what makes the game so engaging. But when a useable card is drawn at least 75% of the time, it does NOT feel OR play right.   Looking at things objectively, I understand why this design choice was made. With the way the game is laid out with each level having 3 rounds (tableaus) to clear on only one deck of cards, too much randomness in the draw would make the later levels practically impossible to complete unless you got super lucky. However, this could have been avoided by splitting the three rounds into their own levels, thus making a random draw deck less punishing. Doing so would even out the value of the bonus cards, which heavily skew towards the “Joker” and “+5 to Draw Pile” cards, and would also add more focus to the unique board goals. Had these issues been addressed, I am confident Forgotten Tales would have been a near perfect iteration of the game I so love. Sadly, it is not to be.  So, it is with a heavy heart that I give you this news. I wanted to love Forgotten Tales: Day of the Day with every fiber of my being, but I simply cannot look past its sole flaw, and that is preventing me from recommending it to solitaire fans everywhere. Perhaps with a sufficient discount it may be worth it if only to experience the beautiful art and music. However, if you’re looking for the next great solitaire game along the lines of  Faerie Solitaire  and  Chef Solitaire: USA , you’ll want to keep looking.  Bottom Line: Not Recommended.

I bought the collector's edition and when started up I though: 'Hmmm it doesn't look like what I expected'.   The gameplay was fun though although it was not scared and felt like a generic shooter. But i did like the story. Some cried that to many human enemies from start but that didn't bother me. On top of that I could play the entire campaign co-op which is a huge plus for me as I love co-op games.   The versus modes and later bughunt I really loved. Reminded me of the old school games with different modes like escape, team deathmatch and some others. Sadly the matchmaking sucked and it was hard to find people playing after a while. But im glad i bought game from start so I could enjoy this.  The DLC campaign 'Stasis Interupted' I really like because of the story and because also that could be played co-op with up to 4 players.  Would I recommend this game? Yes if you like shooting aliens and humans. I bought 2 copies so i can always co-op with a friend/bro or whoever wants to play co-op.  In fact, editing this review made me reinstall the game on both PC's so i can start a new game.

It's a 2D Worms game but with a 3D engine, unlike previous Worm games in the past ,objects you see sticking out of the terrain in a map now have physics properties, they can bounc around,crush other worms,explodes,emit poison gas or even throw water around and there's now a seperate water in the game that also has some very nice looking physics to it when it moves around, this water doesn't instant kill your worms like the regular water at the bottom of the map but instead drains 1-3 HP per turn if a worm is submerged in it and everything in it moves slower (both worms and projectiles). There's also now classes in the game, Scout,Heavy and Scientists (+ the normal worm). Heavy is the slowest but can take the most abuse Scout is the fastest,has the highest jumps and can perform some crazy maneuvers while walking thru terrain buuut has the lowest HP and dies the fastest The Scientist is pretty much a healer, each turn he heals 5hp and that's about it.  However if you don't wanna play with these new classes you can just simply play the Classic mode where every worm is a regular worm like in the older games.   That's pretty much it, everything else is the same, It's the Worms game we all know and love since the 90's. Only major complaint i have, there's a big lack of enviroments, all you have is Beach,Sewers,Horror and a 4th one which i can't remember the name of atm, while in older Worms games you usully had at least 6 to 10 (sometimes more) enviroments for a map to keep things abit more esteticly different each time.  Bottom line, it's still Worms but with a new makeover and some new fun things added it. Totally worth picking up during a nice sale and playing a few matches with your friends either via the internet or on a single computer.

Even though most people say New Vegas was not as good as it's older brother Fallout 3, it still brought alot of new things to the table while keeping the same Fallout 3 mechanics that we all know and love. If you have an extra $10 laying around, I would strongly recommend this game.

I really wanted to rate this game as a classic but for one thing. I played Fallout 3 first. There's just something about 3 that glowingly highlights what's lacking in New Vegas. This is still a fantastic game with plenty of moral and ethical choices that will have your head spinning just like a roulette wheel. Choose good, bad, cowardly or heroic actions to define your place in a post-apocalyptic America.  The world is massive, the missions are many and range from intense to rather stupid, and I praise Bethesda for trying to be true to the original game, and adding their own special touch to immerse you into a world without hope. A lot of work has gone into this game, and you will undoubtedly get your money's worth, especially with all the dlcs added. If you like open worlds and intense armed violence then this is definitely a game for your collection, just beneath Fallout 3.

So I came into the game expecting exactly what it delivered. It's a game about disassembling and assembling guns.   However, even as free to play games go, the amount of gameplay value (if you can call it that) is incredibly minimal unless you feel like chucking in some money to unlock new guns. If you look past the theme of this game (guns) it is basically just a puzzle game with very little replayability. Disassemble and reassemble that gun! Now do it again, but do it faster! Now do it again, but without hints! To me it seems like they added the RPG element of leveling up just so that somone can't just download the F2P game and dish out all of it 'gameplay' in a couple of hours. It's a lazy and pathetic way at trying to make a full length game out of 1/8th of the material. This game probably would have been a lot better off if it offered all available content at a low price, because I would imagine that would bring in a lot more money considering no one in their right mind would pay 10 dollars to click a bunch of DIFFERENT metal bits in the right order.  I can guarantee you that this will just be another F2P game that you pick up, think it's a bit nifty, then uninstall to save space on your hard drive. The only way this game can be captivating enough for you to play more than 30 minutes is if you have an extreme fascination with how you put together a firearm.

While it may not be to many people's liking (FOV issues, the letterbox format, etc), I must admit I enjoyed the game.  The story itself ranges from 'passable' to 'quite impressive', with many of the latter chapters making up for the terrible writing at the beginning. If you stick with it, you will probably quite like it!  Gameplay wise, it is very close to RE4, albeit not as well-balanced as that, and on occasion you will die purely because of the controls being a tad 'slow', but in many cases your death will be because you didn't watch your surroundings, you didn't hide in time or you simply got a bit too careless.   The soundtrack leaves a lot to be desired, sadly, with many of the chapters making use of the same background music, or on occasion none at all, which doesn't necessarily make sense and it just missing.  The environments on the other hand are mostly really well done, some of the levels are really well thought-out and tie in together very well. There are a few instances where textures are still loading in (due to the game, not the hardware) which distract a little, but other than that the environments are great.  The game has gotten a lot of mixed reviews. Personally I liked it a lot once I got towards the middle of the game, so I urge you to stick with it and you'll hopefully enjoy it.  The single player campaign can be completed in around 7-9h, it is worth replaying in my opinion, you get a couple more decent weapons, and you can collect a lot more hidden extras. I urge you to find all the map pieces, you might like the outcome ;)  The DLC itself is okay and adds a bit more to the main story as you are getting to know the main characters a bit better and see their experiences in this nightmare. It can be finished in a few hours.

Gameplay may be less than perfect (AI rather dumb, repetitive fighting patterns, a bit easy for my taste) but this is more than made up for by the coherent and lovingly created cyberpunk atmosphere.  Highly recommended for fans of the genre !!!

app_name: The Incredible Adventures of Van Helsing II
review_text: After a few hours playing this game with two of the three different classes on normal I don't feel like recommending this game to anyone. I grabbed it on a sale on humblebundle for roughly 7 $ so it doesn't hurt much. The graphics are ok. The sound and voiceacting are ok, the voices of the hero and his companion and her not so funny comments are more annoying than I enjoyed them. The main problem for me with this game is the rather confusing and not very interessting skill system, none of the spells I used in the short time I played the game feel like the do much, the animations are also rather unexciting. I don't feel like I'm playing a game released in 2014, it feels much older, other ARPGs do much much better work released before this game. Go buy torchlight II oder Diablo III if you don't already have, you will have much more fun with this two games. Or get path of exile which is also better in my opinion.   EDIT: Pulled myself together to at least finish the story, which was done in 450 Minutes as I can see in the ingame statistics, which is rather short. The story itself is......maybe it's because I have not played the first part but I don't believe that the story can be much better there. The ending is just...yawn.  !!!*SPOILER*!!! you just hunt that one guy for the whole game and then in the end he just 'explodes' in a few guts an blood after the barrier brakes, no voiceacting before his final death, nothing? come on how ultra lame is this?  At least gameplay got a little bit better with higher lvl and gear but still not phenomenal.

a MEH game. A really well made game, with nice drawings, a very good voice acting, good musics (I truly wished for more musics), and an interesting story BUT Puzzles weren't just easy, they were stupid and very few. The games also highlights what you need to examine, so no need to search by yourself, the games does that for you. It would be a good game for childrens, but since a large part, if not almost all of it, is based on dialogues it would cause them to get bored easily. Also, while the games starts like an interesting deduction game, it ends with a dull solution. 'The culprit it's him because why not?'. No actual deduction simply    You are the culprit because you have the last part of the costume with you, but if you didn't you would have been safe    why is X the culprit? BOH? In short, a good game, made incredibly well, but whose short lenght and easy puzzles made me play it just for the sake of having cards and achievements and not because I actually enjoyed it. The idea was good, but it could have been made far better than the actual result

I don't recommend this game to everyone. You should be motivated to learn the given language.   This game is good at what the developers focussed on. You get dropped into a room and can see what the object is called in the chosen language. However there is no real structure in your approach. You roam the room and add arbitrary words to your list. There is an airplane minigame which to my opinion is totally out of context. And although it is mentioned, the game doesn't focus on sentences. Just vocabulary. However I would have prefered some better testing modes instead of the airplane mode (which is essentially 'time attack' from an airplane).  Pros: - Clear UI - Easy controls - intuitive word listing - good time attack - rich amount of objects - time attack and random words are good testing methods  Cons: - Missing variety in testing methodes - out-of-context airplane game mode - no sentence support - no structured approach of learning

Sometimes it really, really pays off to keep yourself in a complete media blackout before a game is released. The only two things I knew about SOMA before booting it up were that it was made by Frictional Games, one of my favorite developers ever since the Penumbra Tech Demo, and that it had a science fiction theme.  After having completed the game, I know so much more. I know SOMA has nothing to be ashamed of when compared with true classics like System Shock and Half-Life, or more recent greats such as Alien: Isolation. I know that even though the game isn't really very scary or even a horror game at all, there is a very real and definite sense of wonder, discovery and adventure.  I know that while playing, you'll witness SOMA redefine itself in front of your eyes - Several times. The game plays with your expectations in a way that I found wholly captivating, and the intriguing storyline with grand scifi concepts creates a real drive forwards to get into the bottom of the mystery.  I know that there's no real shooting in the game, and the pace is mostly dictated by environmental puzzles and logbook musings. I know that the graphics are vastly improved from Frictional's previous efforts, and that the sound design is sublime.  But most importantly, I know I shouldn't say anything more. I hope someone else can also dive into SOMA as fresh as I was able to. This is the best game Frictional have ever done, and most probably the best game of 2015.

I tend to hate turn-based tactical games. Some games that thrill most fans of the genre feature horrible miss chances on normal difficulty, squad members who have no personalities with weapons that are more interesting than the people wielding them, no personal investment, and a boring 'story' make up that game (X-COM Enemy Unknown is an example of these things I detest). Also, though I played Shadowrun Returns, I thought it was okay, but a glitch made the game freeze at the start of a mission quite late into the game, and I wasn't having enough fun with the game to even bother restarting the mission to continue.  Then came Shadowrun: Dragonfall. I lean toward detesting this genre, but holy crap, this game is great! All the drawbacks of X-COM Enemy Unknown that I mentioned above are nowhere to be found in this game. The miss/hit chances on normal are satisfying, you have characters who can boost those chances for the whole team or its individual members from the very start of the game, the characters are some of the very best I have ever seen in a video game, and the story is quite nice with twists and turns throughout. I will say, however, that the ending is a bit of a whimper, but it wasn't offensively bad (Mass Effect 3) or anything.  Both in and out of combat, the characters are great. Each has things you don't know about them, and after every mission or two, you go back to a sort of home base and the city block that it's in, and you get to learn more about your team members. They have some really interesting stories...especially Glory. Wow. All of the squad members and minor characters will generally have something new to say, and there is so much atmosphere. The NPCs look like crap in this game, but with the isometric view above, it's easy to just not care. the art of the areas is great, and the game's setting is so perfect. The game has this cyberpunk setting with an intriguing tone of simultaneous melancholy and hope.  Not only did the backgrounds and the well-written characters draw me in, but the music is so atmospheric for the locations you visit and the events going on in-game, and the narration writing is superb...by that, I mean the word artistry the game charms you with time and again. There is oodles of reading in this game, as there is no voice acting at all, but I honestly prefer it this way despite normally not having the patience to read fiction...like ever. The game's writing is seductive, I tell you!!! You just have to read the narration and dialog when you go to new places and talk to new characters. It's incredible.  My biggest gripe about the game is probably the tight-waddedness of its 'Karma,' which are the points you spend to make your character more powerful. The game is -really- tight on there points. I received 198, and that's with me doing every mission and side mission, and trying to milk every Karma gain I could find in the game by starting off with 6 Charisma and looking up beforehand on forums what the best 3 Etiquettes (speech specializations) were. If anyone is reading this who isn't going to play a Shaman, do NOT take Charisma above 4. You can buy armor in the northeast corner on a 'trial' side mission for a group that wants to hire you in-game, and it gives you +2 Charisma while you wear it. I believe 6 was as high as I ever needed, and Security and Corporate are the best Etiquettes. The Gang Etiquette would let me spend a bunch of money to skip one fight that wasn't all that tough. Whoop-dee-doo. =/ I wish so much that someone could have convinced me to take Charisma 4 instead of 6. I BEG you, do not take Charisma 6.  Back on topic, in closing, yeah, I typically hate this genre. However, Shadowrun: Dragonfall is magnificently atmospheric through its background, its music, all of the story except the -very- ending, the characters, and the gameplay was enjoyable even for someone such as myself who doesn't tend to like this sort of game.  I do not give many games this high of a recommendation, and I generally dislike the genre this game belongs to. Therefore you, dear reader, can assuredly realize just how amazing this game was to drag this much adulation out of a hater of the genre such as myself.

I have to admit I wasn't expecting too much here but I got a very addicting light war game that has the best AI I've seen for this type of warfare. Alot of this has to do with unit changes from say Total War (like units being able to shoot while moving but suffering reload penalties) and AI for individucal units (they choose more or less whether the march in collumns of 4 or line for instance). The graphics and sound get the job done but you can check a video to judge that, where this game stands out is how right everything feels, from volleys to morale you get a sense of being in command of a large force during the time. Hope to see more from the series in the future but this is great start.

app_name: STAR WARS™: The Force Unleashed™ Ultimate Sith Edition
review_text: TL;DR It's a good game, but there are quite a few problems with it. Buy it at a reduced cost, if possible.  The full review contains MILD SPOILERS. Caution advised.  Star Wars: The Force Unleashed was one of my favorite games on my old PS2 when I was younger, and decided to pick this up after feeling a bit nostalgic. This is actually a port of the PS3 / Xbox 360 versions of the game, which disappointed me somewhat, though it's the same game for the most part. (It may just be nostalgia, but I find the PS2 / Wii version better than this version)  The Story is good, probably 6.5 stars. It's nothing spectacular, but it gets the job done. Taking place in between Episodes III and IV, you play as Galen Marek, the son of a Jedi, turned Vader's Apprentice at a young age. Now known as Starkiller, your training is almost complete, with the end goal being to help Vader overthrow the Emperor. However,    Vader kills you in front of the Emperor, in order to 'Gain his trust.' After being brought back to life, Starkiller feels a little betrayed, and tries to get revenge.     The gameplay is absolutely where the game shines. While it does fall into that same problem that every Star Wars game has, where lightsabers geel way too weak, it's still fun when you don't think about the fact that they're supposed to cut through just about everything. Starkiller is also a master of the Force, however, which is what makes this Beat-em-Up different from other Beat-em-Ups. You can pick things up, throw people and things, shock them, throw your lightsaber, push them, everything you'd expect from a Force prodigy. The boss fights are quite a lot of fun, and regular combat is great hoards of Troopers and Rebels alike.   The game suffers from a few major problems, however. The level design is not very good. It asks for some way-too-specific jumps for the game's floaty physics engine, and failing generally results in instant death. Quick Time Events are very frequent, and break up the flow of combat when you fight larger enemies, such as Rancors or AT-ATs. Load times are ridiculous, and far too frequent. Lastly, the game has a 30 FPS cap, though there are fixes for that available on the Internet.  (Also the Star Destroyer mission is atrocious. The PS2 / Wii version may be better simply because it's not there.)  Overall, I'd probably give the game a 6 / 10. Above average, but not by much. Pick it up on sale, if you can.

In general it is a fine game, however i would recommend getting it on sale, not at full price.

Is Firewatch worth the buy? Well it is tough to say, it was short lived and left me with wanting more. Very atmospheric, had parts which were mainly the first 2/3 of the game on the edge of my seat looking behind my back and sprinting through the forest. The emotional attatchment to Delilah's character and her interaction with Henry is so good. Its hard not to reccomend the game because it hits alot of highs. I definetly expected more and maybe more replayability and I will probably play it one more time to get all the little secrets and maybe even collect the books.   This is a very unique game that inmerses you in Henrys escape from real life problems and to many can be relatable. You will find yourself trying to get Delilah's attention any chance you get. The ending might be its only defect. It is not bad and infact can carry alot of meaning and reality but i felt i was expecting it to go in another direction. I think i would have also preffered if the story would have kept its mysterious conspiracy plot from the first 2/3 of the game. This is waht had me on going crazy and scared in a good way, no pop up bullcrap but legit fear of being alone and wanting Delilah to talk to me so i wouldn't go nuts.   I definetly reccomend this game to everyone. If i were to grade it I would give it a 7/10. + Immersive. + Beautiful art style. + The characters and interactions. + Fun and mysterious. - Short lived around 4hrs (I reccommend turning off location dot to emphasize realism and prolong gameplay) - Leaves you expecting more. - Falls a bit in the last 1/3.

This game flew under my rader until a day before release when I saw a video of the demo. The voice acting in the game is what really made me decide to purchase it come release and let me tell you it's phenomenal! The conversations between the two main characters Henry and Delilah are a joy to listen to and are spectacularly acted which really helps the story flow and feel natural above all else.  The game is surprisingly short (finished it in under 4 hours) but for the 'relatively' cheap price tag and the fact that what you're getting is a well crafted and enthralling experience I feel as though padding out the narative could potentially lead to the game feeling empty or bland if not done correctly. Overall the story flows really well and is captivating so I don't particularly mind about the short completion time.  The art style in the game is one of it's main highlights. With the forest open to explore in front of you from pretty much the get go, many games would feel as though getting from point A to point B is a chore but this isn't the case with Firewatch. The graphical design of the game makes wondering the forest, and even getting lost, a pleasure and it really drives fowards the theme of exploration. Exploring the world is made even better due to the navigation system. While not extremely complex, the mapping tools are actually quite fun to learn and navigate the forest with. (However this navigation is about as much gameplay that you will really get in the game)  There isn't much gameplay in the game itself but it doesn't feel empty or lacking in features as this is made up for by the encompassing story and beautiful art style. If you are a fan of adventure games and/or emotional/thrilling naratives then I would highly advise checking this game out.

Graphics: 9 (repeatedly found myself doing playback to watch the scenary or for events) Sound: 8 (sometimes it can be repetetive in regards to voicing, but the sound of roaring engines compensates) Lastability: 9 (I am 5 races in running at 50% real lap distance. I see myself going the distance more than once). Immersion: 9 (Very realistic career mode and during the race). Enjoyment: 10 (The thrill of tearing past someone at 200mph while making an overtake to make first on the last bend only to suffer catastophic gear failure and end in 3rd place was a moment of sheer omgness :D )   Final Score: 45/50 : 90%

I also covered this game on my YouTube channel:  https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mAoCZLFH7PI   I got a review code for this game and had no idea what to expect. I saw it was a shmup which was a plus, a Bullet Hell which was another plus, then I saw it was ported over from Kongregate and I thought like many formerly Flash games, that it'd be a lousy port of a weak game. I couldn't have been more wrong.  I'm terrible at shmups and worse at Bullet Hell games but I love them anyway. I love the thrill of the score chase, the art of the bullet patterns and figuring out the best path through brutal levels. This game has all of that plus an absolutely insane amount of depth, both to the scoring system and to the amount of customisation that's available for your characters and weapons. There are three difficulty levels which can make things accessible to new players but the level of brutally hard you expect from this kind of game. Most arcade shmups are only a handful of levels but this one has over fifty which is crazy, even if they are each rather short. You'll quickly reach a point where things get very tough, even on the lowest difficulty setting so repeated play is encouraged and indeed necessary.  Succeed or fail, you'll earn different amounts of in-game currency which you can use to buy new characters, new weapons and costumes for them which all have benefits and even cheats and handicaps which impact your score when used. This has the major advantage of you still being able to qualify for the leaderboards if you use them. The leaderboard integration is very slick and while it would have been nice to see each difficulty level get its own roster, each level does have its own and you can sort by a variety of means. You're also shown your current position before starting a level and right after you complete it, assuming you're online.  Bullet Heaven 2 has a very cutesy/twee aesthetic which really doesn't do much for me but in Bullet Hell games, you don't really have a lot of time to pay attention to the art. If you are, you're kinda' doing it wrong.  This game is an incredible value for shmup fans and if you are one, you should pick it up without hesitation. It astounds me that this was made by only one person. There are many well regarded shmups with a fraction of the content this has and when I see games like Darius Burst CS coming out for an insane $40+, this is a no brainer. I didn't expect this game to be good and it could very well be in my top 10 this year. Seriously, get it, it's that good.

StarBreak is a fun and challenging platformer/dungeon crawler that plays like a combination of Realm of the Mad God and Metal Slug. Though I recommend this game, it certainly isn't suited to everybody.  After choosing from one of four classes (unlocked through exp gained from playing) the player then has to explore levels, fight enemies and eventually kill the boss. Enemies drop stat increases that are capped based on the difficulty of the level and equipment that increase stats and alter your abilities. Ricocheting bullets, bullet spread, armour piercing bullets, to name a few upgrades. I'd like to see more variation, but it still keeps things interesting. The hit-boxes feel tight and accurate, there seems to be no server lag, and I've experienced no frame rate issues.  There is perma-death, and yes, you will die. You will lose your character, you will lose all your inventory (except your stashed items) and you may feel as though you wasted your time. Some people find that frustrating, and that's perfectly fine. I enjoy that death has a weight to it, and that I am genuinely afraid of being killed. I'm terrified to go into the next room in case it's a boss; I'm scared to jump down that hole because of what might be waiting for me; I'm anxious about fighting an enemy that I don't yet fully understand.  Levels are procedurally generated, so every time you repeat a dungeon it's a little bit different. I enjoy them, but I think they're a little too long, and I'd rather get to the more interesting boss fights quicker. The few boss fights I have encountered have been an absolute joy, and so far they've been fantastic, a true highlight. Punishing, to be sure, but oh so rewarding to kill. I'm really looking forward to encountering more of them.  My favourite part of this game is the comradery I built with complete strangers. When you enter a level, other players may already be there, or they may follow you in. Let's say we enter the 1st level with 20 people. The first few sections are an absolute cluster of mayhem and destruction, with enemies mowed down left and right. A few people teleport home because they're low; a few more randomly die. It's disappointing, but you move on. At the mid-boss, a couple more die, lose their character and miss out on loot. I feel bad for them. Now we're a 10 man group, and some of us are low on health.  Despite being low on health, the 10 remaining are strong players, and they all make it to the final boss. But this one is far harder than the mid-boss. At first he seems quick and easy, but the lowest HP member who wasn't prepared for a boss fight dies the moment he gets too close. Suddenly, the boss changes into it's 2nd phase; 2 members are caught off guard, panic, and die. As you're all sure the boss is nearly dead, a few members get too cocky and die getting greedy for damage. By the time the boss falls, 4 of the original 20 remain. The loot tastes like victory, but there was a cost getting here. The fact that death is so harshly punished gives me a real sense of empathy for those that die along the way.  Now onto my least favourite part of this game - the monetisation. If you feel like playing a different class, you have 2 options - die, and lose all the progress of your character, or pay real money and store your character. If you want to store more than 6 items, you have to pay. This is possibly my least favourite form of monetisation. The developers have a great game, and have created inconvenience that you can remove with cash. It thoroughly annoys me, because I'd pay for this game to unlock cosmetics, but I don't want to encourage these atrocious monetisation schemes that revolve around annoying the player into paying. That being said, it could be worse. At least they aren't offering extra lives for money. If any dev reads this, I'd heavily encourage you to rethink your current monetisation.  As a side note, the game is nigh unplayable with a keyboard. If you do not have a controller, do not bother playing. Even with a controller, the controls can be a little awkward. For example, diagonally aiming downwards is incredibly difficult with an analog stick. It's certainly playable, but it could definitely be improved upon.  Overall, I've really enjoyed this game. It's difficult, but rewarding, with great boss design and fun gameplay. That being said, this does not appeal to everyone. Give it a go, but if you find yourself constantly frustrated, find another game.

There's a reason why a large number of Total War fans revert back to this game- it's an absolute classic. Sure, it's an old game, but don't get me wrong- even now this game looks great, and the gameplay is just as good as ever.From commanding Rome's legionares to directing Greece's Spartan hoplites, the one thing that this game does fantastically is create a real authentic feel. However there are a few drawbacks; the game's strategy is incredibly deep; From managing cities and trade, to Generals and population happiness, the game can not only be daunting to newcomers, but will quickly get out of hand for the seasoned pro when he/she has conquered enough of the world. In addition to that, the game is not completely balanced; sure the Roman's were overpowered in real life, but when you have a Carthiginian army that outnumbers the Roman army 3:1, it's ridiculous to see the Autoresolve favor of Rome. Even with this drawbacks however, this game still manages to please, and after almost ten years, it's easy to see why this is one of the greatest strategy games of all time.

For then it isn't a game, but of a tale, that is of two loved ones. Though not in the sense of boyfriend or girlfriend, but of Father & Daughter. Booker Dewitt, the father in this tale, Is trying to, without knowing, save the one person that truly knows him from the clutches of an egomaniac... But as this tale unfolds, you start to learn that, the one you love, is the one you lock away, and the man you hate, is the man you are... And in the end when it all comes to you, that no matter what you do, no matter how hard you try, there is an impenetrable force, a un-swimmible current. That is keeping you from the one you love... But in the end, you chose to give in to the tide, & sacrifice everything for the one you love. That is what the morale of this story is, that people would do anything for the ones they love. Even if it means sacrificing everything, so that your loved one will live on... That is why Booker Dewitt didn't fight as Elizabeth drowned him

The characters felt just there, with the exception that is elizabeth. The gameplay is a step down from the first bioshock such as the ability to carry only 2 weapons,no hacking, and no alt ammo types which made the original game unique and interesting. There is a sky hook which lets you ride on rails, but It felt really gimmicky to me personally. Now ignoring the fact the game strips of what was redeemable about the previous games, is it even enjoyable on it's own? no for several reason. The game is also Rather Easy, you can't even die in this game, granted this was a problem in the first game but it would have been better if gave you some sort of penalty other than losing cash in the game. Now i've been pretty rough on the game, but it's not bad, however it's not good ethier. What matters in a fps is the core gameplay not the story which As I said I didn't care for. Would I reccomend it? maybe if you are wanting to play it for the gameplay(like anyone who plays first person shooters) i'd say get it for cheap, and if you really want to see how good it's story is, then yeah I would. Global Thermonuclear War? Wouldn't you prefer a nice game of chess? Not really! Wargames was a great movie, and the novelisation was pretty decent too. Now you can play the game! DEFCON lets you feel like you're sitting in the war room in a secret bunker waiting to flip the switch and send the nuke flying. Far from glamourising all-out thermonuclear war, DEFCON makes it abundantly clear that everybody loses when it comes to nuclear war by showing how many casualties each city takes and the radioactivity of each bombed location.

Not only is this a fantastic game, an instant 10/10, the fact that it costs only £2 makes it one of the best value-for-money games I've ever seen on Steam, at least not including sales. It took me 20 hours to complete this game, so that's £1 per 10 hours. The game itself is a classic, growing on what made Abe's Oddysee so great, and while I think Exoddus lacks the atmosphere that made the original so enjoyable, the gameplay itself is much more varied (though I still prefer Oddysee). The cutscenes are better, the music is more complex (if not a tad generic), there are more places and environments to see, and there are more types of puzzles based on the emotion system with the Mudokons. They can be angry, depressed, wired (in other words happy), sick, and blind. This means a lot more is explored as to how the Mudokons interact with you, and especially trying to handle the wired Mudokons running about with enemies, bombs and motion sensors around can get very tense. You can also autosave, another improvement from Oddysee that makes the game much less frustrating. I have 2 complaints, however; the first being there are a few ridiculous chase sections that force you to duck and react quickly with a movement system not internded for fast-paced action, rather slow-moving stealth. The game also overstays it's welcome a bit, getting dragged out for a few more hours than it really needed to last. Speaking of stealth, once again every single enemy can make your palms sweat all over your controller, especially if you have some blind Mudokons you have to escort. One more thing; the graphics, for 1998, are amazing. I found myself stopping and gazing at some of the fantastically detailed backdrops of places like Necrum and the Soulstorm Brewery. It's dark and beautifully rendered.  Anyway, get the game now. It's bloody great.

First the story. I was fairly surprised by just how much the the story elements played into the game's mechanics, providing a rich and deep world ready to be explored. All of the traditional Lara Croft elements seemed to be in place, though mind you I'm no long time expert by any means necessary. For me, story plays a large role into every nook and cranny of the game and I can find a game unenjoyable at too many interruptions into that emersion.  The game's mechanics, upgrades and such fell into a normal progression and I did at times wonder why certain enemies might have different weapons later in the game, within the confines of the story, opposed to earlier levels.  The game did however keep me very involved with it's background and play style which I felt was incredibly rewarding with every failed jump or successful take down.  Regarding the controversy over the strong lead female character being tampered with. You really should play the game if you haven't already. Every hero male or female has a beginning and I felt this installment to the Tomb Raider series really tapped into that idea well. I can see in some places where perhaps room to create yet another pre game could take place but as a fan of the Tomb Raider series, though again not an expert, I felt the character of Lara Croft was done justice not in propelling her from the jump as a BA but giving us a taste of how she overcame her fears and became that BA we all know and love.  In future installements, I'd love to see more of that 'dumb luck' factor added into the series which I think if done right can really give a good feel to these style of games. Kind of helps me believe that the stray bullet factor hasn't been completely removed from the game but instead has been addressed.

The Bridge is a puzzle game that takes place in a series of level that seems to be inspired by the drawings of impossible constructions by M. C. Escher. The game uses two main mechanisms that the player can use to progress in each level - walking and the ability to turn the worlds around. There's a series of submechanisms depending on the level as well, including gravity manipulation, inversions and more.  It's divided into 8 chapters (2 times 4) and as in most games the difficulty increases from each chapter. The first 4 chapter is in the 'standard world' and the last 4 is in a mirrored world. The first 4 are challenging, but can be overcome with some thought, but the last 4 chapters are very, very difficult. Generally, though, each puzzle is well thought through and even levels using the same mechanisms are very different in design and the way they should be solved.  While the gameplay is nice what's the most impressive about this game is its aesthetic side - the music is great and the art style of the game is absolutely spectacular.   Overall, if you're into puzzles you're in for a treat. It's visually stunning and there's different challenges for everyone, although later levels are very difficult.  EDIT 2 months later:   I've reread the review, and while I still recommend it there should be a few notes added. It's a short game and I would not recommend picking it up for the standard €10 it goes for, but it is often for sale at around €2, which is a pretty reasonable price for a handful of hours worth of entertainment. Also it should be mentioned that this is not an easy puzzle game. The earliest chapters are fairly straightforward, but some of the later ones are ridiculously challenging and I admit that I had to check out YouTube for a few of them in order to even make sure what I was supposed to do.  That said, if you can get it for a good price, there's still a few hours of good and entertaining exercise for the brain, but don't be supposed to find yourself a little frustrated with the later levels.

This is an arcadey football game with interesting RPG elements.  You are playing the part of a single footballer. That's a very refreshing mechanic, which hasn't been used in many other games recently. That makes this game fairly refreshing.  There are almost two games feeding into each other: Firstly, there's playing the matches. This is a good, but not stellar, arcade style football game, with a tiled view from behind the goal, similar to the various Amiga games of the 1990s. You just control your own player rather than the whole team. The game is fast-paced and holds your attention. The 'one player' mechanic is also very different to most other games.  Secondly, there's the RPG game. This has you 'levelling up' your player, maintaining relationships with team mates and friends, saving up for houses, flash cars and the like. You can also do several other things, such as gamble your money at the casino or racetrack. The mini-games providing this are all very simple, but there are a lot of them and they're competently done.  The most interesting part comes where the two games overlap. Should you pass to a teammate rather than scoring yourself - because then he'll like you and be more likely to pass to you next time, or do you score yourself because you want the goal bonus to buy some new boots? When scoring a goal, do you celebrate with the fans to improve your relationship with them, or do you celebrate with the manager so he's more likely to pick you next week? I found this interesting and refreshing, and brings some planning and thinking to the arcade-style match engine.  If you're wondering whether this is the same game as its more famous cousin on iOS, it's not. This is much more fleshed out and playable, particularly the match engine which is real-time. Incidentally, if you haven't played the iOS version then it's well worth the price.  It's worth pointing out that the game is nigh-on impossible to play using keyboard and mouse. You'll definitely need some kind of controller. I just used a cheap USB one, which worked absolutely fine. The buttons are user-definable.  The game does get a bit samey after a while - it's essentially the same game 20 hours in as it is 20 minutes in. However, at the price that's not a huge problem.  I enjoyed playing this. If you like arcadey football games, or are bored of FIFA and want something different, then this is well worth a look.

TL;DR Game is brilliant 9.5/10 Get it even if you don't know the alien timeline too well it fits in perfectly Highly recommend  I am a huge Alien Fan boy. But don't let it sway you from what I'm about to say just because I love Aliens. As of now I've got 4 Hours under my belt for this game. I'm only on level three. And I'm horrified. I literally shake when I hear the Alien crawling around in the vents. Prime example. Gotta distract the Alien to advance. I think ill shoot the pistol. Grab its attention, bypass and be home free. I shoot, I hide in a locker. The alien climbs out of the vent closest to me (by the way the animations are so god damn nice its borderline life like) and I stay there for 10 minutes crying because I can't hear it anymore but I know its close. So I decide to get out where I fumble with the reload, start putting the rounds into the cylinder of the pistol when I hear this huge thud behind me and its hiss, not even a meter away from me. I stay still for like 10 seconds when I slowly turn around and then it pounced. Creating this truly terrifying animation where I had to pause the game and revert save as it was too much. This is the first game to have ever made me feel this scared in all of my life and has truly set the bar for new horror games.  CA have made a beautiful organism which it truly deserved, as said before, it could have just pounced and got it over with. But instead, it knew it had me, so it was basically just toying with its food. That's what really scared me. The fact that in the 10 seconds I was just praying for it to get it over with, it didn't. It just decided for me to either run or to turn to look at it. CA have made this creature have a fantastic AI which is fantastic   The game just oozes atmosphere. You don't even see the Alien for 2 levels. Its only till the end of the 2nd mid 3rd you actually see it. But I was still jumping at every hissing pipe, vent noises. Anything which could have been the alien.  Bottom line is. This game is truly a great example of what gaming can make you feel. CA have done a brilliant job and this is what should have been made first. Not Aliens: CM  Bravo CA you have done a brilliant job.

Super Hexagon is a game where you control a single triangle to avoid incomplete hexagon shapes that are closing in, trying to crush and kill you. Overall its an amazing game, very challenging and very rewarding in its own way. There are 6 total levels: Hard, Harder, Hardest, Hardestest, Hardes♥♥♥♥♥♥♥, and the End, each unlocked by surviving more than 60s on the previous level. Now down to the details:  Pros:  -Great soundtrack (if you ever hear it) -Smooth and very easy controls -Unique concept -Very rewarding  The main thing I want to go into depth about is how the game rewards you. If you enjoy techno/electric house music, you'll most likely enjoy the music. The only way you can listen to the music is by surviving longer than you did before. Personally, after playing this game for awhile, getting farther into the game to experience the music became a crave. You know, unless you cheated and went on YouTube to listen to it. The controls are very easy and simplistic which is very good (its one of the reasons why I hate MMO's). Although the concept is also simple, its very creative and makes the game even more enjoyable.  Cons: -Can be rage inducing if you're an uptight person -VERY hard -No global highscore board  Now I would like to say that the game being hard isn't necessarily a bad thing. It creates the sense of accomplishment when you get a higher score than your previous best, which is a main part of the game. I'm not an uptight person, or angry, but sometimes this just isn't the game you want to play if your already a bit salty. ALL IT WILL DO IS MAKE IT WORSE. The last one is just a personal thing that I think would be great, but doesnt actually effect the game. Being able to see where your high score lies amongs the thousands that have played this game to see your progress would be incredible, and something I personally would love to see. Again, it doesn't really matter, I'm practically fishing for anything to put into the cons list.  I give this game a solid 10/10  TL;DR:  Game over. Begin!  Game over. Begin!  Game over. Begin!  Game over. Begin!  Game over. Begin!  Game over. Begin!  Game over. Begin!  Game over. Begin!  Game over. Begin!  Game over. Begin!  Game over. Begin!  Game over. Begin!  Game over. Begin!  Game over. Begin!  Game over. Begin!  Game over. Begin!   ETC. :D

Tactical Realism is something that alot of games tr to capitalize on, but Insurgency and the team behind it hit the nail on the head. in short its basically the action of games like 'CSGO' and 'Day of Defeat mixed with the tactical realism of 'ARMA' And SOCOM' If you have the money to spend, and are looking for a fantastic fps game then spend it on this, and get it for your buddies too, the COOP is hella fun. Review in Progress: So far I think I'm still on Chapter 1. But for the sake of spoilers. I will not talk about the plot or what happened so far, but I'll give you the premise. I'll just focus on the gameplay and everything else.  So as some of you might know already. The Vanishing of Ethan Carter is about a detective, Paul Prospero with supernatural powers who received a letter from Ethan Carter who claims to be in danger. He came to Red Creek Valley to find out what happened to Ethan.  In the game you control the detective in first person mode. The very first thing I noticed is that the game is absolutely gorgeous even on medium settings. On high, it look on par with Skyrim in my opinion. The game run quite well on my GTX 760, though there are some occasional stuttering, which still appear even when I drop down to medium. Though it is not truly noticeable.  The game does not hold your hand at all whatsoever. You are free to explore the Valley, which is a lot bigger than I originally thought. There are puzzles and items here and there that give you some backstory. The first big puzzle you encounter involved a train. (You'll know what I'm talking about if you play it) This is when your paranormal powers come into play. You can investigate the scenes and put clues together. Once all the clues come together. Paul special ability come into play, where you can put the entire sequence back in chronological order and experience what truly happened at the site. It's quite a fun and challenging experience. The next puzzle I encountered was with a house that warped, and you have to put the rooms in their correct place to solve it. (Which I am stuck on at the moment). As I said I'm only in Chapter 1, I think. So I'm sure there are many other gameplay mechanic I have not seen yet.  The voice acting so far is very well done. Paul sounds like your grizzly veteran detective who have seen it all. He sounds unnaturally calm with all the weird stuff going on around him. Which really add to the mysteriousness of his personality and his past. The soundtrack is also very well done. Though there are very, very few jump scares. The soundtrack provide a sense of uneasiness that really keep me on my toes. I constantly look behind my back, thinking there was something behind me. But other times, the soundtrack get incredibly mellow, and when you look at the beautiful world that The Astronauts studio have created. I was amazed at how well the music and the scenery mixed with one another.  If you are have play Murdered Soul Suspect before and somewhat enjoyed it at least. I think you would really enjoy The Vanishing of Ethan Carter. As I said, the game doesn't hold your hand at all. You are left to be your own detective and figure out the gameplay mechanic for yourself. It's quite enjoyable.  The best thing about the game so far it's the environment. Incredibly mysterious and calm at the same time. I'm terrified while playing this because I literally have no idea what to expect next yet I'm intrigued because I really want to find out what's going on.  Right now on Steam reviews. There are 72 positive reviews and only 1 negative. You can see why I'm really enjoying this. I highly recommend this based on what I've experienced so far.

'Grapple'  is an awesome 3D puzzle platformer with a really good designed physic engine without any glitches or bugs.  Slogan: Your skill is the only thing slowing you down.    Desc:  In Grapple you are a sticky blob. Your goal is to reach the end portal in each level. You are moving, jumping and grappling from platform to platform. With each higher level the difficulty level is rising.   Pros:  + you are a sticky blob (stuck to every object) + great physic engine + awesome level design + lot of levels (90) + 3 game modes (speed run, normal run, time trail) + no glitches, no bugs + checkpoints + unlimited lives (+ achievements)   Cons:  - no level editor (no steam workshop) - music is not bad, but has less variety and it could be much more intense   Recommended , even if it's  not on sale , because there are a lot of levels and it has a really good designed physic engine.   9/10

I've been gaming for 25 years, loved the original Syndicate/Wars, backed this via Kickstarter and was suprised at the level of quality from the game based on the funds raised.  Unfortunately at release the pathfinding was broken. I gave it 3 months (which you've pretty much had to to with all PC games from the beginning of time) and its now improved. But still kind of broken. I won't go into the great moments and missions I had in the syndicate games, but the greatest thing about those games (which has yet to be replicated) was sending all 4 agents off to do different strategic tasks, like assassinate, blow up bank, stand ground with persuaded citizens and mow down enemies, infiltrate base steal hover car and fly to a point where all agents meet from all corners of the map to extract.  Although it might be a performance issue:  i7 Processor 6GB DDR3 Ram x2 HD5870 ATI Video cards 64 Bit Vista OS  But until they get it working you'll never get those moments in this game because pathfinding doesn't work. I'm gonna shelve it for now, and see if 5 lives patch it some more. It's a step in the right direction as I didn't expect to see a game like this ever again. If it is a performance issue then maybe I need to hack into the national grid with an upgraded machine and make the winter nights even darker by the amount of power needed to run the game.

Just to start, the game is terribly simplistic. The upgrades on the different ships are the same. Each planet offers the exact same options except for difference in tradeable good prices. The combat is also very simplistic, and the RNG factor can be extremely frustrating, as even smaller ships can destory you with the right set of power-ups. Also, all of your missions after the first few tend to involve fighting larger ships. No matter how much you upgrade, the enemy is always bigger (why bother upgrading, then?). The leveling is INCREDIBLY slow. In around 8-10 hours of play, I had leveled maybe a grand total of 5 times. In addition, you can't run away from combat, so if you're on low health and get into combat, too bad.   All around, the game is very lack-luster. Bad graphic. Bad gameplay. Bad storytelling. I gotta say, i'm pleasantly suprised about how the game turned out to be. When i heard it had a borderlands like loot system i was worried the gameplay would slow down significantly since enemies on looter genre games tend to be bulletsponges. This is not the case.   The gameplay is faster as doom or the previous shadow warrior with the ability to double jump and climb ledges. You also won't die by gravity anymore by dashing from staircases (Finaly). With these 2 upgrades over the previous shadow warrior the gameplay just feels awesome. Even killing the more bullet spongy boss enemies is still enjoyable.  As for the story i do not have a judgement about it as off yet, nor do i really care. I feel like these game is designed to deliver fun gameplay above all else, and this is where the game succeeds prefectly.  And if this is not enough already they've also added coop which is always welcome. All in all this game is a must buy especially since its priced half of the regulary price of new games!

Well for a $5.99 I can't really complain too much. That's not to say it's a bad game or anything, but i just can't bring myself to recommend it to other people if only because it's not quite as interesting as I hoped it would be. There really isn't a lot to do other than 'go here, wait, go there, wait, go here, wait, go there, wait, go back here, end level' all while admiring a beautiful scene of earth as you orbit around it, and the space beyond. Controls are easy if you use a controller, though they might take a while to get used to and gameplay's pretty simple and slow. pretty much your objective is to fly to waypoints and sometimes wait at them before heading back to the ship before your O2 or NO2 runs out. Bare bones and basic really. I was hoping that some of the waypoints that required you to wait by them to 'make repairs' would have involved some small hud animation or even a little quick time event or something that would involve the player doing anything while they waited so that they're not bored twiddling their thumbs. Over all, it's a time killer for people who are really into simulations. If you wanna know what it's like to float around in 'Zero G' while operating an 'MMU' this is the game for you. otherwise, you might be more interested to play 'Moonbase Alpha'

How am I supposed to recommend The Void? In writing about videogames, we simply do not have the right vocabulary to do that. I can merely offer these fragments, shored against its striking ruins:  I. You cannot hope to win the game. You can only survive.  II. The Void is a computer simulation of a highly complex system which you will not understand. From the first moments, you are required to manipulate its many variables, all obscured by very peculiar terms and tangled in a complex web of relationships. Helping the system is hurting it. Everything has consequences. You cannot know what they are. What you are doing is probably wrong.  III. Everyone is lying. Particularly when they say that everyone is lying.  IV. The videogame known as The Void is art. Unashamedly and gleefully art. The whole thing may be an extremely elaborate sexual metaphor. It may be the dark vision of an uncaring afterlife. It may be both. How many poems have you played lately?  V. There is rather a lot of naked ladies in it.  VI. It pleases me greatly to see the old surrealist sensibilities alive and thriving in a new medium. The visuals are bold and fascinating in their crumbling beauty. Erudite, yet still its own.  VII. You try writing about this without sounding pretentious.  VIII. The difficulty might be a bit extreme. I played it with the medium difficulty patch and had my hands quite full.  IX. Ice-Pick Lodge’s use of interlocking systems to tell a story is truly fascinating and pioneering work. Here is a linear game that everyone will experience completely differently. There is no crude illusion of choice and consequence; everything is consequence.  X. In conclusion, I can recommend this videogame product to everyone.
If you like precise and unforgiving action gameplay, this is THE game for you.

Fallout New Vegas opened me to the world of Fallout. It was a game I spent over 500 hours on. It really gave me a feel of what an RPG should be. For instance, the concept of having weapon degredation that were present in the previous Fallout games really made me consider my choices whether or not to use a certain weapon for a situation. Fallout 4 completely removes this and it really took a toll on the feel of the game. I basically just spray and pray during the whole game. Don't get me wrong, Fallout 4 is a great game but as an RPG, not so much. Your decisions are basically predestined and it removes that element of having a choice. The speech choices are simplified and it really pisses me off too. With only 160 hours in, I can be safe to say that I'm done with this game. It got boring and repetitive faster than I thought it would.  TL;DR : More FPS than RPG. Repetitive. Got boring real quick. Hype isn't real.
