Okay the game is fun, you can finish your first run in thirty minutes or so. We've gotten games like this, I've played games like this and its quite enjoyable, especially when there are feelings involved. The game is not quite linear and it has good pacing, also the atmosphere is able to bring you back to those good old days when facebook chat and all the rest of those are non-existent. That makes the game all the more lovely.  So who is Emily? Well Emily is a friend in your life. That's how it starts, and well depending on you and your choices, maybe that'll change in the end.   How does this work? If you haven't played games like this, I really suggest that you try this one out first. It's free and it's lovable in its own simple way. The game is not so immersive in character creation(since you're only really in chat with Emily and nothing else) and sometimes you don't really know what's happening and all that. Some events seemed forced so it sometimes feel weird. But it's fun nonetheless. If you play the game I suggest that in your first time, try to play it like it was you in the game. Don't read other people's tragic or happy stories about this game, just play it in a way you like to play it.   Is the game worth it? ITS FREAKING FREE. So go ahead and try it out. Its not all that immersive(storywise) because of the fact its all about chatting with Emily and at certain points you really dont know what's happening, however a lot of people argue that this depicts long distance relationships, and if we look at it at that stand point, then yes, the game is reasonably immersive in that way. Its fun and lovable in its own way, and that you would really feel something in the end. Give it a try!

I really WANTED to like this game. I'd heard such great things about it, and it seemed like it should be right up my alley. Unfortunately, it was just let down.  First, the gameplay. Nidhogg has simple but slightly awkward controls that certainly can be mastered but aren't particularly fluid on their own. If you're expecting fighting-game style precision, you're going to be disappointed. Once you get past the tutorial, the movement options feel fuzzy and unreliable. The basic combat is... okay. Most actions happen so quickly that it's humanly impossible to react to them, so it's mostly about anticipation and taking risks, and be prepared for a number of frustrating deaths where it doesn't even look like you were touched by the opponent's sword.  I realize that everything I just wrote is directly contrary to what most other critics are saying about the game, but I can only call it like I see it. I've played plenty of other games in this niche, and they all felt better than Nidhogg.  The one place where the game is fun is local co-op, which is the entire point of the game I suppose, but it's fun for the 'wrong' reasons. Playing with a group of friends makes all of the 'screw you, that was stupid' moments fun, because you all get to taunt each other the whole time. So basically, the game is fun with friends precisely *because* of the stupid and lopsided moments that in any other situation would just be frustrating.  Claims that the game's graphics are 'beautiful' are overstated. There are one or two impressive setpieces that will probably catch your eye, but the game as a whole isn't particularly nice-looking. The style is unique and expressive, but it's not objectively pleasing by any measure. Also, the game really needs a colorblind setting, because my character is practically invisible to me against the backdrop of the second stage.  My final complaint, which others have echoed, is that there's not nearly enough content to justify the $15 price tag. I normally don't mind paying a little extra for a good game, but this is definitely more of a $5 title. If it's on sale for that much and you're looking for a new game to play at parties, maybe this is for you, but otherwise you're not missing much by passing it up.

Your enjoyment of this game largely depends on your patience. When you pick an ocean (server) there are 2 distinct types, subscription and dubloon. Each has their pros and cons. Subscription oceans limit you from doing quite a bit in the game if you are unsubcribed. But when you subscribe, you cannot really buy your way through the game. Dubloon oceans allow you play completely for free unless you want to buy dubloons for real money. Dubloons can also be bought through in-game currency, so if you have the patience, you can play the whole game for free.

Excellent! Simple to play, easy to learn, definitely a one-of-a-kind Third Person Shooter + Tower Defense game. Gameplay is easily summarized - Defend your rift(base) from orcs, with melee, range weapons, magic, traps, and guardians. There're over 10 different types of enemies, and 22 weapons, traps and guardians added up altogether. I would strongly suggest downloading the demo first before trying (I got hooked on the game this way). Maps are playable in 3 different levels - Apprentice(Easy), WarMage(Normal), Nightmare(Explains itself). The game has practically no ending, you can replay your maps as many times as you want. No rewards, but the gameplay itself is rewarding. The only 'ending', is probably when you completed all achievements, upgraded every single trap, and 5 Skull-ed all maps on all difficulties. Pretty fulfilling!  Pros - Graphics are cartoon-ish. Great for casual players. Graphics which are too clear makes me really stressed. - Gameplay varies, dependent on the type of player you are. Melee, magic, shooter, you choose! - Achievements are all attainable. Last broken achievement was removed, hence, 27 workable achievements. - 24 different maps, excluding 5 DLC maps(Might as well get it). - There's a difference shooting at the head, and at the body. Dealing different damage really draws the line between players who only want to complete the game, and those that REALLY wants to perform. You can't just spam. - Simple upgrades. Basically you complete a map well, you are given up to 10 skulls per map, then you upgrade it. There is simply no need for a guide on what to upgrade. Just do it. Eventually, all skulls will be used, and everything upgraded.  Cons - Single player. Might not be too optimal for couples, or a group of friends. Orcs Must Die 2 has co-op though(Recommended as well). - Despite 'Practice makes perfect', some really tough maps are really argh. You would be finding yourself restarting the map many times just to achieve 5 Skulls(5 Star Rating). - Endless isn't available, so once you're done with the map, you're done. Clearing the map isn't tough, but getting 5 Skulls for it might be a chore for some. - Too damn addictive. You really can't stop playing!!  On the overall, apart from perfecting this game by achieving 5 Skulls for all maps on all difficulties, even as a casual game, it will not fail to entertain. Definitely.   On a scale of 1-10, I would give it 9.5 (: Lack of companionship in this game kinda killed it for me, but I still completed it nevertheless! Fantastic game!!

Once upon a time There was a game developer. His name was (and is) American McGee. Now this - yes children, his first name is American. Now this man - Yes children, I agree that's a neeto name. And no children, I don't know why he was named 'American'. Ask his mother. Okay. Now. Long ago (2000) this man, along with a huge entity named EA games, released a game named Alice. It followed a girl named Alice as she journeyed through her mind trying to find a path to sanity. Did I mention She was lying comatose in an asylum.  Well, the mental realm she travels in this game is, you guessed it, Lewis Carrol's Wonderland. This game is one of my favorites. I just played it again and despite it's turn of the millinia grafics and such found it still held it's charm. Did I mention the Cheshire Cat is my all-time favorite sidekick. I bought the game based solely on its picture on the cover. Good thing I turned out loving the game. Wouln't want to feel like a tool right? I wholeheartedly recommend American McGee's Alice. Even though it is an anicient video game it's worth a shot. An updated version of Alice was released In a limited edition of Madness Returns. Their is a download for it io the Community Hub. A web search and a couple of bucks should also find you a copy. ! give it ten out of ten Boojums.  With that bit of backstory and the shameless edorsment of a game that is not carryed buy Steam We procede to - Alice: Madness Returns.  This game begins where the first game left off. Alice is living in a half-way house under the care of a psychiatrist. She is working on her recovery when problems begin. Old 'friends' start appearing and pull the fabric of her hard-earned sanity out from under her. Next thing you know she is once again ♥♥♥-over-tea kettle down the rabbit hole and having another disquieting journey through Wonderland. Like the first game we puzzle our way from realm to realm running into Carroll's famous Wonderland denzens as the story unfolds.  From the soot filled streets of London to hieghts above Wonderland this game is beatiful. The puzzles are somewhat challenging and the evil critters fun to fight. The story well-told. It has just enough strage humour to balance the darkness.  And of course the Cheshire Cat is back ready, to once again offer snarky advice or just thumb (claw?) it's nose at you. Alice: Madness Returns was fun. I give it ten out of ten psycho-therapists.

Not being a huge fan of shooters, this game was great fun, and not only due to the shooting piece, which is excellent by the way. It's been long time since I actually liked shooting at things this much.  It has a fantastic storyline - very interesting and original, and with a good deal of humour. The soundtrack is well put out (O Death is one of my favourites), as are the Nuggets of Truth, that help you understand the History behind this westerner. When this game makes go to your bookshelf looking for books about Old West history, then the game is reaching the back of your brain in the best way.  The duels are intense, and the whole environment is very immersive. I played the Story mode and finished it. I also had a go at duels and arcade mode. They seem to add a lot to replayability. You also unlock the 'True West' mode after finishing Story mode, which may get you to try the story again in a different way, retaining all the weapon upgrades you gained before.  Absolutely brilliant game!

At first glance, I Am Alive showed a lot of promise. The post apocalyptic atmosphere is believable. The story feels genuine. But ultimately playing it felt more like a chore than entertainment. The game concepts sound great on paper, but fall short on execution. It tries to pretend it's open world, yet is completely linear. I tried to play as a passive person, purposely trying to save lives and ammo, but nothing I did or didn't do mattered. Just about everyone I encountered had to die because they were the aggressor and wouldn't step down. There was no way to talk my way out of any situation. And if I pointed my gun at someone without the intention of using it, they always attacked as soon as I lowered my weapon or tried to walk away. Killing people became routine and expected so eventually I just started shooting first.  Then there's the stamina aspect which works well most of the time, until you unwittingly find yourself lost with no stamina to get back to safety, being forced to restart a level. And finally there's that genuine story which turns out to be nothing more than a dead end serving no purpose other than to keep the game moving forward. There is no closure here.  Attaching the Ubisoft name to this title feels very similar to attaching the Gearbox name to Aliens: Colonial Marines--setting up high expectations, only to be disappointed.

app_name: The Incredible Adventures of Van Helsing
review_text: I didn't enjoyed The Incredible Adventures of Van Helsing more than any of Diablo games. But for some reason, it was more fun than Torchlight series. Why? Not sure. Maybe because it looks a little more dark and I really loved atmosphere in this game. Having a lovely companion also made me enjoy this game really much and the way you could spend her skill points in whatever way you want was a good idea - you can build her so she will be a real support for the way you will be playing, not some kind of obstacle.  Maybe because I was focused on hand-to-hand combat, sometimes I really had hard times - every new area ended for me the same way, with more deads than I could count. Not that I hated it, maybe it was also my fault, but I enjoyed it. This way I had more fun than just running and killing everyone with 1 hit. Also, the boss fights are great. Graphics looks nice, music fits really well. I could only complaing about how game ends, and while the story isn't the best but not that it really matter, at least I look for something different in hack and slash games. If you are fan of those, if you look for something interesting in this genre - you should try The Incredible Adventures of Van Helsing. Maybe it's no use comparing it to other games like that but for sure it's worth playing. It's game that grabbed my attention for few hours and the time I spend playing it was truly fun.

As someone with over 200 Steam games I keep finding myself coming back to this game when I still have unplayed games I paid for.  I've played the Hack and Slash genre since the original Diablo came out. I fell in love with the genre and have forever been a fan. I've seen the great, the bad, and the ugly. This game falls into the great.  For new players, be warned this game has a very high learning curve. But don't let that put you off. If you put in the time to learn how to play at an advance level, you will find that this learning curve means you have the BEST customization of ANY hack and slash ever.  The worst part of this game is the desync. I'm sure you can find some info more about this issue. Yet, this is what we get for this game being F2P. Though, the developers give us the tools to correct this. Setup a macro and your fine. Can't setup a macro, then back out of fights if you have too (you should know this skill anyways if you get overwhelded).  The great. Awesome customization, so much replayability. No strings attached.  PS. This game can be grindy but just like any Hack and Slash.  If you love this genre, what are you waiting for? Never played this genre? Don't give it a second thought and jump right in. Don't like the genre, WTF are you doing here?! Move on, not the game for you.

Loren has the distinction of being the first game ever where I picked the male PC to use over a female PC for a first playthrough. As a woman gamer, this is really rare! But Loren presents two interesting backstories to choose from and those backstories really do influence how others treat your character and the dialogue options you have.   So, to back up: this is basically a low-fi version of Dragon Age. It's heavily story driven, with several interesting characters, tons of dialogue and interaction, huge amounts of world-building, lots of really cool and diverse types of female characters, realistic conflict between characters, and Loren manages to do it all without BioWare's budget or AAA status. The character designs are really cool (Dora and Chambara are probably my favorites) and once I realized (about 5 hours into the game, whoops!) there was a 'fast combat' option in the menu to speed things up, the classic turn-based combat made me fairly nostalic. My big complaint with the game would probably be the skill trees, partially because for strength-based (warriors) characters most of the skills were useless and for will (mages) and skill-based (thieves) the skills were often unbalanced and there's never a marker to tell you when/how you can gain certain skills (is it progression? A certain level of will? What? Why can I NEVER pick that skill?). It gets frustrating, particularly at higher levels, as the skill points you receive are minimal throughout the game.   Regardless of my frustration with the skill trees (and by about hour 7 I just gave up and started auto-assigning), Loren's definitely one of the better RPGs I've played recently. I'm looking forward to the other playthroughs I intend to do to see alternate paths, romances, and endings.

Monsters are bullet sponges and each have unique attacks that *looks* cool but really doesn't do much in terms of mechanics/challenges the hunters have to overcome. Speaking of sponges the Hunters can take a freaking beating too - everything feels without consequence, the monster escapes and thus the 'Hold W Simulator' continues.  I mean, the premise isn't bad and has had me intrigued since the game was first revealed but I have to admit I'm happy I didn't pay money for this, let alone full price. There's very little on offer and the 'Free Rotation' of heroes just doesn't really work in a game like this. Maybe if you're a Founder it's more fun and it will obviously appeal to some people, just not to me. Very much not to me. After 2 or 3 rounds I felt I had tried everything the game had to offer and was just about mentally spent following glowing tracks on the ground and whatever the tracker does.  It's free now so it's hard to say 'stay away', if you have even the slightest interest in it go ahead and try it out but seriously do not expect much. Would I actively recommend it though? No.  Note: Did not try it pre-'Stage 2' (Free to Play) but to my understanding they've removed gamemodes from the original title. It feels *really* barren of content right now, I can't imagine there's much playtime to be had here unless you truly dig the core gameplay.

I'm in the middle of my first playthrough still, with basic Humans. Already like it a lot. The empire-building is well thought out, exploration is very engaging and rewarding, the style and design are nearly perfect. I like the balance between war and exploration, the details of world colonization, research and ship management. Resources are made interesting again here, by the way, as they all behave in different ways and you need to maintain their flow by taking different measures for each (I mean credits, minerals, influence, strategic resources). It looks and feels like a near-perfect space 4X game to me, although I've never played any other game of that type, coming to this one after CK2 and Civs.  There are some mildly boring things though. Firstly, I've not noticed yet any differences in Species, game-wise. I mean they all have different traits and affinities, but beyond preferring certain climates otherwise they behave similarly. Even in diplomacy the difference between species and ideologies converts to just simple liking/disliking. Then, to make it better for non-warlike players, perhaps other victory conditions could be added, Civ-style, and then I think many would appreciate more nuanced diplomacy and sector management (like ordering your Governor to address certain issues first or to pursue a certain policy beyond basic resource extraction). Also the border instability should be explained somewhere in a tutorial. Your territory expands and contracts slightly all the time, depending on how active your planets are, and this came as a minor surprise.

Look, I get it, it's a beautiful game. If that's your type of thing (and for a lot of people it is), go ahead and get this game. The atmosphere is great, the graphics are great, and there's one model in particular I was very impressed by.  That said, this isn't my type of game. It felt more interested in showing off its atmosphere than providing a gaming experience. I found myself getting bored during long look-at-these-incredible-backgrounds walks. There were a couple of 'Aha!' moments during the puzzles, but not enough to justify the price.   And I think that's the sticking point. If this game had been $5, I'd have considered it a good buy. But as someone more interested in game mechanics than atmosphere, this didn't impress me enough to justify the price.  After consideration, the price isn't really the problem. It's just I have other games I'd rather play in those hours.  One last complaint, and again, people who love this game will question how it's a complaint at all. There's atmosphere, but there's no narrative. If you're looking for closure or an idea of the bigger picture, you're not going to get it. It's more of a series of well-done creepy vignettes. Judging from other comments online, this is something people  absolutely  love about this game. But I personally don't find it satisfying.

This game is #1 of the year so far. I highly reccomend 10/10!!

+ Dual Audio (English and Japanese audio) + Some Character designs are interesting  +/- Random Monster Encounting Dungeon  - Horrible Sound Effects - Horrible PC port (You'll NEED a gamepad/controller and download a software called Xpadder) - Boring Story - Horrible and Confusing Story Telling - Boring Gameplay - Cheesy Dialogue - Game HUD is so horrible, it looks like it was made in 1990 - English Voice Actors sound lazy or tired - Some Character Images are poorly cropped in. You can see small fragments around them, Jesus.  You can tell the developers did not care about the quality of this game, and just wanted to quickly make something to cash grab. I wanted to play a game at least somewhat similar to Persona, but this is garbage. I've seen a lot of other negative reviews about the horrible pc port, which can quickly be resolved with xpadder, but what really makes this game so bad is how the story telling was designed.   There is no narration or 3D models to explain what the hell is going on. It's easy to get lost what is happening to the characters or what they're doing, and the out of sync sound effects doesn't help.  Also, the actual gameplay is horrible. The tutorial is a bunch of text. Some gameplay designs are pointless. And the dungeon crawler looks like it was made from 1990.  Overall: 3/10 F you lazy Japanese game developers. This is just another example of a dissappointing game that resulted from lazy and inconsiderate game design because they just wanted to quickly gain money. Go ahead and pick this game up if you're seriously desperate while waiting for the next Persona game, but don't say you were never warned.

When I heard there was and indie horror game based on the real life Dyatlov Pass incident, I was intrigued. The game is gorgeous and the sound design is even better. Kholat's presentation is what really shines. You walk around the frozen mountains of Russia by yourself at night when you know something terrible happened, something is out there. It really got to me at first.   What is not so shiny, however, is the gameplay... it's too simplistic. It's basically a 'walking simulator' in witch you explore a huge open environement picking up letters and files to figure out the story. It also has a bit of stalking monsters and those are the weakest part of the game. Their AI is less than impressive and it really hurts the experience. Do you want PTSD lite? If the answer is yes, then this game is for you! This game is somewhere on the realism spectrum between a generic shooter, and an Arma game (And it leans closer to the Arma side). The game has a very short time to kill, usually one or two bullets will finish the job pretty well, although if you can disengage, minor injuries can sometimes be bandaged before you bleed out (and you can bleed out). The real delight of the game is its scale and visuals. it's best played on 64 player servers, with as many players as possible, which can make the game fairly chaotic. Visually, the game is not the most beautiful, although it is by no means ugly, but it does a good job at portraying sone of the more horrible aspects of war, limbs can come off, although blood is not overdone, like in some games, and the game is filled with lots of little thing that catch your eye. Anyway, I think I will end with a story from my time in game:  I was in the gunnery seat of a panzer IV, when a T-34 roles onto the street directly ahead. I sight the gun to my estimated range and fire. The shell shattered on impact, dealing little to no damage. I hear a pinging noise on the right side of the tank, probably machine gun fire, I think to myself. I adjust my sights and wait for my loader to finish loading the shell. After waiting some time for confirmation that the shell is loaded, I try firing anyway. Click. There's no shell in the barrel. The T-34 fires, their shell bounces off the side of the tank. I take the momentary break to take a look at the crew compartment of my Panzer IV. Everyone in the tank is dead, and there are several holes on the right side that appear to have come from anti tank rifles. Only my loader is still alive, and he's missing both his legs, cradling a shell and crying for his mother. There's nothing left for me in this tank, and I evacuate out of the Hatch on the left side. Right as I have just exited, the anti tank rifles start peppering the sides again, as I'm climbing down from the tank a round penetrates and impacts the ammunition racks. The tank explodes violently and I'm cast several feet from the vehicle, slowly bleeding out on the ground.

Remember Battlefield 3 from 2011? That 4 year old game?  Well, in Rainbow 6 you can destroy the buildings, too, like in the 4 year old BF.  The graphics though are WAY WORSE than in 4 year old BF. I'm talking CS-GO like sterile levels with NO detail what so ever.   There are no vehicles, obviously, as in 4 year old BF. And the loadouts are not as customisable as in 4 year old BF.  There is a strategic aspect to the game to say something positive. Like in 4 year old .... Hmm... fail.  Ok... So the game is.... well.... aehm.   Ok, it's nowhere near as good as a 4 year old shooter, and don't get me started on the graphics.  Let me tell you, 70 bucks for THIS.... *PLUS* a season pass for another 40 bucks or whatever is a JOKE.  This would be a decent game for 30 bucks. Get it on a sale. Watch some youtube vids before you buy. And pay attention to the level design, to the level of detail. It looks OLD. Repetitive.  And when you watch that gameplay, don't get fooled by the destructable elements they (Obviously) focus on. That's the only thing this game really has going for it.  And to anyone saying 'don't focus on the graphics so much': You're on steam here friend, we PC gamers are all about a high fidelity experience. If you don't want the best FPS/Graphics/Effects etc. you would have bought a PS or XBOX.  The multiplayer is ok. Not great, it's ok. And... that's all there is. Yea, you heard right. No campaign. No Singleplayer. No story.   Ok. I say it again. 70 bucks, plus a season pass of 40 bucks. For THIS. A COD BO3 offers a DECENT CAMPAIGN, LEGENDARY MULTIPLAYER and even ZOMBIES for 10 bucks LESS (60 I think). I mean comon! And if you're more of a tactical guy and think COD is for triggerhappy kids, checkout BF.   Would I recommend this? If you're into that kind of game, get it on a sale. If you like action shooters, don't get this. The basic concept of siege is 'one team camps, one team raids'. So they're making a virtue out of something you would get kicked for in any other game, basically.  To say something positive in the end: Yes, it is very different from any other shoote ri played before. And that's why I refunded. Aehm... fial agian. You get the point. If it's your cup of tea, you'll love it. It's just very expensive tea.

It is a brilliant piece of software for the experienced animator or those who can learn complex things fast. Now, before you unsheathe your Keyboard and prepare to war, I'll point out I know it isn't for beginners, I'm just saying it could be simpler - If it is only simplified in 1 area, make it the mouse controls. Any Gabriel Knight fans out there?  It was many years ago when I first played the first part of the Gabriel Knight series, 'Sins of the fathers', which already had a few years under its belt back then. And it was wonderful. This adventure had a great story, believable characters, a realistic setting (at least in the beginning), great (sometimes quite hard) puzzles, and a writing with some well balanced mixture of excitement, mystery and humour to it. GK1 immediately became my favourite adventure, and it stayed that way ever since.  But what does this have to do with Kathy Rain? Why would I bring this up?  Because KR almost immediately created the same feelings I had when I played GK1 back then. Almost from the title screen it felt like playing a Gabriel Knight game. But this was not only subjective - there are objective resemblances between those two games. And it was not just the visuals: - Both KR and GK1 have a great mixture of humour and mystery, without the humour spoiling the mystery - In both games the main character has a sidekick that does some part of the research - Both main characters are sarcastic, somewhat messy writers riding mororcycles - The family history plays a major role in both stories - Both games play roughly at the same time (GK1: 1993; KR: 1995) - Both games share a large set of common settings (like a church, a police station, a graveyard, a bar,   and the scene in the mausoleum   ...) - Both games pretty much start off with the main character visiting his/her grandmother :)  So... is KR a rip-off?  No, definitely not. While the resemblances may be uncanny, KR develops its own dark story that occasionally makes you laugh, and other times gives you the creeps, and stays strong until the   (semi open, yet satisfying)   end. The puzzles can be quite challenging, but not too difficult and shouldn't get you stuck. And if you pay attention, you most probably will always know generally what your current tasks are and how you might complete them.  So basically: Kathy Rain is a great adventure. I would really love to see a second part, and it was wonderful to play something I would call a well done hommage to my favourite adventure of all time :)

Beautiful art style. Enthralling narrative. Amazing characters and voice acting. Twists, turns, tears, I loved it. Negative points are hard to call out: the game isn't very long, a single playthrough just lasts a couple of hours. However, this is mitigated by two types of collectibles (which can also affect the story) and extensive branching choices/relationships between the characters. Perhaps my biggest negative point is that, while the narrative is well-structured, I feel it's not as fleshed out as it could've been. Some holes are left open, either intentionally or unintentionally. But despite that, it was still a very worthwhile experience, and you shouldn't let that stop you from trying this hidden gem.

(As of July 24th 2016.)   ♥♥♥♥ yeah! For the following reasons:  • A setting not often explored in triple A games. (Cold war)   • Fun, casual FPS gameplay! This isn't Arma. Hell, this isn't even Counter-Strike! You're playing this because you want to have fun spraying opponents with bullets in small maps with quick respawns.  • There are no ridiculously overpowered weapons! (Looking at you MW2.)  • Server browser! (Looking at you MW2.)  • A community that is still very much alive after 6 years!  • Fun killstreaks that make you feel like a god, while still being fair. (Chopper gunner lets you rain death down from the skies with a minigun, but can be taken down with a couple of RPGs,  • Zombie mode! The game comes with 2 regular zombie maps: Kino der toten and Five. Along with a special, more arcadey Dead Ops Arcade! (All are unlocked after beating the game, but they can be unlocked with ingame cheats if you don't want to bother with the campaign. While the maps aren't as good as the ones in World at War, they're still some of the best zombie maps of this franchise.  • The soundtrack is great. From lovely chiptune-ish tracks of Dead Ops Arcade to metal (and some rap) in the zombie mode.   • ♥♥♥♥ing options menu.  • A fine number of different game modes. -----------  It's simply a great casual first person shooter. Well... as a big fan of Cyberpunk, and the source engine built games, such as garry's mod, counterstrike, and the half life series, I can HIGHLY recomend giving this game a shot.  It is very complicated to start, as any fan of cyberpunk styled things would guess from hearing that it has anything to do with cyberpunk... lol, From taking in the Stats of any old Rpg/DnD styled game, with implants from cyberpunk, A decent arsenal of weapons, the researching, plus any upgrades or abilities you buy during your playing through of the game... it can be quite daunting at first. Yet, I can't stay away, even with only a few hours of gameplay, this game has completely captivated me. I love the environment, the semi horror-ish maps, and music, that has a slight electric feel to it, to only hint at a technology based environment, the enemies, who may seem very much split into only a few groups, yet have SOOO much diversity withing those groups... captains, Horde chieftains, You really don't see a difference visually, other than some having differences in their fighting style, such as energy shields, different weapons, armor types, and abilities... the fact that each enemy can be as diversified as you, really makes the game... you never know when you will turn the corner, and be facing a heavily armored cyborg policeman witha freakin minigun, and flying sentry things following him around, or just another grunt who is probly special in his own way, but got blown away with whatever gun you had in hand due to your sheer reaction...  I could go on about what makes this game amazing, but really you should just go in and see for yourself... I HIGHLY suggest checking it out... and for 99 cents where could you go wrong?

I'm a decent way in to the game now - got all the gear, fairly certain where the end game is headed. I definitely have a good feel for the features and style of the game, and while I've selected yes - I do recommend the game - that comes with several important caveats.   iD have created a shooter with some really interesting ideas in RAGE. Unfortunately, it suffers from a stunning lack of ideas in many important respects, also - most of which have to do with the actual playing of the game. It's very old fashioned, in many ways, which you may view as a good thing if you're nostalgic for FPS games of old. Weapons assigned to number buttons, 1-10? Check. Unlock them in sequence, just as they're needed? Check. Tight corridors and fast enemies for shotgun splooshing? Check. Open spaces and cover loving baddies for sniper or machine gun play? Check. Oh, and here's a crossbow. Because crossbow.   The enemies, while spouting some interesting dialogue, are of very limited variety, models reused with alarming frequency. Likewise, their move sets are limited. The challenge arises here in presenting you with more enemies, or sometimes enemies with higher hit points, than in providing smarter baddies. I can't think of a single interesting villian - or hero, for that matter - in the game so far. The characters are massively cliché. Attempts at making the world intimidating, or even horrifying in terms of things you see in bandit camps, are immediately undone by slapstick bad guys with cartoonish animation. Also, it's always jarring to be lectured endlessly about the dangers of this horrifying world, how every step outside the secure towns is taking your life in your hands as hordes of unspeakable monstrosities await the frothy goodness of your very blood...and then to saunter from place to place on a quad bike or stupidly heavily armed buggy without so much as an insult shouted from a nearby hillock. RAGE is not a dangerous world. Glasgow pubs are, literally and figuratively, more dangerous than RAGE.   This is a game with too many ideas, some of which conflict stylistically with each other. And despite, or perhaps because of, the implementation of these ideas, other things are just left to the side. The world is very small, with some areas getting reused numerous times, just filled with different baddies. There's no true free roaming, in that you follow a limited set of canyons to a few major hubs. Occasionally you blow up a dam, or other obstruction, to unlock new canyons and a new hub. Quests are just places to go and collect things, with set pieces all along the way. A little bit on rails for a modern shooter.   RAGE also features an incredibly annoying set of outdated features which aren't directly related to the gameplay. ALL the button prompts are for xBox controllers, even if one isn't hooked up. I know it's a console port, but that's just lazy and off-putting, and the mapping for controls is far from explicit about what the assigned key will do. The game actually prompts you to save on some occasions (only seen this in DLC missions so far, to be fair), stating that 'The action is about to HOT up, better save'. Any immersion you managed to achieve in this game, you just shattered with one pop-up. It may sound like griping, but the menu is just plain silly. Select quit game...get the 'Are you sure' message. Select yes...get dumped to the opening splash screen, prompted to press enter. Press enter, get the main menu. Select quit game, and...get the are you sure window *again*.   The game also features something worth special mention: The Most Pointless Weapon Upgrade Ever. Yes, the MPWUE can be found right here in RAGE! An otherwise neat little gun, the Authority LMG, features an optic with decent magnification, good accuracy, large magazines, and an armour piercing ammo variety. It's a staple, and it comes in very handy. And you can buy a laser pointer for it. Now this would be fairly useless anyway, as the shotgun takes care of anything short range and for long range - duh, optic. But the laser beam actually fades away within a few virtual inches of the gun. Even if you stand beside a wall, you realise it's just 'painted on' - no red dot ever appears. And, and - there's an aiming reticule anyway! Granted, I've never tried disabling it. Maybe you can. Even so, this somewhat expensive upgrade feels like a lot of ideas in this game: it doesn't matter whether it WORKS, we just think it's cool.   So why would I recommend RAGE? The vehicle combat between hubs actually feels pretty good. They got the feel and speed very nearly perfect. The weapons, while outlandish and old school, are very fun, especially the alternative ammunition types. Some of the town characters are kind of fun - Kvasir is a favourite, with the little cute robot head on his shoulder nodding or shaking its head along with his conversation. The crafting aspect is pretty well implemented without being intrusive. The little turrets, sentry bots, RC bomb cars and so on, are all fun to scavenge for and use. The areas you go to for missions - should have mentioned, they load up when you enter, they're not part of the open canyons directly - are well laid out, though the path is fairly linear. A little exploration is rewarded with spare parts and such.   RAGE kinda sorta tries to be a more action oriented Fallout, but falls way short of the depth and story of that remarkable world. It also tries to be a single player Borderlands, a bit, and that's just absurd. A showcase of great ideas pinned on an ungainly donkey, pick it up cheap, laugh at its absurdity, enjoy smooshing mutants with an arsenal of stuff...but don't expect awe or major adrenaline rushes, nor a deep, post apocalyptic experience.

Fantastically fun game, but too short! I won't ding it for being short though.   Graphically, it's on-par with even today's modern games. Gameplay, it's great. I've owned this game for _years_ and until looking to see if the publisher had done any further Frontlines games, I really never noticed that there was a multiplayer; yes, the option was there, but I guess I never really noticed it. I guess that this game never seemed like it would be a good MP, so maybe that was it; at least it didn't devolve in to some POS CoD or BF cheatfest gaming that only kids play these days, so maybe it's a good thing! Apparently you can still play on LAN though. So, can't ding it for lack of MP when I never noticed MP was really there.  If you want a great FPS and will accept kind of a short story, check out Fuel of War, and its prequel or sequel Homefront from the same publisher.

There are many amazingly well executed facets to this game. It provides solid gameplay mechanics that many of today's action-based rpgs lack. There is plenty of variety in melee and ranged weapons alike that all feel equally balanced and offer a unique experience based on your play style. The crowning jewel, however, which makes Bastion stand out so predominately is its narration. From the beginning until the credits roll, your hero's journey feels epic and interesting due to the accompanying narration of one of its key characters. You never feel alone and it adds a destined outcome kind of vibe to all your actions, regardless of how trivial. The soundtrack is atmospherically appropriate to the tone of the game, and it stays with you after your done playing. This is amongst the best rpgs I've ever played, because there is very little more I could possibly want from this title.

I had a revelation with an Alien NPC. I had just destroyed his race's entire invasion single handedly. I toyed with him, dodging his laser shots and laughing, stepping closer and closer to murder the final man of the wave. Something happened, he stopped shooting and we had a moment of silence staring at eachother. Maybe he had a revelation and was going to tell this story as a survivor and change his planet's philosophy on galactic domination. I wanted my story to carry on so I allowed him to live. As I turned to leave he fired his laser at my back. He had betrayed me as quick as I had forgiven him for his actions. I would not allow this act to go unpunished.  Good game, definitely a great dev.

Call of Juarez: Gunslinger is unfortunately exactly what I expected. On the outside it looks like a well polished game with unique high quality caroony graphics. Unfortunately though the reality is that certain gameplay mechanics will infuriate you about as much as a constantly jamming rusty old pistol.  Basically you play through gun-toting stories of a few folks sitting around a table at a saloon. The playstyle is quite unique in that you are playing through 'tales' which can both, change on-the-fly and also which of course contain some exaggerated circumstances.  Call of Juarez: Gunslinger doesn't take itself very seriously at all. You're pitted against hundreds of enemies and are tasked with killing them as a one-man-army. In order to do this you make use of a few abilities like being able to slow down time, literally dodge bullets and a skill upgrade system. Shoot dynamite out of the sky, kill 10 people within 10 seconds. It's all very over the top, and it would be fantastic fun if....  You weren't getting shot through solid objects and from areas where you can't even see your enemies.  The biggest fault of this game is the fact that enemies can see you through objects, and often can shoot you through objects. Shooting an enemy doesn't 'stun' them or cause them to 'recoil' allowing them to basically keep shooting you in the face continuously. However, every time that you are shot, you are hit with recoil that throws off your aim, making it almost entirely impossible to hit anyone until your aim recovers. It's a completely unfair and unbalanced mechanic considering that you are often facing tens of enemies all shooting you at the same time.  Add that to the fact that every couple of seconds whilst you're in the middle of a gun fight, even if you are at full health, you will have to randomly (and with absolutely no warning) dodge left or right, a bullet that WILL INSTANTLY KILL YOU. If you dodge the wrong way. It's called your 'sense of death'. Honestly I wish I could turn this stupid thing off, as all it does is break your concentration to instantly have to choose which side to dodge to, or be killed instantly.  And finally... As if things couldn't get ANY WORSE!  THE FREAKING DUELS ARE RIDICULOUS!  This is honestly perhaps the worst thing about the game. The duels that you do 1v1 with people are so unfair.  First you start off having to keep your mouse cursor (reticle) over the enemy in order to slowly gain focus before they draw their gun to shoot you. Sounds easy right? WRONG! Because the cursor handles like a pig and has some kind of random acceleration applied to it where a small move of the mouse will either send the cursor flying from one side of the screen to the other... or it will barely move. The person you are dueling will also move in a random pattern as well and you have to follow them with your mouse.  BUT WAIT!!! THERE'S MORE!!  Later on you ALSO have to use the freaking 'WSAD' keys to position your hand on top of your gun in order to slowly build up your draw speeds! So not only with one hand do you have to carefully follow the person you are dueling, you also have to f**king watch where your hand is and make sure you use the WSAD keys carefully to keep your hand there. This requires you to basically be 'cross-eyed' and focus on both the foreground and the background. It's such a stupid mechanic, that can probably only be explained by watching a Youtube video.  All in all, it's a good looking game with unique cartoony graphics that reminds me of a 'western' styled Borderlands game that performs pretty well I guess. Performance could be better but I had no real drops or issues on my machine. It's just a shame that there are 3 ridiculously unbalanced and badly thoughtout mechanics thrown into this game that make it a total pain to play. In the end I had to force myself to finish the game.  _______________________________________________  TL;DR   Pros:  - Great graphics - Unique idea for a game - Pretty 'okay' voice acting  Cons:  - Infuriating gun fight mechanics - Completely bugged cover system - Stupid dueling system - Somewhat repetitive gameplay

Arguably the greatest game in the Ys series.  Ys takes nearly all the extraneous elements of RPGs and tosses them all out in favour of a super streamlined action battle system with some of the greatest bosses gaming has ever seen. I'm not exagerating here, Ys really does have some of gaming's greatest bosses.   There were 3 ys games to use this style of engine. The ark of Napishtim (which was the first), The oath in Felghana and finally Origins.  Felghana has the greatest soundtrack of them all and arguably the second strongest soundtrack in the entire series (In a series famous for it's soundtracks (look for a psp or psvita for Ys 7 if you want the best soundtrack)).  Felghana, on it's hardest difficulty has some spectacular bosses. However the game doesn't put it's best foot forward at the start. Initial bosses are pretty simple and hardly inspiring, which is unfortunate because the later bosses are absolutely spectacular. Thematically unique bosses all with their own interesting quirks and unique mechanics make each big baddy feel wholly unique and incredibly satisfying to down. In fact Felghana and Origins are spectacular examples of good boss design (with a few very obnoxious exceptions).  There's also a bit of exploration and secret discovering involved in Ys, and because a number of your abilities give both combat and traversal options there is some fun platforming to be performed in the game.  So this all sounds super positive, are there any negatives then? Actually yeah, quite a few.  This game isn't pretty, I mean let's be fair it's old (2005) but that's gonna be a turn off for some people.  Then there's the plot, Ys' localization is about as good as it can get (Xseed is really good at that) but this isn't a plot that's likely to do anything for you, and not much time is spent on either it or its characters.  The game is also very grind heavy, especially on it's higher difficulties. Levels and weapon upgrades in Ys make a huge difference, to the point where a single level can make your attacks do almost twice the damage and can turn some boss fights from cruel jokes into fun encounters. but there are humungous experience penalties once you start over levelling, and even then you're going to have to farm normal mooks just to avoid being underlevelled, which may have been alright except for the game's final weakness, fights that aren't bosses.  Ys's combat is simple, but the way it blends with each bosses unique mechanics make it incredibly satisfying. This doesn't occur with normal enemies though, which means for many, normal combat in Ys isn't very satisfying, which is unfortunate because you'll need to do a fair amount of farming basic enemies to be able to afford upgrades and avoid being underleveled.  So to sum up: + Awesome soundtrack + Awesome bosses after the first couple of dungeons  - Not very pretty - Grindy on higher diffculties - Fighting basic enemies isn't that fun - Playing on easier difficulties wont let you appreciate it's great bosses properly  Ys will appeal extremely strongly to a certain crowd, and that crowd absolutely SHOULD play it, because it's one of the best examples of it's genre. But most other people will likely find the game to be more trouble than it's worth.

app_name: DARK SOULS™: Prepare To Die Edition
review_text: Dark Souls is one of the greatest games ever made. The game manages to excel in terms of mechanics, atmosphere, immersion, lore, design and replay value. All of this is achieved through tense dungeon crawling impressive enemy encounters, a seamlessly intertwined world, extremely rewarding challenges, complex weaponry and magic, and the flexibility to play the way you want through character customisation.   The online component is an improvement over its 'spiritual' predecessor Demon's Souls, though perhaps at the expense of the dread and feeling of loneliness that Demon's Souls provided. You are able to aid or hinder other players as well as take part in a thriving community of duel based combat.   The world is massive and seamless. Doors open shortcuts to areas you previously visited hours before and the further you explore, the more hidden areas await. The game encourages deep exploration with challenging gameplay simply being a consequence of the setting instead of a focus (something the sequel simply failed at understanding).   The game is not perfect though and I do still believe that Demon's Souls is better. The game requires some modding to improve performance and despite an impressive online system, it simply fails to work 100% of the time and can be frustrating. I still highly recommend the game.

While FTL can be something you sit down and play for short 30 minute sessions, I've also been able to spend hours at a time playing it. There is so much variability to what can happen that no one play through is the same, but that keeps you on your toes. It may seem difficult at first, but after 10 or 20 play throughs you start to get the hang of the general gameplay. TLDR:   The music is beautiful, the art style wonderous and the lore is fantastic. 9/10    I've played quite a few 4X strategies over the years both on PC and other platforms, I have to admit this is my favourite 4X strategy of all time, I am a fan of the Endless series of games, I've enjoyed seeing how the studios games have advanced and how the art style and lore always feels nicely polished and imaginative.  Most other 4X games I have played I've found I play through with one faction and try any other and they are very similar in how they play, this game blows that theory out of the water, each faction has it's own strengths and even differing mechanics, this adds some serious depth to the game, which play style best suits your own?  I've yet to step into the multiplayer side of this game but I am looking to do so soon. I foresee the choice of faction could have a major effect on whether you beat your opponent or not based upon your chosen style of play and ability to action this.  The music is beautiful, the art style wonderous and the lore is fantastic.   There are some minor issues I have but nothing game breaking, battles can get feel a bit slow at times and I think this is made worse by the auto battle option having some insane outcomes at times, especially during the newly added sea battles. The new fire ships they added with the Tempest add on are really unbalanced and just blitz everything.  Finally Endless Legend is one of my favourite games and I can see myself playing it for some time to come, I do hope they revisit this style of gameplay again after a what I hope will be a complete sucess with Endless Space 2.
A 4x game similar to Civ, every faction offers a very unique playstyle, definitely worth looking in to for anyone who likes strategy games and wants something unique.

In a nutshell duelyst is like chess played with cards.  I'll make comparisons with hearthstone to better describe the main meat of the game. Please dont take this as 'duelyst is better' or 'hearthstone is better' , i think both games are very distinct and great experiences, while my point is that duelyst is really easy to get into and enjoy a wide variety of decks(gameplay).   I wanted to emphasize on that fact, cause thinking of 'another huge investment phase to gather/collect before actually enjoying the game' is what made me hesitate to try Duelyst.  So, arena price and pack prices are the same as hearthstone, while;  Legendary ratio is 1 on every 4 pack. Its 1 on 20 pack in hearthstone. (both numbers are taken from developer quotes) Cards are cheaper to craft, legendaries are like half the price relative to hs. You get 15 gold for every 2 wins, in hs you get 10 gold for every 3 wins. You get 2x 25 gold quest + a 5 gold puzzle everyday, in hs you get 1 quest worth 40 to 60 gold. (and 100 gold quest maybe once in a month) Arena is much more rewarding than hearthstone, like in 7 wins you get another free entry+2 cards+gold+dust+pack. I wont even explain hs counterpart, if youve ever played hearthstone you must be familiar with constant complains about arena rewards. You get more rewards at the end of seasons. You can get cosmetics/emotes/cardbacks/skins with ingame currency.  With its non-existent 'invest first' phase, cool different mechanics, basic decks that can actually rank up, Duelyst is a game that you can actually enjoy while mainly playing hearthstone or mtg etc. And one that you'll certainly love if you liked said games above.

review_text: great game with nice graphics, but requires beefy computer. i currently have 60 hrs on the game.

SimTower meets Privateer  Interesting combination of game elements in a manner that shouldn't work, but somehow does. Interesting range of ships (but needs to be bigger and include more classes), the commodity model is reasonable (albeit rather basic), very similar to the Privateer model of base types, except that this game allows for bases to be multiple 'types'.  Crew recruitment and requirements can be counter-intuitive and frustrating, especially with limited options at each base and the need to build up a large crew over time. I would have thought that having a crew that eats different things would lead to better harmony as they're not all competing for the same subset of the food supply (but that's me I guess).  Upskilling crew has one SERIOUS limitation; you don't get to choose the additional skill you would like a crew member to learn if you want to multi-skill them. This to me is a massive flaw and not representative of the real world, where bosses have a large say in what development paths you follow. Additionally, the random assignment of special attributes is great during upskilling except that it introduces negative traits quite a bit. I understand that as people become more specialised they can become less adept in other areas, but given that it's an upskill, at least have special attributes balanced; +1 something, -1 something else.  Combat (before you're ready for it) is even more frustrating because the flee move doesn't seem to actually allow you to evade combat; it should if the game is to be properly balanced IMHO)  Mission choices are extremely limited (like Privateer) but the class of missions seems to be even smaller. Combat, smuggling, or delivery (cargo or passengers). That would be fine if there was a larger range of destinations and missions to choose from, but there isn't.  Finally, I think the biggest concern I have is that the Devs seem to have moved on to new projects, announcing there is no longer full time support going into this game. Don't get me wrong; I like the game, but it's incomplete. There are still some crashes that occur semi-regularly in the game, and there needs to be more content developed for the game to increase choice and range. I'd like to see more effort put into getting this game right before new games receive focus.  Pros: Reasonably simple trading model, nice touch of ship management with crew, so long as it doesn't impact the flow of the game.  Cons: Frustrating crew recruitment and upskilling, poor range of choice generally (ships, missions, new crew, pretty much everything), and no more full time support from the devs.  While I recommend this game, I'd love a button for 'undecided'. It's about a 55/45 split for me at the moment, although I intend to put more time into it to see if familiarity and learning curve change the balance somewhat. Original fusion of ideas, but needs more polish.

It all depends on the DM.   I played about 50 hours in the head start and like a lot of players here I was disappointed. Not a lot of DM tools, tilesets, classes and so on. The graphics are okay for a game like this but far from great.  Without a good DM (or any at all) the game is at best a poor Diablo clone. But then I played with a good DM and LOVED it. He paused the game in every room, described us what we saw, let us talk to the NPCs (ignoring the in game tools) and had us make saving throws (you can roll with /roll d20 for example). He let us decide what we wanted to say, to do and where we wanted to go. It felt like a proper game of table top D&D. So when you find a great DM or know one this game can be great.  I hope they open a steam workshop and let players create new content and don't try to be to greedy, but so far I recommend it.

app_name: DARK SOULS™ II: Scholar of the First Sin
review_text: Normally, I'm a big fan of the Souls games, however, one thing I am not a fan of is FROM SOFTWARE's inability to be reasoned with. I was accused of cheating for using a few mods that made the game playable for me. In particular, I was soft-banned for using DS2Fix and x360ce. These two mods do the following:  -DS2Fix: Fix a glitch in the game that causes the durability of your weapons to decrease at twice the normal rate when playing the game at 60 FPS.  -x360ce: Allows you to use directinput gamepads in Dark Souls (in other words, most older gamepads before the XBOX 360 controller).  I have written a request to Bandai Namco to have my ban lifted after admitting that I used these mods (I later bought myself an XBOX 360 controller after finding out about soft-banning), and FROM SOFTWARE told me that since I violated the EULA (even if it was only so that I could play the game the way it was meant to be played), that my soft-ban will not be lifted.  Since a lot of the achievements in the game are a lot more difficult to obtain without any sort of online interactivity, this ruined the game for me in a lot of ways. That is not to say that the achievements and the game are impossible to beat solo, but it will be a much less enjoyable experience as a whole, considering that several bosses are, for the most part, large mobs, as opposed to the much more interesting and creative bosses found in Dark Souls and Demon's Souls respectively.  The game also puts a lot of emphasis on ambushing and surprising you in inconvenient locations just to make sure they get a couple of extra deaths out of you, which in my opinion is just cheap. After being ambushed and dying as a result of said ambush, it becomes pretty easy to figure out what to do in subsequent attempts, but it just seems blatantly unnecessary. The game is not terribly hard, but you will die simply because you're not ready for every single ambush and surprise the game has in store for you.  Another thing I would complain about (but kind of seems moot, considering that I'm soft-banned anyway), is the concept of soul memory. Every single soul you collect is added to a stat that matches you up against opponents (and potential allies) with similar amounts of souls. For novice players, this can be troublesome, considering that a lot of souls that are collected will inevitably go to waste, either through the purchase of items, inefficient leveling, or simply through permanently losing souls. Demon's Souls, Dark Souls and Bloodborne do not utilize this system, and in my opinion, it's better that way.  Lastly, another thing I don't particularly like is the concept that enemies can disappear. Permanently. If you play through a stage enough times, and keep killing the same enemies over and over, they will eventually stay dead, making soul farming more difficult, unless you have plenty of bonfire ascetics onhand. The only problem with using those is the stage that they're used on is raised one more difficulty level and EVERYTHING is reset for that level, which compels you to have to redo the entire stage (it would be foolish not to), and fight a stronger version of the same boss at the end of the level.  Therefore, soft-banning aside, I consider this entry in the Dark Souls series to be the weakest entry in the series. It does a lot of things that makes it ever-so-slightly less enjoyable than other FROM SOFTWARE gems.  All in all, I would recommend this game only if you're a die-hard Souls fan, if you're very careful not to use any sort of mods whatsoever, and if you don't already have all of the original Dark Souls II and its DLC. Remember, ANYTHING other than the what the game originally came with can result in a soft-ban, and you will NEVER get unbanned ever again.  If you want to be safe, you should probably just stick with the PS4 or XBONE versions. FROM SOFTWARE, like a lot of other 3rd party console developers are not particularly good at releasing PC Ports.  TLDR: -Don't use mods. -Make sure that you use an xinput gamepad (for example, the XBOX 360 controller). -Don't purchase if you have DS II with all of its DLC already. -Not as good as other Souls games. -Might want to play the PS4 or XBONE versions instead.  Overall: 4/10 - PC Port 7/10 - SOTFS (in general)

Hummm good game in general. Not a bullet hell unless you can't kill the enemies fast enough 8) Graphics and music are standard, action is constant, ship can be upgraded. A decent release.   Now about the things that aren't mentioned:  Game has 3 checkpoints and a 'continue' system. At levels 11, 21 and 31 your progress is saved so if you use all your 'continues' you'll restart at these checkpoints. Past level 31 you have to make it to the end with the 'continues' you have. The game does save the level you're playing and the number of lives left so you can stop playing at anytime you want and resume later.  The very bad thing about this game is how you can gimp your ship by choosing the wrong upgrades. The game is linear; you can't just replay levels to make more money or try to get a better score on them so if you reach a checkpoint with the bad upgrades then well.... you can't repick them and chances are you won't be able to advance any further.  Also, once you quit the upgrade screen you are stuck to the secondary weapon you chose and the shield level you bought... meaning that you have to finish the level before changing anything to your ship. When i left the upgrade screen at level 45 i forgot to equip my satellites and was stuck with the beam laser and of course i had only 2 shields. Used up 12 'continues' before uninstalling the game.  I though 'getting stuck with your choices' was a 90's thing. When stuff can be upgraded we need to be able to either reverse it or farm for more cash, espescially if you can end up gimping you to the point you can't advance in the game.  Was my 2 cents anyway.

The Hero of the Kingdom games can best be compared to the point and click hidden object games of Artifex Mundi. While there are no specific hidden object scenes, you do have to look for, and click on tiny objects. Another similarity is the backtracking to pass obstacles.   For example, a bridge is out, and you need three pieces of stone, a rope, twelve ocelot furs, an eskimo translator, and a toaster oven in order to repair the bridge. You remember that there was an old woman in a village a couple of screens back that offered to give you her toaster oven in exchange for a potion that will treat her husbands ED. You also know that a rural healer specializing in potions is in need of an attorney for his practicing medicine without a license case. Additionally, you met an out of work attorney, who will take any case as long as you can provide him with five hundred gold coins, and a 'personal massage device'.   Such is the recursive nature of this type of game, and although it can be frustrating at times, the bizarre Goldbergian machinations somehow appeal to my sense of humor. I mean, there is currency in the game, gold coins actually, yet so many transactions have to be completed with some random item the NPC desires instead. Imagine if you took your car in for repair, and instead of money, the mechanic demanded a live elk. Such is the world the Hero of the kingdom inhabits.   While I'm at it, I must say, this guy has got to be one of the most inept, clumsy heroes around, because it seems like every other time I use any sort of tool, it breaks. Going fishing? Oops. broke the rod, have to buy a new one. Harvesting flowers? Oops, broke the sickle, have to, wait, hold on, why did you even need a sickle to pick flowers? Anyway, you get the point. Mr. Hero is no Bob Villa, and if he ever asks you to borrow any tools, just remember that this is a guy who can break a piece of steel on a flower stem.   In conclusion, to sum it up, to summarize, to wrap it all up, to encapsulate, to distill, um, I forget what I was going to say. I guess if you like pointy clicky roundabout games, this is one and if you don't, it still is.

This game is dangerously addictive. The open world is literally of galactic proportions, and the variety of gameplay options means that you can change your play style and it becomes a different game entirely. Load up a ship with a good scanner, several fuel tanks, a fuel scoop, and head off on long exploration journeys. While out, you can be the pioneer to reach a distant system, forever marking the system with your name as the first explorer, and when you get back, you can sell the exploration data for a small fortune. Or run cargo along trade routes, and pick up missions to smuggle illicit goods while you're at it. Or, perhaps my favorite, hit the civil war combat zones and rack up combat bonds. So much fun.

Yes, this is a TRUE Tomb Raider game!  If you are fond of TR Legend, or TR Underworld Anniversary may intimidate you with its significantly more challenging combat and puzzles epecially if you are not playing with a controller. If the original TR I II and III were your liking however, Anniversary is your game.   PROS: - Beautiful, clean graphics. Game looks 'next-gen' without suffering from the fps drop or freezes that haunted Legend. - Wonderful sound effects and background music. - Fluid, precise control that inspires confidence. - Varied locations, each with its unique settings. - Top notch cutscenes with lots of emotions of Lara showing her exact thoughts of the moment. - Its not a high demanding game. If you have an older laptop that is having trouble playing old games like Team Fortress 2 this will run way better.   CONS: - Horrible checkpoint system. There is plenty of rooms in this game that you will spend 5-10 min that will restart if you die. - Hints are subpar for this game when it comes to puzzles; this leads to potential backtracking and wasted trips. - Old-fashioned, non-streamlined inventory system that requires arrow keys, TAB and Esc key to cycle through. - Random difficulty spikes in some of the levels make this game harder than other Tomb Raiders like TR Underworld.  - There are a couple of rare glitches that occur for some people that has Lara unable to grab a ledge that's needed to be grabbed to progress the story. You will have to exit out to start menu to fix.     For a 2007 remake this game was immense fun and even great frustrations over 90's TR games, Anniversary delivers a truthful TR experience. It's challenging, but equally rewarding. Even if you already beat the original a handful of time back in the day I can still safely recommend it.

Borderlands definitely gets my reccomendation, I would say that in short the game is a rather fun FPS with a good chunk of Rpg'ing, The landscape and oppenents can get quite repetitive after a while however the game attempts to make up with this by the sheer amount of guns. When i say sheer amount i mean that there are literally hundreds of different guns, some may feel much like a copy, however this is countered by the level system meaning that as you progress you will begin to see more and more types/combinations of guns and gun parts. Now the main problem with this game is the repetivity, this comes in the form of both landscapes and enemys, though a few different types of enemys are added occaisonally it stays mostly the same throughout the story, (this could be a problem fixed in the DLC, i'm not aware if it is or not) however the game trys to make up for this with several various jokes and or references that make the game entertaining at points. To wrap up i would like to say that what really makes this game worth playing are these two things. One is that you can literally jump into the game and have no idea whats going on and still just have a good time shooting things. Two is the multiplayer, You and four of your friends can jump in at any time to play together, which can lead to some interesting competitions, forced teamwork, hilarity and sometimes fights over guns, So overall i'd say if your going to get borderlands i'd reccomend you have a friend that plays it as well.

8/10  Only played this game recently, so have to say was not really impressed by the graphics. The vechicle missions were average at best, and thankfully were removed from the sequel.  I have to say I saw this game as a prequel, and it was interesting to see the start of Shepards adventure through space. Saren is at times a charismatic antagonist, and the game is quite challenging on the higher difficulties.  As an introduction into the ME universe, the lore here is deep, rich and well thought out, seeding races, histories, and conflicts for further games, including the future ones. Overall this game is still worth playing, most noticably for the skilled story-telling and character progression that Bioware has become noted for.  Recommend picking it up as a pack, either with ME2 here, or the complete pack on Origin when on sale, There is at least 20 intriguing hours of gameplay here without diving too much into everything the game has to offer. €10-15 for 60-80 hours of gamplay across all games is amazing.

Having picked up the game during the summer sale I wasn't expected much from the title and even with low expectations I can now see why QC never received much attention. Before buying it, I knew it had John DeLancie doing the voice acting and that really adds character to the game but the game itself is.. well.. it's dull.   The game also chooses to lean more towards platforming skills rather than puzzle ability as a large number of tasks involve creating platforms to reach other areas. The difficulty in the game mainly comes from precision jumps rather than your ability to solve puzzles and as such it varies from tediously easy to frustratingly hard. It's also a matter of pacing. The various dimensions are seperated out into different wings of the mansion. The ability to slow time to a crawl is certainly entertaining but that alone does not justify how tedious the soft/heavy dimensions are and you have to trudge through them before you get to that.  I picked this up for a few quid and frankly I don't feel it was value for money. Playing with aspects of the Unreal physics engine does not alone make for a good game and this has escaped the developers understanding. It's rare for me to lose interest in a game to the point of not wanting to finish it, but QC managed to be dull enough for me to uninstall it before I reached the very end.

Really amazed at the positive reviews, but time for me to tip my hand into the pool. The gameplay is fantastic, and the work done by High Moon Studios is stupendous. Writing is unbelievably fantastic, and animations and character designs are beautiful to look at. Levels are also well designed, and the sounds and music is also really decent. Some hiccups here and there with the levels and creatures you have to fight, balance seems to be a bit off.  Now for the fun part.  There is no MULTIPLAYER FOR NEW USERS. Brother bought this for us when he ended up having IBS and Chrons attacks so there was something he could do to take his mind off of pain while in bed. After contacting support and trying whatever I could, there has still been no solution, and all current new users are reporting the same problem. Activision has stated it is due to microsoft's sign in software they use, and if true, I can understand they are beholden to them. Writing an entire new system would take a very long time, and could be very costly for minimum return. Problem is, it also can come at the cost of reputation.  The biggest problem I have is they do not display this information on the page, so any player who plays single then goes to play multiplayer just spent a whole lot for quite a lot less then what they expected. For that, and the fact they just released a new version on consoles and haven't done anything to fix any of this, I'd rate this game a thumbs down. Get it on console or on a 10$ or less sale.   Tl;Dr Awesome story mode, cool graphics, some hiccups, but ¾ of the game does not work. Get at a major discount and you'll love it. If you pay full price or buy any DLC, and your gonna have a bad day. :P  EDIT: After about 6 months or so, the servers are finally fixed to allow new players. Highly suggest Escilation with buddies. Have some fun!  EDIT 2: Online matches seem to be down again. Might just be me, not sure.

Hotline Miami is a top down 80s styled shooter but it is so much more than that. For you to progress in this game you will need to be quick and have fast reflexes to kill enemies before they kill you but at the same time it is manditory to strategically plan your way through the level meaning it's a game requiring both thought and skill. As for the enemies your only advantage over them is that you have the magical power to 'press R to restart' (which you will use to your advantage quite a lot, trust me) because your health is equal to them, you can die in one shot, they can die in one shot, which leads to one of the most prominent features of the game; it's difficulty. If you are a casual gamer not up for a challenge this game is probably not for you, but if you're up for a challenge playing through this game is really rewarding. Killing enemies will result in a satisfying display of blood and gore and exploring through the levels looking for secrets (  masks and letters for the secret password  ) will also keep you occupied. Regarding the story, as for the most part it is pretty dull until the major revelation at the true ending which I found to be pretty cool but otherwise I think the story is a bit overrated in this particular game. The story element gets expanded upon much more in the second instalment but as an Australian it is not possible for me to legally purchase Hotline Miami 2: Wrong Number as it is banned here. :(
Hotline Miami is a striking blend of fast ultraviolence, a dense, challenging story and brilliant presentation.

Act 1 was an amazing game with great art and a ton of potential, even if it was a little short.  Act 2 is a miserable exercise in illogical tedium, and probably the worst adventure game I have ever played. The wiring puzzles are irritating and tedious, and most of the rest of the puzzles are illogical chores that are primarily solved with trial and error. Some of the puzzles rely upon a connection between Vella and Shay that never exists - in other words, Vella can only solve a puzzle with knowledge that Shay has, but they have no way of communicating and it's up to the player just to do it for them, despite that Vella never actually has the information to solve it.  The writing in Act 2 is bad, and the ending is utter nonsense. This game is a major disappointment after how great Act 1 was. I don't care about the money I spent on it; I wish there was a way for them to refund me the time I spent on it, however. Stay very far away from this travesty.
