app_name: Ring Runner: Flight of the Sages
review_text: I had this game on the back-burner as I was busy with school. At first I thought it was a difficult game, getting used mostly to the controls and interface, but once I got into I COULD NOT PUT IT DOWN! It was an awesome campaign more, the story is fantastic, the customization has me playing in deathmatch mode more than anything, the MOBA and minigame modes are fantastic features too. This is a fantastic gem and very well crafted, I wish I had a joystick to test it out better but even with the slightly clunky keyboard controls it's still a fantastic game that has me on the edge of my seat as I blast away at differently customized AI ships. Deffinetly get this game!!

The Vanishing of Ethan Carter revolves about finding the boy (Ethan) who wants to be a writer - so he creates many interesting supernatural short stories. Who are you, you might ask - let's say that doesn't matter. You are there to investigate what happened with Ethan and his family that got possessed by evil.   What distinguish this game from other walking simulators, is definitely the graphics, sounds and music. It's made so delicately that you'll enjoy every second walking in the Red Creek Valley. And if you are more impatient or just want to quickly return somewhere, don't worry, developers allowed you to sprint (this is not Dear Esther). The nature around you feels like modded Skyrim that even on newest desktop builds run on low FPS, but Unreal Engine 4 makes a difference and this game, since the Redux edition that you get for free, runs great!  The gameplay itself is casual, most of the time you'll be exploring the valley, with interludes of dark memories of Carter family, and interludes where Ethan's short stories become real. Those stories are really nice and there's the one that stands out from the others - I won't spoil much, I'll just say that the Lovecraftian horror fans will get excited.   What's worse is the length of the game, the game offers about 3 hours of entertainment with getting all achievements, and not much replayability. That fact is compensated with the beautiful environment and precious soundtrack. In the end it's all about your priorities, if you prefer the environment and story, or the length.  I understand if you don't want to pay 19 euros for a short walking simulator but trust me - if you will see it on sale, buy it. It's a great experience.

Much like a any Bethesda game if you come into it with certain expectations you can come out of it very satisfied. Civilization has historically always had rough launches in regards to AI, multiplayer stability, and various mechanics that need to be smoothed over by patches. So do not buy this game now if you want a polished game, Civ V is still amazing.    That being said this is my favorite Civilization game.    They took the best aspects of previous civs, enhanced those mechanics, and added a few new changes that really change the game for the best.    Elements Kept:    Hex System (civ 5)  Movement System (pre-civ 5) Multiple Leader Civ Ability (pre-civ 5, only for greek civ as of now) Modding Available   Elements Enhanced:    Graphics Policy System Government System Religion Diplomacy (Also see negatives) More Detailed Diplomacy (also see negatives) Civs More Aggressive (also see AI negatives) Music City States (more involved) Happiness now city to city (pre-civ 5) Great People now visually competitive Great Wonders   Elements Added:    Barbarian Raid System (barbarian scounts will bring raiding parties) Religious Battles District System Quicker *Online* Speed Option Lenses (changes view for religion, settlement ect)  Individual AI Leader Temperment    Overall Negatives:    AI needs a LOT of work  Diplomacy needs some numbers seriously tweaked & more explicit Some mechanics need to be more explicit Had a few disconnects from Multiplayer Few things should be added in the options (ex. unit auto selection) Mini-Map looks pretty awful   I'll probably edit this as I play and as my opinion evolves but I'm personally very happy with this launch and am looking forward to the upcoming patches to fix the negatives.

I can't express how disappointed I am with this game. Far Cry 3 and Blood Dragon were both surprise personal favs that I played to death on the 360, so I was excited for 4. Sadly, it ended up being a total slog that I could only tolerate for a couple hours at a time.   There are so many little annoyances that build up over time. Predatory wildlife is hyper-aggressive and always seems to come out of nowhere. Enemies can shoot the wings off a fly at hundreds of yards. Stealth is less effective, and your thrown rocks are almost always ignored by all but one or two guards. Healing animations are somehow slower than 3, so enjoy crouching in a corner and watching them over and over and over again. The story is even less engaging than 3's, largely because you only see the villain a few times in-game before he's regulated to the boring task of talking your ear off via your radio. Performance is awful, too - just check out the multiple reports of random stuttering on systems that are more than capable.   It's not total unrepentant garbage. There are some moments of brilliance in the rare times where everything works right together. But they're so few and far between in such a long game.

Cooking Academy Fire and Knives is a collection of three Cooking Academy games, which are a series of time management games. In each game, you learn a variety of recipes by following the instructions accurately and in a timely fashion. In doing so, you can earn various trophies and depending on how well you master the recipes, the trophies can vary anywhere from bronze to crystal level. (Silver and gold are the in-between levels.) The gameplay itself is pretty much the same in all three games, with there only being slightly more variety in each sequel.  There are very little differences among the three games. Besides the slight variety (a.k.a. addition of cooking techniques/instructions), the basic story slightly varies. In Cooking Academy 1, you take on the role of a student at the cooking academy and learn to make dishes ranging from appetizers to desserts. In Cooking Academy 2, you compete among other students to win a cash prize of one million dollars to open up your own restaurant and in order to do so, you have to master cooking dishes from around the world. Finally, in Cooking Academy 3, you return to the cooking academy to write your own cookbook and want the help of the teachers to contribute recipes for your book. Despite these slight differences, though, the basic gameplay is the same-cooking dishes by following the instructions given and to do so accurately and timely.  I enjoyed this collection of Cooking Academy games and found them to be challenging and fun. Cooking games are probably one of my all-time favorite time management games but that might be because I am way better at computer simulated cooking than real life cooking. lol I liked the accompanying music and there was plenty of variety in gameplay. Some of the steps were more challenging than others but the challenge only motivated me more to earn specific trophies. I have to admit that Cooking Academy 1 was the most challenging game for me but that might be because certain game mechanics for it (at the time) weren't thoroughly worked out so you had to guess instinctively as to whether you were doing certain steps correctly, while this issue was ironed out in the later games.   Overall, I really enjoyed the Cooking Academy collection and had a lot of fun playing through all three games. It definitely took a while but the time was very well spent. I hope that the devs will consider bringing Kitchen Brigade to Steam someday. (From what I understand, Kitchen Brigade is the spin off to the Cooking Academy series and it takes place after you win one million dollars from Cooking Academy 2.) If you like time management games and those that have variety and are challenging, then I'd definitely recommend getting this collection for yourself. ^^

This isn't my favorite game of all time, nor is it my least favorite, and yet it's the first game I'm going to write a review for. Why's that? Because this game needs to become way more popular before the sequel to 'to the moon' comes out, so every review counts.  TL:DR 8/10. Short, not as emotional as to the moon, and kinda predictable, but it's a really good story, and fits in nicely with too the moon. But! it is a must buy if your looking forwards to the to the moon sequel.  Full review:  I think most people came here after playing Too the Moon (which I will now shorten to TTM) TTM is a must have game for anyone who is addicted to the 'Story Rich' Tag. Free bird studios was successful in making a game that fits alongside Underale, LISA, The Walking Dead, Life is Strange, and countless other games with a heart-felt story, likeable characters, a good soundtrack, and some sort of applicable moral or lesson.  And what do people usually do after playing one of these games? well if the story was good, they'll usually think about it for one or two weeks, tell some people about it, maybe write a review, and await a sequal if the story didn't have complete closure.  For me, TTM was no different. After playing the game, and watching that final scene, I sat in my chair and thought 'Damn, it's over, isn't it?' But I wasn't able to shake it out of my mind. So I listened to the soundtrack, played some of the free games on Free Bird's website (Which you should really check out!) and read the comic that they made. It was finally getting some rest in the back of my mind, when one day, i saw.... that a sequal was planned.  I couldn't believe my eyes! It was like christmas had come early! I knew that I would have to get it. But why tell you all this? well because, and minor spoiler for Bird story here....... This game Doesn't really have much to do with TTM, but it has everything to do with it's sequel.  And thats what people need to understand. Sure, as a standalone game, it's only an 8 out of 10, a solid B. But in context, this game provides the foundation for an amazing sequel, one that could even be better than TTM!. I can't really go into why without spoiling anything, but just know that this game will have you even more hyped for the TTM sequal.  all around a great way to spend a cold winter afternoon.

The people, while simple, are also well done) the story behind this game is by far enough, to say the least. Just to re-itterate, i was ASTOUNDED by this games storyline. Ive found few other books that can compare. For a free game and my first attempt at this genre, I am honest to god stunned. Happy reading!

A fun game that gets increasingly challenging (and a little less linear) as you progress. For long-time Jagged Alliance fans, this is more similar to JA: Deadly Games than JA1 or JA2 due to it's mission based approach. If you buy this expecting an open world, RPG/Strategy game like the classic JA2, you will be disappointed. If you are looking for a well made turn-based strategy game, then JA Online Reloaded might be exactly what you are looking for. Just give it time because the difficulty of the missions, and the quality of the AI, improves after the first dozen or so missions.  I also want to quickly say how impressed I have been by the responsiveness of the developers. There was a major issue on launch day that prevented me from playing the game. A developer responded to my query within 30 seconds of asking it and they worked through the night (on a weekend) to fix it.
Should not have been released as a full game.

app_name: EARTH DEFENSE FORCE 4.1  The Shadow of New Despair
review_text: It is, what you expect it to be, when you're reading this review. EDF 4.1 even went beyond my expectations, so likely it will also top yours. Come, join the 2-player split-screen or 4-player co-op battle now! Hesitation may mean the death of many...

KotD is not really a sequel but it's more like an expansion of DreadOut, taking place just before the final events of the previous title (  after being dragged into the mirror, before facing the Lady in Red  ), which tries to shed light on some details but at the same time generating new questions..  The game mechanics remain the same from DreadOut: the protagonist, Linda, armed only with a smartphone/digital camera have to confront and exorcise with photo shots the spirits that appear in front of her to continue; also unchanged is the feel of clunkiness in the transition from third to first person which still leads sometimes to small frustration during combat (yes, giant head, I'm talking to you..), but fortunately, unlike the first chapter being defeated during a confrontation does not lead to a total 'reset' of the fight (which in the case of DreadOut's scissors guy could lead to a fast ragequit..).  What has changed instead is more freedom of action: from the starting room, the order in which to explore the different areas (a minor gripe, most of them are recycled from the previous one) and defeat the various guardians that inhabit them is up to us. Another noticeable new feature is the 'wildlife' of the game that, apart from a couple of old 'friends' from the previous episode, with more than a dozen new spirits, each one with its own tactics and movesets.  Overall a good horror, maybe with a bit cryptic story; an almost obligatory purchase if you enjoyed the first DreadOut.. especially on sale! ;)  -----  KotD non è un vero e proprio seguito ma più un'espansione di DreadOut, collocandosi appena prima delle fasi finali del titolo precedente (  dopo essere stata trascinata all'interno dello specchio, prima di affrontare la Signora in Rosso  ), che cerca di fare luce su alcuni particolari ma allo stesso tempo generando nuovi interrogativi..  Le meccaniche di gioco rimangono invariate rispetto a DreadOut: la protagonista, Linda, armata solo di smartphone/fotocamera deve affrontare ed esorcizzare a colpi di fotografie gli spiriti che le si parano davanti per proseguire; invariata rimane anche la sensazione di legnosità nel passaggio da terza a prima persona che porta ancora a qualche piccola frustrazione durante gli scontri (sì, testa gigante, ce l'ho con te..), ma fortunatamente a differenza del primo capitolo venire sconfitti durante uno scontro non porta al 'reset' totale dello scontro (cosa che con il tizio delle forbici di DreadOut poteva portare ad un rapido ragequit..).  Cosa cambia invece è la maggior libertà di azione che viene concessa: partendo dalla stanza in cui ci si trova all'inizio, in che ordine affrontare le varie aree (piccola nota negativa, la maggior parte è ricilata dal primo capitolo) e sconfiggere i diversi guardiani che le popolano è una decisione che spetta a noi. Altra evidente novità è rappresentata dalla 'fauna' del gioco che, a parte un paio di vecchi 'amici' dal precedente episodio, vede oltre una decina di nuovi spiriti, ognuno con tattiche e movenze diverse.  Tutto sommato un buon horror, forse con una storia un po' criptica; acquisto pressoché obbligato se avete apprezzato il primo DreadOut.. specialmente se in offerta! ;)

Falling Simulator 2016   Pros: -Beautiful graphics and map structures. -Freedom of movement when it comes to 'spiderman-ing/AoT-ing' around the map. -Overall atmosphere and calming audio.  Cons: -Walking in the game feels like you’re stuck in mud. Unless momentum is already built up.  -Not enough check points. You can zoom past 3/4 of a puzzle then get stuck on the last quarter for the next hour or two.  -The games physics can seem a bit wonky with wall jumping at times. Sometimes a simple tap on the jump button launches you hella high in the air, while other times not so much.   Overall this game has been equally frustrating as it has been enjoyable. If you are patient and enjoy an epic jump puzzle adventure this game is a good pick up.   8/10

Fun romp through a magical dreamland where you leave your common sense at the door and embrace the craziness~  With lovingly crafted retro graphics authentic to the PC 98 era, and an awesome soundtrack to boot, it's clear every aspect of this game has been carefully set up to provide a unique experience you won't find in any other game.  At its core, it focuses on providing the player a very particular atmosphere and sense of mystery as you poke about the various rooms and locations that seem to shift and shape on a whim--meaning you'll never know where you might end up next. And while confusing at times, there's a steady sense of progression to be made as you gather new items and unlock new parts of the dreamland. For the completionists, there's also a set of secret items that reward you for exploring every nook and cranny of the place, ultimately altering the ending you may get once you finish.  It's something that should be experienced, rather than described in words, really. And you can't really beat the low, low price of 'free'!

This new Dante feels like an old friend.  I'll admit, I was skeptical of this series re-launch, especially when it was first announced back in 2010 and the first promotional image was this:  http://static1.wikia.nocookie.net/__cb20120923082161/devilmaycry/images/1/12/Dante_dmc_2010.jpg   My gut reaction to seeing that image back then was 'You just ruined Dante and the DMC franchise.' and to an extent, that gut reaction sort of stayed with me until I finally had a chance to try the game for myself.  Thankfully, the end result was far from my initial fears.  DmC does a good job of managing to reinvent the so-called 'origin' story of Dante and Vergil, and yet by and large once you manage to get used to the new face and very different attitude (at least at first) of the new Dante, you'll discover that the gameplay itself is nothing short of the classic DMC style you've come to know.  Controls have been somewhat re-imagined in a way that neither takes too long to get used to nor feels terribly unfamiliar. By and large they are handled well, and a lot of the familiar signature aspects that the franchise is known for are still here as well.  For example, orbs and the time statues for 'shopping' are present here. Dante still has Ebony and Ivory and they still get unlimited ammo. Signature abilities like Stinger make their return with a new control schemata. Devil trigger is of course also present, though it works in a slightly different way than in most of the past entries, it nonetheless manages to impress just as it always has.  While I did have some initial complaints about Dante's voice acting (the lines seemed really flat early on in the game) once you give it some more time it fleshes itself out better, and that also gives you a chance to get adjusted to the new Dante as well.  And it IS an adjustment. Gone is the super confident whimsical and snarky don't-take-life-too-seriously Dante of yore, for the most part. This new Dante is younger, more apathetic and less enthusiastic, but to an extent this new attitude is plot related as well, and for what it's worth I think it is a convincing way to show character growth and progression.  Gradually as Dante begins to open his eyes to the situation around him through the plot events, he starts becoming more empathetic, and discovers his own true nature in the process.  I won't spoil the details too much, but it was good to see his character maturing over the course of the game. And while I do feel like he never reached the same height on the badass meter that classic Dante manages to capture, by the time all is said and done it's clear to see he's squarely on his way to becoming more like his platinum haired alter ego.  Take away from this what you will, but if you're willing to brave the initial storm and wait for the seas to calm, as far as the new Dante goes, you'll see this is another great entry in the DMC series. It takes a bit to get used to the changes, but change can be good sometimes.

Took me a while to get into but once i did, i loved it.  It's better then Fallout 3, why ? Because it lets you do whatever you please, you can kill everything if you feel like it, even important characters related to the main plot of the game can die and the world will react to it properly with many dialogs with NPCs changing to what you did and some quests might not even be open to you anymore due to what you did.  Quests in New Vegas tend to have alot of multiple choices,solutions and endings, not just one neutral ending,one evil ending and one good ending , instead there's a fine line of varying shades of grey which will often make you think about what you're about to do instead of doing the usual video game trope of 'Well, i wanna be a good guy/a**hole so I'll pick text that's in blue/red'.  This is why people tend to recommend Obsidian's New Vegas over Bethesda's Fallout 3, New Vegas has far more intreasting things to do in it with an insane amount of choices and outcomes which make replaying it a second and even a 3rd time still a somewhat refreshing experience without resorting to installing community made quests via the Nexus mod site.  However, the problem New Vegas still has to this date is the engine it's powered by. Even when it came out back in 2010, the engine it had was considered a fairly limiting and old engine, it can only use 2GB of RAM on a system which is just one of the many reasons why this game loves to crash so much.  It also comes with an annoying micro-stutter bug that's caused by the fact than the engine was for some reason coded for 64 Hz at 64 FPS for some strange reason and since pretty much no one in this world has a TV/Monitor that's 64Hzs, it ends up causing weird micro-stutters in the game. Thankfully the community created various mods which fixed these issues, there's a popular mod which removes the micro-stutters so you can enjoy a a nice smooth 60 (or 120/144 ) FPS experience without any stutters and there's a community made launcher which enables New Vegas to properly use at least 4GB of RAM and not just 2, which is a must if you plan to mod New Vegas with graphical mods that increase texture sizes alot or maybe add alot more models and other things in the world to be loaded by.  Overall, it's an amazing game that's sadly sometimes hampered with technical issues it has due to the engine it uses.

After hearing about Gone Home topping many game of the year lists I was interested, but the review that had me hooked was one that simply stated “I can’t tell you anything about the game, just play it!” Two days later, I can honestly say, they were absolutely right!  I preface this post by saying that I will not give away anything about the game, because that will only take away from the beautiful experience that the first play through is. The story takes place in 1995 where you, a college student after spending a year abroad in Europe, returns to your family home. You get to the new house your family just moved into in the middle of a stormy night to find no one there. This is where your story begins. Where is everyone? What has been up to for the past year? What’s the story behind this old house?  The developers manage to tell an amazing story strictly through the environment of the world around you. You learn things through pictures and notes. Every item you find has its own history, and does it’s part to shed some light on the history of a particular person or event in the world. The fact that they devs made gamble to not hold the player’s hand allow them to piece things together on their own was not just refreshing, but impressive. 2 – 3 hours in, I completed the game. At that point, as the final credits rolled, I smiled, slid my chair back away from my desk, and actually said “Well done, guys”.  Truth be told, I cannot tell you what the game was about. Well I could, but I would be doing you a great disservice. The feeling you get when the pieces start to come together is incredibly satisfying to say the least. So perhaps I should say I will not tell you what the game is about, it’s something that each person should experience for themselves.  After completing the story, I was left with several things running through my mind. First of all, the story was not only very well told, but the story in and of itself is one that will stay with me for years to come. There was a good deal of mid 90’s nostalgia that hit me, what can I say, I’m in my early 30’s… I was there… They managed to nail it…  The last thought I had was about the story itself. Not so much the content of the story, but the whole story telling method. For a game with limited character interactions, there were plenty of characters involved. For game with no puzzles in it, there were several lifetimes worth of pieces to put together. For a game that was developed by an indie studio with a limited budget, there was a better experience put together then any of the triple A studios have done in years.  This game is proof that the adventure game genre is alive and well. It shows that a little imagination goes a long way, and it proves that a solid story wins out against gimmicky gameplay mechanics or shiny graphics on any day of the week.  If there was any doubt by this point, I was a huge fan of the game, and I highly recommend everyone plays it. Though not the longest or toughest of games, I promise those few hours you spend playing it will be special. The guys over at Fullbright did an amazing job with this one, and I’m looking forward to what’s next from them. As far as I’m concerned, they have my money the next time around, I just need to know where and when.  Check this one out, guys… …And you’re welcome…

This game does not do a good job of explaining its mechanics, let alone its higher-level concepts. What begins as a generally fun and interesting educational puzzle game quickly devolves into a frustrating mess. The conveyance and presentation of important concepts, most notably the algorithm puzzles, is opaque. The paper-thin storyline, flat dialogue, and cardboard-cutout characters do nothing to make the puzzle headache worthwhile.  There are definitely a couple of good ideas that went into this game, but they're not developed adequately in the short playtime. The scarcity of interactive elements in the world makes the whole experience feel perfunctory and lifeless. With the sole exception of the clever and engaging boss fight with the corrupted turtle king early in the game, none of this really felt very fun. I managed to inadvertently skip about half of the prison level by rushing past the boss with the movement speed hack, which screwed me a bit later in the game as I was unable to decipher the runes made visible with the Third Eye Hat. The feeling the experience most left me with overall is confusion and frustration.  It took me about 3 hours in total to complete Hack N Slash, but I gave up and consulted a walkthrough upon being trapped in the algorithm puzzle caves of act IV. Had I not, I would likely never have been able to finish the game. Perhaps everything would have made more sense to a programmer, but it didn't to me and Double Fine made little effort to ameliorate that. Overall, this game is not worth $20. Pick it up for $5 if you can, but you're not missing much if you give it a miss entirely.

As a game, NecroDancer is great. The gameplay is novel and appealing, and the rhythmic combat works because of its premise and visual design. The writing and voices are the opposite: tired and uninspired. The introduction was so clumsy and full of flat monologue attempting to be snarky that I questioned if the gameplay is worth it. At the second story sequence it just continues ruining what otherwise would have been a great roguelike without exposition interrupting play. From what I saw online, the narrative was written by Silverstring Media. I think the game could have been better with the developers' own ideas than diluting it with others' visions.  I can't recommend it at its asking price of $15.

It does provide a good challenge on Normal, and the higher difficulties really are tough, and require a lot of coordination and team work from everyone playing. The Co-op is fun, and the more the better. Where the game falls short is in content. I Have 6.6 hours on record at the time of writing this review because that's about all the time it took to finish the game (the extra time was spent playing some on the 'Hard' difficulty and trying the arena mode or whatever its called). If the game is on sale, I would reccomend it for you and your friends, but my friends and I found ourselves saying 'That's it?' when we beat the game. Beyond the milestones there isn't much replay value here, and the game itself is just really really short. 3 levels, divided into 4 stages, and a boss on the fourth stage. That's it.

Stupid, stupid fun that combines a very particular style of humor with zombies. Plus floor mouths and vomit-throwing floor flesh sacks. And, evil sandwiches. It's best not to think about any of it for too long, and considering how short the game is (I beat it in 2 hrs), you really wont have much of a chance to if you don't mind the occasional design flaw.  The meat of CLS is built around either killing or destroying everything in sight save for your fellow coworkers. Most of the time you'll be 'rescuing' your useless coworkers by walking within a certain range of them, after which your coworkers will follow you around until they die or you make it to the end. It's basicly an isometric Dead Rising without an annoying time limit and better writing...though not great writing, so don't expect too much.  The design flaws appear in several of the levels that try to mix up this formula. One level in particular forces you to try and NOT destroy things, a stupid annoyance that the game itself impedes through the slight inaccuracy of the perspective combined with the controls. However, extra activities outside of the main goal of 'kill everything' don't repeat, so any extra level may easily be fought through and left for dead.  Overall, a good stress reliever at the end of the day...provided you can stand the humor. There is a LOT of it.

So I think I've played this game long enough to give a decent opinion. Maybe.  The multiplayer sucks, it really does. Why?  -The toxic community (People that bicker and whine about each other and are extremelly confrontational or ignorant)   -Cheaters  (Some people call them hackers, when it's really just someone running a plugin in the background and harassing other players)   -Rockstar's tendency to 'patch' anything remotely fun.  (What do I mean by this? The Cargobob's characteristics being changed a while back so it's unable to carry any vehicle larger than a Panto, it's also unable to lift or pick up any vehicle carrying supplies or crates. Minor modifications to the physics that make trivial things like parking cars in the back of trailers impossible, and removing the ability to lower your car with the Bikers DLC. These things were in the game from day 1 on console in 2013, and they decide to patch it because it's either too convienent or unintentionally effective to what Rockstar had planned. People are going to be creative in an open world game, and removing a bit of that ability to 'do anything you can think of' irritates me to no end.)  I apologize for the long rant about the last point, but that's something that I couldn't just shrug off and leave alone.    The GTA:O scene is an absolute nightmare and the only reason why I still play this is because I've pumped SO many hours into it, that I feel like I'd be doing an injustice to my character and friends I play with if I stop. Another reason why is because I actually like the updates that R* implements on a monthly basis. (Apart from Cunning Stunts which was just Hot Wheels: The Game)  The single player is good, it's really good. But if you expect to play GTA:O without anger or frustration everytime you go into a public server, you're in a dream world.   Each freeroam server is heavily populated with cheaters that teleport, drop you off the map, eject you from your car, use explosive rounds, drop money and cause performance problems. If they're not cheating, they're terrorizing the server in a Hydra jet, this is especially prominent when you're doing CEO work and collecting crates.   The system that R* has in place for crates and biker supplies is just stupid, it offers little reward for destroying your supplies, and it just deters the hard working players that actually want to profit from the freemode work. If you're going to make people transporting cargo a target for other players, either give us more incentive (More money, RP, drop the additional ammo because that's stupid. I always have 9999 bullets on all my guns anyway) Or just remove this useless grief-inducing feature entirely.   This goes with bounties also. Bounties placed on you by AI when you steal their car is a useless feature. Rockstar intended you to set bounties on someone when they won't leave you alone, or when someone annoys you, and since it's not very common for someone to do that; they decide to make the pedestrians of Los Santos do it instead to keep it from dying out. I'm not going after someone for 9k. They should raise the limit to the bounty to maybe like 25k or more to make it worth anyone's time.   Until another banwave is implemented; I cannot reccomend this game if you're buying it to play online with other people.

I used to be a fan of Dragonball 15 years ago, but I thought I outgrew anime altogether when I absolutely hated the Shonen Jump stuff that followed it like Bleach and Naruto. Last year's release of Dragonball Z: Battle of Gods piqued my interest in the franchise a little bit and reminded me what popular action anime/manga was like before Naruto and Bleach; classics like Fist of the North Star and Dragonball are, as I have come to realize,  timeless .  It is all here, the original Dragonball Z story with enough alternate twists to keep things fresh and interesting. That is a good thing too, because at the moment the servers are  rarely available . You had best plan to get your entertainment value from local multiplayer or story mode in lieu of working online. I intend to finish story mode and then move on, and even as someone who has not been into DBZ in years and years, I feel I got my money's worth.  As for scoring this game - thumbs up / thumbs down is a terribly flawed way to review games and I wish it would go away along with Steam user tags. Shadow of Mordor was not a terrible game, but it certainly was not a great one, so I gave it a thumbs down (which signifies the end of the world to most people you ask). Objectively, I would give this game about 7.5/10 (1 point taken off for server instability), or what I consider enough to earn a thumbs up.  If you are a contemporary fan of Dragonball Z then picking this game up is a no brainer, if you are a fan who thought they outgrew it over a decade ago you may still find the game enjoyable and worth the asking price (I know I did). Just do not expect much in the way of online functionality; it's broken.

review_text: Now I would like to clarify that this is not TOO terrible but there some problems  - Some of the choices really don't have any affect and lead up to the same conclusions  Sure, it's exciting the first time around and that curisoity on what could be done differently is always exhilerating   But unfortunately on the second go-around, you find there's not much to the alternative  - Characters die left and right.  While I realise that is one of Game of Thrones most distinguishing heart wrenching characterisitcs, it does NOT work for a game with only 6 episodes. I don't want to spoil it too much, but some pretty bad casualties make the player wonder if there's even a point to playing this game--it's one thing for the show & book to kill everyone--as there are still interesting people to follow around. Not so much with the game.  - Ramsay Snow  Introducing a character you know FULL WELL is still alive in the series is kind of unfair. Ironically it represents the pointlessness of your decision. Overall, this was not telltale's best--I think this is a game you should experience, but just not at full retail price.

In a nutshell, Pester puts me in mind of Galaga on crack. It's an old-school shmup that would've fit well in an 80s or 90s arcade--but with a bullet hell twist. Overwhelming waves of projectiles and wonderfully weird enemies fly toward you, and you have to blast everything in sight. The result's brutally difficult and quite a bit of fun. If you're masochistic enough to collect all of the in-game trophies (there are no proper achievements, sadly), you'll have to become an ace at timing your bombs and hyper mode. Unlike in Flump Studios' underrated Super Killer Hornet: Resurrection, there are no auto-bombs or continues here: it's pure finesse all the way.   For me, the game's one real downside is its lack of online leaderboards. Pester's a very well-balanced shooter--projectiles never unfairly trap you, it's always possible to recover after a death and every boss can be beaten without dying. It's perfectly suited to competitive scoring. Still, it's hard to complain given the $1 price point. Not many games on Steam offer this much game for so little money. I'd recommend it to anyone nostalgic for arcade cabinets, or to any fan of oddball shmups that don't just rehash the Touhou paradigm.  Finally, if you've played Pester on Desura, there's good reason to revisit it here. Among other things, the Steam release is faster and its new visuals by Rail Slave Games are absolutely bonkers. The difficulty is higher, but a lot of the annoying stuff from the Desura release (like enemies that kill you from off-screen) has been removed--so it's harder but fairer. I was one of those rare people who enjoyed Desura Pester. This version is massively better.   Full disclosure: I received a key from the developer during beta. On the other hand, I just bought two copies with my own money to give to friends. Is my review biased? You tell me.

I've played Wurm Online on and off since about 2006. My only gripe with the online version back then was the absolutely horrific graphics and the fact that a good 80-90% of the map was populated with people's old ♥♥♥♥, so it was very difficult to carve out a niche for yourself in all that.  What Code Club AB have done by releasing Wurm as a standalone version is nothing short of amazing. This game planted the seed in Notch's mind for Minecraft, and in itself is a much more complex, deep and time consuming game with much the same premise: exist and thrive in an empty world.  But the only real similarity that Minecraft and Wurm share here that I want to bring attention to is the core principle of the entire game: Teamwork and creativity.  You can terraform, build a house, tame animals, fight monsters, craft armour and weapons, and even commit yourself to one of the two religions in the game, but it does take a lot of time and a lot of teamwork. Whether that's something you find appealing or cannot stand, there is grinding in this game. But it's a grind that pays off with legendary tales and a real world that you strived to construct from nothing.  If one were to take even a cursory glance at Wurm Online's colourful history, they would find a completely player driven lore comprised of the rise and fall of empires, epic constructions of vast cities and trade highways, and to me, that highlights what Wurm is: stories. Being a part of something bigger. Helping to construct something lasting that you know took a lot of time and effort between friends.  With the release of the standalone version comes the chance for everyone to be a part of their own player driven history. While the MMO version has only a limited amount of resources and space for only so many cities and empires, this is not so for Unlimited. There's also the potential for mods and an ever increasing skill set for even more stuff to build and do in the game. The possibilities truly are unlimited.   While I hesitate to draw more comparisons to Minecraft, I think for the benefit of the layman it'd be fair to say something like: In Minecraft you can create a house and perhaps even a town in the span of a day; it's really not a big deal and even less of an accomplishment. In Wurm, when you crest a hill and find a city that is truly impressive, you can count on the fact that a lot of people were here and they worked very hard to create it. That itself lends appeal to people who find enjoyment in coming together and working to build something cool. And whether it's a lone shack in the middle of the woods or an epic castle that you've just completed, the satisfaction of striving to build something from nothing is what keeps people coming back.

Dark Souls 3 is a fantastic game. The PvE has well designed areas and boss fights, so if you were dissapointed with Dark Souls 2, you most likely won't be with this title.  The PvP however, is a mixed bag. Invasions are imo NOT as good as they used to be, as you are always at a tremendous disadvantage, both in health (30% less which is fine), but also in numbers. In short, the game prioritizes players who are doing co-op when invading. This, mixed with the fast paced combat system, makes it very hard to win in invasions, as you can't punish the host for healing (they also has more healing items). I do however like the duelling (done with red soap stone sign). The slow paced PvP in DS 2, was imo better, but i do like this as well.  10/10 buy it. Poise was dumb in previous games.

I played the 14-day trial back in December and have been hooked since. This is the first MMO I've played in a long time that has truly presented a world where I can get lost in its vast splendor. The cities, while not super cramped and crowded, feel alive, and the NPC's interact with you in a way that makes your character feel like a part of the world - and not just 'HERO #9825782165.22'.  The story and gameplay start off fairly simple but build up at a pleasant pace, as more content opened up I found myself being genuinely curious to explore it. And before I knew it, I had wandered so far away from the main quest line that I had completely out-leveled it lol. So I took it as an opportunity to begin leveling an alternate class.  The ability to try/level every class on a single character is a godsend. It helps prevent your gameplay from feeling stale since you can change on the fly, and leveling alternate classes is even incentivized because of the way skills, specializations/jobs, and experience work. You could level up multiple classes in order to unlock skills that are beneficial to round out your main job, or you could just be interested in trying new playstyles. And experience gained is increased based on the ratio between the level of your currently selected class, and the level of your highest class/job.  The story itself feels a little archetypical in the base game (A Realm Reborn). It's entertaining and well-executed to be sure, but I was actually pleasantly surprised by the patched/added content leading up to Heavensward. I won't spoil anything, but I definitely wasn't expecting some of it. At the time of writing I've been too busy leveling up a Dark Knight, so I haven't done any of the Heavensward story content lol.  Overall, FF14 doesn't necessarily do anything ground-breaking for an MMO. The combat is still based on tab-targeting, although it's a little more active than older tab-targeted MMO's. The only really 'new' thing I can think of that it does is the fluidity of changing classes. But FF14 basically takes all the best points of MMO's and combines them into a game with a world and setting that feels both comfortable and compelling. I would highly recommend it to anyone.

The Purring Quest is a short, but sweet, platforming adventure game that really touched my heart. The premis of the game revolves around a kitty, Kimchi, who is in search of her human who has gone astray. During her adventure Kimchi runs into all sorts of obstacles and baddies while searching for lost kitties, collecting fishbones as well as other goodies. While the game is short -- about two hours to complete for myself -- it's definitely something I would replay just for the story itself. The only annoyance I had experienced with The Purring Quest was the atrocious control that made me feel like Kimchi was on roller skates, but I stuck it out and I'm glad I did. While playing this game I couldn't help but think about all the kitties I've had as a part of my life, how they've touched my heart and all the ones that are still out there who need homes that I wish I had room for. I'm grateful that the developer donates a portion of the game's proceeds to charity to help these innocent souls. <3

my absolute favourite game ever.  - mechanics aren't even that great. stealthing around humans is pretty predictable and easy. but i like the details (glas on the floor gives you away).  + best thing is the story tho. you just feel it. your character is mute, and you only get a few informations at a time, so it is hard to follow, until you realise what is happening, what you're doing and whats happening. you really feel as a small player in a larger frame of things. I love if the story is not 'oh enemies coming there, what shall we do'. this one is more like, you float around with your own aims, but others don't care much for you because they have their own problems. you float in this immersive world. love it.  + gameplay is diverse. I love stealthing around humans, but monster levels in between can be really scary aswell. playing in metro is totally different from playing on the surface. you have levels on foot, on vehicles, alone or with an ai comrade.  + weapons: one of the best things ever. they feel absolutely aweful. you cannot just spray like cod, you won't hit anyhing. and it's not like the monsters or humans are unrealisticly strong, one revolver bullet usually does the trick if it hits. but the weapons are creative. besides normal weapons like ak there are improvised ones, like the air pressured tihar or helsing which you have to pressure up from time to time, or electrical weapons. it really gives you the feel, that the inhabitants build them from scrap, and they are not good but do the trick. a super feeling. and still diversity is there, attachments exist aswell.   + immersion. i pointed some ♥♥♥♥ out already, here some more points. Money system: Doesn't exist. Money are bullets, pre-war military grade bullets to be precise. you can trade them for worse metro-built bullets, but in the worst case you can also use them as ammo. and there are not plenty of them around, leaving you to conserve as much as possible. The peaceful stages are beautifully designed, with a lot of people doing the stuff you would expect from them. people have these ♥♥♥♥ guns and use improvised technics to protect themselvs. (cans as alarm system...). you can really feel the desperation of the inhabitants. also your gadgets scream 'desperation', even your torch needs to be recharged alot to help you out.  - a bit an older game, considering movements of ai etc. but nothing bad that would stand out.   metro is like dead space. if you liked metro last light, you will not be able to appreciate this one (which is far superior to ll imho). but like dead space, the sequels move in the wrong direction, from athmospheric story teller to COD-Based action shooter with a generic story, and loss of athmosphere in favour of high end gadgets. (LL weapons seem to be powerful beasts, 2033 weaons seem to be scrap and ♥♥♥♥) Yep, like dead space, the sequels were designed for a more mainstream buyer base, which is a pity if you ask me.  I give this a perfect 10/10. From today's point of view you could subtract 0.5 for not smart human ai, but consider, this is a few years older. I hate to be 'that guy,' but anyone who says the plot was any good is either a liar or an idiot. The 'story's' main premise is parallel realities, which would be fine if it was actually done well. The only explanation the player is offered is in a couple of the Voxophones (this game's version of the Audio Diaries) and in one of the video recordings. And the explanation amounts to 'Quantum Mechanics!' Even one of the head developers of the game flat-out admitted he didn't actually understand quantum mechanics and he just included it because it 'blew his mind.'  Anyway, on to the part that matters- the gameplay. I found it to be repetitive at best, and mind-numbing at worst. The fact of the matter is, the game is way too easy and the battles are all basically the same. Compared to 1 & 2 where Splicers could ambush you at any given moment, the enemy encounters were all predictable and easy to counter.  As a longtime fan of Bioshock (I've played 1 at least 20 times and played 2 around 8 times, on my ninth playthrough now) I am willing to go so far as to say this isn't a 'true' Bioshock game. A lot of what made the first 2 games so special for me was simply not present in this installment.   -A Utopia turned decrepit from someone's ideals gone awry? Nope. Columbia was basically 'white people and America' and that's pretty much it. None of what happened in Columbia's history matters, as opposed to Bioshock 1 where a lot of the story takes place in the past.   -Big Daddies? Nope. Nothing even close. The game tricks you into thinking you'll fight a big mechanical... thing... by showing Songbird off early on. But no, sorry, you never even fight him once, let alone kill him and grab the spoils. Simply put, there was no 'oh god I have to prepare for this encounter' enemy.   -Vigors (Plasmids, basically) being integral to the plot? No. Whereas in the first 2 games it was EXPLAINED (by the way, no explanation for Vigors either) that to use Plasmids you had to twist your genetic code, and eventually succumb to addiction to a substance called ADAM, which had the side effects of mental and physical corrosion. Plasmids started a war in the first game and continued it in the second. Vigors are just kinda there. You drink them and you have magic powers.   There are several more issues I have with this game but honestly to sum it all up: This didn't feel like the Bioshock series I've played over and over again. This felt like a generic FPS with very little thought or love put into it. It brought nothing new to the table- instead it just took a lot of the cool stuff already ON the table, away. A 6/10 is generous.

I bloody loved Jet Set Radio, back in the days of the Dreamcast. Stylistically, it is still a striking product, with an art style distinct enough that it transcends the need to keep up with technological improvements in gaming. This means that graphically, it is still likely to impress, even though the cel-shading technique it pioneered is now ubiquitous and about as surprising to see in a game as a henchman crouched next to an explosive barrel.  The gameplay, however, just doesn't stand up to scrutiny anymore, mainly lending to a control scheme so incredibly archaic and clunky it makes my grandma look like a futuristic ninja. I would liken the controls in this game to trying to pull a pallet of bricks around on sheet ice with a bungee cord. The characters seem to want to deliberately cause rage-induced aneurisms in its players, and repeatedly I swore at my screen as the once-charming little punk skaters fell from bulldings as the ADHD camera spun in circles, desperately indecisive as to what to lock onto. I don't remember it being like this when it came out, but then again we were more patient gamers back then.  And storywise, it's not anything remarkable, but the charm exuding from every pore in this game is enough to make you want to see it through. A typically Japanese touch sees a racially stereotypical dreadlocked black DJ introducing each story beat, but more importantly the incredible soundtrack. The tunes in this game are probably now its main selling point, and well worth the admission price alone if you're into your chunky beats and late 90's sampling. It certainly takes the edge off when you're trying to wrestle the player characters into doing even one thing you've asked them to.  I feel like I've been very negative about this game, but I still love it. The rose tint of nostalgia will certainly help if you've played previously, but I think theres still enough going for this game that it's worth a go for new players looking to take down the Japanese government with inline skates and spray cans.

After playing the previous Assassin's Creed games, Assassin's Creed III is a disappointment for me. That being said, my feelings are in no way to deter anyone from playing the game. There a few things that make this game stellar and more things that make it rather lackluster:  Pros: -Amazing graphics, with beautiful scenery and (new!) changing weather -Likable characters, although lacking in development -Even more possibilities in battle and free roaming -Tons of stuff to do -Historically accurate (most of the time)  Cons: -Glitches everywhere. Aside from falling through the floor a couple times, they didn't really ruin the game, but invisible Benjamin Franklin was rather disconcerting. -Plot inconsistencies and strange pacing. I won't spoil anything, but if you actually look at some of the situations logically, make little to no sense. Sometimes the characters do things completely contradictory to their personalities. As a person who essentially played this game for the plot, it was rather disappointing. -Tutorial spends too much time on plot and not enough on actually explaining ♥♥♥♥. Stores are a little bit difficult to find in the beginning and the game spends little time describing what can be upgraded, discovered, etc. I didn't even recruit any Assassins because it was too much trouble. And they just briefly describe how to manage the homestead and the like. -Several nearly impossible sync missions. While the previous games have had difficult quests to get 100% sync, some of them in this game are near impossible due to glitches or bad programming. One asked for an air assassination in a place where there was nowhere to air assassinate from. The quests themselves sometimes had rather disappointing glitches or problems that were overlooked. There was a one quest with a follower that was strangely prone to being shoved into the water by civilians.   Toss-up: -Battle mechanics. They've become harder, which is not a bad thing in and of itself. The problem lies in that sometimes the mechanics are glitchy and lead to some strange situations like enemies clipping through walls. In one instance, I threw a redcoat down the well, but he didn't die and so he kept trying to kill me from below. Harmless, but rather annoying. -Good guys vs. Bad guys. Ubisoft said they didn't want to villainize one side over the other. Instead, AC3 villainizes several historical figures, like good ol' George Washington. Nothing wrong with that, so long as it's historically accurate. The problem is that although the head of the Patriots may not be a 'good guy', the Patriots are clearly favored. Although it's supposed to be Assassins versus Templars, it ends up looking more like America versus Great Britain. The game developers claim this was not the case, but after playing through the game and seeing how almost all of the targets were British or Loyalists, it's kinda hard to say the game doesn't favor the victors. Some people might not find issue with it, but I found it rather annoying since it seemed like the game developer did a real half-♥♥♥♥♥ job on trying to emphasize this point.  TL;DR Play this game if you want a conclusion to the first four games. Unfortunately, it has quite a few problems and glitches that take away from the overall enjoyment of the game. If you can overlook the aforementioned cons, then you ought to have a great time. Expect to invest a lot of time to enjoy this thoroughly.

The game mechanics are simple and easy to learn. The game itself is hard. I keep watching as my crew dies or my ship blows up AGAIN. Yet I never feel like the game is killing me in cheap ways. It makes me wonder what I need to do differently next time. I fail to beat the game and immediatly restart. The game is frustrating in a way that doesn't want to make me quit, but instead try harder or change my strategy. I played for about three hours without noticing the time that had passed. I must have hit restart thirty times without even noticing.   I unlocked a new ship and decided to try it out and felt like I was back to square one. It played completely differently from the first but it created a new challenge. This game is highly addicting, a lot of fun and a great challenge.  I think I'll cry when I finally beat the game.

I used to think this was just Rogue in Space, but this game has defined a whole new genre. I remember the first several times I played it, I thought it was impossible to beat (even on easy!). Now I realize that there is something special about the randomness of the universe and the way previous plays teach you how to plan for some of the unexpected.  I will never forget the first time I saw a new option for a familiar scenario. Giant Spiders.... I just happened to have a Bio Beam installed, and there was suddenly a third option for that encounter! After that I had a whole new appreciation for the depth of the game.   Even when I take a break from playing, I always come back. There are still ships to unlock!

Guacamelee is a 2-D platforming action game set in a Mexican themed world, with a smaller emphasis on story than pure action (although there's still enough there to make you care). It follows the adventures of Juan, an everyday farmer who is resurrected after being killed by the evil Carlos Calacka, as he punches, jumps, and kicks his way to freeing the daughter of El Presidente.  The fighting in this game is superb - there is a great mix of normal attacks and more powerful special attacks that are unlocked throughout the game, and moment to moment action feels great. Trying to chain up a huge combo by pummelling enemies into the air, picking them up and throwing them into other enemies so they all land in a huge heap is fast, fluid, and feels amazing. There is a large variety in the enemies you face - starting off with standard brute enemies, and eventually leading up to giants with hammers, spikey armadillos with deadly, fast attacks, four armed sword wielding skeletons, and flying lightning priests. The game slowly introduces these enemies to match your rising skill level, and starts mix and matching them cleverly which leads to some genuinely difficult fights. That being said, on Normal mode (Hard mode is unlocked only after beating the game, and is much harder – enemies have more health, and you have less) the game is not very difficult, due to a forgiving checkpoint system and it will only take about 10 hours to finish the story. However, there are many collectibles hidden around the maps that can keep you occupied as you try for 100% - again, this will only take an additional 4 hours or so. There are also additional challenges (the El Infierno levels) that test your fighting, platforming, and thinking skills – these are easily the most difficult part of the game, and getting a gold medal in all challenges is a worthy achievement. The art style in Guacamelee is one of the most striking things about the game – the graphics are crisp and look beautiful, all presented in a lovely and vibrant, if clichéd, Mexican style. The soundtrack is much the same – beautiful, and easy to listen to for hours on end. These two combine to form a convincing and engaging that makes the game fun to play for hours on end. Summary: - Beautiful aesthetics, both graphically and musically - Fast, fluid and fun combat - Great boss battles that test your fighting skills - Responsive and challenging platforming. A great mix of platforming and action, combined with a beautiful world make this a must buy, whether you like platforming and/or fighting games. This game is worth full price, and definitely grab it if it is on a sale.

This shooter tries to be as realistic as possible, and they do a pretty damn good job. A couple of bullets, and you are dead. This means people aren't charging around lobbing grenades blindly and shooting from the hip like Rambo. You MUST move carefully, slowly, and methodically as you check every corner.   Graphics: They are pretty decent, but not the best to be honest. But this does little to take away from the gameplay. Were they lack in texture details, they make up for it in ultra realistic reload animations, etc.  Sound: In FPS games, this is very important to me. This is the FIRST game I have ever played were the gunplay sounded genuine. I am a United States Marine combat veteran, and I vouch for the auhenticity of the sound in this game. Because the sound is so realistic, it makes you move more careful. Bullets cracking over your head tend to have that effect. Hearing that harrowing sound of an AK has that effect.   I hope this game gets some nice texture update patches and a bigger following. The game is cheap, and I play it more than I do BF4 or anything else. I love it, and I suck at it terribly. I get murdered on a daily basis, but it is good fun. I hope it catches on!

★½ out of four.  Mad Max the video game really does feel thematically similar to the movies so far.  It has an open world map cluttered with a handful of similar activities to go complete and several hubs to upgrade. Your stomach for this type of design will mostly decide your enjoyment; Tedium abounds in the objectives whether they be overworld or checking boring 'camp' compounds for 'scrap' pick-ups. Instead of running across dunes or flats, you're made to keep your car on thin roadways as leaving them drops your car's speed too much. Playing on a controller, the sensitivity of the vehicle was always too great, despite efforts in the Settings menu. Your car does noticably handle better as you upgrade it, but the sensitivity issue on my controller was never improved. Driving was still ok, but improvements here would have paid off well.  Stand outs of the game include the mentioned themeing, which particularly shines in your constant companion Chumbucket, who is excitable in his religiousity for all things automobile. Sound design is also effective in the engines, explosions, and combat. Music is functionally absent, though... there were combat themes I recall, but outside of this catching wisps of music was very rare. The hand-to-hand combat systems expand elegantly as you earn more skills.  The ending is perhaps a bit disappointing, but fits fine. I was hitting the various objectives well-enough in the game, but after completing the ending, my motivation to continue stopped. Still, I had completed many things, leaving only mainly the races you do in specified vehicles. These races could've been mildly interesting, but again the driving/sensitivity let it down. The volume levels couldn't be made as quiet as I would like, so this hindered enjoyment of playing the tedium while listening to podcasts. The game is well-optimized.

Don't bother unless you have a proper steering wheel. Unplayable with a controller. Also has a weird Glitch where the game changes your DESKTOP BACKROUND to something random.. Easily fixed by saving the theme but WTF?

Be advised o ye potential newcomers, there are four issues with this game as of October 2014. First, be advised that there is only one 'book' available in chapters, i.e. one episode, so you're only getting roughly 4 hours of gameplay out of this. Second, you're going to want to have played both The Longest Journey and Dreamfall completely before picking this up or you're going to feel like this game is an acid trip. (Frankly if you haven't, they're incredible games worth the money.) Third is that the graphics are a little unoptimized so it will make even some rigs capable of running Glorious Star Citizen cry in pain. Unfortunately, this is not helped by the fourth concern, that the character models animations are a little sub par and that does cheapen an otherwise brilliant and immersive experience.  That said, there are a ton of positives. The setting is fantastically designed and believable. The writing, dialogue and choice system are on par with anything put out by Telltale. Zoe's internal monologues are plastered all over the game and are reflective of the choices she (ahem, YOU) will make, Effects on the storyline are VERY noticable and yet seamlessly woven into the game. It appears that quite a lot of effort was put into making the consequences of actions matter, at least in the short term with Zoe's attitude.  TL;DR - I like where Tørnquist and RTG is going with this. This is what TLJ/Dreamfall fans -- as well as fans of the adventure games genre -- have been waiting for. I recommend a buy after more chapters have been put out there. (And FFS, if you haven't played TLJ and Dreamfall, drop everything and do that right now.)

If you liked Portal, definitely pick this up. You're manipulating (parts of) your environment rather than creating teleporters, but either way it's a really fun first-person physics puzzle game. My only complaint is that it's kinda short...hopefully one day we'll see a Q.U.B.E. 2.  This is the 'Director's Cut' version of the game, which is almost exactly the same as the original, with the following differences:  1) In the original, you're just by yourself with no idea of what's going on, where you are, why you're there, etc. The DC version has some 'narration' that provides something of a plot. Whether this is a good thing is kinda a matter of personal preference; the game doesn't really  need  a plot, and some people like the mystery. Personally, I felt that it added an extra dimension to things, but I see where the other side is coming from; just look at the movie Blade Runner for an example of how adding a narration track can ruin the atmosphere.  2) The hardest puzzle in the original game (Section 6 Puzzle 4, which corresponds to the original's 'Tough Going, Part 2' achievement) is gone. Now, I can already hear the Super Meat Boy/Dark Souls fans who haven't played through Q.U.B.E. lamenting the 'dumbing down', but trust me...this is a case for rejoicing and fanfare. The puzzle was really hard to begin with, which is fine; however, the game physics weren't (and still aren't, since that part of the engine hasn't been changed for the DC version) up to snuff for producing the required precision, so 75% of the time even when you did the puzzle correctly it still wouldn't work. For the curious, the DC version's 'Tough Going' achievement is the same as the original game's 'Tough Going, Part 1'.  3) A few extra puzzles (fun ones, but IMO no new noteworthy ones) and some 'filler' areas were added to allow the narration to occur between puzzles without making the player stop.

Dominions 3: The Awakening (and I imagine all other Dominion games as well) promises much but - aside tons of statistics and sprites - delivers a little.   You choose a god, well a sprite for your god and go through pages and pages of descriptions and statistics while doing it. It's fun if you're into details like I am but the ingame version of my God wasn't nearly as exciting.   You start from the rather ugly map that consists most of the gameplay. There's your starting grid where you click recruit troops to... recruit troops. Then you assign these said troops under commanders which are like better troops than 'just troops'. You can choose the actions they take in battle... wait, oh, that's it! The problem is that you don't play any part in the combat itself. You move to a grid containing enemies and the combat auto-resolves to show you the outcome as a text-file or you can choose to watch the sprites go at it. It's fun for a couple times but you just start to slowly wish you could do more. But no, that's it.   Next turn begins and you recruit more troops and set the ones you recruited last turn to move and capture more grids until you find your opponents. There's endless amounts of content here if you enjoy reading a novel of statistics but besides that there's no much to do. You might argue that there's a deeper level to the games strategy and there might be, I would've died to boredom if I tried to find out.   I'm deeply dissapointed that there's also a fourth instalment of the series and by the looks of it's store page nothing has changed. It's just an add-on for Dominions 3 with now even more statistics to play around! Yay.

This game is absurdly overpriced. Sean Murray CLEARLY lied about numerous features that were not included in the game. All you do in this game is explore and obtain resources. That's literally it. That would be enough, maybe, if all planets didn't play out exactly the same way. Hot/Cold/Toxic/Radiated, they all are the same. Those factors don't change anything. The life on it will vary, but even creatures are the same besides looks. Few will 'attack' you but they're rare and are easily killed/avoided. Planets have cosmetic differences but all have the same general resources available aside from some that have special things like 'poison sacs' that can be harvested and sold. Some rare variety would have been fantastic, something like really rarely finding a small civ on a planet in the stone age or something. But no, there's nothing besides numerous outposts with on alien sitting in a chair not moving.  Ship customizing hardly exists, they all operate the same way in combat. There isn't 'classes' as we were told.  There are no factions. The 'intelligent' life in the game you come across has literally have no effect on the game at all. They occassionally give you a blueprint for a tech you probably don't need or want if you answer their vague ramblings with the correct response. It would have been awesome to see actual life in giant space stations other than one alien sitting in a chair.   The galaxy doesn't feel alive. Planets don't rotate around their stars as we were told.  I won't spoil the ending, but I will say I have no idea at all how they thought that it would be satisfying or players would enjoy it. None at all. It's as if they didn't even test it on people outside of their 15 strong development team. There's no way anybody, even the biggest supporters, like the ending. It's absurd.  This game could have been great. Most people probably would have been okay with it if it had the price of a normal indie game of $20-30. $60 is entirely too much and I can't recommend it at all.

Hard to say what hasn't been said in other reviews, but I guess I'll start with the original doujin market game, which had incredibly high production values, a good soundtrack and polished gameplay. It's hard to tell it's a doujin game as opposed to a commercial one The general gameplay may remind you of a mix of things (Dondonpachi, Raystorm), but the main thing that makes this stand out? The break mode system, which not only allows the bulk of the scoring but also provinging a boost to your firepower. Double Break mode is something that has to be seen to understand what a boost to score and firepower that is.  This is a port of the Arcade version which was previously japanese only, and with it come some changes, mainly the limiter shop being removed but in return you get 3 ships to start (and one to unlock, though easier than the original), all levels accessible in training and free play in most modes (gotta 1cc if you want to see the True Final boss). The general changes are double edged, making the game easier in some ways and harder compared to the original game (stage 3 in particular). But for less confident players, there's Novice versions of both original and boost mode (I'll get on to this later)  There's three modes to the game:  Original: The game's normal mode, where you can see the full scoring system in effect.  Unlimited: This is basically to CC what Ultra is to Cave games and Lunatic is to Touhou games: The game unleashing bullet hell to its fullest extent.  Simple (original game only): A toned down game in both difficulty and mechanics which used your break/bomb bar to determine rank/points/attack power.  Boost (World Ignition only): It's Simple Mode's rank dynamics taken up to 11 as when your Break/Bomb bar fills, you will enter break mode that goes on until you lose a life or you bomb and the longer you're in it, the more the game's difficulty ramps up during it.  Time attack: in the spirit of the old Hudson star soldier caravan games, this has you with a limited time (and unlimited lives) to score as much as possible.  And each mode has a small tutorial bit before you start that explains things pretty well.  The sound track is very good with separate stage musics for normal and unlimited (by default but you can change them around in the options) with its bosses each getting their own theme. Not sure if it'd justify spending extra for the soundtrack, though it slightly makes up for the removal of the sound test that was in the original.  And the plot? There is none beyond 'Blow them up'  Update:resolution/screen issues fixed.  Highly recommended for any shump fan.

I loved Fable TLC and have played it at least 4 times over the years. When I heard about Fable Anniversary and saw some of the preview pics I made a point to pre-order. That was a mistake.  The graphics are largely better than Fable TLC, but still underwhelming. On close up the hero's hair looks bad, and Whisper's looks even worse. There's odd lighting around the hair and face of the models. The hero's eyes look dead - totally a 2 dimensional surface. You can see this in TotalBiscut's review at about the 11:25 mark. The eyes in TLC by comparison look much better (which you can also see in his video). There is a bit of a blur over everything and some of the ambient occlusion looks pretty bad. There's already user-made .ini tweaks on the Steam forums to deal with some of these issues and using them does provide some improvement.  The real problem is the horrible controls. There is no mouse control for the game menus and the keyboard controls are nonsensical. They literally mapped the A, B, and Y keys of the Xbox 360 controller to the A, B, and Y keys of the keyboard to manipulate many of the menu functions in the game. I've never seen the B key used to manipulate menus in a game before. It's not a great innovation.  The inventory screen is completely changed from TLC's for the worse. To do anything inventory related in this version you start with the Insert and Home keys to navigate tabs, then use the Delete and End keys to navigate sub-tabs, then switch to the arrow keys to navigate lists and sub-lists, then the A key wear, equip, or bind something, then B to exit out. Sometimes Esc will get you back directly to the game, sometimes not. It's really clunky. The layout itself has a ton of wasted screen space on colors, tabs, and subtabs with only a small amount of screen real estate devoted to actually display the items you are carrying.  There is no mouse sensitivity adjustment. When I first started playing in Oakvale just turning the camera was strikingly jarring. I don't know why, I just finished Skyrim a few weeks ago and had no issue with swinging the camera around in that game, but here in Fable Anniversary I found it terribly jarring visually. I thought maybe turning down the mouse sensitivity would help...but there's no way to do that. Maybe this is just a personal thing that I'll get used to.  The oddness of combining orb gathering and spell use on the left shift still persists and is just as troublesome as it was in the original. Blocking and dodging are on the middle mouse button. Maybe that was fine 9 years ago when I played with a gaming mouse that actually had a middle mouse button, but today I have a scroll wheel there and that scroll wheel button is a terrible way to control such critical functions like block and dodge. It's not as sensitive and fairly uncomfortable.  There is some key remapping available once you have loaded a game, but critical keys (like the A and B use to navigate the menus, attack, block, dodge, etc.) cannot be remapped. I wish I could remap dodge and block to some of the other mouse buttons on my Death Adder 2013, but that's not possible.   Exiting game is probably the worst I've seen in any PC game in years. You hit Esc, then A to select save game, arrow keys to choose a save slot, A to save, B to go back to the menu, hit the arrow down key 4 or 5 times to select Quit, A to quit, wait for a loading screen to the main menu, hit the arrow down key 7 or 8 times to select exit to Windows, hit A to exit. Every.Single.Time.You.Quit.The.Game. In 2014 it should be substantially easier to save and quit a computer game.  Some of these control issues can be mitigated by using an Xbox 360 controller. I happen to have one and tried it but I thought the quick menu to access the many expressions and inventory items to be atrocious. There is no key remapping for either of the 2 controller layout options.  To sum up: I'm disappointed. I expected better graphics. For regular (non menu) gameplay the keyboard/mouse controls aren't too bad or too changed from the original but they certainly weren't updated or improved. The inventory menu layout is overly complicated and the menu controls are abysmal. It all works, mind you. It's all functional. But it's not very well done and absolutely mars a really great game.  As for stability, I've only played a little over 2 hours and haven't had any stuttering or other visual issues being reported on Steam forums. No crashes either. I have an i7-3770, 8 GB RAM, and an Nvidia GTX 770.  I wish I had waited for this come on sale on Thanksgiving or maybe Christmas. I still love Fable and still think it's a great game, but Fable Anniversary did not live up to expectations. There's a really great game underneath the clunky interface and inventory menu system. It's a shame that Fable Anniversary didn't really polish up Fable TLC and really make the game shine.

Update: Many of the issues below have been fixed, and the game is now quite enjoyable. I would still say it errs on the side of stinginess for many loot drops, but this is now a matter of taste rather than a frustrating result of gameplay.  ____________________  There's a tragic design flaw that turns this amazing game with great personality into a monotonous chore:  It's a game with super fun guns that discourages you from using those fun guns.  The game needs to lean into its strengths. It's fast paced and it's got cool weapons. Unfortunately it's not doing that. it's discouraging them with spongey enemies and stingy keys/ammo. There are so many small changes that could fix it (more ammo, more keys, less trash mob health, 'no hit' bonus activates if you only lose armor) but attrition is getting to me and it feels like a slog.  I'm not interested in excuses of 'Git good'. A difficult game can be fun, even amazing. The problems with this game aren't due to difficulty, at least not directly. I want to love this game but it's just not happening.  Would not recommend in its current state unless you're desperate for a new bullet hell game.
