This is a beautifully animated, lovingly crafted game. None of these negative reviews can deny this. I loved every second of this game, as few as there were, and I can't say I wouldn't buy it again knowing what I know about the game.  The criticisms must be met with some agreement; the wizard loses out on some of his original feel without being able to pile up an absurd number of objects in the environment, of course, and the thief does feel a bit more simple as far as aiming goes, but don't let that fool you into thinking this game is a cop out from the developers.  The true fault of the game is that Frozenbyte, a young, ambitious studio, bit off more than they could chew with the third dimension. Once again, the studio did amazingly well in the visual department, and have a good framework for an amazing game...  But they ran out of money, so the game's short. It took me six and a half hours to beat this game with some extra work put in for some collectibles. Take that how you will: they cut off the story, so it doesn't seem hasty or rushed; they just have about 10-12 more levels to make.  I highly recommend that Trine lovers do buy this game, albeit wait for a sale maybe. Frozenbyte hasn't made a cash grab, and it hasn't abandoned their players; don't abandon them. Please. Not yet.

Been a big fan of the Total war series since the year 2000. I saw my cousin playing this game when I was only 12 years old and I was instantly hooked. When CA announced that they would make a fantasy themed Total war I had my doubts. I was not very familiar with the Warhammer universe, and with Total war I used to be a very conservative type of player. It had to be a historical era of mankind, otherwise it would suck big time. Boy was I wrong.... This, in my opinion, is a great Total war. I am not saying it is flawless or perfect, absolutely not, but it is a great Total war game.  I miss the proper naval battles that Napoleon and Rome 2 had, not having my ballista boats to be a bully on the ocean is a shame. The lack in 'premade' formations for your army is a small point aswell. did not use the option that often but it was nice to have just in case. But in all honesty I can't think of many more negatives right now with this new Total war.   The game so far has been a great experience, the units are great, well balanced and look awesome. There are dragons, giants, rocket shooting dwarves and a bunch more. Especially the hero's are cool, all come with their own specialties and a nice talent tree. The battles are intense and impressive, if you use a nice sound system with a good surround and a powerful subwoofer you will feel like a true commander in the middle of a battle. And last but not least, the global map looks great, its detailed and always changing. Vampire lords try to spread corruption and settlements have racial based looks, I like it.  My rig is a fairly decent i5-4440, gtx 960, 8GB of RAM and I installed the game on a samsung evo ssd. It run very well, never had a gamecrash and smooth framerate with high graphics on. I can even alt-tab during the game without any issues, might sound like a weird comment but many games tend to freak out if you put them in the background for a moment.  This game gets my insignificant seal of approval, i spent 50 euro's on it at a local retailer and I have been very satisfied with it. If you like strategy games and do not specifically hate fantasy genres and your pc is not a lemming trying to do calculations go for it.

Example: You can whistle to get guards to leave their posts, but they can only hear you, if you whistle in the dedicated whistling location. Whistle anywhere else and the guards can't be bothered. Because. Come to think of it: This is not a puzzle game either. This is a guess-the-developer's-thoughts kind of game. There's no room for creativity and your problem solving skills won't be challenged either.

I have played Spec Ops: The Line for ~20 hours, and I have gotten all 50 achievements. Here is my list of pros and cons regarding the game.   Pros:  Fairly easy achievements (with dedication)  Good colors  Skippable cutscenes   Cons:  Horrific checkpoint system that makes the game an incredibly boring grind on harder difficulties  Terrible and incompetent AI  Bad sprinting system  Plot that makes you think you have some control, but all paths are scripted to lead to the same stupid edgy ending  Ammo capped too low. When on harder difficulties it takes a whole clip for a single enemy, might as well give us a higher ammo maximum...   Overall  This game was mediocre. 6/10 for achievement difficulty because of FUBAR one shot deaths, decent scenery at some points, but overused purple colors and sun flares. If you like scripted third person shooters (with chunky cover mechanics) that horribly attempt to convey a psychological breakdown in the protagonist, this game is perfect for you. If you like a game where the choices you make actually change the outcome, like in the Walking Dead, and there are checkpoints every couple minutes so that you don't have to grind through the same ♥♥♥♥♥♥ cover shooting battles over and over again, then I recommend getting something else.

7/10 If your a fan of WH40k and you can get this cheap (I was able to get it around $8), then there's no reason not to get it.  The main draw of this game is to see the Warhammer 40000 universe in the flesh. The Dawn of War series was an excellent way to portray the universe supplying plenty of flavor and the kind of combat one would expect. However, the world still lacked the scale and grandeur one expects from a culture engrossed with the gothic style of art and a universe where there is only war.   This is not a problem with Space Marine as straight from the get go your thrown into a world literally at war. The world you fight on is a forge world under siege by the green menace, the iconic orks. A world where every resource, be it natural or otherwise, is dedicated to the manufacture of weapons and materials to feed the war engine of the Imperium in its everlasting war against the enemies of mankind.   You play as a Space Marine, a genetically enhanced post human angel of death. Arguably the Imperiums greatest weapons of war. So mighty, often a single squad is enough to pacify an entire world. So rare, it is said that there is but one space marine for every world in the imperium.  You know what the biggest issue I have with this game? It's that barely any of this is explained to the player. You would think that for everything a player sees and experiences he would get a little information to provide context and information. Does a stranger to WH40k know what is a forge world, a space marine, the imperial guard, chaos, the warp, psykers, titans, bolters, the Emperor himself etc, etc. I'm particular annoyed that not once in the entire game did I see someone say 'The Emperor protects'. An entire universe of lore and barely any of it is expressed.   What little there is however, is really impressive and gratifying to see. On to the details of the game.  Duration Very short game. Approximately 6 hours on the hardest difficulty depending on how adventurous your feeling during the fights. Though for the type of game this is, adequate enough. Because of it's length the repetition of gameplay doesn't hit you nearly as much as it could've. Haven't tried the multiplayer as I wasn't expecting much from it.  Graphics Impressive not because it looks good, even though it does, but because it does what virtually no other warhammer game has for me. It brings the WH40k universe to life. The armor the space marines wear still has that blocky feel one would expect, but the armor has a sense of antiquity, rigidity, and beauty to it that is always hinted at in the books and artwork. The manufactorum fills the screen with it's grandeur despite it's distance to the player. The titan towers the way a machine god on the battlefield should. Everywhere you go everything is made in such a large scale that befits the style. That being said, the world itself is very uninteractable with the player being able to 'touch' very little.  Gameplay The combat is solid enough. It fits the tone being brutal and visceral in ways that it should be. It gets repetitive pretty quickly in that there are only 1-2 kill animations per melee weapon, but it scales things in such a way that your still getting something slightly new throughout the game mostly in the way of new weapons.  Sound The music is somewhat bland despite it fitting the game, there just wasn't anything particularly 'epic' about it. Everything sounds the way one would expect it to from the impacts of the melee hits to the discharge of the range weaponry.  7/10 Again, if your a fan of the WH40k universe there's a lot of little things that will be fun to see. Get it if its cheap as it's a treat for any fan.   For the strangers you'll be missing a great deal of the lore in this game with no context to help you understand this grim universe. In all likelihood it'll just be another small action game to you which you'll forget about.

From the moment I booted it up, I knew Bastion would be one of the most amazing games I will ever play. Here are some of the things I love about the game; all are solid reasons to pick it up.  1) The art - The brightly colored, oil paint style art mixed with the minimalist level design of only showing you areas you can explore make you want to explore and live in the world. The feeling of the art reminds me a bit of Braid, in that on the surface it seems childish and harmless, but also strongly hints at the darkness and uncertainty in the plot by painting in constantly shifting colors that vary in brightness and hue.  2) The gameplay - Bastion features an arcade style 'weapon 1 / weapon 2 / special' button masher combat system which is both simple enough to completely understand by the end of the very well designed tutorial level and contains enough innovation to remain interesting through the end of the game. The variety of very distinct weapons (as opposed to gun 1, gun 2, etc that many FPS employ today) allows you to choose your own play style. The upgrade system for weapons encourages striving to find the material to upgrade your favorite, but doesn't require crazy amounts of grinding to do so. Finally, the base challenge level of the game is low enough that you'll be able to beat it, but early on you have the option to raise the difficulty in exchange for XP, (psuedo) money, and achievements in a variety of different ways.  3) The plot - Bastion has an unbelievable plot. A real plot, to the level of a fantasy novel. Early on, you are tasked with rebuilding the bastion, a safe haven in case of disaster that has fallen into disrepair in the wake of 'the calamity'. It seems simple at first, but wrinkles appear - what was 'the calamity'? Who caused it? Who are the Ura? What are the backstories of the characters? And why repair the bastion, what will that do? I won't spoil any more, but suffice to say at the very end of the game you are offered two choices, and while I've played other games with choice systems, I've never thought this hard about which choice to make. The fate of the world that you very much feel a part of rests on your shoulders.  4 and my personal favorite) The sound - The entirety of Bastion is narrated by an old man who you meet very early in the game. He describes your character in the third person, as though all of your exploits are an old tale. And when I say all your exploits, I mean it - he narrates every blow you strike and boss you defeat in a fashion that makes you feel like you're writing history throughout your adventure. Interspersed in his narration are bits and pieces about the world that tie the plot together. The music, especially the bastion theme, perfectly complement the art style and the gameplay. The bastion theme starts out bare and thin, but as you rebuild it the music grows in strength and instrumentation in a rustic style, with a message of 'it's going to be rough but we're getting through this'. There are a few songs with words in the game that are used to great affect to drive a plot event home.  Overall, Bastion is unbelievable. It is a game that seeks to be more than just a game. It is gripping, dark, fast-paced, thought-provoking, and challenging in more ways than one. It is a masterpiece.

Stunning visual style, creative world, gripping story, enjoyable grind. The narration is top notch, nudging you towards the goal while revealing the background and explaining how the game world works - very similar to Transistor, Supergiant games' other project. The music is also of similar quality, although much less noticeable in Bastion except for certain areas. The battle system itself is pretty straightforward, nothing too complex, while it feels like there is a lot of innovation in the customization, especially the Idol system for providing more difficult challenges. The ending to the story is very well done, in an otherwise linear game, you are given the hard choices at the end. I'll be honest, I had difficulty choosing the 'right thing', which only goes to show how the game actually involve the player somewhat emotionally with the story.  Overall, it is undeniable that Bastion and Transistor are from the same developer, and that is a really good thing in my opinion. This is a very good game and I can recommend it wholeheartedly.

Crusader Kings 2 is an amazing game. Its been along time since I've played such a deep game. It's fun, rewarding, and sometimes intense. You play as a noble dynasty, and try to move your way up the ranks. I went from being count of Anjou, to duke of Holland, to king of scotland, to king of scotland ireland and england, and then back to only king of england. Then I became king of only Jerusalem, then Emperor of the Byzantine Empire, then I lost the byzantine empire! And while this is happening, stories like this are happening: Duke Matthew was the rightful heir to the throne. He had just received a message that his cousin had died of an illness, right after being diagnosed as a leper. Shame. He liked the boy, and he had often visited him when his father was alive. His brother, the rightful king, had been deposed, imprisoned, ransomed and assasinated. Now, HE! Not some usurper, deserved the throne. He sat down at his desk and started frantically writing letters.......'. Now, I added a bit of thematic stuff there, but all of the events mention HAPPENED! But the thing that impressed me most is the historical accuracy. I swear, if I find a family on wikipedia, I can find it in the game. Even tiny counts are mostly accurate. It's impressive, fun and historically correct! What else could you want?

I have some mixed feelings about this game. I still recommend it, but with a huge grain of salt since this game isn't perfect and it isn't for everyone.   The soundtrack and visuals are beautiful, and the story and concept are fairly solid. The atmosphere was very well set up, and was able to be haunting or beautiful or calm or tense or mysterious at all the right times. The beginning of the game sets up a great sense of wonder and mystery. However, unlike other reviewers, I did not gain some kind of deep emotional experience from this game. Like Dr. Watts said at the end, the story was mostly 'a lot of cheese that sorted itself out'. River and John's relationship wasn't all that compelling besides their one childhood meeting. And the ending was, well, predictable. You knew what the ending was going to be before it happened, and I think that reduced the emotional impact for me. At the end I was just thinking 'Yeah, I knew this was going to happen...so what now? That's it?'.   The characters, besides Dr. Rosalene, Dr. Watts, and I suppose John, were pretty shallow. River's only personality trait is basically 'weird autistic person' which isn't very compelling and mostly comes comes off as stereotypical; I found her to be unlikeable and I kept wondering throughout the game what the hell John ended up seeing in her. Isabelle was also another one-note character that only served to preach about autism. I actually thought Dr.Watts and Dr.Rosalene had a more interesting, albeit non-romantic, relationship. On another note, I found the humor in this game to be funny, especially Dr.Watts' and Dr.Rosalene's banter, but I can see in other reviews that this sense of humor obviously doesn't appeal to everyone. And yes, the gameplay is a bit weak, but the story manages to keep the game interesting and fun despite the story's flaws.   So yeah, this is a solid, interesting game, but it's definitely flawed and I can see why some may find it unappealing. I'd give it a 7/10. I'm still recommending it because it still managed to be interesting, but I was a bit disappointed since it wasn't as emotionally compelling as I thought it would be. I don't regret buying it though, as I did find it enjoying. I would like to see the upcoming sequel to this game to see the creator's improvement. I'd say check out To The Moon if you're interested, pass if you're not.

Wolfenstein 3D was the game that brought the FPS Genre on he map. At least until Doom came out then everyone forgot about it. You play as William B.J. Blazkowics, an imprisoned U.S soldier at the notorious Nazi occupied Castle Wolfenstein. You kill one the guards, take his gun, and now your off to excape Castle Wolfenstein. Gameplay was simple, you loot ammo from dead Nazis, discover secrets, and capture Nazi gold while you're shooting yourself through each floor of the castle. Overall, the game is pretty good. The only gripes I have with it is how repetitive it can get. You shoot Nazis, take gold, find secrets, then rinse and repeat until you eventualy get to the final boss on floor 10. Then do it all over again for 5 episodes. The second thing I dislike about it is how easy it is to get lost at times. All the rooms have the same textures on the walls, so it can get pretty confusing on figureing out the direction you didn't go in already. Wolfenstein 3D is a fun peice of 'Gaming History' that any gamer needs to try at least once with the proper mods installed. I give it a 78/100. So GET PSYCHED and buy the game already!

app_name: Half Minute Hero: The Second Coming
review_text: Like the first game, 'Half Minute Hero: Super Mega Neo Climax Ultimate Boy', you need to defeat the evil lords in 30 seconds. There's a Global map where you can run around and visit towns and find quests with no time limit. When you enter a quest, you have 30 seconds to finish it, which as far as I've seen involves defeating the quest boss, though everything between the quests start and the bosses defeat varies greatly, so there's no tedium there. Of course, realistically, you can't finish the quest in soley 30 seconds. You are 'aided' by the Goddess of Time, who will restore the time limit back to 30 seconds for a fee, a fee that increases each time you use it. So while it's not merely 30 seconds, ya need to get the lead out of yer ♥♥♥. :D:  Unlike the first game, you have a party, better equipment management, and instead of a caravan you see at the end of a quest/level, you have an overworld to explore.  It's a great RPG, and if you liked the first game, you'll love the second(this one). Even better, if you don't have the first game, you get it for free when you buy this game

I decided to recommend this game because there are already so many negative reviews out there, and I feel like the game is being underrated. But the fact is I wouldn't recommend this game to everyone, only if you fulfil the following criteria:  1. You liked Deus Ex: Human Revolution 2. The above made you go out and buy the book Deus Ex: Icarus effect 3. You liked the book  The reason for this is that the game focuses on Benjamin Saxon, one of the main characters in the book, and it’s really cool to play him. The story takes place right after the end of the Icarus effect and it’s quite true to the events that take place there, there are even some recreations of the events in the book (I won’t get into details so there are no spoilers), and the dialogs are pretty much untouched from what you’ve read in the book. All of this makes for a good overall experience when playing the game, because you get to know what happens after the end of the book, and the story is very solid.  The thing is that that’s pretty much it… The graphics are indeed horrible, the mouse look doesn’t work at 100% (the vertical axis his way less sensitive than the horizontal axis), you can’t change the controls (although for me that wasn’t really a problem). As for the audio issues, I didn’t come across anything. If you play the game with a headset, you should be fine.  This being said, I’ll go on the L.I.M.B here and dare to say that gameplay wise, there are even some small improvements compared to HR. There are some cool new augs like the stealth dash and the body armor is no longer a passive aug. In HR this made the body armor a must get upgrade, because the result was that it made the game easier without any management. In The Fall, you have to manage when to activate it (which consumes energy) and when it activates it creates a glow around Saxon and you see a meter that tells you how much armor you have left and it drops when you take damage.  There’s been some talk about the game ending in a cliff hanger but I don’t think that’s the case. The story isn’t finished, that’s obvious, but it wraps things up in that specific mission scenario. If part 2 ever comes out (which looks very unlikely, considering how things are going in Square Enix), I’ll want to play it, even if it’s flawed like the first one because for me the story is worth it and the flaws don’t make the game unplayable.

app_name: Nightmares from the Deep: The Cursed Heart
review_text: Got this in a bundle and played it for the cards, but I must say, if you are into hidden item/Myst-style games, this is a very good game to get. Puzzles are not too challenging, and there is definitely a lot of backtracking throughout the map. The skip functionality doubles as a hint tracker that recharges, ensuring that you wont be stuck for too long.  There is also a find-the-items minigame (among all the other minigames) and you can play mahjong as an alternative and I thought that was really nice.  The story is meh, nothing too special, but for the price I would personally wait for it to go down before purchasing (but I am also very frugal lol)

app_name: Lara Croft and the Temple of Osiris
review_text: This game is fun and polished. Great graphics, tombs, puzzles and boss fights. Very close to the first game. I'd give the first game a 10/10 and this game a 9/10. The dungeons are linear compared to the first game, but still fun. This time around puzzles revolve around a staff instead of a spear. You use the staff to shoot light beams to close portals or at mirrors to solve puzzles. The staff also allows you to speed up or slow time for some objects in the game such as time bombs or gears.  The game ran smooth for me at 1080P/60fps, max settings, but with no AA and DOF off. My 980 video card stays at a cool 50C will running this game. So it's not demanding at all. A demanding game will cause my video card to run at 75C. The people complaining that this game runs worse than the first need to realize the first was on par with 360/PS3 hardware and this one is on par with PS4/XB1 and looks better than the first game.

Beautiful, challenging, and mysterious.  Take a deep breath, fire your singular arrow, and awake the slumbering beast.  Titan Souls has no intention of grasping your sweaty palm.  Observation is key to discovering the weaknesses of the imaginative and vastly varied Titans. Avoid their attacks and your opportunity will reveal itself, though how and when to exploit it often remains unclear. Fortunately, you have infinite lives to try different tactics, because you will quickly burn through many of them. As unforgiving as this process is, I've rarely felt truly frustrated - I've simply not understood what needed to be done, or failed to execute it within the narrow window. You face a short walk back to the battle arena after defeat. I used this time to reflect on why I'd failed and how I could change my approach to succeed. Still, over time these treks become marathons, and an option to cut straight to the action would have avoided this. Controls are tight and responsive, provided you heed the strong recommendation of a game pad. Seriously, keyboard controls are futile here.   The world is pretty and detailed, yet feels slightly hollow - its a lonely journey. This was most likely to convey the isolation of your vulnerable protagonist, and this works, but some rewarding exploration would have created a more vibrant world, rather than just a hub leading to battles.  The score is glorious. Wistful, ethereal music accompanies the main world, ominously fading to silence once you enter a dungeon, replaced with pounding tribal beats and darting strings once battle initiates.    When that perfectly aimed arrow connects and the giant is slain, it is satisfaction and disbelief combined. Only one task remains - retrieve your lone weapon.  An immensely rewarding game, it deserves to be valued on its own merit, not stand in the 'Shadow' of any other.

Shadow Warrior 2 is a great game. Why is that?  MOVEMENT This game is clearly a gem because it has one of the best movement systems in any recent FPS game - and that includes DOOM. Never ever has hopping around a map felt so excitingly awesome - the dashing and double-jump system is the best I have ever encountered in any FPS game to date. Excellent job.  COMBAT Combat in this game is fast paced and gory. Enemies can be taken apart almost surgically, regardless whether you are using one of the dozens of guns or melee weapons - all types of FPS players will find something that appeals to them in the 60+ weapons arsenal. Shooting and slashin feels meaty, brutal and fluid - another excellent job here.  PC FRIENDLY The game is very PC friendly - it supports a wide array of configuration options and it also allows us to have up to eight active weapons. EIGHT. Once you experience this freedom of choice it's hard to go back to more conventional shooters that restrict our armament needlessly.  Other devs should take note. This is what a PC game needs to offer in terms of options.  GRAPHICS The graphics overall are good. I like the lighting effects and the level graphics, but the character models (both enemies and NPCs) are not up to current standards. The facial animations are weird and generally characters do not feel very alive.   LOOT MECHANICS Shadow Warrior 2 has a basic loot system that helps to kepp players motivated by dropping a wide array of upgrades to be used on your various weapons, which all come with three upgrade slots. While the looting itself isn't spectacular the added customization of your character is a great addition that adds some more depth to the character development.  COOP Why don't all games have a coop mode like Shadow Warrior 2? It's easy, it's simple and it's tons of fun to play with your mates or some strangers.  OVERALL VERDICT Shadow Warrior 2 is a perfect blend of DOOM and Borderlands - it has fast-paced, fluid and flawless gameplay that keeps you engaged on its own and adds in some character progression, customization and tons of gameplay goodness. This feels like a game that has been made with a lot of care for the core areas: it runs smoothly, it has an appealing art style and the main gameplay is absurdly entertaining.  This game was clearly made by people who love gaming. Go buy it.

app_name: Game of Thrones - A Telltale Games Series
review_text: >> This review DOES NOT contain any direct spoilers but it DOES contain some discussion about the flow of events. Nothing revealing tho.  It's ok, I guess. I love Game of Thrones but this game is just as every other game about GoT: Dull, boring and average or below average. I trusted that TellTale could make a decend GoT game after I played Wolf Amongst Us which was a masterpiece. This on the other hand... is not. It's average.  It certainly has some GoT-ish aspects. Voice actors from the show are used poorly and the overall 'you choose what happens in the game' feel is gone. When those major characters from the show are all the time messing around, you know that anything significant can't happen. The whole game feels just like playground for children. Only the last episode gave you the feel that your actions can made something important to happen, but otherwise I didn't feel the freedom of choice. It felt like whatever you do will make you lose in some way. It has the GoT-ish feeling that everyone can die, but only sometimes. Sometimes you just know that something is not going to happen. Only the ending was really surprising.  The story as a whole was a kinda messy and confusing. The whole point of the story ended up very lamely and the possibility of 2nd season was way too obvious. It feels like Telltale is only milking from GoT's popularity by doing this kind of games.  Other telltale's games are fine, good or amazing but this is so far the worst of them. I really wanted to like this game but it's just not possible. The game could have been better and I hope that season 2 does something better. This game is not worth its price but if you catch it from a sale, it's certainly nice to play trough. Just don't expect it to be amazing because it feels like it's a bad fanfiction.

Take away the excellent death metal selection and some solid character art, and you're left with a very simplistic and generic stick shooter. Your choice of character and map doesn't matter, it's all just a texture change. You're basically a one hit death character frantically shooting everything in sight with your wand. You can get shields to take a couple of hits, but once they're gone, they tend to be very difficult to replenish via power ups. Your wand can get three different temporary shot power ups to mix it up a little, but I gotta say the red laser one is more of a hindrance than anything, as it makes you fire even slower. Your character already fires off center and the enemies that flood the screen take multiple hits. This is made even worse by the fact you can't see them coming very well off screen and there's no barriers to duck behind. It's just 'throw everything at the player, see how long they can last spamming bullets'. Don't expect too much skill, it's about as luck based as possible, unfortunately. Your character moves at light speed (you also have a worthless boost button... great for walking faster into enemies you can't see are there) while your character's hit detection is overly wide, making dodging the ever homing in enemies largely impossible.   There's a number of things that could have been done to make this a better title outside of the character selection art and ♥♥♥♥♥in' metal. Characters with different abilities, actual terrain instead of a blank textured field, maybe upgrade power ups, etc. As it is, Death Goat is a mindless one trick pony, er, goat... that doesn't live up to even basic potential. Just buy the music, the game is a non-entity that could have been churned out by a beginner on Newgrounds or something slapped together by Adult Swim.

a 1-4 player fighting game with a nidhogg-like map scrolling progress component.  In samudai you fight each other in stages. A victory will earn you progress towards your teams final location - and a loss will push you back towards the opponents final location. This can either be achieved by pushing an opponent off the screan or by the opponent 'drowning' twice. At the final stage your opponent will transform into a boss with HP that you must defeat.  The maps are dynamic environments. Water levels will rise, wind will blow, spirits will assail you.  Still feels somewhat incomplete but fun and original enough mechanics, not at all bad for the price. 2D platforming and shooting with stealth elements. I was sold on that description alone. And Aerannis almost works! It's a painful non recommendation.  I can't comment on the story because I didn't see it through the end. It's pretty confusing so far. I think it's trying to approach topics like prejudice and sexual identity.   Right! let's talk about the meat of the game. Action platforming is good. You got your shooting and jumping, a small variety of enemies to jump and shoot around. It's not too deep though, it gets boring after a while. It also introduces a human shield mechanic but it doesn't fit the action segments very well most of the times, as it's better to just shoot them directly. Except when you have to use human shields to open doors! that is pretty neat. It's like finding keys, but the keys shoot back.  I was looking forward the stealth mechanic but it's the most disappointing part of the game. It's hard to describe why it doesn't work because stealth is extremely delicate, and tiny details can make or break the experience. Considering they went for missions where you get insta-killed when they spot you, they should have been extra careful with it. The lack of visual cues to read the guard's patterns is one problem; They just turn around instantly. There is also no timer for the guard to go from unalerted to 'you're dead'. A chance for you to get back to your hiding spot and correct tiny mistakes, while the guard goes into suspicious mode or something. There's also the case of climbing stairs (door like stairs that teleport you to neighbor floors) that lead you straight to a guard. Guards also bug out sometimes, forcing you to get caught just to respawn.  Again, tiny details. That make the whole stealth experience is unfulfilling. It's made even worse when you get the run upgrade or, as I like to call it, the double-tap curse. When trying to move slow and carefully it's normal to tap the directional. With the double-tap curse you'll often find yourself doing quick runs in place. Not enough to actually run but enough to alert guards around you.  Then there are the bugs. I was actually willing to finish the game with the criticism above in mind already, but the game has the awful habit of bugging out during dialogues and there's no way around closing the game by force. All 3 times that happened to me made me lose a huge chunk of gameplay, as they are usually at the end of missions, before you had a chance to save. So, if you're already cursing at the game's imperfections, having to replay an entire mission because the game bugged out is a deal breaker for me.

First off, this game is very challenging and I have only played it a little bit. I think the game has the potential to be a lot of fun, but this is about the LAZIEST game port I have ever seen. All the control representations and beginning tutorial is all in XBOX controls so you need to lookup the settings and google how to do what. Why bother with releasing a game for a platform when you can't even just swap out the XBOX control icons with the correct PC controls? Is it THAT hard? Well, it's hard to judge this one.   This game feels like a Facebook game. You have a few set locations you can travel to by your map. Then the only thing you can do is to sell stuff. It's the main purpose of the game and the worst aspect of the game. You own that little ♥♥♥♥♥♥ lunch truck that you can upgrade to a luxury truck. Now at the beginning your storage is pretty small. Imagine a table with 3 columns, each column stands for a different type of item. In the beginning you own 1 item of each column and you can storage 36 pieces of each item. Each new truck increases the storage. Then you have to refill your stuff.  Since the first lunch truck upgrade alone costs you 10,000 Dollarinos and you just start off with 600 bucks, getting 1$ for each sold item (more if you set a higher price), it would take AGES to gain finally 10,000 bucks. Because on each 'day' you make between 5-35$ profit. The developer(s) thought it would be a good idea to put some challenges in the game, for expample you have to sell 10 ice teas in 3 'days' to get 1,000$. With these challenges it becomes rather easy to get enough cash to get a new truck.   During the lategame you have to chose which items you offer, since each colums contains like 15-20 items. This sounds a bit weird and I guess this isn't what you would expect from a Tycoon-game. The gameplay itself is very repetetive and becomes boring very fast. I bought it for only 0.99 $, so you could give it a try for yourself and be the judge. It might amuse you for a couple of hours, but it's too simplistic to be a good tycoon game.

Oh boy were to start.  Mount and blade warband a game that has been in my heart and my top 10 games of all time list for a while now.  I have always loved the idea of rising to the top as 1 person and being just that 1 person on the battlefield as a commander, so that made me wanna try mount and blade warband a lot.  When you first start this game out it will probably give you quite a hard time since the tutorial barely explains anything to you or give you any indications as to what you should do next, but thats the beauty of it, if you push through that rough patch at the beginning with barely any money, weak fighters, bad equipment and everybody looking down on you it will become great... let me rephrase that even the phase were everything you have sucks is great, but it will get better if you push through that.  After grinding quests from lords, villages and cities for a while and killing looters, sea raiders, steppe bandits etc you will be looking to get an enterprise ( a steady income for you so you can pay for your group of soldiers ) and from then on you can probably start out being a mercenary shifting the land to your favour.  You can help the nords defend against the veigirs or help out the sarranids against the khergit khanate and all that good stuff.  To sum up the good things and worse things about the games I will just make a pro's and cons list.  Pro's:  - The game is absorbing and interesting - The gameplay is good on the map its just pointing and clicking and the combat feels fluid and great - if you like medieval settings ( dont confuse this with fantasy ) this is the game for you  Cons:  - I can look past this personally, but the graphics aren't really all that great certainly not bad, but not great - Can be really frustrating when you lose your soldiers and your companions start leaving because you lose them ( which is the beauty of the game and this could be a pro aswell as a con ).  Cant think of any other cons right now though  Conclusion: just buy the game.

Spec Ops: The Line is one of those games that, every now and then, reminds you of why people keep making shooter games, and most important of all: why we must not lose faith in the genre.  Set in the current era, in a sandstorm-ridden Dubai, you play as Captain Walker, a guy leading a pair of battle hardened soldiers that are tasked with finding survivors of a previous rescue attempt and what little civilians may still be alive amongst the ruins of the buried city and retrieving them. What initially seems like a rescue op, soon goes awry, as not only they find hostile locals, but also signs of rogue elements amongst the US troops stationed there. As you traverse the game, level by level, you can't help but feel like you're losing grasp of what's wrong and what's right, as the carnage of war forces you to make ever increasing tough calls, and that's where Spec Ops: The Line's beauty truly shines: it's not an ode to war, liberty or freedom - much like other shooters nowadays are - , but a tale of how easy it is to lose one's self in the midst of war; of how easy it is to cross the line.  The story, apart from being deeply engaging and as stated, morally challenging, is deftly told by superb voice actors who make the beautifully crafted monologues and dialogues come to life with utter credibility. Also, the explosions, and effects, generally speaking, are top-notch. The music choice is SUPERB, even getting Verdi's Dies Irae in a running sequence that caught me unawares. It is worth noting, however, that for all the good this campaign is, it can be finished in about 7 hours, standard difficulty. And while the game does ask for a second playthrough to tie up some loose ends, it doesn't have much to offer in terms of further replay value.  Gameplay is great, but in a game that excels in every department it may stand out as a somewhat bland and 'been there, done that' thing. Still, it never gets boring, firefights being intense all the way throughout the game by either a change of pace, weapons or availability of squadmates.  Finally, it is worth mentioning that the game features coop. It's nothing worth acclaim, but a nice plus should you have a friend that happens to own the game.  While a tad short, a gripping story, a state of the art sound and music department and great gameplay makes Spec Ops: The Line the best shooter I've played in a long while: 8.5/10.

It is fun to play with friends , but the servers are dead . The graphics are very nice , for a 2010 game . Valve do know how to make coop games , but this one was just a commercialized product rather than something real , and I will tell you why :  -- Remember those times back in 2004 when Valve launched the Source engine for the first time ? That was the time when Half Life 2 came out , and along with that , Counter Strike Source and Day of Defeat . The engine wasn't shining because it was just stealing from the PC's resources . So Valve anounced that they will improve the engine . That is how the Source SDK Base public tool was launched . It was , and still is a tool that allows the community to use the Source engine in creating mods , games , or even used to run Source-based games . To prove that it can be used , Valve launched the 'Swarm' project , a closed and very secret project that will prove to the players that the Source engine can be fixed . So , after 3 years development and updating the SDK , Alien Swarm came out . Unfortunately , it did not last long , as it could be compared to a free and a more strategic Left 4 Dead 2 . -- That is my theory . I think the game is great , and the fact that its free makes it even better . Overall , for the gameplay and graphics , but with poor level design and AI , i give this game an 8.5/10

Bought this one WAY too early. Should have waited for the first DLC.  Right now the game feels very lacking... well, everywhere.  - Start with the load times - they are ABYSMAL. This game has got to be the slowest loading game I have ever played on any system, at any time since the 2600. Not only that, there really isn't any way to alt+tab out of the loading screens without causing a meltdown... so you can't just 'come back when it loads.'  - Next, the actual game doesn't feel like an epic struggle to tame a new planet. Instead, it feels like a race to see who can develop the specific technologies to reach the scripted endgames the fastest. You have all these cool Affinity techs, but you barely hve time to take advantage of them ... instead you have to rush through you chosen victory path, only getting to really explore your affinity at the last minute. On the playthroughs I have tried to explore the Affinities as a primary goal, I end up losing to the factions that raced through specific tech web patterns early on. Basically, you have to metagame the hell out of it... not enjoyable.  - Finally, trade or die? REALLY? I mean, Earth just emerged out of 400 years of isolationism, and I am racing to this alien world that may or may not be habitable in order to establish a new planet in my own image (aparently as fast as I can) and I am suddenly expected to trade with EVERYONE? That is such a stretch of the imagination that I get mad enough that I almost shut off the game at least once a playthrough based on that fact alone.   So, No, Frixas, I am NOT IMPRESSED with this latest offering. It has some awesome features, and some novel uses of existing Civ mechanics... but you really bombed out here.

The game has potential but for now I'm not impressed compared to Civ5 game play and graphics. The most I'm disappointed with the graphics which look bad for what a Sci-fi should offer (bad mix of colors and crowded small objects and units). As Civ5 started with a disappointing basic game play and so this one but DLCs will save and complete the game later in 1+ year timeframe.  You can find Civ 5 Sci-fi Mods that offer a better experience for free compared to this game.

app_name: Max Payne 2: The Fall of Max Payne
review_text: In my opinion this game is in most aspects an improvement over the original. But be warned: Steam version is as bad as Max Payne 1. If you have an OS that is newer than Windows XP, you will need to find and install some fixes for the game to even run, and then some more to fix the sounds issues and give it proper widescreen support.  After you get it up and running properly, expect the following:  - This game is much, much easier than the original Max Payne. Probably not too easy, but at least you will suffer much less cheap deaths, especially instant ones. Aiming is still hindered by forced vSync, but feels significantly more fluid than in previous game. There are harder modes for more challenge, but you need to beat it on default difficulty first.  - Combat feels less luck-based and more dependent on player's actual skill. Enemies, however, are not as mobile as in MP1, but still move around quite a bit. Weapons are rebalanced and are more diverse and shooting feels, for a lack of better term, just better. However, in most situations the player is stocked with enormous amount of weapons and ammo, which is nice from the variety perspective, but it also means that there is only a handful of instances where ammo conservation is a thing. The boss battle is also much more interesting.  - Physics are improved and expanded. There is semi-decent ragdoll in place now, and there's a lot of reactive clutter all over the levels, which adds quite a bit to the spectacle, especially when there are explosions going off. Speaking of spectacle - it looks just phenomenal for its age. Only human models are somewhat sub-par, but still huge improvement over the original.  - There is a metric ton of side content - TV shows, conversations, environmental storytelling, and thankfully you can skip it all very easily.  - The tone is entirely different now. MP1 was gritty, its humor was dark. MP2 is still a blood-soaked tragedy, but much lighter and cheesier, shifting from noir to alsmot being kitsch. It is both serious and deconstructive at the same time, hard-hitting the player with tangible drama, and then outright mocking events that predated the sequel - just watch a couple of 'episodes' of ♥♥♥♥ Justice on MP2's TVs. MP1 was suffering of both character and player, and MP2 leaves the pain to Payne(see what I did there?). But despite being somewhat bipolar, it delivers the 'feels' when it has the intent to do so.  - Bullet time is revamped. It regenerates now and is easier to use. Whether you'll like it or not is up to a personal preference.  If you enjoy story-rich third-person shooters this is one of the better ones. Buy it and play it. Just maybe try to buy it elsewhere first.

Castle Crashers is exactly what a 2D Beat Em Up should be. It's got good combat gameplay, excellent visuals, a fantastic variety of multiplayer modes, allows up to four players to play the game, great humor, plenty of levels, and a number of playable characters.  The game is cartoonish-styled, so expect a lot of over the top humor. Some of it is some mild black comedy, and there is some degree of blood and violence, comparable to TF2, but is slightly more mild.  The game has you in a (mostly) medieval setting, with the initial playable characters being four royal knights. There isn't much of a story outside of an evil wizard uniting several other kingdoms to attack yours for some kind of Magic Crystal (that the wizard directly steals) and claim the four princesses for varying reasons (only one is actually known to be held for forced marriage). Honestly though, in a silly beat em up that's as colorful and full of life as this one, it doesn't exactly need a big driving story. It just needs an atmosphere and charm to put on top of it's fun gameplay. And that's exactly what it has.   The gameplay is action-packed, wave of enemies after enemies show up constantly as you move forwardi n each level. You learn more attacks and abilities as you level up, and you can also combo enemies through various means, but the main means behind doing that is to jump and knock them into the air, where you can stay airborn for a while by striking your trapped enemies in the air repeatedly. You can use this kind of tactic to trap multiple enemise at once.  Of course, not everyone is going to enjoy the genre, so for those that do, the only real downside to this game is that it's gameplay can get repetitive. Sure you learn more moves after a while, and enemies and levels definitely change up, but the core gameplay stays the same, and when the action is so constant, it might get tiring for some pretty quickly. All the more reason to take breaks from the game though, right?  Also, there are a HUGE amount of characters to unlock. It can be tedious (some methods are particularly complicated, requiring that you beat story mode all over again with one new character just to unlock another one) so that's one thing about this game I kinda dislike. Not personally, but I can definitely see why other people wouldn't find value in it. If you enjoy the game enough though, you might just see it as replay value instead, especially if you're planning to bring some friends along. And while playing through the main story in singleplayer is definitely enjoyable, this game is most definitely more fun when you're playing with friends.

I paid less than a dollar for this, and for the first time in my life I legitimately feel like I underpaid for a game. All of the different mechanics to make you feel like a real gunslinger - dodging bullets in slow-motion, bullet time, ADS with a pair of revolvers, the duels, and the reflex QTEs. Everything just adds up.  My only real complaint is subjective, even. There just doesn't seem to be enough weapon variety, even if I am typically sticking to my revolvers and rifle. But that's okay. They work, and do they work well. The fact your weapons upgrade through your talent system is weird, but I can accept it.  I don't think I've ever had as much fun with a shooter since I played Borderlands, Ubisoft and Techland? Do this again. A MASSIVE improvement over Bound in Blood (Which still was an A+ title for the record).

It’s not a *bad* game. The concept is novel, the graphics feel pretty polished, and the team obviously cared about the game they were building.  But the sign of a great strategy game is balance. Unfortunately Cubemen lacked this in 2 important ways.   1) The strategy to beating every Defense level is the same. I thought the game was extremely hard at first when I was trying to sit back and defend, but then I discovered that if you rush the spawn point with the 2 basic units, you can win nearly every Defense level. A few of the 35 levels did require some thought, particularly the last one, but really only in the early game. But even these ultimately came down to the same strategy, it was just harder to do immediately: surround the spawn point, then wait for the win.  2) The wide variety of units doesn’t really matter. This piggybacks off #1. You can win every level (except the last one) with just Grills and Flamers (maybe even just Grills). If you have enough to build Rickies, you probably are going to win anyway. This made the game fairly one-dimensional. I think this aspect actually drives people away from the multiplayer; they get a sour first impression and can’t get over it.  Best of luck to the team in their future projects, at any rate! I know there has since been a sequel; I may give that a spin to see if some of these things are addressed there.

I was born from the depths of the demon realm, fought monsters, demons, warriors and even my own kin to build my powers and seek the truth of my existence. Along the way I chose which people I would help, what alliances I would form, who I would trust and whom I would betray via conversation trees and my actions throughout my story. With every monster I killed I became more powerful by upgrading my skills, abilities and magic. What powers I upgraded determined how I interacted with the medieval style fantasy world I existed in. Upgrade my healing abilities and not only did potions work better on me but I was able to heal others on my journey. Upgrade my plant knowledge and more potions became available to me to be crafted, for healing, buffing and to poison my enemy. I was able to choose which magical attacks would best fit my play style and upgrade them accordingly to wreak havoc on my foes and seek vengeance, love, sex or various truths of the day.   Demonicon was an above average action RPG overall. It did everything an RPG is supposed to do well enough and has lots of flexibility in character creation, an enjoyable story that you get to sway here and there with meaningful choices, and solid if unspectacular graphics and voice work. I really enjoyed Demonicon more than most RPG’s I have played, the “Dark Eye” universe was new to me and I enjoyed exploring it and the character development choices and powers really made me feel like I was evolving into a demonic weapon, very satisfying experience and very underrated game.

This game, while not perfect in my view is a great close quarters tactical shooter, or rather it is when the players try and think that way. This game rewards tactical behaivior like fire and maneuver and punishes the rambos by filling them full of lead as they sprint across a killzone. I personally can only nit pick a few critisisms.  In combination with high damage and recoil environment which makes every contact a real threat, the game manages to introduce a reasonable and important customisation system where choosing your supplies is an important decision. This allows the various classes making up both teams to adapt to the situation and give the player some flexibility.  The night maps add a further element to the game play, forcing the player spend points on either a flashlight (which lights up the area and shows his position to the enemy) or night vision googles (which are expensive and can easily be blinded by light sources).  My only siginificant dislike for this game is the respawn system. Instead of operating on the ticket system (where on ticket equals one player respawn) the game respawns purely on a limited number of a respawn waves. While in some modes it is very effective, in others it results in a player being left out of the fight because he only died once all his team's respawns had been used. A mix of ticket and wave respawns would be better in my opinion. My other complaint is (in my case) what appears to be poor optimisation. Even Arma 3 seems to be more stable on my system.  Negative points aside, if you are into tactical shooters, I highly recommend this title game. However, if your definition of a 'tactical shooter' is the latest Battlefield titles or (heaven forbid) CoD, stay away from this unless you want to end up raging about the fact you can't 'n0 sc0p3' the guy 'camping' the road.

I'd be very much inclined to say 'It's worth it... If its price was 20€'  As the game kept me in just over refund time, I pray the devs will accept my money and spend it on improving this game... Because right now it is a shameful display. Really. It needs a 'remastered' -very- badly. Please convince me that I did not make a bad investment.  But! Do not dismiss this game out of hand. Come back in a half-year's time. If it is given -a lot- of loving care, it can still be saved.

Time to make a wall of text.  Morrowind was not only my second TES game (my first being Oblivion) but it is also personally my favorite. It's sad to see a lot of people be put off from experiencing this game due to things like ♥♥♥ graphics or a less-than stellar combat system. Certainly, the game has it's flaws, but from my perspective it trumps TES IV and V in what it does right. Morrowind is not for the faint of heart who just want to ignore any plot-lines and kill things. The game requires patience and a little bit of creativity to make the most out of it.   Being a mage sucks at first because learning spells is really expensive and magicka is a ♥♥♥♥♥ to manage in this game. Being an archer sucks at first because landing sneak attacks is finicky and whiffing all of your shots due to low agility or fatigue can be VERY frustrating. Even being a warrior sucks since melee combat can be a bit of a slog and heavy armor sucks in the early game.  But the important thing to remember in Morrowind is not to focus in one particular area of expertise, but rather try hard to excel in all of them. Quickly switching weapons and being able to cast spells on the fly makes being a diverse character infinitely easier and more enjoyable. A good warrior is bound to keep a bow around as a ranged weapon and know a spell or two to work themselves out of a difficult situation  But even more interesting than the freedom of character building is the world building of Vvardenfell. The lack of an omnipotent compass and a map that can't warp you around (gasp) make exploring the world to get things done a mandatory step in any player's journey. No spoilers, but the number of things to distract you as you journey across the island are, for the most part, all interesting and only serve to better you as a character and inform you, as the player, of the ever changing world.  TL;DR: This game requires patience, but will lead to a massive payout in story and character growth.

TL;DR: Buy this game right now.  There's something modern games have been lacking recently. And that's a little something called fun.  There are various arguments as to why fun has been missing from video games as of late, ones that vary from person to person. Some people don't even think fun is missing at all. Personally, the reason I haven't been finding fun in video games recently is because they're too focused on trying to be pretentious, too focused on trying to be 'Cinematic experiences', too stupidly easy to come away with any kind of self worth for beating it due to a massive lack of challenge since they want to pander to casual audiences. MGR is none of these things.   Fun is that exhilirating feeling that widens your eyes and puts a huge ♥♥♥♥ eating grin on your face. Fun is that motivator to do something more than once. Fun is exactly what Metal Gear Rising is. Metal Gear Rising is at heart, a Video Game. And a good one at that. When an uptempo music reaches it's crescendo as you lift a giant robot 50+ times your size and throw it several yards away to then cut it into ribbons with a small sword, as you feel your cheeks stretching into a smile, you realize that you're having fun.  On top of that, this game is actually challenging. You have to react quickly to attacks, you have to work at becoming better at the game, and that's part of the fun. Sure, I may have beaten the game in a short 8 hours or so, but I didn't listen to codecs and the game is nowhere near finished. There's still unlockables, harder difficulties, and S ranks that await me when I feel like 'Rising' to the challenge. Which I will. Besides, those 'Just 8 hours' contained some of the most memorable soundtracks, boss fights, and setpieces that I've seen and heard in over a decade.  I've been waiting out this port for a long time. I could have just gotten it for PS3, sure. But it was so worth it to wait for the PC version. Besides, I want to help show Platinum Games that there is a market for their work on PC.   This is actually not that bad of a port. I thought it was going to be a disaster of Dark Souls and Deadly Premonition proportions considering the fact that Platinum Games is new to PC, but the port is actually not bad, and the recommended specs on the store page are slightly overblown a bit. I think I know why. This is the kind of game you want to run at 60fps due to the incredibly fast paced gameplay and have a little leeway with due to the slicing system. Totally understandable. I hope that we can see future Metal Gear installments and games by Platinum make it to PC. Platinum Games is an awesome developer and pretty much all of their games deserve your money, so if you have a console I would go pick up more by them if you like this game.

War Thunder is a free to play massively multiplayer online game for the PC and PS4. Developed by Gaijin Entertainment, it started off as a World War 2 air combat game, but has already added tank combat, which this review will focus on. Just like the Aircraft mode all the vehicles are from World War 2. If you’re familiar with tanks from that era, or play World of Tanks then you’ll recognise most if not all the tanks.The game play, just like the aircraft mode, is based around capturing and holding territory. Think of it as one big 15 vs 15 king of the hill match with tanks. Each team has a health bar. If one team has more captured territory then their opponents health bar begins to decrease. A team wins by reducing the enemy teams health to zero. There are two main variations of this mode. One has multiple points (usually three) on the map to capture, and the other only has one point on the map. Regardless which game type is on, almost all matches are won by the team who controls the center of the map.The vehicle physics are great. Tanks move like tanks. Unlike in World of Tanks where the tanks move as if they are slow heavy cars, tanks in War Thunder behave like tracked vehicles. This mean that they slide around, struggling to get traction, especially if you are traveling at speed. This can be used as a good tactic when attacking an enemy tank, skidding around them to get a clear shot of the less protected back of the tank. You can also embarrassingly flip your tank upside down if you’re not driving carefully. Graphic wise the games mostly looks great but there are a few areas that look dated. The tanks and other vehicles look amazing. The developers have put a lot of detail into the tank models, from the detailed textures, weld marks on joints, and just little imperfections on the tanks. These give the impression that the tanks were hand-built in a factory like their real counterparts, rather than crafted using a 3D modeling tool. However the terrain looks a bit dated and can sometimes look very angular. Some of the ground textures also look blurry and stretched.The game’s audio is top-notch. It sounds like a living battlefield. Headphones are a must as you’ll hear every creek and sputter your tank makes, and more importantly the sounds your enemies will make. The best sounds are the ones made when firing your tanks cannon. No other game has weapons that are as satisfying to fire than these!As with War Thunders air combat a lot of people will compare this to World of Tanks. Comparing the two though is a bit like comparing Counter-Strike with Battlefield 2. Both share the same weapons/vehicles and on some maps have similar objectives, but they are different enough that playing both will give two different experiences. World of Tanks is more like a team death match, while War Thunder plays like large game of King of the Hill. The most obvious difference is that in World of Tanks you only get one tank per match, while in War Thunder as long as you have spare tanks and crews you can keep respawning. This makes a big difference as people are more cautious playing World of Tanks while players are more willing to take risks to grab objectives in War Thunder. Overall War Thunder tank battles are great.The combat is exciting and rewarding.One shotting a higher tier tank is a great feeling. War Thunder is free to play, and while you can play this game without ever needing to spend a single penny, if you get into it then there are plenty of tanks to buy. Don’t worry if you don’t plan on paying for anything though, as the game is balanced enough to stop people from paying to win. If you’re a fan of World of Tanks or enjoy tank combat check this out. War Thunder plays different enough for you to happily play both.

The Wolf Among Us was an interesting story, and is based from the Comics called 'Fables' who are well known Fairytale Characters living amongst the Real World in New York City.  You play as the Big Bad Wolf, the Sheriff of Fabletown with major anger issues, and the thing I loved the most about this Game, is the extent that you can choose to unlease your rage, or you could just play the 'somewhat' niceguy.  The Gameplay focuses on Detective work, trying to get to the bottom of some Murders which happened in quiet Fabletown. It doesn't really offer a challenge to solve the cases, but rather about sitting back and enjoying the ride, choosing Dialogues that suit your style.  The Voice acting was well done, and the Animations fit in well. The Characters were well made, and even though I had the Music turned down to 3, it was still loud enough to really enjoy.  If a sequel were to pop up oneday, it would be a definite buy for me. This game has simple but aesthetically pleasing graphics, nice character outfit customization with a large variety of items (gotten at random from missions) and gameplay which is easy to learn but can be hard to master. It's turn-based strategic shooting in the same vein as Fallout Tactics or Xcom, though with less focus on the story aspect.  The game has its faults, chief among them the chat system which at this time offers no way to communicate privately with other players, as well as frequent disconnections from the servers which ejects you from the game entirely. However despite this, the enjoyment factor is extremely high and the game is never more fun than when you have four players in the same experience range taking on a mission together.  The co-op element, whether you are two players with a NPC each or a full team of four, really makes me happy with this purchase. And as it stands, this is my favourite co-op game of the year (and in several years). If assembling groups was a bit easier, the communication more streamlined and the characters a little more customizable, it would a must-own title.  As-is, I would recommend to to anyone who likes tactical co-op or shadowrun/sci-fi.  For anyone else it might be a gamble.

Ignoring the obvious and expected release 'issues' (all of which are very minor and have not affected my enjoyment whatsoever), this game is an incredible sequel to XCOM in every possible imaginable and theoretical way.  Whereas the first game suffered from a pretty linear set of missions and lack of 'stuff' to do between said missions, XCOM 2 offers almost too much stuff to do! You have to prioritize certain needs and desires over others, sacrificing more warm bodies to throw into the meat grinder for more supplies (money) to purchase gear for your current roster for example.  Basically you should buy this game now and get the Deluxe edition because you will be playing this game long past the expansions release dates. You will probably be playing this game on your death bed whilst surrounded by your disappointed family members that only vaguely remember what you were like before locking yourself in your Game Chamber and coming out only to refill your 2 liter of Mountain Dew and grab a fresh bag of Nacho Cheese Doritos because you absolutely HAVE to finish one more mission.

Let me start this review off by stating that I am actually having a lot of fun playing this game. It’s a direct continuation off of Civilization V, minus a few features that I personally was not a fan of, and many additions that I welcome completely. I’l section off my views into categories: Pros, Cons, Final Thoughts.  THE BAD: My big gripe with this game is the A.I. The decisions the A.I make are arguable worse than that of Civilization V. Civilization IV introduced a new system called agendas. Each Civ has an agenda and will find any means possible to fulfill it, even declaring wars on turn 10, but i’ll get more into that later. The agendas system is interesting in theory, but it’s broken in practice. I’ll use Montezuma as an example. Montezuma wants nothing more than have all luxury resources in his kingdom. If you have a resource that he does not have, he begins to act like a child; refusing to communicate with you and even denouncing you. The system has no prerequisites that must be met, and are at the very start of the game. This system more often than not leads into my next biggest issue with this game; surprise wars. Surprise wars are wars that happen because your civ neighbors just decide they dislike you based on HIDDEN variables that only the civ is aware of. A surprise war can happen anytime and anywhere, even on turn 10. This is a big issue because war hurts your economy largely. Trying to advance into the next age whilst fighting off Gandhi is very negative towards your Civ and can reduce your lead if the war is not solved quickly. It doesn’t just stop there, you can have a surprise war after surprise war waged on your for NO REASON. The A.I needs a fix very badly. Whether this a bug or just an intended feature, it does not work. It’s very flawed and makes the game more tedious than it should be.  THE GOOD: Though it did have to grow on me, I really like the way social policies are handled now. Having a branching tree just for civic policies really makes the game more strategic than it was with just the tech tree. It really makes you feel like your empire is growing socially. The expanding empire so far my favourite feature that Civilization IV has added. Watching my civ expand from a small one-tile village to a lively city with many districts and wonders is a great way to feel immersed in what you’ve built. Firaxis largely expanded on the wonder system in a wonderful way. Instead of just seeing a little pyramid pop up on your screen, they have added little scenes that show the wonder being built in 2x speed from the ground up, along with relating quotes narrated by Sean Bean. They have added a multitude of new wonders & natural wonders to be discovered. Each of the wonders takes up a tile space but the benefits they provide really outweigh the cons of losing a tile. They really improved the art style and made it a lot easier on the eyes. While Civilization went the realistic map-style in V, VI makes the turn into a more cartoonish style, more akin to Civilization Revolutions, however more refined and pleasing than Revolutions. They removed the happiness system which was introduced in Civilization V. No more will your happiness turn to -49 because of a trade deal that ended.  FINAL THOUGHTS: This game is an absolute blast for something who is a gigantic fan of Civilization and strategy games in general. They made a big effort to improve upon the formula right out of the gate. I have full confidence that Firaxis will fix the problems mentioned above in a patch or possible DLC they have planned. Overall, I recommend it for any big fan of the Civilization series.

Decent enough game but far too short on gameplay. Though I do recommend it, I do so with a few caveats.   - The main storyline is pathetically brief in duration without any side-quest fillers to speak of.  - It is impossible to get into the top-of-the-line craft with full compliment of level 6 weaponry before completing the main storyline.  - People have compared this to Privateer [DOS] but there is no true flying in this game whatsoever. The closest it gets to that game is through their use of a missions system to make extra money.  - Stellar mass inconsistencies... one minute you're dropped out of warp by a marble-sized asteroid and the next, you're barreling into a structure or a miner that is crossing your path.  - Too many times, I've gone on a mission to save someone only to get almost all of the big enemy ships killed but then the person that I was sent to save and who has survived the barrage of damage from many ships is suddenly killed by the lone attacker, mission over...  - Or, go on a mission and without any warning at all, unknowingly stray too far from the mission epicenter and, you guessed it, mission over.   There are other things of note that have been posted by others. This game can be had for half the cost if you shop around like I did so I highly recommend doing that in order to purchase it. Like I stated, it is an ok game but a bit shallow in both gameplay and storyline.

The Witcher 3 brings the tale of Geralt to an end and does so with a game of the highest quality, one that rightfully sits amongst the best rpgs of all time.   PROS:   -great graphics on characters, monsters and landscapes  -amazing sound design, the ambient sounds (wind, distant monster roars, rain etc) are especially well made and make you feel truly inside the game world  -fantastic voice acting  -great soundtrack with the folk songs by Percival as the most notable highlight  -amazing main story with a truly powerful ending questline  -a metric ton of side quests, witcher contracts and loot hunts  -meaningful story choices both during main and side quests  -lots of loot to find and craft to suit your needs  -excellent character progression with different trees to put your skill points in  -Geralt of Rivia is an excellent lead character  -lots of interesting and well rounded characters, both new and returning  -a good amount of interesting and original monsters to fight  -an expansive game world with lots of things to explore, hidden loot, unmarked quests etc  -said game world truly feels alive thanks to the npcs populating it and acting their everyday lives out, the weather also changes constantly with wind howling in trees, heavy rain, gorgeous sunsets etc  -the game sports a few moments of true art with a 5 mins long bard song and an opera play where you participate  -gwent substitutes dices as the board game of choice in the game world and is actually an interesting and fun game in its own right  -just as the previour games there's alot of pretty explicit mature content (sex, violence etc) firmly expressing how gritty and dark the game world is  -despite sporting a serious and tragic story the game isn't afraid to also show some humour through npc quotes (from movies or memes), funny side quests or Geralt's dry brand of sarcasm    NEUTRAL:   -there is so much to do that sometimes it can be easy to lose yourself following a side questline or just exploring around: once you get back to the main plot or a quest you accepted a few hours earlier it might take a bit to remember what you're supposed to do and why  -certain scenes might feel a tad too verbose for some   CONS:   -the combat can feel somewhat clunky, there's a certain inertia behind each move or strike and it never feels completely fluid or responsive: it's certainly an improvement over the first two games though  -movement also feel a bit sluggish and lacks precision, especially if you're trying to finely control where you're moving/pointing: the turning circle feels too wide and underwater controls are awkward  -AI can sometimes get stuck on scenery/objects during fights  -inventory system is badly designed and cluttered, there's no way to sort stuff and finding what you need can be a bit painful considering the tons of different objects/crafting components you can loot  -the horse you can use to get around is generally pretty glitchy and has a tendency to slow down or downright stop either when you collide with small scenery objects or just for no reason at all  -the horse challenge rides are boring and just boil down to running as fast as you can then blocking your opponents by running into them while you recuperate stamina, there's no strategy at all: also the aforementioned horse controls can ruin your day   -the map quest marker can spaz out a bit at times either pointing in the wrong direction or disappearing for a few seconds  -an assorted array of small glitches like slight clipping on certain armor/objects, npcs repeating things over and over, slight model/texture pop-in, animations occasionally spazzing on certain npcs, too sudden weather changes etc   http://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=449657005   Make no mistake Witcher 3 is a true masterpiece and the game the saga deserved quality-wise: the cons I've mentioned are blemishes made more noticeable by the fact they might at times slightly mar such an enjoyable and well crafted experience but none of them are game breaking .  The game truly shines thanks to its engaging story, huge amount of content (took me 90 hours to finish), well written characters and its immense, believable world: a world that, thanks to its polish/slavonic influences is also original and way far from your usual generic fantasy realm.  Everyone interested in deep and truly adult rpgs needs to play The Witcher saga from start to finish: this game is just a fantastic end to an already amazing saga.  Hats off to CD Projekt.

This game has one main purpose: to tell a story. And it tells it, beautifully; makes you experince the story within the gameplay. There are tons of secrets to find and information to uncover. A whole planet is yours to explore. It is not big, not varied in nature but never boring. Best way to describe the planet -and the game- would be to say that it is a 'compact' experience...  When playing on normal difficulty; survival is kind of there, just to keep you on your toes; give you a bit of stress and excitement and keep you focused. thinking this game is just a Survival game is a bit of understatement. There is adventure, survival of humankind, history and a whole science fiction universe burried in it. All you have to do is play and experience it...  Only bad thing I can say without giving any spoilers ending is a bit weak... The twist is good enough, but it lacks the force of a real conclusion... I can only hope that there is a sequel or a spin off planned to bring that much needen finale to the story.  (This is a foremost a VR game, yes, but it is still fun without the gear. )
