I've logged 34 hours into the game as of writing this review.  The game has been nothing short of an exceptional experience. From the very moment you start the game, you get a good sense of what you're getting into. The music sets the tone to this sort of grungy, rockabilly space Western. It's full of character has a good sense of 'space'. All this despite playing like a sort of 'World of Warships meets Elite: Dangerous' hybrid between space exploration and naval combat. I highly recommend buying this game.   Pros:  Full controller support (including optional Playstation layout). Tight Mouse&Keyboard controls. Very pretty graphics and effects. Visible modular ship damage. Many graphics settings in the launcher. Superb soundtrack. Optional custom soundtrack to replace OR supplement the original soundtrack. Fluid combat mechanics. More lasers than I sometimes know what to do with. Unique and lively NPC interactions. Moderate learning curve. VERY attentive and responsive developers constantly patching reported issues.  Cons:  Enemy dialogue is sometimes repetitive. No vertical travel (2D movement only). Objects (like Cargo) sometimes block docking or other actions. Slaves (Cargo) have no clear way to be saved other than forfeiting to Militia (-4 rep penalty). Single-player only (for now).
Simply childish.

Expendabros is basically a free double-ad demo type deal. It simultaneously promotes the game BroForce and the film The Expendables 3, and does a pretty good job of it. This acts as a great demo for BroForce, and I'm much more interesting in buying that now that I've gotten a taste for it. As for Expendables... well, I respect whoever decided to partner with BroForce, anyways. That was a total bro move. You'd honestly expect people to threaten lawsuits over the game, but then they make this happen. Pretty baller, guys. You done alright by me.  That said, I'm still not sold on Expendables 3. It's not the game's fault, the game was awesome. But I saw the first two movies, and there was less ♥♥♥♥♥in' action in either of those films than in a single level of this game. I'll tell you what, though: Expendabros  did  convince me to at least give Ex3 a proper chance to redeem the franchise, rather than just writing it off like I was probably going to do.  So pretty big waves being made, here.  The levels are fast and sometimes chaotic. Moreso the further in you go. It's a lot of fun. Everybody plays differently, and everybody plays well. Characters each have their own pros and cons, and since which one you'll be playing as is random, you'll just have to git gud with all of 'em!  Also, there were way more levels than I was expecting. I figured three or four. Five if I was lucky. But it just kept on going. I got a good hour and a half out of this, which is impressive for how fast the levels actually flew by.  Final boss was balls, though. At least on single player. All the chaos and screen shaking made it tough to tell what was going on. I eventually just 'cheated' and tunneled under the entire level and sticky grenaded the boss from behind. Makes me wonder if this or the main game scale to single player, or if the game doesn't change at all between player counts. Guess I'll have to find out.

Some people actually hate this game because they see it as cheap jump scares, the dumbest and oldest way in an attempt to scare someone. Those people are missing the point of 5NAF2.  What makes the game is not the death, but what leads up to the deaths. The new electronics have this friendly design that seems like they won't hurt you, but end up being just as buggy as the originals. The originals end up looking so ♥♥♥♥ing scary that it makes the previous game look cuddily by comparison. All the world you work in just lies on top of you, choking you with a horrid enviorment where nothing is your friend no matter how friendly it looks. If you mess up your swing, take too long checking the cameras, don't flash the light enough, don't wind the music box fast enough or long enough, don't pay attention: the bots are going to punish you for it. They might kill you, or might make you panic, or make it so you aren't paying attention to another threat. The fact that its you against everything and just hoping time ticks fast enough paints a horror experience that very few recent games have gotten (I'm looking at you Resident Evil 6 and Dead Space 3)   The game even has a twist, given the game play and the first one is not as subtily woven in but still hits just as hard. And is a hell of a lot more then we expected.   Where the game lacks, although maybe I haven't found it, is some subtile things that really make your hairs stand out on edge. An example would in the first game would be the subtile death moans the robots gave when they snuck in. They don't seem to have that and seem a bit more in your face (sometimes pulling the monitor down when they're in the room). granted there are still creepy things about the game, but they're a little more public and evident then they are in the first game.   Think of it like Portal vs Portal 2, or Dark Souls vs Dark Souls 2. Where there is more of the game, and while it doesn't appear as deep as the original or explode in the same way, it can still pile drive you and make you ♥♥♥♥ yourself with the experience.  I highly recomend this to any game library. Don't expect to sleep well though. Since getting it, I haven't slept well without hearing a bump and having my heart race.

Military cover shooters have become a genre onto themselves in recent years, thanks to the success of franchises like Call of Duty and Battlefield. Gameplay in these titles focuses on tactical teamwork and army culture, turning away from the more fantastical elements of modern action gaming in both story and implementation. Insurgents sit in for aliens and narratives spring from newspapers instead of novels, all in the service of 'realism.' Realism means different things to different people, however, and hardcore players have long wished for a game that combines a multiplayer military tactical approach with the level of detail seen in proper simulations. Czech developers Bohemia Interactive have been dabbling in this field for almost a decade following the debut success of Operation Flashpoint, and ARMA II: Combined Operations ($33, free demo) is their most recent marketplace deployment. ARMA II: Combined Operations has a realistic look that makes the imprecise controls all the more frustrating.  Combined Operations bundles several releases under a single title, including ARMA II and the standalone Operation Arrowhead expansion. That translates to a lot of mission for your money, but keep in mind some of the content shipped in 2009 when ARMA II was initially released. The gameplay found here is of a specific flavor and the difficulty level an order of magnitude more challenging than a traditional FPS. Not all of that challenge is due to the gameplay, either. There's a general imprecision to the controls and sluggishness to movement that pervades Bohemia's 3D engine and makes game interactions feel like your computer suddenly skipped two upgrade cycles. Other game mechanics are equally poorly implemented. Climbing ladders, utilizing inventory, and operating vehicles are all examples of obscurely designed mechanics that feel more like add-ons than actual core code.  To counter this, Bohemia offers a sandbox experience par excellence, with mission design tools and an easily moddable structure that invites users to take chances and play around with the results. This more than anything has been the key to ARMA's success. For example, you might ask why this elderly niche shooter leads the sales charts at Steam so frequently, but the answer is simple: This is the package that's required to run DayZ . Forward thinking and trust in their community paid off to the tune of 1.3 million new sales and development of what's sure to be a hit sequel, largely without Bohemia lifting a finger to do anything other than count all the money. I wonder if encrypted game files still seem attractive to their competitors.  Most of ARMA II's real action takes place in multiplayer mode, where cooperative teams can employ small unit tactics and other military strategies against one another. The levels are vast–hundreds of kilometers in size–and the game's architecture supports dozens of players, vehicles, and aircraft simultaneously so aerial support, heavy weapons coverage and more are all part of the tour. Weather changes, terrain, and day/night cycles are all crucial tactically, while enemy AI is good enough to be uncanny at times. The promise of all this is tempered by the reality of the engine's shortcomings, but it's an impressive feat nevertheless. Night missions utilize vision enhancement gear and other stealth goodies.  With ARMA III on the horizon, Combined Operations is difficult to recommend. Hardcore players may be drawn to the high levels of detail and realism, but they are sure to find disappointment in some of the awkward systems that lay underneath the uniform. For these users, I'd recommend waiting for a sale at Steam or GOG before taking the plunge. Players curious about DayZ shouldn't wait however. ARMA's flaws aren't nearly as disruptive in that environment, and the excitement around the mod makes playing sooner rather than later worthwhile.

With the only five hours of playing RWBY: Grimm Eclipse, me and my friend have BEATEN this game. That is right, me and him have BEATEN the campaign in a five hour sitting! Was it worth the money? Definitely!  This, hack and slash type of gaming for RWBY makes the player feel like they have got EVERYTHING under control, until those Grimm start to haggle over your life by clawing you to literal death! There is a basic type of combo system to it, something as simple as Super Smash Bros., except without the excessive use of holding your joystick to the right or left, while pressing B. With an Xbox 360 controller, it moves marvellously well and performs pretty spectacular (No idea if PS3 support works, but I guarantee, 80%, PS4 and the Xbox One controller would work)! I have not tested the mouse and keyboard, but according to my friend, it seems very tolerable and very easy to use!  Here is the cons... if you have a below average computer/laptop like me, I had to turn down my resolution in order for the game to even move without a one or two second delay when moving across the menu or the actual gameplay (640 x 400, to be exact). Putting up with low quality (or what the game calls 'Potato' quality) in fullscreen, or squinting at a small window is a bit of a drag, BUT, if you have an above average tower or laptop, then you should be fine!   The second con (without mentioning any spoilers), I personally felt that the storyline came to an abrupt ending, without any satisfactory closure. Playing the campaign was a blast, with a friend, of course! :) I did not try to play alone, but if you would like to put yourself through waves of enemies... go right ahead!   Hopefully the creator of the game plans to add more to the campaign, as I would like to see how it progresses! Adding more levels/chapters to the campaign would be pretty cool, in my opinion!  The levelling-up system is rather fun to play with, they do not cough up the abilities to you! Some are already unlocked, as a vast majority of them are locked until you fill out their given quota, when viewing the ability. Unlocking ALL of them is not an option, since each character can only level up to ten. However, when you use up your skill points, which you can earn each time you level up, you have the authority to 'reset' your skill points when out of the game in the preparation lobby, every chapter. In-game, you choose which ability to spend your skill points on.  My overall verdict, an enjoyable hack and slash game with Team RWBY as your first class, and the rest most likely coming out as DLC (like Team JNPR)! I would rate this an 9/10, the remaining 1 being the lack of bosses.  My advise, play online or play with friends through the neverending waves of Grimm! Oh, and be sure to avoid going into the Horde mode with less than three people... otherwise you would be setting yourself up for a death wish!

app_name: Tibetan Quest: Beyond the World's End
review_text: i was _soooo_ looking forward to this one (even posted about it in advance on G+), and it slapped me down in the very first few scenes...  a) the very first set of puzzles insults the user's intelligence. Three blocks of ice, one pickaxe. Only one of those blocks can be broken with the pickaxe. One cannot, for reasons it explains, and the third cannot... simply because. i.e. arbitrary. You've got to get a near-identical tool to break _that_ ice. WTF?  b) it won't let me melt ice using non-boiling water (whereas physics allows me to just fine - try pouring cold water over ice and see what happens). WTF?  c) one of the early-on HO scenes wants you to find a ring. The ring is placed almost directly next to a glove. The ring is big enough to fit around at least 3 fingers of that glove. i.e. it's the size of a bracelet, not a ring (except that that HO calls it a ring). WTF?  i far prefer a mediocre but internally consistent HOPA, like the Queen's Quest or Demon Hunter, to a HOPA with any notable amount of arbitrariness (which this one is full of).  Maybe someday i'll get over these offense to my sensibilities and give this another try, but i can only assume that i'll be faced with more 'WTF!?!?!?' moments than i care to encounter.  Next, please...

DISCLAIMER: My first review on Steam and is simply my ramblings and praises for the game series. This is not a critical or professional review and will try to be spoiler free. I have personally backed and funded this visual novel on Kickstarter and have read the series when it was first released.   After 13 long years and a with an astoundingly successful $1.2 million dollar Kickstarter, the first of most highly acclaimed adult visual novel series arrives to the west in English and on Steam!  Muv-Luv (pronounced Mabu Ravu) is comprised of three parts: Extra, Unlimited and Alternative (the 3rd which will be released in English on Steam later by the end of 2016). Muv Luv (originally released in 2003) begins as a simple highschool slice of life romantic comedy that slowly evolves into a post apocalyptic science fiction mecha story of survival. If you can look past the quirky character designs and well-aged old art style (the game series being more than 10 years old) Muv-Luv can be charming and an enjoyable read on its own.   As an enthusiast of Japanese Otaku subculture, I can say that Extra/Unlimited will probably at first read and appear as mediocre, average, or even perhaps boring to anyone that has had previous experience with all the various anime/manga or visual novel mediums out there. However, do not be fooled by Extra/Unlimited's cheerful, laid back and moe filled story line. Although it appears innocently dumb, a plethora of allusions, foreshadowing and subtle hints litter the first hour of the story and onward, which when looked back upon when one completes the series, will amaze you. You can even say that the entire narrative of the first two games were confidently written in preparation for the final game in the trilogy.   Extra can be seen as introducing the characters and relationships, while Unlimited shows you the world and background for the grand finale which is Alternative. Extra will bring you laughter and joy and Unlimited will bring you sadness and hope. Essentially, Extra and Unlimited lay the foundation that leads to one of the most highly acclaimed visual novel of all time. It is well worth the read to to get there and is an absolute requirement in understanding the characters and its story.  The Muv-Luv series is one that is near and dear to my heart. An exceptional experience that I will never forget. A story of unique quality that is rarely written once a decade. One of the few visual novel series that I'd wholeheartedly recommend to anyone that is a fan of romance/comedy, mecha and science fiction. Lighthearted, goofy, and sweet... Muv-Luv Extra is like any other generic romantic comedy visual novel out there, while Muv-Luv Unlimited introduces a unique sci-fi genre twist. The appreciation of their value and beauty can only be truly seen once one reaches the Alternative.  “Time is the cruelest kindness.”

The game itself has a well implemented progression system when it comes to developing your abilities. Or at least that's what I thought initially. This progression system unfortunately goes to hell with the use of cards that can buff you up. Why? Simply because this is where the F2P character of the game is lost to you. You either pay to grow fast or face being wiped out.  On the other hand, if you want to give the game a chance, consider that the major roles in the game are monker, warrior and supplier. So, to be of service to the region you are placed in you might want to talk to the people that are around you and see what's needed and what you actually want to do. The game in the end intends to be a social game. A very important part of the game is your keep. You have to properly layout your defensive structures and troops to not be wiped out. The same comes to placing structures in your town. Depending on the role you want to play you need certain structures to produce armor and weaponry, faith, food and other resources.  Your economy in the end depends on this and having a good economy is key. Ahh, and let's not forget about happines. Your villagers must be happy to have good production and attack/defense numbers.  In game communication overall is lacking and frustrating due to the methods used to do so. The more organized groups depend on other services to properly do their work; skype, teamspeak, etc. With so many hours spent on this game and having experienced the ultimately imbalaced gameplay that is generated due to card bonuses, which you have to pay for; a bad communication system and a really bad protection system for new players I can't really recommend this game.

At first, I thought Left 4 Dead 2 was an average game. Simple gameplay, simple mechanics, okay graphics. After a few hours of gameplay, you start to realize that's the exact reason why this game is so good: everything is simple and very well done. L4D requires efficient teamplay and responsiveness to be played correctly. Fights are fast-paced, and special infecteds will simply screw your team if you don't handle them correctly. They have simple, yet various and efficient abilities which can really make your progression difficult.  To me, this game's best attribute is its level design. Campaigns feature well-built, semi-open maps. They never feel too closed, yet you never feel lost in the environment. The game always points out the way in a natural, almost undetectable way, with smart placement of light sources for example. Little exploration is advised as it allows to find items, some nice easter eggs and level-design surprises.  The game has an active modding community, and the workshop is full of model replacers, skins, custom audio and, last but not the least, a bunch of custom campaigns. Some of them really are amazing (try Urban flight !) and are one of the reasons why this game is worth playing. Beware though: if you're a solo player, this is isn't for you. Play it with friends.

Sid Meier's Civilization V is a roundbased turn-based strategy, 4X ( = eXplore, eXpand, eXploit, and eXterminate) game. I'll review and recommend this game together with its expansions, as those DLCs add important things to the main gameplay, making this game so outstanding good. If you like games like Crusader Kings or the Total War series, you definitely should check this one out. (There are of course some minor to major differences)  You pick a leader/country for a game and have to reach different goals for different victory conditions.  My (formerly) most played game on steam, here's why:  + addictive as hell + high replay factor + you get so much game for your money + every country/leader has different strengths + you'll be able to play against friends + lots of diplomacy and different victory conditions + it is so much fun to watch your empire grow + every game you start is quite different + with DLCs: there is now religion and even more ways to win a game + many different characters, with DLCs: even more different characters + Graphics are actually neat, can look quite nice but does also run on older pcs (I guess)  About the music: Some parts of the soundtrack are very nice and beautiful, but after playing a long time they can get repetetive and annoying, so it's neither really bad nor good.  Some small things do bother me:  - I guess this is a matter of taste, but I think there are way to many achievements, like 280 of 'em. - the scenarios are quite boring - sometimes I feel like you should have more diplomacy options and sometims it's annoying how fast another nation dislikes you (but I guess this is somehow realistic =) - Battle is really simple, if you want a similar game with more focus on battle/war, try the Total War Series (e.g. Medieval or Rome 1)  Overall, this game is giving you extremely many hours of gametime due to its addictive gameplay and I guess you'll easily get plus 100 -150 hours with this one.  Rating:  94/100

review_text: Two words would change my mind about my recommendation: controller support.  This game begs to be played with a controller, and it seems like it would be easy enough to implement, but this is not an option. Even programming joy2key is an awkward control scheme due to having to use the right stick as a cursor control while moving around independently.

This game has elements of Left 4 Dead w.r.t. to it's interface and some of the game play. If you play L4D or L4D2 a lot you'll be at home when playing online co-op with three of your friends. The menus are exactly the same.   The game is playable; I just hope there will be a community creating extra maps for the game. What let the game down for me is the lack of weapon choice and the overall darkness of the maps as well as grainy graphics. Even when the graphics were maxed out it looked like a game from several years ago. With respect to weapons, what you get is a painkilleresque like weapon that's affixed to your arm. It's effective, but it would be nice to have some variety. To recharge health you pick up the hearts left by the natives you've killed. These restore anything from 5 to 15% of your health for each heart grabbed.  To be honest, I wish I'd waited until this game was on a deal despite it's already relative cheapness.

French Developer, Arkane Studio's, game Dishonored was one of my favorite games from 2012. While the narrative suffered from the Peace-Choas binary, the gameplay was anything but constricted. While a number of ways to play Dishonored will be a great time for anyone that loves stealth based assassination.  In Dishonored you play Corvo, the bodyguard of the late Empress, headed to his execution after being framed for her murder. It's around this time that you are given supernatural powers by a mysterious entity. The first power he gives you is blink, which allows you to jump from place to place instantly and silently. This being the main power of the game it feels tight and responds to your actions in well telegraphed ways. The additional powers must be purchased and upgraded through the use of collectables. The additional powers are insanely fun and are great to use in conjuntion with your gadgets and the environment. The levels themselves are well designed and allow you to use your variety of tools and powers to either butcher hordes of guards or go in silently and send your quarry to a poetic demise. You can even go the entire game without killing a single person, including your targets.  Unfortunately the story does not match the variety of the levels, have a very basic divide of High Choas and Low Choas depending on how many hapless guards and townsfolk you decide to kill on your way to your victim. In addition, while the story has some twists, most of it is largely predictable and the real story exists in the amazing world building that was undergone to create this game, told through various books and overheard conversations.  Ultimately Dishonored is worth a try for anyone interested in stealth or assassinations games. It brings its own variety to the genre and sets to hopeful tone for future titles from Arkane Studios.

On first playing this game, I found it a little bit difficult and frustrating. However, after modding my weapons a tiny bit and getting used to the bizarre control scheme, I really started to appreciate the intense atmosphere of the game. Whilst the game is unfriendly to newcomers, it is - no doubt about it - one of the most intense third person shooters that I have ever played. I particularly enjoyed the aspect of Greek mythology that was included in the game, though there were some elements that were incorrect. The soundtrack and atmosphere of the game really set the mood, and the random 'Tests of Faith' sometimes had me laughing, and other times cringeing. The 'Spirit Guide' can sometimes be a real pain to listen to, but otherwise he is genuinely amusing and likeable.  Whilst this game gets quite a lot of negative reviews for being a mindless shooter, I think that this game is of great value for it's price, though would maybe recommend buying it when on sale. I feel sad that I can never find anyone to play the game with as well, as I think that it would be a lot more fun with friends. Overall, this game satisfied me after a few brief moments of frustration, and I think I will definitely continue to keep playing the game.

CONS: -Story's kinda crap. -The levels are waaaaaayyyy to linear. Dust is a really fun combat/platformer in the 'Metroidvania' style, with a lot of charm to boot. Don't let my play time fool you, I have a platinum trophy for this game on Playstation Network. The basic gameplay mechanics are solid. Dust controls very well, and pulling off combos is a breeze. The platforming also feels tight, which is a huge selling point for me as I have always loved 2D platformers. They have successfully combined combo-based fighting and platforming, to the detriment of neither aspect of the game. Ripping combos for hours on end can get a bit tedious by the end, but that is one of the few criticisms I have of the experience.  The art speaks for itself. This is a cartoony, but beautiful game world, and was a joy to explore. The characters are fun and interesting, and the voice acting is mostly very good. Fidget is my favorite, with the right amount of humor to avoid becoming annoying. The world offers a varied selection of environments to explore, and many different enemy types to battle. This is a really fun experience, and does not take very long to get through for those of us who no longer have time to complete massive 50+ hour games. I highly recommend it to anyone who enjoys 'Metroidvania'-style exploration.

The goblin where I could upgrade units. The magician to upgrade the dragon. The council to make decisions and the politics. Where one commander didn't like you and changed his mind after a few battles. Stuff like that great. But other than that the game was not for me.  I would have enjoyed to have a more strategic way of fighting. Would be cool if each zone needed to be taken over by creating a base rather than just killing off the enemies with some RNG mechanic. TL:DR  Buy the game if you enjoy driving around a track on your own trying to beat your personal best time.  HANDLING IS SOOOOO realistic.

I'm giving this a thumbs-up, but just barely and only for those that realize this game is painfully slooow (and made purposely slow by many design decisions!), with little to see or do.  My main complaint is a real lack of IMAGINATION in the visuals of this game! All the planet terrains I've found basically look like some of the slightly rockier areas of the USA with a few oddish plant and animals scattered about. You could find more diversity and variety in most single US states, than in all of these many planets combined! In a game where checking-out all the cool visuals of fantastic worlds and creatures is the point, we're given I child's view of space and alien life on distant worlds. I see no WOW factor in ANY of the planets or NPCs, and few creatures - so you end up just grinding away on dull planets, with irritating sentinels and even creatures attacking you on sight, while you explore the same planets again and again. It gets old fairly quickly.  Yes, there was time, at the first of the game, that I didn't realize how dull this game was going to be, where fun seemed like it might be just around the next corner - well, I haven't found that corner yet, but I'm continuing on because at this point I want to see how far this 'game' will go and if there is any real pay-off to all this endless grinding.   TL:DR - Barely recommended for those who want to waste a bunch of time 'exploring' very similar unimaginative planets over and over in a pointless exercise in futility, in a child's view of space and alien life, sort of. Where's the FUN, Hello Games? Never forget THAT'S the point of virtually all games!! (IMO). But there are SOME interesting aspects to what there is of this game. If it had been released as Early Access and price cut in half, the issues would remian, but the overall value would seem much more acceptable.  Steam really needs a Meh... Thumbs Sideways choice - because this game would get that selection from me!  And I really have to comment on those 2 promo videos. Watching them after playing the game, I'm struck by how misleading and phoney they are! They're sped-up or time compressed because those actions shown are basically true, but extremely 'cherry-picked' to make the game seem so much more fast paced and engaging.

This game has my vote for Indie game of the year. The choices you make need to be thought out as some actions have either positive or negitive effects on your suriviors. You start with 2 ~ 4 random people in a shelled out home in the middle of what seems to be a civil war. The games art design is fitting to set the emotion & the somber music helps with this a lot. The game cycles between day ~ night. Day you fix your home ~ build items ~ get rest for the night. Night hits and the fun begins. Pick one of the 3 members to go looting, one to stand guard at home and best to have the 3rd rest for day time activities. Pick what tools you want to bring with you as you go out looting (saw blade~lock pick~crowbar~etc.)    You pick where you want to head off too, sometimes you don't see anyone and looting goes smooth, but what fun is that. Some of the survivors you come across are harmless and are just out looking for surplies like you, others maybe living where you go and you need to decided to loot them or leave and of course there is the chance they are bandits. We all know they just want to sell you the best long distance plan. There is plenty of random events and every time you start over your 3 survivors could all be different, the weather might of changed and I can promise you while the locations on the map might be the same going there will not be. Giving this game plenty of re-play ability.    Average game time is between 30 -50 days, thats right a survival game where there is a goal! Finally you have an objective, outlast the war & win, Don't be fooled this is not an easy game and has the ability to make you care about your survivors wanting to see them live to the end & sometimes having to choose who eats and who don't.    Final verdict 9 ~ 10  The only thing I wish and would make this game better is more of the same type of music, not the same thing on a loop.

Bullet Hell, done well.  If you remember the old stand up arcade games from the 80s-90s - stuff like Twin Cobra, Two Tigers, Sinistar, Sky Shark, Ikari Warriors... you know the basic feel of this game. Four levels of difficulty (and yes: it gets hard, then harder, then absurd) and various vehicles that unlock as you progress (collect in-game coins and spend them on new cars at the garage - all ingame functions), as well as survivor modes for all difficulties, makes this a game with a solid couple of hours of potential, or more.  If you're looking for easy achieves, this has a couple that unlock easily through game play (kill this, beat the game, don't use a certain gun, etc), but some are going to take time (kill 200 bosses, for example).  Considering how many quarters I've pumped into games not half this good over the course of my life, this game is easily worth the cost of a decent pint of beer, and will give you loads more in return.  Well done, Ghost Crab Games!

Beautiful game. Very unique style that, for the most part, works well with the game, conveying cones of vision and cover/shadows well (with some exceptions).  The music sounded really nice, but I can't say I like that way it was implemented into the game. A suddenly epic 'thump' when you get sighted is completely out of touch with the rest of the music, and gets very repetitive as you play along... Didn't help with the flow at all, in my opinion, quite the contrary.  Ok, the game itself. Not a fan either... A big part of that may be because I just decided to try it, without any motivation to do so. That said, what I saw didn't seem particularly impressive or original.   The game itself is solid, mechanically. You have a wide array of different gadgets that I can imagine being put into creative use. Some of them. The biggest problem is how the game compartmentalizes them into sets of levels/obstacles. The game is incredibly linear in 2 different levels: how you simply go through 100 levels, one after another; how each level itself is very linear, consisting of going around it collecting gems to open the exit, and then reaching the exit. There are 'lore' pieces throughout, but these follow the same linear path as the gems do. They don't provide an extra layer of challenge or detours.  Back to the creative use of gadgets. As the levels are very linear, the game gives you some levels with gadget X, and then moves on to Y. Then to Z, etc. They don't really overlap (with very few exceptions). What this ends up meaning, is that some gadgets are essentially redundant. Their functions overlap, with some being objectively better than others in similar functions.  This... is weird, but seems a natural progression for the compartmentalized nature of the levels, I guess. Since each item is isolated, their functional overlap isn't really a problem.  This plays a large part in what we're used to with Stealth games, then? Where you're presented a fairly solid challenge, but then can combine a wide array of tools to overcome it. This to say you expect very different tools and ways to combine them.    The main think I dislike is how linear it is. It's just a set of small challenges. There are no optional objectives (like not getting seen, getting extra data, incriminating someone, etc. etc.), the point is really just following the planned path of each level and moving on to the next one. It made the game incredibly boring, to me.  That said, I think this may appeal to people who are very into stealth games. Assuming you like the game loop a lot, you'll probably want to constrain yourself to never getting seen, completing the levels faster, etc. This is all fine, and I hope this is the real point of the game?  Because otherwise, if you're expecting to be surprised, there isn't much here. The levels are too short, and the enemies too transparent, which creates some silly moments.   Some people called this a puzzle game, more so than a stealth game. But then, you'd expect that most levels required a certain element of 'outside the box' thinking, using the gadgets in unpredictable ways to overcome a particular challenge. And then building on it, etc. etc. But that isn't really the case. The game seems to guide you for the whole set of 100 levels. There might be some slight deviations for each player, but the levels' paths are mostly telegraphed, and don't really require much experimentation and 'aha!' moments.    Anyway.  I don't know, maybe I just wasn't in the right mindset to enjoy this game.   If there's anything to take from here (in my perspective) is the artstyle. That was pretty amazing. Both when levels start and when enemies attack you, the way it affects the geometry looks really great! And, in general, I liked the way the game looked.

I'm going to preface this by saying this game is worth your time and easily worth your money. If I'll pay $8 to watch a 2 hour or less movie, then $10 for a game that took me 7 hours is completely fair. Regardless, let's move onto what worked for me.  The story's pretty grand, and much of the dialogue is silly and humorous. The game does dwell on dark themes from time to time, but mostly this game is a wave of good vibes. I like that you can go through the game as a pacifist, a lot of games could benefit from this design choice. The game even advertises itself as the game where you don't have to kill a single person. There are misleading moments that irked me about this, but it does not waver from that stipulation.   I've been a fan of Toby Fox's music since I've heard his Homestuck tracks, and it's up to the same quality here. Each piece sets the perfect tone for any given scene, and it helped keep me placated during any prolonged treks I had to make in case I missed something or needed to go far back for items.  The combat's, uh, unique. It borrows the Earthbound method of enemies doing strange things in battle that made me chuckle every so often. Unfortunately, here's where my gripes come in. Your options appear like typical rpg fare; fight, act, item, mercy. These are fine, act giving context sensitive options depending on the monster, and you're encouraged to use the right actions to get the monster unwilling to fight so that you can spare them. Alright, sounds good. The part I couldn't stand, though, were the defensive options. At first it was novel, you dodge attacks like a shmup, pretty straight forward. Eventually though, these sections feel tedious because its what you're going to be doing for the bulk of the game, and any wrinkles that show up later show up in the most unfortunate times-- Boss fights.  In most games, boss fights are where the player uses the skills they've accumulated to take on a tough challenge. There might be a curveball here or there, but it's a test of your preexisting skills. Here, they completely change the fundamentals of the game and almost never resurface again. So your boss encounters aren't a culmination of everything you learned, but are just you being thrust headfirst into something new without any warning. It's frustrating and honestly made me dislike a lot of the boss characters because I didn't like their encounters. I'm looking at you, Undyne.   So, ok, at least there'll be a healthy buffer since it's an rpg and you're getting tougher with each fight, yeah? If you're playing how the game wants you to, then tough cookies. You'll never level up, and you'll be stuck with 20 health the entire game, constantly scarfing down healing items on the tougher fights because you can't sustain much damage at all. The core draw of an rpg from a stict gameplay perspective to me is the fact that my character will constantly improve and won't be limited by my own skills to a certain degree. If you're playing pacifist, that draw is gone. Items sort of barely mitigate this, since getting higher defense will do what you imagine, but it doesn't really improve much. If you want the best ending, be prepared to be stuck with 20 health.  Did I mention a best ending just there? This game's made me realize that 'true' or 'best' endings really ♥♥♥♥ me off. If someone told you there was a best ending to a game, then you'd probably want to aim for that, regardless of the personal decisions you'd actually make if you were in this situation. The game stops being about the personal narrative you're crafting for yourself and becomes completely about the developers. You might think, 'Well, duh, of course the developer would want you to experience their story,' but if I wanted to immerse myself in someone else's story, I'd read a book or watch a movie. The magic of video games, especially ones with choices like this, is that they afford for you to give some of your essence to the storytelling, but the existence of a best ending ruins this entirely for me. I didn't make these decisions, a guide did.  And honestly, this game forced me into an unideal situation for that best ending anyway. An early boss encounter explicitly stated to me that talking wouldn't produce any further results, so the only thing I had to go on was to just sit there or to fight. Another rule the game explicitly told me early on was that you could win a fight by wounding the enemy then sparing them. I figured this was the only route I could take, but there's a scripted critical hit at the very end that ruins this attempt, so I killed this enemy. The dumbass solution to this is to sit back for twenty turns and hammer the spare option while you get your ♥♥♥ beat. Like, why provide one solution then turn around and say, 'Nah, changed the rules.' It's gross, I didn't like it, and I can't let go of it. Everyone hates the dreaded 'This fight is unwinnable, you have to lose,' trope in rpgs, and this one dumps it on you in the first hour of the game. ♥♥♥♥ that.  Pretty much that's how every boss encounter goes. Sit there while they say their schtick after you did what little act options you could. For a game with such active defensive measures in place, it felt very unengaging.  I could harp on more about how my last two hours were predominantly occupied with finding an npc to trigger a cutscene that's somehow pivotal to unlocking the last moments of the game, or how this isn't really an rpg but more of a minigame collection with rpg trappings pasted on its antlers by some cruel teenagers, but it's probably in these flaws that make the experience feel that much more real, I guess. You're not going to have a good time convincing people not to murder you, you're going to be bored trekking back to the item shop for some stupid cinnamon bunnies, you're going to be furious at Alphys for constantly taking control away to tell you something inane or end up making you get hit by lasers, but the good stuff is so much more and makes it worth it. You walk past a post and a blind dog freaks out after you stealth pet him. Then there's more dogs and you pet all of them! Or the kid who keeps faceplanting as they run at top speeds and honestly felt like my only actual friend compared to the rest of the cast. Things like this make all the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥t alright, and I can safely say I don't regret playing it.

This game hurts me as a long follower of the OMD! games. I played OMD! 1 and 2 through, even the endless maps ontop, so much fun did i had with the games. But this, this is not the sequel i wanted nor needed.  It looks like OMD! but, it feels so wrong, so very, very wrong. And maybe thanks to Gameforge, did i just now learned about OMD! U. And thank god for that.  Pros: -Interesting Heroes to choose from, once you unlocked them either through grinding or RNG shop with money -All the traps from the earlier games are there! But not all, you have to unlock them again through grinding or RNG shop -The levels and survival maps (runs? Missions? Not sure how to call them) are nice and fitting into OMD! But there are only few now, and the few are getting boring quickly. -Good ol' OMD! humor is inclusive and free! -Ummm...skins for your heroes? No, wait, you have to unlock them through leveling and RNG shop too.  Cons: -Almost all the mentioned points up there -Graphics are looking horrible -Tutorials are stuttering! No joke, my pc is able to run The Division on ultra smoothly, but the tutorials of this game are stuttering for no reason! - GRINDING! Everyone playing this game can assure you, that if you want to play free, you have to grind, ALOT. -Crafting. Yes, the tedious amount of crafting is a con too. You have to craft almost everything in this game. -MOBA. Sadly yes, the MOBA aspect is a con too. Other players with money can unlock early on Heroes, you don't know and know how to fight. Put in the level imbalance and upraded Traps imbalance, that can happen, and your game is over. -It feels like you have to put in cash to get somewhere in the game, into a shop to buy chests with RNG. Great. -As some others mentioned, the Community isn't happy at all about Gameforge and Robot, ignoring and stealing ideas for Patches and more.   Overall, ignore it, or better, play some round first, than get back here and give it a negative Review. This game doesn't have deserved it, to be here on Steam, insulting the OMD! series. Kill this game out of mercy, not out of hate.

Fate of the World is a wonderful game about the possibilities the future might have in store for our planet, including but not limited to, climate change through global warming, oil peak fuel crisis, food and water crisis, nuclear armageddon, species extinction, and the possibility of humankind navigating through all those catastrophic scenarios without dooming itself. Special edition 'tipping point' includes a new denialist scenario where no global warming happens, and a population flood scenario, where population displacement due to climate change is the main issue.  This game is a must have for anyone interested in climate change for both educational value and, most important, entertaining value.

If you want a rich, immersive story-driven experience with amazing soundtrack, this is the game for you.  It's only 4.5 hours and it is best to play it in one sitting to get the most of the immersion, and believe me - once you start it, you will be hooked and you won't want to let it go until the end. The game does a very good job of pulling you in by presenting mysteries and questions early on and keeping the tension up while gradually revealing everything about its plot.  The gameplay is almost non-existent - there is some walking around to get from place to place and some trivial jigsaw puzzles. It chooses to focus on its strong points - the story, the sound, the characters, the dialogue - and that is where it shines.  It has a perfect blend of both lighthearted humor and heartbreaking drama.  A must-play for anyone who enjoys a good story with a very emotional plot.

This game has great nostalgia value as it does the original versions of THPS justice, working with XBOX and PS3 controllers but with a few issues. Sadly it's is more of a Greatest Hits album only featuring a handful of levels from the originals.   A few things that brings this game down in value are that you can't change the key bindings. It's likely possible to change them using a keyboard emulator but it's very much a pain in the behind. Another awful thing about this game is that the menu where you pick which tricks you'd like your skater to be able to execute, it only shows icons for your PC's keyboard and not XBOX or PS3 controller key icons. So if you play using a controller, you have to remember that (, = X) and that(. = Y) and that (/ = B) Confused? Yea that'll happen if you try customize your tricks at all.   In summary, this game is worth $10 if you're willing to deal with the lack of levels and poorly thought out control scheme.

app_name: STAR WARS™ Knights of the Old Republic™ II: The Sith Lords™
review_text: I played through this game the first time several years ago when it first came out, and found the first half of the game to be very fun. It has a very intricate story, excellent plot and character development, and fun gameplay. However, as you progress through the game, the quality really drops off. The story begins to feel very rushed, and some of the plot just doesn't make any sense. The ending was very unsatisfying. I finished the game with a bad aftertaste.  Earlier this year, I heard about the Restored Content Mod that was available and thought I'd give it another playthrough. The mod restores a lot of the content that was originally cut by the original devlopers due to schedule and budget limitations. The second playthrough with the RCM made the game much more satisfying.  Do yourself a favor. Play this game with the RCM. You will not be disappointed.

This game is amazing. It take a couple of minutes to get use to the ships, guns and equipment but after you learn the basics the aerial combat is fantastic. It's slow paced flying until you start shooting at another ship, then things get intense for every crew member. I recommend doing the tutorials before jumping into a game, they're very informative.  If you like team based combat where you get to fly a freaking Zeppelin and shoot people, you will enjoy this game.  My only negative I can say about this game is that it does take some time for a match to start. The small player base means you usually have to wait, but honestly if that's the worst thing you have to do that's pretty good in my book.

Once the dust had settled I tried Oblivion for the first time in close to a decade, and I found a game I really didn't hate anymore.  Oblivion strikes the balance between depth (Morrowind) and playability (Skyrim). It features very well designed quests and far more improved combat. With a world that is full of color, and a wide assortment of locations. From snowy plateaus to sandy beaches filled with twitchy Argonians.  At the same time the game is still flawed. It's a game that hasn't aged very well graphically, and the RPG elements are very hamfisted and lacking. There are thankfully mods that fix all of this, and I don't recommend playing this game vanilla. If you do, all you'll find is a game filled with potato people. One where you are legitimately stronger at the beginning of the game due to how badly coded the levelling system is.  http://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=303467106

Biff Benedict Pocoroba (born July 25, 1953 in Burbank, California) is a former Major League Baseball catcher, who also played a few games at third base. He was drafted by the Atlanta Braves in the 17th round of the 1971 amateur draft.  As a rookie, Pocoroba got attention during spring training in 1975 by throwing out 11 straight would-be base stealers. [1] He made his major-league baseball debut on April 25, 1975, as a defensive replacement in the Braves' 5–3 loss to the San Diego Padres at San Diego.  His best season was in 1977 when he achieved career-highs with 123 games, a Batting average of .290, 8 home runs, 24 doubles, and 44 runs batted in. In 1978, he made the National League All-Star team, finishing the season with a .242 batting average, 6 home runs, and 34 RBI in 92 games. A torn Rotator cuff injury slowed his career [2] and he played his last game on April 20, 1984, at the age of 30.

This game has to be the most fun I have had with a game since  Call of Duty: World at War.  When I first saw it, I expected it to be a run-of-the-mill sort of PvP Free to Play game that is attempting to mirror the style of  Team Fortress 2,   a titan of F2P. But, I gave it a chance and boy was I wrong. This Co-operative space ninja game with out-of-this-world graphics blew me away and I asked my friend that surely they would charge money for this but he said,  'Free forever, forever fun.'    The customization of this is astounding from various weapons, colors, materials, and even warframes (which are utilitzed as weapons too). The game designers also implemented loot rooms in the more difficult 'Void' tilesets that require you to use your Warframe's ability to basically do parkour to go places where you typically wouldn't think. Personally, it feels like if  Mirror's Edge  and  Team Fortress 2  birthed a demigod of a child that had some awesome genetic mutations.   I currently have logged over a  hundred hours  and with the release of Update 11, I'm predicting many more.   My rating?  10/10  for superb graphics, engrossing gameplay, and endless customization.

Now, I originaly wrote this review before I played multiplayer. But let me tell you, Multiplayer is by far the best thing in this game. In multiplayer you scavange for ammuntion for your troops as they will run out quick. Tanks getting blown up left and right. Panacking when you come under fire from an unknown possition. The singleplayer is still boring but the multiplayer makes it worth it's asking price. If you get it for the multiplayer, you get a good game. Get it for the 'story', and good luck finding any story in there.  As much as I want to say that this game is good, I can't. I can't as it feels more like a DLC or an Add-on rather then a new game.  First off the missions are boring and repetitive, they are the same rinse and repeat capture the flag and hold it to get more units. The maps are so small they allow for no tactics (apart from a few defending missions). The best way to win is to get about 4 squads and do a suicide charge. However, The one thing the game gets right is the graphics. I actually enjoyed the game at the start but you will find that that shine quickly wears off.  There is also no story what so ever, unlike in the original Men of War (The best one I think so far, not including Vietnam as I have yet to buy it) you are known as 'major'. After the first mission you are made a colonel.... It is pointless.  To summerise if you love men of war like I do, I think you would like it but there is no replay value at all.  +Units +Graphics +More depth with combat -Stupid and braindead AI -Easy as hell -Turns into a borefest -No story or any characters -No tactics needed  Final Score: 8/10 (Great)

I highly recommend Dungeon of the Endless. The game combines action rpg, tower defense, and resource management elements into a rogue-like structure. It's hard to get a bead on what the gameplay is like from watching trailers. There is a lot of planning that goes into each turn. Then once the turn is executed, you have to manage the waves of enemies in pausable real-time.  At first I felt like I was easily mastering the early levels. Then I realized the game challenged me more than expected. As I play, I continue to discover more nuggets of strategy and unlocked content. Dungeon of the Endless provides a ton to explore, and you find it naturally as you go. I have yet to find tedium in that exploration.  Graphics and sound form a retro sci fi atmosphere that I find a pleasure to spend (a lot of) time in. The style and humor tell me the devs put a lot of love into their project.  I've run into a few minor bugs, but am more than happy to ignore them. I once couldn't get an item to equip or sell, but then it started working later (possibly my misunderstanding). Another time I saw a variable name for a line of dialogue instead of the actual text (dev: it was the mini-story between the doctor and warden).  In a nutshell, you won't regret giving this a try. Play it!

Hyper Light Drifter is a phenomenally pretty action game. The gorgeous artwork combines with the moody soundtrack to evoke senses ranging from awe-inspiring wonder to suffocating menace. Hyper Light Drifter displays a masterful sense of atmosphere that few games match, which in addition to the quick and brutal combat, hightens the excitement of fighting your way through desecrated homelands and ancient battlegrounds. Your repetoire of tools for dealing with the waves of varied enemies in a given encounter is relatively simple; you have your basic three-hit combo of sword atttacks which charge ammunition for your ranged firearms , a purchaseable grenade, and your dash. Waves of melee enemies will attempt to flush you from cover to give ranged attackers a shot at you, forcing you to dodge back into another group of deadly foes. You need to quickly identify who, what, and when to attack. If you make a mistake, you'll likely be punished, and punished severely. As you die and retry, you'll go from reacting to impending threats to conducting the cadence of battle. Combat is less about flashy button combinations and manual dexterity than quick wits, careful positioning, target prioritization, and stricking swiftly when you identify the opportunity. The combat in Hyper Light Drifter is a dangerous and exciting joy. However, murdering your way through the beautiful environments alternates between being the only enjoyable way to interact with the world and being the ONLY way to interact with the world. Exploration involves fighting the game's nigh-useless map in order to reach the next combat encounter until you come face to face with an area's boss on the surface. Delve any deeper, and finding the hidden secrets in the game involves obsessively hugging walls and edges to the point of tedium in the hopes of locating a hidden path or invisible walkway. These secrets can lead to extra health or currency, indecipherable glyphs, a new color scheme for your character, and, if you're lucky, an exciting new encounter to test your combat skills in. Never do you find any narrative payoff. Which is both astoundingly dumbfounding and disappointing. For all the care gone into crafting the world, the game has, literally, NO plot. The events of the opening cinematic, the events you play through in game, and the events revealed once you vanquish the final combat challenge are never connected or given reference in-game. Whatever transpires between starting the game and viewing its credits is entirely dependant on your interpretation of how to connect the vague dots on display. In practice it could mean anything. In effect, it's meaningless. And so Hyper Light Drifter is a phenomenally pretty action game. If you want anything else out of it, be prepared for disappointment.

This game is horribly flawed. Read professional reviews for more information. I'm with the majority of players who are very disappointed that almost the entire game as it was promoted simply was not delivered. Almost every individual part of this game sucks. Trade is boring and unprofitable. Combat is boring and frustrating. Mining is boring and monotonous. 'Exploration' is a joke. You know what you get when you randomly mix all the colors together? A homogenous, oversaturated mess. Screenshots are pretty. Sometimes vistas are pretty. But most planets that you'll visit look pretty much the same. Worse, they all work exactly the same. You're never far from critical resources, so staying alive is more a matter of mastering the IDIOTIC cursor-based inventory system than resource management. 30 hours in, I think I've unlocked just about every blueprint. I've done the Atlas 'story line.' Now I'm heading to the center of the galaxy. Nothing left to do but make enough money to buy a bigger ship so that I can make money and buy bigger ships faster.  OK, OK. So that's a big, disappointed paragraph. A lot of cons. But I still love this game. And the list of pros is short. I enjoy this game's particular resource grind. Mining rocks with my laser (which is the only reasonable way to make money, by the way) while listening to podcasts is peaceful and relaxing.   So I kind of love this game. And I think it's a huge disappointment, especially in the wake of Elite: Dangerous and StarMade. But maybe the existence of those other games that justifies this one. If you want solid combat, you can play Elite: Dangerous or Empire of the Dying Sun. If you want deep or fun trade, try X3 or Elite: Dangerous. If you want deep resource management and crafting, there's StarMade or Minecraft or a plethora of others. If you want a living galaxy with actual planetary physics and more realistic procedural planet generation, Elite: Dangerous is there.  But if you want 18 quintillion suns orbitted* by a handful of mostly same-y planets where you can literally spend as much free time as you want shooting rocks with lasers while things are kinda pretty, this game is solid. Some are holding out hope that Hello Games fixes the game, but I honestly don't see how they can make it much better without changing parts of how generation works, and that'd change the whole universe and wipe out everybody's discoveries. They'll never fix this game in a significant way.  * No Man's Sky has no planetary physics. Hopping around on a planet's surface (all of which have identical gravity), debris tumbling down hills, and rag doll effects for slain creatures are about all the physics you get in this game.

Although There is no Neutral Catagory when Rating Games, My impression of the Mordheim Game Is Neutral. on the one hand It is Challenging and Requires Thinking, LOTS AND LOTS OF THINKING. and can Be Quite Rewarding.  However on the Other hand, Two of the Six Warbands Are DLC and Can be Encountered in Singleplayer; Even if you do not own the DLC, One of those Two Said Warbands is completely Game breaking and overpowered. I am Of Course referring to the Undead. the Undead are the Most Frustratingly Broken Opponents to face, and from my point of view, are so powerful that they make playing the game Rather Pointless. Their Leader Is Almost Unstoppable and their Foot soldiers Far Outstretch Everyone Else. You can Have Good Equipment and Good Strategies, but the Undead are Almost Always Even better.  It is Frustrating and Sometimes even Aggrivating to Encounter these Opponents, as it makes it almost impossible to play the game and NOT get the Pre-shipped DLC that you must pay for before Using. The only way you can Even the Field is with Long Ranged Troopers. and they will Immediately get Run over by the Undead's Footmen. If you are into XCOM or any other Game like it, I would Heavily Warn you before Purchasing this Game.  But Aside from the Undead, the Rest of the Warbands seem to balance each other out, The Human Mercenaries are your Run-of-the-Mill Mercs. who are almost always going to Beat the Rat-Peeps, and the Cultists who are almost always going to lose to the Sisters of Sigmar.  The Witch hunters are another DLC Warband that Comes Pre-Shipped with the Game, and you can Encounter them within the Game even on Single player. I have only encountered them once, So i don't have much of an Opinion on them.

Skinned a bear alive. - 11/10
You Like Nuclear Throne And/or Binding Of Isaac? If You Do I Highly Recommend This Game.

GAMEPLAY 9/10: Enter the Gungeon is a bullet hell shooter. Take Nuclear Throne's fish, put him in Isaacs' dungeons, and add way way more bullets than he usually sees, and you have Enter the Gungeon. This game is a great example of incredible difficulty being enjoyable. Just as Dark Souls was so much fun because you knew that almost any time you die it is your own fault, Enter The Gungeon has that same feeling about it. It is cirtainly not easy, and is still kicking my ♥♥♥ all over, but I can see the progress with every attempt, with every run knowing slightly more and having better moves. If you don't like games that kick your ♥♥♥, and you have to fight tooth and nail to complete the first time, DO NOT get this game. I found The Binding of Isaac like that, and loved it for it, but if that isn't your thing you wont enjoy this. That all said, this game could do with some 'tuning' to make earlier runs better at teaching gameplay techniques, or a practice room ala Crypt of the Necrodancer.  Graphics 10/10: So many games have taken a pixel art aesthetic as a way to cheap out on art assets, but not Gungeon. The graphics have clearly all been hand drawn and animated with amazing detail, charachter, variety, and colour in every pixel. That said, if you cant get past the first floor you'll miss most of the variety, but even then the little touches they've added everywhere are stunning. For example, in the Breech you can see the outline of the skull mouth door cast faintly on the floor, and that attention to detail is everywhere. A lot of Love has clearly been put into the art of this game.  Music 11/10: The music of this game is so good that I'm seriously glad I bought the verson with the OST. In the game, one track is attached to a set floor (mostly), so you need to progress to hear them all, but every track is amazing. Even if you decide not to buy the game because you don't like hard games, do your ears a favor and go get the OST. I've no doubt it will win an award of some type, probably a prestigious one.  Sounds 8/10: The sounds are good, they fit really well in place, but so much so you dont really notice them. They go almost unheard, besides the 'secret room found' tones, as you'll be focused on the amazing music, art, and trying to not die. Absolutely do what is needed, but do not stand out at all. A few of the bullet kin make cute noises though, so thats a plus.  Multiplayer ?/10: I can't rate multiplayer as I don't know anyone patient enough to come and couch coop with me. My brother would probably play if the game had online coop, but not having it I would need someone to visit who is willing to learn all the moves, patterns, items, and guns, its taken me a long time just to get to the end of the fifth floor.  Performance 10/10: A bit difficult to rate any lower, the game runs flawlessly at 60fps on my system, and i've no doubt it would do so on my windows tablet. entirely undemanding and buttery smooth. Unless you installed it on your toaster or telephone, you should be able to run this fine.  Overall 9.5/10: Almost perfect, with the only flaws being its a little too unforgiving to get people into, specially for couch coop being its only multiplayer. However if you're willing to learn this game you'll love it, and it will love you back.

A fun game if you've enjoyed games like EVE, Freelancer, or if you're a long-time fan of the series; Elite: Dangerous offers a totally immersive player-piloted experience in a 1:1 scale Milky Way Galaxy. The learning curve is a bit sharp in the begining, but things start making sense after a little time behind the keyboard. (Or Joystick and Oculous Rift, as this game offers support and encouragement for both.)  The biggest thing that keeps me coming back to Elite: Dangerous is it's insane amount of immersion. If the mention of EVE started to make you groan and have cubicle/accountant-related PTSD, fret not. The main screens are increadibly light and minimal, and your UI is relegated to four main components; two of which are off of the screen until you decide to move your pilot's vision toward them. The rest of the elements on your HUD are very easy to understand and are meant to relay information at a glance. Long story short: It's hard *not* to feel like you're the one sitting in the cockpit at all times. Hell, the game only has one loading screen, and it's right as you start the game.  Weapons and features are well-balanced and much of the 'harder hitting' weapons require a bit of skill to use. Ships are balanced well and all feal a bit different, especially once you begin piloting ships with a lot of mass. The trade-off is pretty expected: lighter ships will offer you greater mobility and speed, while larger ships come with more 'hardpoints' (The ammount of weapon and utility slots you can hold) and internal components, and are much tougher. There is some inbetween of course, as your load-out can easily compensate for any weaknesses you feel you might have (granted you own the credits.)  Now, there are some down-sides, and some might consider them deal-breakers. For one, The game kind of feals incomplete at times. Frontier *did* release an update that makes co-op possible, but it's far from optimized. It's feels a lot less like 'Hey, let's take this mission and kick some ♥♥♥ together' and a lot more like 'Hey, you should come take the same mission as me, and hopefully our separate, similarly named targets are in the same system.' The entire point of a co-op experience should be that the players all feel like they are a part of one, inseparable unite. Elite: Dangerous simply created a way to keep tabs on other players in your squad, and didn't really change any other aspect.  Another issue seems to be the lack of world. There are ways to keep tabs on factions and political parties, but nothing really seems to change while jumping from one system to another. All the factions are somewhat the same in many ways, and you never really become attached to any one faction. I ended up becoming a bit attached to Federal-ruled Kremainn, but most of that is due to the opportunities I've discovered there, and that it's become familiar to me. I must mention, however, that an update has been announced called 'Power Play' that claims to resolve this issue and make you much more a part of the political goings-ons.  As a counter-argument to these issues, Frontier has been more than willing to listen to the community and offer solutions. I feel that the company knows that the game isn't at it's finest yet, and plans to improve until a thoroughly enjoyable gameplay experience can be achieved. As for now? Well, let's just say you'll still find me shootin down wanted criminals and zippin around the galaxy. Laughing. Histerically.  See you later, space cowboy.

A lot of the hate surrounding this game has to do with 'connection issues' or 'worse graphics than advertised.' Does the game have some connection issues? I suppose. I had a brief issue connecting when the game became available at midnight, but have had no problems since. Now, for the 'graphics being worse than what was shown at E3.' The game looks fine. Absolutely fine. Anyone who says otherwise is simply a PC snob who is resposible for console gamers viewing us in a bad light. People also accuse the game of being too repetitive. I honestly think the game is very fun. I love games that have you grind in order to improve a base or something. Darkest Dungeon is an example of this. As you complete missions and gain materials, you can improve your base thus unlocking new abilities and perks. Does the game have very many enemy varients? No, but it's a military game, what do you want? Would you rather it have outlandish enemies like a giant barn? (Looking at you Final Fantasy). Destiny is the same type of game and hardly had more enemies. The only reason it did, is because it is a sci-fi game that takes place on multiple planets or moons. Transitions from the main world and the Dark Zone are seamless. The loot system is decently fair and way more frequent than that seen in Destiny. Is it the best game I've ever played? No, but it definately isn't the worse. To me this game is far superior to Destiny due to its advantage of witnessing a guinea pig. If you like Destiny, get the game. If you like Tom Clancy games, get the game. Co-op fan? Get the game with a friend and show the looters who's boss. Don't let these negative reviews turn you away from an enjoyable co-op experience that has given me nearly eight hours of gameplay and I've only done about a third of the base upgrades. Happy Divisioning...Dividing?...screw it, just have fun.

Love this game! But there are a few things you need to know before buying it:  1. If you wish to have all the content that you may see youtubers (VanossGaming, Double, ect.) you will need to download content packs. Most are free of charge however. Also, if you want to play of most multiplayer servers, then extra textures will be needed for a full. immersive experience. I recomend  {LINK REMOVED}  http://kajar9.wix.com/♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥2#!downloads/chgd  because it has the most reliable content, just don't get tricked by fake ads (Ex. On an adfly page wait for the main timer to go down, then skip the ad. Don't click that fancy ad in the middle of the screen or you will be redirected to download possibly malware or spam).  2. While this isn't intented to be, this game can be very gory and sexual if you dowload a certain content pack or go on a certain multiplayer server. Because there is a very good chance that you may find a server with very few rules on offensive speech or sprays (a spray is a picture someone can 'spray' on any surface) I would suggest to find a good, safe community if you are not up to all that. While there is ussually no boundries to cursing on most servers (except if they are harrasing you with it) then be prepared to hear the F'bomb alot.  3. Although I recomend this game otherwise, I consider this a reasonably T rated game. I won't say that you shouldn't give it to children under 13, (I started when I was 12) but it is what the rating is for and probably don't give it to kids too far below that number (remember the possible gory and sexual content).  Now that you know these things, I would like to talk about the game now:  There are two parts to Garry's Mod. There is the singlepayer sandbox mode (what you may have seen on Youtube) and there is the mutiplayer custom gamemode servers. I will now explain the difference between them:  Singleplayer: Sandbox is a create anything kind of game (with the proper addons LITERALY anything). It at first contains content from Halflife 2 (This was originaly a mod after all) but different addons bring in content from ALL Valve games. Alot of custom sandbox items have been created too and all addons are excepted by the Valve Community. Don't worry about rules against it, you're just modding a mod, there are no rules (not really, but you get it). It is fun for the creative and people who want no limits.  Multiplayer: Servers are a big part of Garry's Mod. They add a bountiful ammount of fun games to play. These games can range from Deathrun to Attack on Titan. I spend alot of time on them as I like a more goal oriented approach (still play tons of sandbox though). But as I said before with the potentially gory and sexual content you wantto find a good, safe community to play on. Here are some things you should look for to decide if it's a good community.  1: Do they have a good and clear rules list/book? If they do not then chaos is sure to be nested in that server. Be sure that you agree with at least most of the rules before joining the server.  2: Do they have a forums/steamgroup? If they do not the they are probably not well organized or not very popular for good reasons. Try to look them up on Google. If they don't pop up on the first page, then you probably shouldn't join them.  3: Is the owner ever on? A good owner plays on or at least visits his server often. If nobody even knows the name of the owner, that is a sign that the moderation of that server isn't the best around.  4: Does the server ever get full? If they do then this is a good sign that they are very popular and ussually you want to join them.  5: Do they ever get DDoSed? DDoSing is when a hacker uses malware to crash the server. While DDoS on it's own is bad, it can show that the server is well known and yes, has some haters. This shows that it is popular/good since the person DDoSing is ussually trying to bring attention to his server by bringing the good ones down.  Now that you know all the content Garry's Mod has to offer, if you decide to buy it, do these things:  1. Download many addons, especially the essentials. Go to  {LINK REMOVED}  http://kajar9.wix.com/♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥2#!downloads/chgd  for those essentials you need on many multiplayer servers.  2. Find a good community. Shoutout to LifePunch, the best Garry's Mod community out there!  http://www.lifepunch.net/forums/   3. Have fun with it. This is not Call of Duty. If you get mad, then take a break and go to a different gamemode or relax with some sandbox. There is no one, set game you have to play. This is a mod containing many creative ways to play.  Hope I helped you out to decide to buy this game. It really is great! Now all that's left is a rating...  10/10 Would play forever!  -Boreal

If you liked Amnesia, you will enjoy this. It's not remarkable or especially innovative in my opinion, but it is still effective. You have stalkers, like Amnesia, but they're varied (like Clocktower 3 or Haunting Ground) instead of just one design; this is a plus. Both the environment and puzzles get a little tedious at times, since you're in a mine pretty much the whole time, but there is enough creepy ambience to make it work.  The plot doesn't really do it for me, but I was never a fan of a lot of post-Amnesia stories (I'm not even a fan of Amnesia's story). The purple-prose, the befuddled young male narrator, the family secret, the crazy experimenter who wants to unlock the potential of this and that - it's all been done. I'd say even without the plot however, it's a decent game.

This is good stuff.

First of all, there's some really bad mouse smoothing or acceleration going on, which took a lot of getting used to and took away much of the enjoyment.  New lighting effects are neat, but make some places feel much darker than they used to be. There are no graphical options so you can't e.g. use 3d rendering without the other visual effects.  Rerecorded lines are very hit and miss, some sound like they should, some sound bored and out of character (especially the 'ugh, where is it').  Developer commentary, which is the main reason I was interested in this game, is really bad. Barely any trivia or insight, just them sounding bored and saying how cool the stuff on the level is. And it's only available on 2-3 levels per episode (except for E5 which is fully commentated and possibly the most interesting one).  And finally, the episode 5 itself. It's good, but not worth more than $5 in my opinion. Introduces only 1 new enemy which is more annoying than anything, setting you on fire, and goes for more modern level design, with very little backtracking. The levels themselves are much larger in size, but they feel like they're borrowing from the modern-retro level design of Painkiller and Serious Sam, with loads of ammo, big arenas connected with corridoors and huge hordes of enemies from the start. Which also means almost no pacing, they're throwing Battlelords at you from the first level. Yes, the episode is harder than the previous ones, but it's not because of smart enemy placement or puzzles to figure out, but the number of enemies thrown at you at once.  Overall I can only recommend this version if you haven't played Duke 3D at all since it's the only version available right now, though you can still find Atomic/Megaton on key reselling sites, which is a much better option.

Having also played and positively reviewed the first Dead Space, I can gladly say that the second game is a brilliant follow up. It begins in the asylum on the  Sprawl , a space station surrounding a shard of Titan, one of Saturn's moons. Isaac Clarke is awakened with no memory of the previous years since  Aegis VII  and with an already ongoing Necromorph outbreak.  This time, the character actually has a voice and faces a true challenge from the very beginning. The gameplay in Dead Space 2 remains pretty much unchanged. Not that much has changed, in fact, apart from the bigger diversity of areas Isaac has to go through, which are not only more interesting but also a lot more colorful. It may even seem awkward to have such bright areas in a horror game but it definitely adds to the tension.  I advise everyone who wants to play this game to go through the previous one first. It is an amazing experience and the story logically advances. The scary bits become predictable at times, once you've played for a while, but that's pretty much the only negative thing I have to say about it.
