Another World (Known as Out of This World in 1991)is one of those nostalgic oddballs that you never expected to be re-drawn. The game puts you in the shoes of a scientist Lester Knight Chaykin. A lightening strike messes up one of his experiments and he is transported to a brutal alien sci-fi world, where he is pushed into fleeing from the alien inhabitants with the help of an unsuspected new friend. This is basically all the game has to offer story wise, but this isn't an issue as the game doesn't need an amazing story for you to stay interested.    The new art style is beautiful. While the game always had a beautiful art style, the 20th anniversary edition cleans up a lot of the pixels and replaces it with jaw dropping smooth backgrounds bringing a breath of fresh air to the cult classic. Although the few characters the game has may seem basic in design, they still offer charm and hold true to the original game. A smart choice the devs of the 20th anniversary edition made, was allowing the players the ability to turn the classic graphics on, for all those that get a nostalgic boner replaying it in its purest form.    Now of course before you go out and buy the game, I must warn you. This game is very veryy hard, and expect to die and die again. Now for this game I would recommend using walkthroughs whenever you get stuck as there is many different paths you can go down that may get you confused and frustrated. Hell even if you use a walkthrough I'm sure you are still going to have a little bit of trouble with this game. Frustration aside, the hard difficulty is very rewarding whenever you finish a puzzle, and it won't feel like you've wasted your time when you reach the end of this journey. With a walkthrough you should be able to finish the game in around 1 hour (Unfortunately it is quite short) and I imagine without a walkthrough the game would last you maybe a couple more hours... Maybe. But to me the game is worth it.

F2P spiritual successor to Diablo 2. This game is everything that Diablo 3 should have been. The microtransactions are, for the most part, purely cosmetic. You can buy stash tabs which increase your storage capability. This should be unncessary for most players, with the exception of those who like to hoard and trade a lot.  Speaking of trading, the game's economy is dynamic, following the supply and demand of different crafting items. Certain crafting items fall into and out of favor especially as temporary leagues are added and mature.  All in all, if you're willing to climb the experience curve (the passive tree can be very daunting), the game is very rewarding and definitely worth your time. I find myself coming back to PoE time and time again as the new leagues are released.

Neat game, I engaged with it enough to ponder the decisions with actual ethics, and gave the climactic choice a good deal of consideration, not every game can pull that off. If you're the sort to pay attention to the mechanics of the 'branching' aspect of the story, the way it works here will constantly break immersion because you're really just funneled in one direction with minor callbacks to past decisions, but it's all internally consistant and works really well if you don't mull it over. Since this already has 17k reviews, I'll take the rest of this space to discuss the game's core mechanic.  I've seen people drag this game while discussing Undertale and this highlights the philosophy of the 'decisions' you make here. There's one path with some variable details, as opposed to Undertale and it's 2.5 wildly divergent routes. That's probably a good way to structure a game like this given the quality of the animation, but it might be a turn-off to some. I'd call this an upgraded visual novel where you get to make decisions you feel good about, and it executes that experience very well. If real branching is more important than shiny animations, check out Undertale and it's ilk, there's a reason they diverge a lot more than, say, Dishonored.

This is exactly the kind of game that makes me love Steam so much! The game's mechanics are simple, clever, and flawlessly executed... The art, music, and little touches (beautiful quotes at the beginning of each race) make this game a polished masterpiece. Race the Sun is a game that is also art; beautifully composed, full of nuance and hidden meaning. Even if the game didn't have much replay value (it does!!!), I would have purchased it just to support the people who made it, as well as proudly display in my personal collection of games.  The game plays a lot like a simplified F-Zero; it's fast-paced racing, and uses only 3 buttons; left, right, and jump (when you have jumps stored; they're a consumable item). The pilot races towards the sun on a solar-powered craft, which sounds terribly boring, but is executed so well it astonishes me. The sun starts high in the sky, and not-so-slowly dips below the horizon, which invariably brings about your demise, and is the source of all the game mechanics, as well as the beauty of the game. You ship slows down every time you hit an obstacle, or are caught in the shadow of one of the many obstacles to be avoided, and can be sped up by staying in the sunlight, hitting sunlight boosters, and possibly by hitting little white panels on the ground (I haven't played enough to be certain). One of the really cool aspects of the game is that the lower the sun goes on the horizon, the longer the shadows are cast by your obstacles, which slows your craft down, adding more danger, and more immediacy to your doom! The inverse is true of hitting boosts; your craft speeds up so much that it makes the sun RISE again, because you're moving faster than the rotation of the planet you're racing on... how freaking cool is that? Others have erroneously said that the boosters reverse time; this is not the case, the truth is much cooler, and much more insightful; kudos again to the dev team!  The mechanic I think is one of the most impressive plays on the impermanence of life, and the theme of the sun and the day's passing. There are no levels in RtS; every 24 hours, an entirely new track is generated, and just like IRL, yesterday can never be repeated, and we must constantly look forward. That's way more interesting, and deeply philosophical than I expected out of a minimalistic racing game! Because of the ever-changing nature of the tracks, you'll be tempted to get the most out of one you like, because soon, it will be forever gone, but the joy of discovering tomorrow's path is just as enticing... so profound, so cool!  Each race starts with an interesting quote, taken from a variety of sources, with some fun shout-outs to dev team members and friends... this is completely unneeded, but adds to the gameplay experience a lot, IMO. Crafts are somewhat customizable as one levels up, and the challenges also add variety to gameplay. I really like how said challenges aren't progressively more difficult (and more grindy-repetetive, like most challenges become), they're just different; some are easy, some are hard, but they always give you something new to do.  This is an absolute must-but for anyone who even remotely likes racing games, and definitely those who collect videogames as art! I'm proud to have this one in my collection, and you should be, too!

The main point why i started playing again is because it is for free, so my friends started playing and i can teach em almost everything. This was definitely the right decision. ATM the new people say that the game is unbalanced ot stuff like that, but this is mainly because they stick together waaaay to close at stage 1 (because they dont know the game/abilities of the monsters), making it easy for the monster to win at stage 1. So with some playtime i hope and think people with love the game as much as i did after the first release.  My advice to people: give the game some time to get to know the maps and hunters/monsters!! because if you know some sneaky hiding spots, there is nothing funnier then juking the hunters by hiding somewhere they'll never expect!!  So atm i rate 7 /10 for this game, simply because they need to add more maps and game modes, then i am fine and will rate 10/10. (Read update at bottom)  This game is a good game, it's just not for everyone. It looks and feels like an indie game, so if you don't like indie games, don't buy No man's Sky. It's also grindy. Not 'Grindy RPG' grindy, it's more like Minecraft grindy. So if you don't like grindy games, don't get No Man's Sky. This game is $60. If you can't afford this game for $60, don't get No Man's Sky. But I do think that the $60 is worth it.  If you do like grindy indie games then continue reading! Okay, this game is amazing. Most of the planets in this game look ugly and are barren. But that only makes it all the more better when you see a vibrant looking planet.  Also, there is more to do than I originally anticipated. I was originally going to just go to the center of ther galaxy, as you're supposed to do in the game. Then the Atlas told me to go to it. And without thinking I did. And I kept doing as the Atlas told me, and I still am. After each task you do for the Atlas you get    KNOWLEGE(learn alien language) and the Atlas also gives you some cool space time stuff to think about   . So I have been playing for 15.6 hours as of typing this and haven't even started to do what I'm supposed to, yet I've still been having tons of fun.  I'd just like to compliment the soundtrack for the game as well. If you like progressing you should get this too. No Man's Sky is based around not only exploring, but also upgrading your multitool and starship and yourself. You can upgrade your ships weapons to shoot faster or do more damage, or upgrade it to consume less fuel. Or you can upgrade your multitool to mine faster or do more damage. Or you can upgrade your suit thingy to breath underwater longer and be more durable in general. Upgrades to your multitool, starship and yourself all take inventory slots, so that will effect how your game will be played. Also you unlock milestones to help track your progress as well.   Also, if you're considering buying this game, and I assume you are if you're reading the reveiws, don't base your decision on the total of good/vs bad reveiws. Read through them and make sure that the reveiws arn't rated bad simply because the game is $60.  9/10  (UPDATE)  This game is still everything I expected it to be. But, it's not that great. It's OKAY. The game does get repetitive, after you've neen everything. But that does take a while. But after that while passes, the game is boreing. Long story short, it's a rather short game. Every game eventually gets boreing after a while, but this one faster than most. I'm still going to leave a 'recommended' on this game, because this game made me feel anticipation that I haven't felt in my whole life up until a few months ago.  5/10 - Don't buy this game for $60

If you're of a certain age, then the name Geoff Minter and Llamasoft with bring back memories of bonkers games played on the Commodore Vic20, C64 and beyond. Full of sheep, graphics that induce LSD type trances and later, hypnotic trance music, Llamasoft games are an experience beyond just merely shooting things, you find youself zoning out and playing with your subconscious. Gridrunner Revolution is classic Llamasoft at its best and the bonus Vic20 mode unlocked after completing the first level set (all levels named after increasingly strong curries) will take you back to your misplaced youth.  If you grew up playing Playstation or Xbox games, then there will be a huge dose of WTF and this game will probably not be for you. Go search youtube for some gameplay videos if you are unsure. If you want a surreal, hypnotic blaster, then this is money well spent.

Take note of the difficulty of the area in the overworld your exploring (top of the map) trivial - mundane - dangerous, you dont want to be exploring the overworld and have a random dangerous battle appear that you cant avoid. Dont forget to hand in all your legendary artifcats / books and battle skills to the headmaster and then spend you skill points. Use scrolls to escape dangerous situations or spikes to block doors to run away (especially from them white rabbits) Enemy champions are highlighted by a glowing red. Until your powerful enough dont take on hall of champions, monster party portals that will randomly appear, portal of hives however, is a great boost if you have the money.  There are many more I could offer but just go purchase the game and find out for yourself, definitely recommend this especially when on sale!.

While I can see some appeal here in the attempt to tell a good story, and the attempt to look pretty in the process, the constant crawling pace, combined with a mash of low-res textures, buggy movement/jumping, and absolutely no guidance as to what's happening, why, and what you should do about it, Toren reaches for the stars and falls down into the abyss in almost every aspect with the only exception being it's fantastic modelling.  Toren is an abstract, 3D story game, with about 2 hours of game play if you don't throw down your controller (or moon forbid, using a keyboard and mouse) in frustration from missing a jump because the camera angle decided to wildly change mid-air, or you miss safety by a hairs-breath and the game forces you to go back 2-3min of slow backtracking.  If the movement speed was increased, and cutscenes were skippable, you could probably finish this game in about 20minutes without trying too hard. Alas, even the invisible ball-and-chain wrapped around Toren's ankle doesn't provide much for gameplay like many other story-based platformers. All you have to soak in is moving objects clipping each other, blurry rocks next to highly detailed stairs, while your camera spins jumps and judders going from too-slow to too-fast in a few seconds.  Perhaps if this game had a team of people building it and some sort of quality control, instead of seeming like one art student's University project, it might have been better. Oh wait. It did. Then how did _this_ ship?

Belladonna is a strange game, although at about an hour of play time one should really call it a 'short'. It assumes the guise of a typical point-and-click adventure game, but the puzzles are much too easy and I spent as much time reading the in-game 'letters' (journal pages) as I did playing the game. I guess it's best described as a cross between a visual novella and a short adventure game. Regardless of how one might best categorise it, I found it somewhat lacking at the end, and it left me thinking, 'Now what?'  The plot is interesting, very much like Frankenstein, as you will already have gathered from the Steam game page's description, but isn't followed through to a satisfying resolution. It's almost as if the developers ran out of time and finished it before they'd really got into their stride. Certainly in the short timeframe in which you'll play the game -- it took me 67 minutes to complete the game; the rest of my Steam-reported game time is idling for the remainder of the trading cards -- there's no opportunity for in-depth character development or anything but the most obvious plotline.  The graphics are good: cartoony, with an attractive visual style reminiscent of many point-and-click adventure games. The main character is reasonably well animated and the title screen is particularly attractive. The few still images present in the game are well done as well.  Sound is so-so. Music is good and sets the mood for the game. Voices are not so good. Or rather, they're okay, but don't fit the characters at all well. The main character sounds like a well-spoken, but somewhat pretentious American teen, whereas the other voice (yes, there are only two in the game) is a bit mumbly and not so well spoken. Given the roles they are meant to represent, the voices really don't fit.  So, overall, I was disappointed by Belladonna. I had expected more than what I received, but what was there was, on the whole, pretty good. It's just a shame there wasn't more. It doesn't really offer anything new, but it is quite nice all the same.  I recommend it, but with the caveat that you should take into account the very short play time.

I liked Limbo, I really did. It didn't need much of a story, or any real fancy gameplay dynamic. Just some nice art style involving silhouettes and simple black/white backgrounds. It can be described as having a minimalistic art style. Antenna does the same, though with far less content.  Personally, I looked a little deeper into what this game is about. The description for the game: 'A machine ponders its loneliness. It scans the radio spectrum for an answer to its question.' I think a little imagination is needed to fully encapsulate what this game is about and what it attempts to achieve. The robotic creature scuttling along seems to act as a sort of caretaker for the structure which the game revolves around. You control the creature to raise radio antennae and activate generators, to power up/turn on parts of the structure and to strengthen the radio waves needed to access the underground area and progress.  The games' greatest flaw is definitely the lack of content. Plenty more is need. However the content that is there is good. The puzzles were fun and I'm sure a story could have been made to accompany extended gameplay to give this game more than half an hours worth of gameplay. But for what its worth, I enjoyed it. For a free, short game, you can't go wrong and I just don't see why people write negative reviews when this game didn't relieve them of a single cent/rupee/rand/ruble/peso etcetera, and didn't take much of their time. As for unique, I dunno man. If you're after a unique game these days, you're in for a struggle. But I suppose there will always be whiners and whingers.

BioShock Infinite had big boots to fill. Following one of the greatest games of all time (and one quite ok one), Irrational Games has done it yet again. I am not going to try to explain the story, because A) I am still scratching my head and thinking it over, and B) it would be an injustice for anyone who hasn't played it to spoil it for them. After having said that, I can assure you, that the story is top-notch, and very engaging. Though there was no twist with the impact of 'Would You Kindly....' (or any at all), I couldn't wait to see what would happen to Elizabeth and Booker, expertly voiced by Courtnee Draper and Troy Baker respectvely. The world of Columbia is a beautifully dark and evil place, which the story enforces. I kept getting pulled deeper and deeper in, just to see and learn more of Columbia.  Game play wise, I have only a few small complaints. I missed the weapon wheel from the original, and 2. In Infinite, you are relegated to only two primary weapons. This saddened me, because I felt that there were a few times where my current weapon might as well be a butter knife for it did to help me. Also, BioShock, though a very, very good game, isn't the strongest FPS there is. The controls did feel clunky, and slow to me. Combat is in my opinion very well balanced, and just challenging enough on all difficulties.  The game looks ABSOLUTELY stunning on PC. It ran very smoothly, and had no hiccups whatsoever (I played the game on High, and sometimes put it to very High for kicks).   The music is also top-notch. I have listened to the soundtrack multiple times already, and it is one of my all-time favorites. Though there is no incredibly sad and emotional piece, such as BioShock 1's 'Ocean On His Shoulders'/'Empty Houses', it is still very engaging, and fits the world incredibly well. The piece 'Baptism', still brings chills to me every time I listen to it.  All in all, should you get this game? Abso-fricking-lutely! There is no finer writing in any game on the current generation, or the next, and the game play doesn't need any help from it at all, standing on its own very well (except for the few complaints, above). Pure perfection!  10/10

Please forgive me if this review is nothing but gushing, but this game deserves to be gushed about. It really, really does.  Never before have I played a game with such satisfying combat. It’s fluid and responsive, perfectly balanced with the story and all round great. In fact, that's something this game has over most video games ever. Somehow Supergiant Games have been able to perfectly balance story and combat in a way that neither disturbs or lessens the other. Both compliment each other wonderfully.  The art style as well is wonderful. A beautiful, watercolour artstyle, like pictures in a storybook, it suits the storybook style story that the game utilises. The game is told as if it was a bedtime story, with the narrator recalling the days after the calamity that has pretty much destroyed the world in which our main character, “The Kid”, inhabits. Many parts of the world are now suspended in the air, and build themselves as you walk across them (it makes sense in context).  The story uses one of the most effective means of telling a video game story at this point: narration only. Other games such as Thomas Was Alone did the same, and like TWA, Bastion doesn't let the fact that is doesn't have a full voice cast make it a lesser experience. Instead, it uses it to its advantage. Bastion only needs one voice actor, the rich-voiced narrator, who speaks his lines with such laid-back brilliance. The actor does an incredible job. And the narration responds to nearly everything you do. Every little action, every weapon choice, iof you break more boxes than normal, etc etc etc; whatever happens, the both humourous and compelling voice of the narrator will respond to it. It almost felt reasurring; that in this bizarre post-apocalyptic world there is still a constant companion for the Kid.  That’s not to say that the game is faultless, after all, what game really is? The bird enemies that appear at occasions are rather frustrating to deal with, and some of the checkpoints are poorly placed indeed.. But these are but nitpicks in the grand scheme of things, and after all, the rest of the game proves so enjoyable, fluid and engaging, both in story and game mechanics, that those quibbles matter not. Bastion is most certainly worth its price tag.

This movie is truly incredible. I'm a huge fan of both documentaries and indie gaming so when this became availible as part of the Humble Bundle it had me reaching for the ol' bankcard before I'd even seen the other games included. I've always been interested into the development aspect behind games but hadn't really researched it in any depth and aside from the childhood dreams of one day making games for a living I really didn't know much about development. This movie interviews some of the most prominent people in the Indie Game movement there's the creator of Braid, the creators of Super Meat Boy and the creator of the long awaited title Fez. It shows the sheer turmoil these men put themselves through in-terms of working ludicrously long hours trying to create the next build of their game or even just trying to squash bugs that have been putting their stress levels at all an all time high for months. This documentary had me totally captivated from start to finish and I highly recommend you give it a watch.

The horde-type bonus challenges are neat, and I like the little option to customize palettes for your troops. Makes it easier to tell at a glance who is who, which actually matters since you can level and spend skill points differently making even identical character classes possibly very different in combat. Even paying full price for this would probably be worth it. I can think of PLENTY of expensive triple-A games that I didn't play for almost 50 hours, or get nearly as much pleasure out of playing.

Evoland is silly nostalgic trip to anyone that has played RPGs but mainly Final Fantasy. Evoland puts you on a quest to save Evoland and you along the way you unlock tons of stuff by opening chests, it can be higher quality soundtrac, the key to move left, 3D graphics, pixelated textures, you name it, it can be pretty much anything a game needs in order to be a game, if that makes any sense whatsoever. Speaking of which, the soundtrack is amazing, specially the turn based battle theme along with the victory theme which remind me alot of Final Fantasy VII. Let's be real though, the combat is pretty clunky and you might get annoyed due to the fact that when you're travelling you get a battle every 5 seconds. Evoland is not a really good game the way I see it but the way it was executed based on the 'unlocking stuff mechanic' (excuse me for my english) and the nostalgia that it brought to me I think that's all it needs to redeem itself and be worth the time to play it, it's a fairly short game, 3-4 hours. That said, if you're a fan of RPGs and have been playing those for a few years you should totally play Evoland, you might like it and you may not still, I think it's worth the risk.

Let me say that while this says I have only 20-something hours on record, I've been playing it for months and months before it came to steam.  First of all, I've invested hundreds into this game. DO NOT DO THIS. I bought the bloody eagles to convert to experience for my flight crews. Leveling them up creates no visible advantage over another player. You're essentially wasting your eagles. Buying the upgrades creates no visible advantage, with the exception of more guns, different ammunition and more bombs. Since the 1.37 patch, not only is your eagle now less valuable than a peso, you get far less return from them, which is more like a great big 'PlSS OFF' from Gaijin.  The new leveling system is the exact opposite from what they promised. Instead of making leveling up easier, they've placed all the aircraft into 5 tiers now, and you must now research each plane individually, instead of just having access to purchase the planes you level into. But don't fret: After you research the planes and parts, you still have to buy them. This has slown the previously percieved grind into the equivalent of pusing a car uphill. While in park. And with all flat tires. With six Ralphie May's sitting in it. And three dead bodies in the trunk.  COMMUNICATION. This is key to maintaining teamwork and winning any engagement. However, when you are in the middle of a dogfight, this is impossible, as they have STILL NOT IMPLIMENTED any form of voice communication. Typing in the middle of a dogfight equals death. Hence the term 'typekill'.  PLANES. Gorgeous planes. Beautiful selection(When you finally unlock one). However, most of the battles boil down to who has the most bombers. Not really much balance in that aspect.  This game is like a beautiful woman: No matter how pretty she is, someone somewhere is tired of her ♥♥♥♥.   Final Straw: After engaging in a conversation in the game's chat rooms with others, expressing our disdain for how abyssmal the grind and lack of true dogfighting this game contains, and how this game feels more like a chore now than an actual form of entertainment, I have been banned from chat for the offense of, and I'm quoting, 'Insulting the developers.'   UPDATE: So at the beckoning of a few fellow gamers, I reinstalled the game to test out the much anticipated tanks. Someone had mentioned in reviews that the simulator battles in the flying part of the game were broken, because they would go on for long periods at a time, and would almost exclusively be Germany vs Russia. Well, the only tanks available are from either Germany or Russia. Tested the tanks. The T50 is by far the most overpowered tank in Tier I vehicles. Almost completely covered in 40MM armor, there are only a few tanks that can actually penetrate the armor, let along damage the tank. The T50 belongs in Tier II at the earliest in the game, and if you find yourself in a match against three of them, consider the match lost. Don't get me started on that goddam ZIS - 30. Three DIRECT hits on one of the most lightly armored vehicles in the game, and it still won't die. Can't say I should really be surprised, that Russian tanks in a game run by Russian developers would be so strong.

app_name: The Legend of Heroes: Trails in the Sky SC
review_text: In Chapter 4, there is a segment of the story where, if you choose to visit the general store, you can spend a little bit of money to buy chocolate for your party members that have been staying up patrolling the whole night. This action does not change the course of the main story or unlock additional sidequests or equipment. It provides absolutely no benefit other than a couple of lines of dialogue.  It is the little details like these, put into the game with the hearts of the developers, that are what makes Trails in the Sky SC amazing.  Trails in the Sky is not an open world sandbox game, but the amount of effort put into the world and characters of the story is simply astounding. Your party members are a vibrant, well-developed cast of some of the most memorable characters you will meet, and a great deal of thought was put into each and every line of dialogue as the characters interact with each other, bringing their personalities to life. The NPCs which inhabit the cities and villages of Liberl are also lavished upon the same treatment, with almost all the NPCs having unique names and distinct personalities, down to even the airship crew and shop owners. And given that all of the NPC dialogue changes after each segment of the main story, you'll also be able to follow the little happenings in their daily lives. Yes that's right---most NPCs in the game have their own mini-storylines, a few which will tie into your Bracer Guild sidequests, but most which are just their to simply add life to the world.   My favourite of these even spans multiple regions and games, involving the grandma of Rinon, your Rolent General Store owner, who wants to find a wife for her son. She eventually gives up on Rolent and travels to Grancel at the end of FC, to no success. On the airship back though, she meets Kitty, a tea seller girl from Edel Department store, who is on the way to Bose, and gets along quite well with her (while her sister rejoices at being able to take over as #1 tea seller). Well, thanks to the airships being grounded, Kitty ends up working at the store in Rolent, and Rinon while suspicious of his grandma, can't help but admire the girl's enthusiasm and hard work. Later, in Chapter 8 the wedding of a couple (whose story began in FC, and whose ring you retrieve on a sidequest in Chapter 4) takes place, and well, no points for guessing who catches the bridal bouquet.   To anyone who loves a sprawling world with many secrets and lifelike characters, I can't recommend Trails in the Sky more.

I also did a video review of this game on my retro YouTube channel.   DotEmu has done other great retro compilations before like the fantastic Raiden Legacy but this package (which was ported over from mobile phones) is lazy and does badly by the legacy of Double Dragon. The UI of the first two games has been replaced by an ugly version that I assume was designed for scalability on mobile phones and it looks terrible. The games are artificially sped up to compensate for the slowdown that was normal in the arcade versions and you can't toggle that off to play the original way. The original music is not emulated properly and the available re-mix music is awful and sounds like it was done on a mid-90s MIDI keyboard. They also removed any ability to coin up which breaks Double Dragon 3 as that game uses additional credits as a means to buy powerups.  There are modern resolution options, local and online multiplayer (though  no one  is playing this online and you can rebind all your controls, though the Xbox 360 controller buttons are not properly represented in-game.  If you just want to play Double Dragon and aren't a retro enthusiast who wants the original games properly preserved, this is OK I guess. However, if you are someone who at least wants the original games as an option and thinks original preservation of retro titles is important, this is a poor choice and DotEmu should have done better.

While Bit Dungeon II appears to have the substance of innovative game design, it deflates almost immediately into a repetitious and uninventive experience. Artistic direction is consistent and gorgeous, with beautifully illustrated environments and visually unique bosses and enemies. Unfortunately the music does little to add to the haunting visuals, so the sense of ambiance is lost fairly quickly.   Despite having several types of weaponry and various stats and effects, each playstyle archetype essentially boils down to button mashing. Combined with the completely random loot generation which seems to provide both the most basic and the most overpowered equipment with equal fervor, the player will most likely find themselves effortlessly steamrolling over even the more powerful bosses. Of course, some bosses will also utterly destroy the player, and the enemy difficulty scaling and tiny level screens mean that neither of these problems can be fixed through any player-implemented strategy.  For a gamer who loves effortless grinding for loot drops which are few and far between, this game might serve as a suitable form of 'click to win' relaxation. There are also a few mechanics for which the developers can be commended. Still, the content does not justify the price of what is essentially an empty shell of a game.

The best recommendation I can give for this game: This will quickly become my most played game.  From the Atari 2600 to today I have always been a fan of games. In particular I love MMOrpgs and Rpg's as they tend to offer some of the best value (Money invested into hours of fun played). This game has elements of Raiding, micro and macro management with a nice level up system. There are three ways to play this game: Active (You click a lot or use a clicking program), Inactive (You let burn damage kill creeps) and hybrid (Inactive on mobs, active on bosses). If you like a game you can get satisfaction from while multitasking, watching tv etc etc then this is possibly the best game you have never tried. The game is cute and satisfying as you make deeper and deeper runs. The game uses a soft reset mechanic where you go as deep as you can (As fast as you can) then reset and repeat with bonuses you can from varius level ups. The best thing about this game is it is 100% FREE and you get rubies (In game currency you can pay for but do not need to at all) for buffs VERY fast. The best way I can describe this game is a Raid game like wow mixed with a monster mashing slasher. Without the aggrivaiton of playing with others =). Even the guild system ONLY helps you never causing headaches. If you are on the fence about this game I hope this review helps.

This game makes you remember what old school JRPGs used to be, Hard and sometimes frustrating but once you manage to go forward past these parts you really feel like you have accomplished a quest in real life ;D .. the battle System is based on Xenogears for the combos and Suikoden for the MP system, two of the best BS ever created in one :D .. the story is very interesting besides an initial feeling of being rushed in a world setting that isn't explained enough, this first part is a prologue and I managed to finish it taking my time in almost 8 hours but the game as it is should be beatable under 6 hours, but the devs took their time in creating complex areas and dungeons that are WORTH to be explored to find rare items and gears so you should do so. Another thing I liked is the fact that the choices that you do will have an impact on the future chapters, creating rivals or changing the monsters that populate an area... a nice touch by the devs was also putting some optional bosses that is not possible to beat right now besides the fact that you can actually challenge them... it makes you understand that it will be worth revisiting some areas and so some backtracking ... I hope the next chapters come out as soon as possible because as it is it feels a bit of an incomplete experience.   PRO  - Above averange difficculty - Deep battle system - Worth to take your time exporing the maps - Very good graphic and music to be an RPG MAKER game - Interesting world setting and charaters - Good voiceovers  CONS  - Too short as it is now - Some boss fights should be better balanced - The movies are not subtitled, this makes a bit difficcult to understand some lines.

Undertale is magnificent gift, it is hands down my favorite game of 2015 and Honestly probably my favorite of all time, this is one of the greatest experiences I've had in a very long time. Toby put a lot of love and care into this game and it really shows, I have never been so invested in a story and it's characters in my entire life. Undertale is definitely a game I'm going to continue coming back to every now and then just to revisit all the characters and places I love.  Narratively this game is a paragon of interactive story telling. Every choice you make weighs on your conscious because every connection you make with the characters feels organic. Every character and their dialogue are all so charming it's hard to dislike any of them, even the background and non story important folk have a lot of character to them, the art style while simple is appealing and fit the world nicely. Don't get me started on the sick jams the music is perfectly composed, every part of the soundtrack fits the moment flawlessly really helping bring out the emotion in the scene. I could listen to the OST all day and have and continue to listen to this magnificent soundtrack for years to come.  An RPG where playstyle actually changes the way the story shapes up, depending on whether you go down a pacifist, nuetral or genocidal path, characters, dialogue and places change A LOT, allowing for several playthroughs if so desired. Befriend even your enemies by choosing to just show a little mercy or murder everything if you so desire these options take you on completely different stories. Toby thought of every contingency when it comes to decision making, almost everything has some sort of different dialogue depending on what you do, or don't do.  I wanted to add on some things since I didn't talk about game mechanics: •One warning I would give is that this is NOT a traditional RPG, that term is kind of just sprinkled ontop of the game, the defensive combat mechanic during the entire game is an AVOIDANCE / BULLET HELL type of mechanic and is always in use, so if you're not fond of that type of gameplay or just not very good at it you're better off watching a playthrough. •Linear, if you have a problem with games being linear than yeah that's a thing I guess? I don't count linear as a negative if it's done right. •Some jokes/gags do run on a bit, some characters can be a bit two dimensional with their personality (on a surface level, there's more to them if you play down certain paths), I felt it was written well and honestly I liked them through out the entire game. •Playing the game completely blind is honestly the best way to play the game, not knowing the characters and locations makes everything so much better. •This game isn't for emotionally detached edgelords either, it's a touchy feelsy type of story.    TL;DR: I legitimately have nothing negative to say about Undertale, I have never been so enchanted and enamored by ANYTHING in a very long time. I think about this game every single day, I listen to the music every day, I've lost sleep over how much I love this game and I want to cry. 11/10 Would true reset my mind to experience it all again.  Gonna go lay down while I continue to experience an existential crisis.  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 2016 notes  After playing this game a year later, on the anniversary of its release date, I can honestly say I still feel the same way about it. It's still a really, really good game with fun characters and dialogue, amazing music and a nice story with interesting game mechanics. It's a shame this game gets such a bad reputation because of a lot of rabid fans and its popularity on the internet. Allowing otheres to ruin something beautiful for you is a real shame.   I feel bad for those that didn't get to experience pure, unspoiled, completely blind not knowing any of the characters or music. That experience was so amazing, seeing and learning everything the first time without prior knowledge was well... Magical. Going in knowing nothing was honestly life changing for me, never being able to experience that again is heart wrenching, I truly wish I could.  Even now the game still brings out beautiful emotions other games and media fail to bring out and just makes me really think and want to be a better person. Thank you for everything Toby Fox, you've really inspired me to be more DETERMINED, I can't wait to see what you do next friend!

Great mix of sim style and RTS gameplay. The atmosphere created via visual and musics will set you to a correct mindset.  The economy chain is a lot fun to create and watch. I can explain what I mean by this with a quick example. You create a farm which yields wheat. Then your villagers takes that and proccess it in to flour which then taken by bakers and turn it in to bread for your granary. There are a lot of cooperational buildings that you can construct this way and each have different direct impact or indirect impact on your society. Building an economic balance between happiness/production and army is key to success in this game and there are a lot of different play styles which you can achieve it. I have already played and squeezed all the fun out of Stronghold way before I had a Steam account, but this is a gem that must sit on every gamer's library.

I'm a fan of Bossrush games, but this one just has too many annoyances in it to be recommended.  The controls are kinda clunky sometimes, which is especially bad, since this game demands quick reflexes and high precision inputs in about half of what it wants you to do. The simple fact that you can't even dodge on button press (rather it's on release, cause in theory you can charge it) makes me wonder if the dev ever had other people playing the game, cause most of the time you won't be charging your dodge, but rather want to quickly evade something. Then about a third of the time it won't work, cause you're just those few frames toos low to release the button.  Another problem is the sometimes really precise directional input it demands when dodging. Since you always dodge a minimum distance, sometimes it's hard to (practically) impossible to dodge an area attack just by where you accidentally stand when the enemy starts it.  Most of the games challenges come down to simple pattern memorization, there's hardly any actualy skill involved (imo), cause the first time you see a new pattern the controls are too bad to really deal with it on reflex, and after a few tries you know exactly what's coming and can avoid it easily most of the time.  The enemies are divided on phases, which is ncie, also, cause if you die and lose a life you start over at that phase and not the beginning of the fight. On the other hand, if you lose all your (3) life you DO have to start at the beginning of a fight. While this may sound reasonable in theory, in practice what ends up happening is, you get one or two phases further in a bossfight, then lose all life (cause you don't know the pattern yet) then do it all over. Since the hardest phases are towards the end, you end up wasting a lot of time doing the earlier phases over and over.  All in all I'm not sure why that's in the game like that. It seems more like artificially increasing playtime than anything else really, since once you understand how to deal with a phase it's not that interesting anymore (safe maybe for postgame speedrunning, but you won't do that in the first palythrough anyway). Combine that with another problem, since most moves are similar (especially in meelee range) but always have some small twist, you end up misremembering sometime (oh, was that the phase where he hits 3 times instead of 2 before you can hit him? dang)  I've heard people say the game is fair cause the moves are telegraphed, I'm not sure I'd agree with that. The meelee (parryable) moves all have a white blink as a tell, but since it happens so quickly before the actualy execution, you won't be able to do it on reflex anyway (i.e. if you don't know the combo is a 6 hit instead of a 2 hit there's no way you gonna parry on reflex just cause there was a tell, thus making it another pattern memorization exercise)  Another thing that can sometimes be an issue is the camera. Occasionally you won't see shots coming cause of the camera angle, but most of the time it's fine (like 99% of the time), so it's not THAT big a deal, just really annoying when it does happen.  All in all, I really like the design of the game, I just can't recommend it cause the execution is just a tad bit too bad and ends up being frustrating especially the alter you get (the cynic in me would say that the early game is purposefully working really well, so that once people get to the problematic parts they can't refund it, but I doubt that was done with the refund system in midn and is just an unfortunate side effect of the game's bad core design that's not immediately obvious on the easier parts of the game).  Maybe someone can do a mod or maybe the dev is gonna fix some stuff to make it less annoying, but unless that happens I'd rather go with other similar titles

If it does, it's great fun, even if I'm personally really bad at attacking without getting blown up first. The controls are also like the ui, not great, but not what I'd call bad either. The thing I like most probably about this game is the AI though, at least the fact that the air support can choose targets of opportunity and not just kill the same tank with everything even after it's destroyed. app_name: The Witcher 2: Assassins of Kings Enhanced Edition
review_text: Rank:  8.5/10  Recommendation(s):  Play another Witcher title before (The Witcher 1)  Major Theme:  Slightly sluggish, but a great mix between 1 and 3.  Bought at Price:  $2.75 - Steam Sale  I honestly thought I was going to have enough will power to play the Witcher series in order, but that turned out to be not the case. The first title turned out to be so dry; it was equivalent to having a dry mouth, being dragged face down in a desert for kilometers. There was a serious lack of humor, but the combat system proved to be greatly overhauled. While the system having its own new issues, made gameplay more pleasant than the machine like structure of the first.  Graphics are vastly enhanced from the first, but still do not compare anywhere close to that of the third (and last?) Witcher title. Maybe that’s why they called it the “enhanced edition”. On that note, I cannot describe the difference between the two editions, as I have not played the original release.  While the story was logical, and easy to follow, was a little let down. By no means was it horrible or not worth playing, it just again lacked humor. At some points in the game, you will find yourself playing a part just to move the story along (which felt very much like the first game in the series).  I probably will not be replaying this title as I will be doing with the 3rd, but that should be no judge of quality.

app_name: The Incredible Adventures of Van Helsing
review_text: Van Helsing was, to me, one of those games that was only pleasureful to play in c-op. I've started out playing it alone, but quickly grew tired of it. There isn't much holding this title up in terms of story or novel game mechanics. This is a classic, if a little simplistic RPG through and through. In co-op, however, I had the chance to play polar opposite builds with my gaming buddy which made for a more interesting, strategy-driven experience. Without this additional challenge, I wouldn't have found the incentive to ever finish this game.  Your ghostly companion has a very enticing voice, though.  All in all, I don't recommend it.

The first installment of this franchise was outstanding. In the beginning, this sequel was even better. Then they started churning out one or two five-dollar DLC every month with a few weapons or a new map. I was okay with that for a while. It was optional and half of them were free.  But they really started to pile up. Where other game studios would have been working on their next game, Overkill decided they would just work on one game forever, apparently. To fund this obsession/laziness/whatever it is, they decided to ♥♥♥♥ their customers out of as much money as they would allow. Sadly, many customers allow them to bend them over and have their way with them and then ask for more.  They introduced CS: GO style skins that are acquired from paid unboxing, except unlike those kinds of systems, the skins rarely have an additional stat boost. This would already be unbalanced and an unacceptable pay-to-win mechanic that shouldn't exist at all, let alone in a paid game. But to make matters worse, they nerfed many of the weapons already in the game so that people would have to get these skins just to have weapons that work at all.  This ridiculous skin fiasco was the immediate predecessor to their still ongoing attempt to make this game a viable 'e-sports' title, because they've ramped their greed all the way up and have decided they need some of that pie in addition to the princely sum they've already milked from their dwindling playerbase.  The outcry over their actions was such that even their forum moderators went on strike over it. In their arrogance, they announced a hollow apology while steadfastly refusing to back down from their exploitative practices.  Following this game's current trajectory, I expect them to announce a monthly subscription fee some time in the near future. I could say more, but I better hurry up and get this review posted before they start charging $2.99 for it.

I was skeptical to spend $40 on a Steam game. It's been awhile since I've paid more than $10 for anything, and even those I try to get for less if possible. Was waiting for this to go on sale since January 2014. It hasn't that I've seen. You needn't bother waiting. Dear God I've been waiting for an RPG of this caliber for years. It's epic. It's gorgeous. It's fun. It's deep. Soundtrack is awesome, voice acting is awesome. Fights are HARD. I feel like I'm playing a combination of Ultima VII and Baldur's Gate II on crack. I've put 50+ hours in, and my characters are level SIX. I've seen level 10 spells available. They tease things out JUST right. Gold is tight...you really need to 'need' something before you buy it. Co-Op has been FLAWLESS. This game is just plain brilliant for any Iso-view RPG fans. Absolutely AAA top-notch. Did I mention it's fun to look at also? You will get your ♥♥♥ handed to you until you fully understand the elemental mechanics...finally got that fire ability? Be careful =) But once you get it, DAMN there are some amazing combos available.  It is (appears to be anyway) VERY bug free. In fact, I haven't seen one single major issue yet. No crashes, no graphics problems, no soft-locks, no file issues, no inventory problems, no spell problems, etc. etc. etc. One small issue (very small) is a sound (just music) hiccup maybe occassionally, but hardly worth noticing...it's music between areas and there's a very slight stutter. There are just no big issues of any kind. I'm playing on a slightly out-dated laptop with general High settings. It's so good. 100% recommended for RPG fans.

Teleglitch is a rogue-like, atmostpheric, survival horror game set in the future and populated only by things that want to murder you. Ammo is scarce, health is only replenishable by healing items and all of your foes madly crave your blood. You'll face mutants and their bigger counterpart, gun-wielding guards, zombies, merciless bosses and a whole load of other truly challenging baddies. The shadow effects in Teleglitch: Die More Edition are beautiful and dynamic, providing a real sense of nausea, desperity and oppression via your knowledge you're probably going to die. When playing this game, I'd highly reccomend wearing a headset, the sound quality is great and the noises themselves are so disturbing, heavily atmospheric and psychologically painful, you'll feel tremendous joy in conquering this game's daemons. Like I've already said, this game is really hard, you may feel totally confident in your abilities and gear one minute, and then be forced into a corner whereby you can either run into the screaming crowd of mutants and other repulsing eyesores trapping you in, or headfirst into the 'void' behind you, a black lump of nothingness that anybody, or anything, unfortunate enough to touch it is atomised instantly. Overall, Teleglitch is a difficult, immensely atmostpheric b*tch of a game that'll keep you coming back for more, and making you it's b*tch in the process. The Guns 'n' Tunes DLC available for the game is a steal at 99p and is a considerable addition, especially for the pricetag. However, the game's asking price itself is a tad steep, and I've seen it 80% off countless times, in said times, I reccomend that you definately pick it up.

Pros:  -Challenging in the greatest way possible. I usually find games that provide somewhat of a challenge, but not enough to really test your abilities and get you thinking of what to do differently. This game has that extra challenge that gets you thinking of new ideas and tricks.   -The ability to create your own virus and give it unique effects that you can generate in the game. I do recommend playing the game first and understanding what does what and such before experimenting with this tool. It will make creating your own virus much more fun.   -Different plague types that really change up the gameplay, there is tons of clones of this game that fail to do this, and usually each type of plague in them play a similar style, or can be beaten using the same strategy. With this, each plague plays drastically different than the other.   -Unique Scenarios that depict real life plagues or something completely random to occupy you after you beat the core game.   -The ultimate feeling that will please all sadists knowing you are killing off all of humanity.  Cons:  -The Fungus plague type is extremely boring, and if you get too impatient (Which probably will happen) it will anger you to the point you will throw your controller because it has been 6 years and you finally infected everything, only to end up losing because you dont have very much DNA. So to get around this, only increase the infectivity a little, and let the game infect everyone THEN when you save enough, Get to total organ failure, and then you should win.   -I really dont have other cons personally, if I play this game more and I find some other things personally tedious, I will add them.  I recently bought this on PC, and I will say it is definitely worth picking up if it goes on sale, It is quite the upgrade from the mobile version, which is good, but doesnt feel as massive as this is. I have already pumped 22 hours into this game, which is a rare sight for me since I do not really play games any more. If you like a challenge, and you like moderate paced action, this game is for you.

OH MY GOD OH MY GOD OH MY GOD GET THIS GET THIS GET THIS!!!  Sorry. It had to be done. But seriously, get this game. If you ever enjoyed the old arcade version of Gauntlet, or if you ever spent a week locked in your next-door neighbor's basement trying to get to the next board, you will LOVE Gauntlet.  If you ever played the N64 or Playstation editions of Gauntlet, this is nothing like them.  Several words to the wise:  1. Get a gamepad. Don't play this with mouse and keyboard, it is nowhere near as fun. There were a couple things I found more convenient with the M&K controls, but the fun factor greatly increased with an easy-to-hold gamepad. Try it both ways, but srsly: TRY IT BOTH WAYS. Don't buy this if you don't have a gamepad.  2. Get x360ce (Google it) to make sure your gamepad works with Gauntlet. Put it in the 'binaries' directory, not the game's root directory.  3. Don't even bother with single player. You'll have trouble getting past the first monster spawner on normal difficulty.  4. Start with the Valkyrie. Your natural inclination may very well be (as mine was) to start with the warrior (generally the simplest class to play in any game), but I found it much easier to play the Valkyrie. Consider the Wizard an 'advanced' class. You need to stay on your toes and you need to remember the button combos for all your spells.  The graphics are... whatever. Who cares? They're good, but they don't need to be. The sound effects are modern, but they very effectively hearken back to days of yore. Controls are... preeeeetty good. I'd prefer if the Valkyrie's dash ability (for example) allowed me to rotate while it's 'charging' after I push the button.  The difficulty is good, the multiplayer works smashingly with only the hintiest hint of lag and the level design is enough to keep me interested.  The one big issue I have with this game is that there basically is no single player mode. You can play by yourself, but on normal difficulty the enemies are already scaled up to the point that you can't really do well. On the face of it, that doesn't bug me THAT much. This is a fairly new game, and there are always people on. But in a few years when it's not the latest and greatest anymore, I basically won't be able to play it outside the context of a LAN party.  If you were EVER a fan of the Gauntlet genre back in the day, you will love this game.  EDIT: Don't buy this game if you don't have someone to play with over a LAN. The community is absolutely dead. Due to a completely inattentive developer and a couple much-lamented gamebreakers, you're going to sit in the matchmaking lobby for ten minutes or so before you get hooked up with ONE other player (this owing to the fact that you can't join a game in progress). And then pray whomever's hosting the game has a steady connection, because if they dump out the game ends. The one time I got in a game with two other people, it lasted about three minutes, then the host dumped out and we waited five minutes for him to get back.  It sad, really. This game is a lot of fun, but because of the absolute ridiculous design choices of static game hosting and lack of jump-in/jump-out games, it's essentially dead after a month and a half.

An endless adventure This being my first step into the genre of turn based strategy I might be a little bias. But here it is: I absolutely love this game. The first thing that got my attention was the beautiful world. Vibrant colors, very detailed, and just pleasing to the eye. The second would be the sounds and oh boy are you in for a treat. Zooming into a city you'll hear...well the sounds of the city, the bustling of everyday life. Rivers, the wind, sounds of wildlife. Not to mention durring a battle, it sounds like a battle should. Very pleasing to the ears. So +1 in both looks and sounds. What about gameplay? As fuild as the river nile! And this being a turn based game, there is a lot more thinking involved in every move you make. Things like, 'well if I make an alliance with them I get trade, meaning more money and materials. But then again if I do that I will become enemies with these people, and said people border some important cities.' Things like that. Deciding what to equip units with and if its cost efficient. Wondering if you should branch out or build up certain areas. Giving people cookies hoping they wont come to your main city with a vastly superior force and destroy you. Those are things I never really thought I would have fun doing.  What can I say, I love this game and I cant wait to see what more it offers.

Tharsis, like many other dice games, is about thinking several steps ahead. It's about risk management. Many players do not seem to understand this. Yes, dice rolls are random, and can screw you over, but the game is about using the most out of every thing you're given.  Essentially, you and you fellow crew of space travellers are stranded on a doomed ship after a cluster of meteorites struck it. Your goal, is to survive 10 turns, while all hell breaks lose. During these turns, you use your crew to repair various sections of the ship. Not fixing these areas often has catastrophic consequences. The worst cases being that your ship either explodes, or your crew dies. To fix these areas, you roll a group of dice, whose collectice value represents the amount of repairs a crew member can make at once. If you pass a randomized thresh hold, you fix the section, and no penalties will inhibit you. If you fail, you'll need another crew member to come in and complete the needed repairs.   This sounds all nice and easy, except that their are a multitude of resources and factors that can slow you down. Hunger being the primary one. Your characters carry a maximum of 5 dice at once. After completing an action, their total number of dice reduces by 1. This introduces a sort of fatigue system, where your crew gets worse and worse. However, if you feed them, they'll immediatly recover 3 maximum dice. The problem is that food is scarce, and fairly difficult to procure in large amounts. with 4 crew members, it can be difficult to get everyone working effectively.   As you could imagine, the luck of the draw can come into play here, as practically everything is related to dice rolls. However, there is a mechanic in the game called research. Effectively, at any point when you roll dice, you can choose to sleight them into a research tray. At any given time you'll be given 3 'cards' which you can spend research on to unlock for a once use consumable. For example, if you have a 1, 3, and a 5, you could sleight each into research, and allow you to unlock a card with a maximum cost of up to 3. Each of these cards helps you do various things. By preemptively stocking research, you can prepare for the storm thats coming later on down the road.  Again, the game is about budgeting your dice accordingly. There may be scenarios where its better to leave parts of the ship damaged, while you work on resting and getting food for you crew. The challenge however, is in finding the best solution to a run. Tharsis is almost like a puzzle in that regard. Indeed at times, it may be impossible to win, but you shouldn't let that stop you, as thats how dice games work in general. Its what makes it special. I just finished a run where I got so many 1's and 2's, and high count repair rooms that my head was spinning. However, I managed to scrape by through the action of killing and eating one of my squad mates, a liberal use of research cards, and pumping everything I had into repairing the hull with nothing but super glue, duck tape, and spit. It can be hard to get that decisive win, but when you make it its extremely rewarding.

Since this beta was opened the only notable changes have been new Mercenaries te play. However, I can't bring myself to play this game enough to earn sufficient credits to buy them.  Once in a while I give this game another chance, because it's core gameplay is really solid. But then I'm confronted with the reasons why I'm not playing this game consistantly almost instantly: - First and foremost, a proper matchmaking system seems to be completely absent. I was just playing a game where the enemy team had an average level of 10 or so and ours had an average level of around 7. When a new player finally joins the match he joins my team and turns out to be a level 1. (Level does not only indicate experience and learned skill, but also indicates the probability of higher tier loadout cards) This was the final straw that inspired me to write this review. - Once you get past the newbie stage, the fast gameplay turns into instakill action. I don't mind running and gunning in fact I love it, but it settles into a rythm of better players killing you every time and you having no options to turn the tables. - Despite the fact that matchmaking seems random, the game sure does take its sweet time finding those random people and when it does it quite often freezes for a few seconds at every stage between clicking 'play now' and actually spawning in the match. - Finally, when you've had enough and you want to shut the game down, trying to exit the game often locks up the game and not even the task manager can save you as the game holds a black fullscreen that blocks any other windows.  Of course all these problems are my own subjective experience and this is a beta after all (I've left out the server overcrowding in the first week because that's only a measure of popularity), but the problems are unexcusable for an open beta.  Its biggest redeeming factor is the solid gameplay mechanics, wich deserve a second mention. So try it out and see for yourself and I hope you have fun, but I'm done...

One of those games practically designed for the casual gamer, Bejeweled and all of its partner games are a fun and easy romp through a colorful matching game. Partnered with simple yet pleasant sound effects and music, this game is easy on all of the senses, and satisfying for those who don't seek an much more than that. Both the Normal and TimeTrial modes have their advantages, and add just enough to this game for it to feel complete. Seeing sister games like StarJeweled (In StarCraft II) is a wonderful experience as well! If you are looking for challenge, this is not the game for you, but if you want something to keep you busy for a while and to help sharpen your eyes, you might have just found the right game.

After just under an hour of playing, I'm afraid I couldn't take 'Eternal Senia' any more.  An interesting, yet incomprehensible game with characters and plot that feel like they were cut out from cardboard, game design more backhanded than a Rakshasa and all put to the most baffling musical scoring I have ever encountered.  The game's writing is full of contrivances, grammatical errors and scenes that are either unneccessary or perhaps just a little bit too long.   I remember a key instance where an elf showed up in my path, armed with a bow and arrow. Immediately I saw that the bridge I was stood on was wide enough to sidestep their attack, and that they had a missile nocked and ready to fire, but the game decided that Senia hadn't fulfilled her moping quota yet and needed to strain out a tutorial disguised as poor character development, instead of me just knocking the forest dweller onto his silken britches.  The gameplay is fundementally flawed, if at times entirely broken. The key to interaction here seems to be a unique, action-oriented combat system in which mobile foes wander around aimlessly on a grid, until you walk up to them in order to initiate an auto-attack, slicing them to peices with your sword. However, attacking has a high chance to knock you back one space (Though not into hazards like trips and traps, because that would be engaging), and very quickly it devolves into 'Butt Heads with Creepy Mc. Crawly #394 encounting'.  Bosses tend to shake things up a bit more than regular grunts, with actual attack patterns that force you to move around the arena instead of just bumping into them repeatedly, but they're all far too easy and exploitable to see the full potential of this system. The first boss in particular, was a dragon with a mighty breath weapon... and an arthritic neck. I slew him easily by moving up to his left flank and carving it into wyrm-burgers.  There are also puzzles - which are nearly all beatable from brute force - traps that pose little to no threat when your Hit Points regenerates faster than lightning and a crafting system that aims to releive frustration, but this is mitigated by the overly fast pace and the fact that a girl I found in a magical forest, deep within a ethereal kingdom of swords and sorcery knows what 'Facebook' is.

I'm a puzzle gamer. I play 90% puzzle games. The best puzzle game I've ever played is the The Witness. Stephen's Sausage Roll comes in second place, easy. I knew that two worlds into the game. Why? Let me tell ya.  Everything about the design of this game is amazing. Yeah, it's varied and ingenious every step of the way, it teaches you new mechanics without needing to explicitly tell you them and it makes you think outside the box constantly. But above all, the design of the game is KIND. Some puzzles games make me tear my hair and curse the people who could've crafted the nightmares before me. But in SSR, whenever I got stuck, I just sat back and asked: 'What am *I* missing? Why can't *I* see it?'   The solution (solutions, sometimes) is never obscure, and it never makes you feel like 'oh, how I was I supposed to get that?' after a level. All the solutions make sense and feel good to execute, leading to such a feeling of satisfaction that SSR should probably be classed as a drug. And like a drug, I got addicted right quick, playing every day and never getting bored of it. The difficulty is very consistent throughout, starting of challenging and gently bobbing up and down, and the way the environment is woven into the puzzle solving is nothing short of mind-blowing.  I'm not going to talk about the mechanics of the game. You push sausages around. There are other reviews for that, if you really need more information. I'm just here to recommend the game to anyone who likes puzzles game and who doesn't despise games where you push objects around. This is a design MASTERPIECE, and I would gladly have paid triple the original prize.   Buy this. Now.

DOOM is so much better than I expected. It's fast paced, it's brutal and in moments really gets the adrenaline going - especially if playing on a higher difficulty. The campaign is where the game really shines, I've spent a good 7 hours or so on the single-player and I am about half way through, mainly because the maps are pretty fun to explore when looking for upgrades and collectibles. The maps are also pretty big and get significantly harder as you progress - but this is balanced by unlocking weapon and armour upgrades as well as new equipment. It all looks really clean and pretty as well, as Hell goes.  The multiplayer isn't awful. It feels a bit unbalanced and chances are if you or the enemy team are significantly ahead in a match then there is no way you/they can catch up - you can judge a win or loss pretty early on in the round. It's not bad but gets the same fast, there's not much bringing you back except for the random customisation prizes you can win at the end of a match. Theres a nice variant of game modes, some objective based, others kill based, and there is a lot of nice customisation you can do with your soldier and with the guns. So there is some good to be had here, just don't expect anything too special.  All in all, I thought DOOM was really going to shine from a MP front but it turns out it is one of the best SP games I have played in years. This really is worth picking up if your looking for something fun or if you are just looking for a good well rounded shooter.  FIGHT LIKE HELL.

I love this game, I really do. I like the concept of the 4 v 1 game where you have to work together to survive. And yet I'm uninstalling it because of the matchmaking system.  A game like this should be challenging, but not discouraging. This game is the latter and I feel impossible for latecomers to get into. If you don't already have 300 plus hours in this by now, you don't have much of a chance to get into it.  If I play survivor, I'm going up against either a killer who will sit and camp your body, or I have a selfish team who lead killers to me or let me die. Or both, both happen more often than not. And there isn't a punishment for letting a killer sit and stare you in the eye while you bleed out.  If I play killer, I'm going against survivors who have ten times my play time on record, and have unlocked all the overpowered perks and put them on their character. The fact you can teach the ability to heal, run fast and destroy hooks to one person, rather than letting each character grow into a speciality build, is what ruins the game. Teachable perks just should not exist. I get the point is to experiment with builds but everyone just chooses the same three perks as survivor and unless you're a killer with at least four perks, you don't have much of a chance. I don't want to camp your body, I want to hunt people down, and I can't because my hooks are gone, you heal behind an infinite wall and you've been playing far longer than me.  Matchmaking should not be based on rank, it should be based on character level or number of hours a person has in a game. And expressing an opinion on this game gets you labelled salty. The community to this game is honestly, one of the worst I have ever experienced because both sides claim the other is overpowered. So I'm retiring this game because I'm tired of getting ♥♥♥♥♥♥ off at both my teammates and the people I play against. I have better things to get hacked off at.  I know there is a new update tomorrow but unless it's going to put me against other people with 40 hours in game so I have a chance, I'm not interested. I want to play this game, and I love the idea of it, and it is fun when it works, but the broken matchmaking system means I can't go against people of a similar level, and it's no fun going against people I don't have a chance with. I didn't dodge lobbies because I want to learn how to play well, but now I just don't want to play because it's always against a team I can't even hope to compete against.  Devs, please listen to the community and fix matchmaking. We don't need new content, we need a fighting chance.

Imagine if you were playing Pokemon for the first time, and when you fought your rival (You probably named him 'Sir Garbage') at the beginning of the game he died and was completely out of the story. No more taunting you throughout your adventure like an the annyoing brat Sir Garbage was. Just dead, and you are the murder. Right in front of his own grandpa, Dr Oak, who cries out, and then he dies too. Soon you meet other well known characters in the franchise. They too die after you fought them. The world becomes a dark and lonley place. It's a shock for the whole population. Until now murder was an unknown concept. The other Pokemon trainers now starts to fear you, others seeks to punish you for your crimes. They too starts to fall one by one, and soon whole villages flees you. Some seeks the protection of Team Rocket in hope of having enough people to counter attack you. A last desperate defence from all Pokemon trainers are made. But they all die. You leave trail of dead pokemons and humans everywhere you go. At last your own pokemons , the once who has always served you, has had enough. In one last epic final, they go against their whole life of training, to finally follow their own hearth, the whole world prays they succeed. But they too fall, and in the end, you are the very best, like no one ever was. Because everyone else is dead.  Or, instead, of killing Sir Garbage, you refuse to fight him entirely, and you become friends, and share this lovely little adventure together to two of you, and your Pokemons, who are released from captivity.   Thats Undertale.
No mater how much or how little you may know about this game, it's sure to provide you an experience that will linger with ya, even when you move on.

app_name: Warhammer 40,000: Dawn of War II - Retribution
review_text: When compared to the original Dawn of War, this game does have some pros and cons. While taking away the original form of full on tactical strategy of the original, the second installment takes the dark, war-torn, bloody, hostile universe of Warhammer 40K and brings it into a new era of gaming. Adding some elements that bring games like Company of Heroes and Diablo to mind, the gameplay is much more exciting to non-RTS fans by making it more of a game where the point is to use limited knowledge of the terrain and limited population capacity to obliterate your opponent in the bloodiest way possible. I agree that it lost some RTS feel with this sequel, but it added and evolved the original into something that wasn't as RTS but instead more focused on combat and making the game more cinematic which is a fresh taste for those gamers who hate RTS's for their seemingly endless missions. Also each race has its own campaign and allows a diverse experience across the horrific 40K universe and does an accurate portrayal of each of the races if the player thinks realistically and not as the game is on tabletop. Overall, this game has brought me many hours of entertainment in just the single player alone. The Last Stand game mode allows for a fun and challenging experience for those who just want to drop in and kill stuff. Last Stand puts you in charge of a field commander for the race you choose and you get to operate in a small area in a wave survival gamemode with cooperative play. All in all, this game is fantastic and Retribution includes the best of the whole entire DoW 2 package with extensive DLC and all of the races from the previous two entries. Do I recommend this game? Absolutely.

I have a soft spot for the 2008 reboot of the Prince of Persia series, if only because it was Ubisoft's attempt at selling a game without DRM (sadly, their only attempt as they quickly jumped to SecuROM- and later UPlay-protected games thereafter). And for many, that was the game's only saving grace, as the general consensus was that the game was a poor follow-up to the acclaimed 'Sands of Time' trilogy. But not me; I liked this game.  One of its major failings for some was that this was a game that was impossible to lose. Jump off the wrong ledge or get your ♥♥♥ kicked by a monster, and your AI partner Elika would invariably be there to bail you out, resetting you to the last checkpoint. While this doubtlessly removed some of the challenge, it also removed a lot of the frustration of having to replay the same jump-n-run segments over and over (imagine that, a Ubisoft game that tried to LIMIT the amount of repetitive grinding!). The game was also far more relaxed in the timing of its button-presses, playing more like a series of casual quick-time events than a fast-paced platformer. But both design choices allowed the developers to emphasize the setting and characters (not to mention that, by slowing down the pace, I actually get to watch the action on-screen rather than focus on what quick-time button to press next). It becomes a game more focused on environment and experience than difficulty and - while not necessarily the direction I want all games to go - I was fine with that choice in this title. The story and characters are appealing, the visuals are colorful and the gameplay supports the mood rather than - as too often happens - opposes it. It's a relaxing and enjoyable romp that won't appeal to everyone but was a pleasant way to pass the time between more hard-core games.
