This game is big. I mean, it is huge. If you are worried it has too much for your liking, you are quite probably right. But then again, as individuals, we are known to acquire tastes that we might not first envision having and that is actually a nice thing; it keeps our experience of life fresh. CKII is one of my acquired tastes. My strategy background is basically age of empires and warcraft. I never really liked the concept of games that give you a big schematic map, to mess around with abstract numbers and statistics to guide your empire. I always liked the rather personal, down-and-dirty action of RTS and the hability to arrange buildings and troops in creative ways aligned to my aesthetical likings. I have never played a game like Civ, because turns+maps really seemed to take away everything I actually liked about strategy games.  I, rather inadvertently, started to enjoy some of the more 'abstract' strategy games through the Total War series. I bought it because of the real time battles and never really intended to play the campaigns due to the turn based quality of it. Only after I finally lost interest on the general purposeless quick battles I begun to play a grand campaign just to give my battles some meaning and, what can I say; I actually begun to enjoy the turn based genre almost as much as the RTS of my childhood.  When I first learned about CKII I wasn't very sure about playing a game without any real time battles and troop commanding I grew up with. To jump head-on on a game that was pure number-crunching (in my opinion at the time) was more akin to a leap of faith. I really only bought the game because it was medieval and I like all things medieval; so why not give it a try, I thought. How happy I am I did. Yup, the game is pure map view, no real time battles (not in a AOE or Total War sense) and no little units to order around building things, but it is still one of the most personal, immersing and satisfying experiences I ever had with a game. How Paradox Interactive managed to do that is no short of pure genius. You do not play as some god-like ruler who wills something into existence, rather, you play as character on a world full of similar characters with all sorts of aspirations, ideas and skills. You are guiding not a nation, but your estate and your family through a very dramatic time in history where everything could be lost with badly arranged marriage. You will feel like some medieval Godfather, looking out for your family (dynasty) and making decisions that most of the time will get some of the members angry at you, but all in the greater interest of kingdom and your dynasty.  Its not a game where you loose your capital and you loose, or where they kill you and game over. It is a game where, wherever someone with your blood is alive, there is a chance that you can build everything from scratch; provided you are cunning enough. It is this focus on individual characters and their traits that make the game so personal and immersing. It is not uncommon to actually feel good when your heir is a very skilfull administrator to continue guiding the empire after your demise, nor it is unheard of people actually making some serious soul-searching after murdering digital babies in order to inherit someone elses kindgom due to lack of heirs. Bottom line is: the game is huge, it is overwhelming at first and visual panache is not its thing. If you are looking for something other than that, do buy this game and give it an honest chance. It is quite probable that you will play nothing else for some good 40 hours after things really get going and you are totally absorved by all the intrigue, plots and political chaos this rich virtual medieval europe throws at you.

A huge update went out about a week ago: Specters of the Rail. Honestly there's never been a better time for new players. Things are laid out a bit more linearly now (for better or worse) and there are very easy indications what to do next. Knowing my way around the game as I do, it feels a bit more restrictive than it did, but I don't think it's at all a bad change.  But the game itself is completely in a class of it's own. The developers are constantly fixing bugs, throwing out hotfixes, and you'll often see a developer interacting with players through the general chat. It's amazing to see developers so involved with their game's community.  It's not a game you can really binge, though. I like being able to sit down and just lay 6-8 hours on a game in one session (with a break for snacks, natch). But it is a wonderful thing just to boot up after a day at work and just slip an hour in. Missions are easy to get into, groupfinding (if that's you cup of tea) is super simple, and there are roughly a bagillion weapons to try out against various enemies and objectives.  It's free, fun, frantic, and there's nothing else like it out there. No reason not to try it out.

I've been playing Prison Architect for about 30 hours per alpha release for the latest few versions that came out before the final release came out. An early access game that not ONLY has relesed new versions on schedule for a long time, and now actually has reached version 1.0, is, sadly, something to celebrate on Steam.  Aside from the fact that the devs have used the Early Access platform the way it was meant to be used and that should be commended, it's also a pretty damned good managment game. Prison managment has been done before, but not for a long time, and never well, and it's a change of pace from the usual town managment sims. Now your 'population' is the enemy, as well as your source of income.  The number of systems that works togheter to form the complete simulation is very impressive, and even a small change can shatter a very stable system. For example. I had a prison with 750 prisoners, running very smoothly. Then a gang leader arrived on the bus one morning and started organizing the red gang, one of the three gangs in my prison. I had never had a gang leader before so I let him work for a bit before sending him to my supermax facility, so I could experience how running a prison with an organized gang was like. In supermax he lost his temper, and ended up in solitary. This angered his gang enough that they started a riot. A couple of hours later when the dust settled and the riot police left there were 54 dead, over 60 injured, and 200 prisoners who were up for solitary for their actions during the riot. The rioting prisoners broke into protective custody, where snitches and former policemen who ended up in prison are kept, and killed half of the protected prisoners. I was put on notice, and risked ending up as a prisoner in my own prison. Luckily my lawyer got me out of that... A prison is a delicate system, and care needs to be taken when introducing anything new.  There is now added a story mode, to work as a guide for new players, and an escape mode, where you get to play as a prisoner, as well, but I still feel that the sandbox mode is the meat of the game. You learn some new tricks each time you build a new prison, and as you can sell your old prison for an extra cashboost in the startup phase of your next one, you will get more and more funds for each prison you build.   My only problem with the game is that it starts to lag when your prison grows too big, depending on how good your computer is. I first start to notice it at 750 prisoners and roughly 350 staff, so at just over 1000 individual characters present in the same prison, but my computer is pretty damned good. I know that a lot of people can't even get 500 prisoners to work well.  tl:dr Damned good simulator/managment game. Buy it, play it, profit. A bit unforgiving if you make a mistake though, but that's the way in a prison. Highly recomended.

'FIGHT!'  The long-running Mortal Kombat series once again heads to PC, with a pared down fighting system and character roster that evoke the series' simpler days. There's no 'run' button, though you can double-tap movement keys to dash back and forth. You don't have Animalities, Brutalities, Aggression meters, fighting-style switches, in-stage weapons, or goofy puzzle minigames. Combat takes place on a 2D plane, so you're not moving toward or away from the camera.  The game has three primary modes--the story, the traditionally structured Arcade ladder, and the challenge tower. (I don't count the multiplayer since I have never ~once~ gotten it to work properly.) Yes, the game does have a plot, and it's surprisingly well done. Many of the older games' most iconic characters--Jax, Liu Kang, Sub-Zero, Sonya Blade, and so on--make their 'return' in this retelling of the series' central narrative themes. Johnny Cage and Sonya have their goofy interactions. Liu Kang and Kung Lao do their thing. Sub-Zero and Scorpion have their drama, and so on. Instead of keeping the story in the background and limiting it to text epilogues--other than in the ladder mode (quick to finish, highly replayable) -- the story unfolds in a format that feels like a movie but is more entertaining than a great deal of actual movies I've seen.  The campaign is fairly lengthy without feeling drawn out, and one of its best decisions is to force the player to constantly switch up characters. You start off as the movie star Johnny Cage, allowing you to get used to his mix of close-range and projectile-based battling before the game gives you a perspective shift. You essentially have so many player characters that I'd have to stop to count them, and while some of the excuses for fights are silly contrivances, the game uses these misunderstandings as a really good excuse for exposing you to a wide variety of fight arrangements in a nicely diverse array of settings, some of which will look familiar to series veterans. The actual story gets off to a rough start, as Cage is rather patronizing towards women, but even if his personality can be rather aggravating,  1) He has a personality at all, which is more than I can say for too many game characters, and  2) He eventually grows out of it and becomes more tolerable. That, and this does lead to some welcome comic relief.  The voice acting throughout the campaign ranges from decent to laughable, but the capable writing actually manages to evoke legitimate emotion at times without becoming melodramatic. You really feel like something has happened by the time you reach the end of the story, you won't be able to predict every little thing that happens just by playing the earlier games, and even though the ending is bittersweet, it's satisfying and nowhere near as much as a waste of time as the plot was in, say, Deception (no points for guessing why). The story mode's difficulty can be changed from within the options menu, and the challenge scales up really nicely by the end of the campaign. The final boss fight--and you will know it when it comes--is HARD but is extremely satisfying to finish. The game is generous with its autosaves after fights and cutscenes, so you rarely have to repeat content if you want to take a break. You can't perform fatalities during the campaign, though, because you will routinely see characters exit the story and come back in later on. This being Mortal Kombat, people do die, and the opening of the game does a very good job of establishing a grim atmosphere. There are also scenes that shift into some really, really well done albeit grisly animation, making for some of the best moments in a story that feels sincere without coming across as exploitative toward either extreme (whether inappropriate comedy--usually--or excessive tear-jerking).  That being stated, even as much as I really enjoyed the story mode, it pales in comparison to the Challenge Tower, which in my view is the single best reason to buy this game. You have hundreds of ridiculously creative self-contained challenges that pit a wide variety of characters against one another under unpredictable circumstances. The humor is scaled up a lot here from the story mode, and there's a much larger variety of things to do when compared to that mode, which has basic matches, handicap tag matches (which you will feel awesome after beating), and little else. Try defeating your opponent when neither of you has arms. Try doing it without the use of throws, or special moves, or the ability to block. Sometimes you have to contend with a slot machine that randomizes the terms of your next fight. At one point you play a cyborg whose internal systems are shutting down, making each of his attacks deal less damage with every one you launch. Make them all count! There's even a bizarre variant on H-O-R-S-E, where you try to mimic or exceed your opponent's percentage of damage dealt across a number of miniature rounds.  There's a lot of stuff to unlock, such as fatality design-concept sketches, alternate costumes, special codes to change the terms of multiplayer engagements (remember these?), and tons of concept art. Having to buy things by walking up to headstones and torture devices in a really creepy graveyard, in first-person mode, can be a bit aggravating. You do get a map, which is also good for auto-transporting you to locations you've overlooked, so you don't get lost.  The game's learning curve is very gentle. You don't have ridiculously long combos to learn, and the tutorials (which love the use of a game controller but are doable with a keyboard) perform an adequate job of teaching you basic and advanced concepts, some of which have changed from what you may remember. You do get a special meter that you can expend in whole or in part to enhance your attacks or to break your opponent's bones, which usually takes off about a third of their health but doesn't affect their fighting abilities. You can even learn the basics of fatalities--per character, not just for one individual as a 'demonstration'--which is a really nice touch. You can also practice, but in my mind you might as well just play the arcade ladder and keep referring back to the move list. The AI is patient enough to wait.  Even though the game does have its camera leer over women who are for the most part stuck in sexualized outfits (as are a number of the men, really), the girls can generally hold their own, and they're every bit as enjoyable to play as the guys are. The game does pretty much everything it can not to show nips on the ladies, but if you want to see Liu Kang's or Quan Chi's, well, there you go.  Overall, I still would name Deadly Alliance my favorite of the series, but since that's not on Steam, this is still worth a buy. You may as well wait until it's on sale for $5 like I did, because even though the multiplayer is ... shall we say, not up to the level of Skullgirls', the single-player game still has just enough content to let you experience its extremely gory thrills to the joy of your heart, if probably not your stomach. (You may seriously want to check out YouTube videos of fatalities to make sure you can handle them before buying.) Local multiplayer also works well and will probably not cause frame drops even if, like me, you game on a system with integrated graphics; the graphics and especially the character models still look really good even with the resolution and settings turned down. There is no open world that the game has to render, so that probably helps. This is worth a look!

After Bastion i was so exited for this one, and man does it not dissapoint. I usually dont like theese turn based games, with strategy elements. But this one changed my mind on the Genré. The Art-Style and Sound-Design is beautiful and is blended together with the gameplay in sweet harmony. This is worth every penny.

Another excellent if somewhat short game from supergiant. If you played Bastion then the art style and storytelling will be very familiar to you.   As far as the art goes its extremely well done in my opinion, the color palette is bright and vivid while the art style itself reminds me a lot of bastion in that its 2D and hand drawn. This is not to say that they look like the same game however, Transistor's art style is very strikingly different, being much more futuristic with its environments and enemies.   The combat is very fun and has nice turn based aspects to it, and there are tons of combinations with the upgrade system. Functions are your abilities, and these functions can do one of three things: be used as an active ability, augment another ability, or be a passive ability. These are all interchangeable at the various access points placed around each level and combinations are vastly different, such that for example Crash()+Breach() is not the same as Breach()+Crash().   The music is from the same guy that did the music in bastion and the vocalist is the same as well, so as you might expect it's excellent throughout.   The only real problem I have with the game, albeit a small one, is that its a bit short, clocking in at about 5-6 hours per playthrough depending on how many of the challenges you do. It does offer a bit of replayability in the recursive mode once you finish the story, however.  Overall though I can't really recommend this game enough to people that liked bastion, and if you haven't played bastion, Transistor is still a fun game with interesting combat, story, and artwork, so it's well worth the $20 in my opinion.   Also if you haven't played bastion, go do that.

So much potential, squandered. They released the entire game in stages selling it as DLC. The simple fact that normal things like basic replayability was sold to us as DLC was absurd enough, but the hostility of their community managers towards the earlier critics of the game is what caught my interest most of all as I take a keen interest in the use of suppression tactics against gaming media who aren't part of the sycophantic love-bomb crowd. You can tell a lot about developers and companies based on the way they handle criticism. Some people, like Squad, will cyber stalk their critics and try to 'expose' them for past misdeeds (in their eyes), others will wear it and even address it and repair their game improving it's standards. In the case of State of Decay it seemed they would actively delete any criticisms they encountered which left me far more cynical of their game overall. But even with a fanboy attitude when I first discovered the game it was another 'great potential, poor execution' zombie game, sadly. :(

This game is probably the best if at least not one of the better Assassins Creed games. To start, all that horrible, impossible to manage parkour as Desmond is gone - instead, when not in the Animus you are a first-person Abstergo employee who is working on a game using the animus (and some hacking [part of the story]). This is a refreshing break from the previous games, and delivers the story a lot better. Overall, the game is a general improvement on AC3 with few wrong moves. The separation of Islands and having a naval map helps spread out the game, create more content, and resolve past fast travel issues. The game is larger and better than the previous ones. Multiplayer is good but too much content is unlocked by leveling giving experienced players greater advantage. Obviously you buy it for the story and gameplay though. In general, only issue is after playing for a few hours you will have major lag and spasming spawns/ light splitting your screen (this is on an asus g46vw laptop), however, since you can always open up the menu (and not skip cutscenes!), you can simply quit, saving progress and avoiding a crash/task manager/restart hassle, and let the computer cool off. Overall, definitely worth a buy, and us stingy steam users can wait until it goes down at Christmas if necessary.

Performance Catastrophe  For all guys out there thinking of buying this game, I can only say, don't do it. The game doesn't work at all. Me and several of my friends tested it on diverse PCs. Even on High-End Laptops with i7-processor and recent graphics card, single player has performance problems. These problems getting even worse if you try to play online. Moreover, playing with some friends doesn't work at all due to a terrible bug. You can change everything in PC settings - the game remains unplayable.  These horrible performance problems are on their own a reason for keeping one's hands off the game. But please consider: The game is more than 10 years old and nothing, really nothing has been improved - The graphics look exactly the same as the original. Yes, there is a change: The original was working. It's just ridiculous.  So guys, if you want to play AOM, just search for you original CD and play it. Otherwise, you invest almost 30 euros for nothing.

This game is tough, I'm an old school XCOM fan and I've played almost all of the XCOM franchise from the early days of the XCOM: UFO games. I like what they've done in terms of game mechanics but not really happy with the whole 'cookie cutter' path they've taken with this new game, I feel like playing the vanilla version you just shooting yourself in the foot coz you have the characters in the game all pushing you to take the wrong decisions, and there's always an imminent threat that just adds too much to my stress levels,   I understand that you fighting against a well established force and you are effectively the 'terrorists' in this version but dam, its XCOM! can you please make a game where I have time to explore/create/use all the bits and pieces I discover and then decide which of these I like to use before upgrading to something else.   and if you follow the direction the game wants you to follow you effectively throwing rocks at enemy while they shooting you down with highly advanced alien machinery.  So if you want to enjoy this game, install some mods that will at-least give you some breathing room and allow you to think for yourself.   I love the game because of the franchise and the aesthetics but quite disappointed that I cant enjoy the vanilla version especially since these are the same guys that developed Civ VI which is an awesome game!

A couple minor jumpscares (which I hate) but were fitting and not cheaply placed for the sheer purpose of making you crap yourself. I only actually jumped 2x... but Im extremely high strung and should really never play scary games because im a huge baby.   PLAY ITTTT!!

I like this game. I play it on iPad more often than the computer.  The game is slow and you never fight anything, although you can craft weapons to sell to traders. It is a cute town builder where your character has to do everything until you can afford to pay workers and have a house with a bed for them. No battles and no fighting for your life all the time. You harvest, craft, build, and walk around.  It has several quirks and you sometimes need to cut down trees to reach parts of the map. Things are sometimes much simpler than you'd think. Like building bridges across water isn't anything special, if you have the wood, click on the water, ta da. Other times it is really complicated, farming with a worker means two wide strips of farms so the worker doesn't get stuck inside as the crops grow up around them.  I think it is worth the buy if you want something that you can just pick up and build and put down again. If you want combat you won't find it here. Although, get it for the pizza trees if nothing else.

Okay, so I never like writing bad reviews/responses for things. Positivity is very important to me. But this was a complete waste of my time. Look, I'm familiar with the 'masculine, vaguely threatening dude gives mysterious and inscrutable tasks to you through a radio while shouting obscenities at you' and 'bizarre lateral-thinking puzzle type gameplay revolving around vague keywords' cliches of modern gaming (which I totally unironically love), but they have to lead to something. This was completely pointless. The annoying banter with your captor is utterly meaningless and the ending resolves nothing. I guess the gameplay is pretty surreal, but it's never interesting. On top of that, I was expecting a story-based type of game experience (at least for a generous definition of 'story'), but this is definitely not that. Near-constant, terribly voice-acted cursing, drug references, memes (yes, actual memes), and a ♥♥♥♥♥♥ 'twist ending' that had no relevance to anything previously established do not make a compelling story. This may honestly be one of the worst games I've ever engaged with. Please don't bother playing it.  And I know some people will say 'oh, but it's free!' Time is more valuable than money. I'm not criticizing it on the grounds of the engine or the limitations of a small-scale game (although the bizarrely antiquated save system did raise an eyebrow). I'm not complaining about spending money on it. I just think that all of the effort put into this game was clearly spent in the totally wrong direction.

app_name: Call of Duty: Black Ops III
review_text: UPDATE!   This is the time when I would like that Steam had three options for recommending a game: Yes, Neutral and No. This is more of a Neutral recommendation than Yes.  My review on launch is available below. Now, 5 months later, game has reached a stable version. I have 60 FPS on High settings.  Campaign is bad, really nothing new or revolutionary, but the fun part is Multiplayer. Addition of Black Market is also interesting and great for 'one more match' feeling.   If you can get a Starter Pack or when it is on sale, I recommend you to do so.   Review one month after launch:   It was a really bad on launch. FPS drops, crashing, etc. One month later and after two patches I have similar problems. My rig runs GTA V at 60 FPS all on Very High... what to tell. Other than these problems, game is really shallow.  Campaign is just a click simulator as you can pass some situations without shooting because everything is staged. Story 0/10. Multiplayer is ok for few hours, interesting mechanics (jumping, running on the wall...) but gets boring after 5 hours.  TL;DR Nothing revolutionary, don't get this.
If you like the grim universe of warhammer 40K, if you enjoyed panzer general back in 90-s, this is a must have for you! Modern gamers who value graphics over the game spirit - please stay away.

Each turn is meaningful and will have you thinking through your options and best course of action.  The game could do with some graphics optimization. I get big FPS drops on the main menu and during cutscenes, which is odd for such a simple game. During gameplay it works great, so it's not such a big issue.  If you're looking for quick but intense play sessions and a game whose mechanics will provide tons of replay value on their own this game is for you.

Got the game for when it was 40 cents or something. It is an okay game for that price, but full price is not worth. The idea and concept was nice and unique, but the gameplay was not as good. Tower defense games can be done in multiple ways, but they need a real challenge that requires real strategy. In this game, all you do is click little circles to build a defense tower. You can upgrade a tower's damage and speed.   The towers are not in danger of being destroyed, which isn't uncommon in tower defense games, but this game needs to make up for it in some other way to create a challenge beyond 'upgrade this structure, sell this tower for a more expensive tower, repeat' mindset.   If there were a variety of different enemies, such as aerial or something, it would be a bit different. However, it's just the same enemy, just has more health and bigger numbers of them over time.   I say yes when 90% off, but not full price. It's something most people will get bored of imediately, but I kinda like it. Unfortunately, my friend doesn't seem to so I wont play it ever again anyways.

The game is pretty and interesting, but I found the combat system frustrating.   Pros:  + Interesting main character + Interesting story, though I'm not entirely sold on the ending.   Cons:  + Popelle, who seemed to be the secondary main character, really rubbed me up the wrong way. + It's unclear what the traits do - I finally realised that agility increases the number of attacks per turn, but I'm stuff not sure what dexterity does (crit chance? dodge? Who knows...) + The healing spells depend on you not getting hit for an entire round, but the tanking abilities are next to useless. (You can make up for it with the trap ability, but trap only becomes truly dependable with Oboa.) Everything became a lot smoother once I gave up on the healing abilities and focused on potions with the double effect talent instead. + Maybe I just didn't get the combat system, but the difficulty level seemed to vary from laughingly easy to dead-in-two-turns between fights, for no discernable reason. + New characters start with no skills and levels that are significantly below your existing party members, meaning you have to spend time powerleveling a useless character before you can continue playing. + The airship was buggy and only allowed me to land 5-10 % of the time.

'Hope and dream fill you with determination.'  Why do we like playing games? Is it because we just wanna run away from reality, or be a hero in virtual world, or just need to avoid being bored? After playing undertale, maybe you’re able to answer the question: because games fill us with determination.  Western people do value personality. Their reality shows don’t sell tragic stories, but they do tell stories, just for creating personalities. There can be a cute silly little boy, a wise elder man, a warm-hearted lady, and a mean white girl in a show. Personality is the key to make audience love a person and a show. And when I started playing undertale, the strongest feeling was “what strong personalities!”. Undertale created personality for every single character or monster, and all the personalities are strong which are so memorable. I believe it’s also the main reason why it gets so many positive comments on Steam. You may see a lot of fan art pieces on Instagram and Tumblr which is really rare for an indie game. It means a lot. Strong personalities mixing with simple graphics bring an amazing chemistry. The contrast brings bigger astonishment.  After playing games for so many years, we all have got a “pattern”: kill monsters, gain exp., level up, beat boss, end. So every time we start a new game, we just try to figure out how to attack, jump, beat enemies and what skills we will get after we up the level as soon as we can. In undertale, it seems to create the same atmosphere, and there are “EXP” and “LV” too. It tells you “EXP” stands for experience, and LV represents “LOVE”, so you have to beat monsters, gain “EXP” to rise your “LOVE”. You may meet monsters anytime, and it may be a frightened dog, a sad ghost, or a narcissistic horse. You have four choices: FIGHT, ACT, ITEM, MERCY, and in ACT, you may PET, FLIRT, TALK with monster, just like other RPG games! Your choices really mean a lot in fight, however, like the classic saying gose, you have to “stay determined”, especially when you choose MERCY, you will face a number of attack until the monster gives up fighting. Of course sparing others requires determination, dosen’t it? Sure, you can choose fight as well and kill the monster, even though it’s just an overwhelmed dog, a terrified bee. Playing so many games, we already become numb and ruthless and we don’t regard it weird to kill monsters and gain experience. That’s what we want actually, isn’t it?  You have to be responsible for your choices. In undertale, a little choice will probably result in a completely different end. At the beginning, you will meet Toriel who is gentle, patient, compassionate and lovely. She makes cinnamon pie for you, calls you to check if you’re safe, arrages a room for you... But we all know that we have to keep moving forward and leave the ivory tower she builds for us. So, we’ll start the fight with her. I had no idea how to end the fight for the first time, and I tried to spare her 2 or 3 times but it didn’t work at all. So I guessed I had no option but to kill her. My heart sank deeper and deeper when I saw her HP get lower. “Am I really about to kill her?” I felt horrible but couldn’t stop it so I just kept doing it. When my HP was 2 points, I found her attack was dodging me like a real mom by no means would hurt her child. Finally, I killed her, kept moving on sadly and was thinking about starting over again. After watching some playthrough I noticed that if I had chosen mercy for 20 times, Toriel would let me go then I could move on without killing her. Ultimately, it was all because I didn’t “stay determination”. There were so many cute characters afterwards, dream boyfriend sans, silly and cute THE GREAT PAPYRUS, brave and responsible Undyne, drama queen MTT, and really helpful professor.... The developer designed exclusive lines for every single character which made them alive and vivid. Due to the fight with Toriel, I spared PAPYRUS, gave up killing Undyne. However, it was too late, after killing Toriel, I was already in a hypocritical line. No matter what I did after that, I couldn’t reach the “true end”. In the final fight with Asgore, the developer just slapped in my face again. He told me “EX” was “Execution points” instead of experience points, LOVE represented “level of violence” instead of love itself. That moment, I had to admit it was really an excellent game and started regretting to do what I had done.  Undertale is filled with irony and black humor. It makes players feel empathetic. It wrapped its simple and thin fighting mode with abundant personalities. It’s like watching a movie more than playing a game. After finishing it, players will almost love every monster and can’t wait to play it again!(spare Toriel!) Besides, the soundtrack was amazing too. It really helped us feel immersed.   Anyways,  keep calm and stay determined.  9/10

I had a massive amount of trouble installing this game under Windows 7 64bit. The installer would hang during the UE4 prerequisites installation.  After a couple of hours I worked out that BEFORE you try to install this game, You should download and install the following:  Microsoft .NET Framework 4.6.1 (NDP461-KB3102438-Web) Visual C++ 2015 redistributable x64 (vc_redist.x64) UE4 Prerequisites x64 (ue4prereqsetup_x64)  Make sure to restart windows after each installer.  Now the FPV Air Tracks installer should complete sucessfully.  I've played this game for about 20 mins and I wasn't so impressed... the Video makes it look much more interesting than it really is. Hopefully it'll grow on me once I manage to get the controls set up properly, which at the moment are terrible. Many other bugs.....  Refund pending.

It is often compared to Assassins creed, yet that comparison is a bit belittling to Dishonored in my opinion. Smooth control both out of combat while exploring as well as in combat, realistic detection system and guard ai, and a multitude of different methods both to kill an opponent or complete a mission set Dishonored apart from 'similar' stealth games. Oh and there are magic abilities, which are pretty fun too!

Transformers: Fall of Cybertron is a fun game, and I think that's the reason I don't recommend it. Because while the fast-paced-three dimensional shooting action is great, the single-player campaign is criminally short (heck I think it might even be shorter than 'War'), after you start to have fun with one bot or con you're immediately switched to a different one with different abilities. Multiplayer does sooth the disappointment somewhat, but it doesn't have the same awesome moments like Jazz grappling between sniper points or Vortex swooping down to blindside some Autobots. If there was an in-between 'meh' option I would pick that, although at it's current $50, my thumb is weighed downwards. If you can find it on a big sale, then you should probably get it, it's a blast while it lasts.

app_name: Total War: ROME II - Emperor Edition
review_text: Total War™: Rome II - Emperor Edition is a historical turn based RTS game based in.. Ancient Rome. I thoroughly enjoy playing this game, and perhaps this review will persuade you to try it out and, hopefully, enjoy it.   Rome II is a sequel to the famous Rome: Total War game. It's a part of one of the largest historical strategy game franchises to date. Total War games are similar to the classic board games Risk and Axis and Allies. So if you enjoy playing those games you should feel right at home here. It is true that as of late the Total War franchise has the infamous reputation of rushed releases. However, I find that this game, after over two years of being out, has come a long way. It may still not be what people expected at release, but it's definitely come a long way.  There are mainly two parts of this game, the Grand Campaign and the multiplayer. The Grand Campaign lets you play as multiple types of factions ranging from Rome to barbarian to all sorts of others. Each faction has unique units that all have their strongpoints and weaknesses. When you start off you can come from a small state with a handful of soldiers, to domination of the ancient world. As you make your way from the bottom to the top, you'll have to deal with foreign threats. These threats can range from other nations declaring war on yours all the way to widespread hunger across entire provinces.  A Grand Campaign completed game can last from a couple of days (Speedrunners) to months of hard work and effort. So you should be able to get a large amount of playtime out of this game, if you like it. Grand Campaign playthroughs may or may not get repetitive based on what you classify as repetitive. In my honest opinion the Campaign can have very rich gameplay that does not involve doing the same boring task over and over. The battles against the AI can become a bit repetitive, but that's only if you exploit their dumb nature.  The AI in this game is by no means the best out there. But it's definitely improved from the release. The AI can sometimes be incredibly smart and display great ingenuity, but they have a bad habit of running headlong into a pike phalanx. Not to mention misusing artillery, chariots, and elephants on mroe than one occassion. This doesn't mean the Campaign can't be fun against just AI, it's incredibly fun for me at least, especially on Very Hard or Legendary modes.  The multiplayer. In my eyes this is where Rome II truly shines. The multiplayer is where you can go onto servers or create your own, where you can battle other players with factions of your choosing. This can range from 1v1s all the way up to 4v4s. Connection issues is a common problem among multiplayer games, considering you're playing with people from all around the world. However, the sheer amount of units you can have on one single battlefield when you have multiple factions going against eachother is insane. This can definitely cause major lag and a lot of times people ragequit when it's so bad everythings in slowmotion or the framerate is below 15.  Like I said earlier, each faction has their own unique set of units for their choosing. This makes for very dynamic gameplay especially in multiplayer. No faction is the same. Granted there are very similar factions but they still have their unique ups and downs. With these in mind, you can choose from dozens of factions to find your right playstyle. If it's tanky sword infantry that can still pack a punch, you're in it with Rome. If you like to cripple people with charges, you can go with barbarians or cav heavy factions. If you like to use pikemen to protect valuable skirmishers to pick at enemies from a distance, then you can go greek factions. These are just a handful of examples of how dynamic the multiplayer battle gameplay can be.  You can also play multiplayer campaigns. This goes for quite the interesting time. Play with your friends as allies or enemies. Multiplayer campaigns can be a lot of fun if you can get your friends to play with you, random people joining isn't really recommended as you probably couldn't play an entire campaign playthrough in one session. One of the biggest problems I have with the multiplayer campaign is the fact that when the other players turn cames around you just have to sit there. For 5 to 10 minutes you just sit there doing absolutely nothing. You can use this time to your advantage by trying to help your friend out with what you two should plan with the help of your trusty paintbrushes, but normally you just sit there. Like a lot of multiplayer games, voicechatting can really help. It might also help make the time it takes for the other player to play his turn go by with a bit more excitement.  This game has a serious learning curve to it. So if you really want to get into this game and know how to counter things with the right tools, then it may take you a lot of playtime and a lot of watching videos. This isn't necessarily a bad thing, but for those of you who like to just sit down right away after getting a game and then being good at it in the first 10 hours, then you may be out of luck here. In my opinion the large learning curve just makes for more time to enjoy this game.   The battles. The battles are probably one of the main reasons why you're looking into this game. With thousands of soldiers at your command you can fight equal amounts, smaller amounts, or overwhelming larger amounts of enemy troops in a dynamic game of intense strategy and timing. You'll have to move your troops around to effectively counter the troops they have in a certain place, make sure you have backups, move those backups in when necessary, and strike at the most opportune moments. Learning all of the stats of every unit is a big help. Knowing the stats will greatly benefit what you know will kill and what you know will not kill an enemy unit with the smallest amount of casualties on your end. You can see why there's such a large learning curve now.  Overall this game is relatively well optimized, for my PC the game runs into a bit of trouble when there are 6,000+ units on the battlefield at once. But to be honest, that much is to be expected. At max graphics I can get a steady 50 FPS in normal battles. On the campaign map I get lower frames, around the 30s or so. So if you want solid frames all around, go with a lower graphical setting and you should be fine.  This game is very fun, and I recommend it to you as well. Especially if you're a Strategy game fan.  To Me: 9/10 I love it  To Strategy fans: 9/10 Definitely look into it  To Average gamer: 8/10 This game is a lot of fun!

app_name: Brothers - A Tale of Two Sons
review_text: Brothers - A Tale of Two Sons is an an amazing game filled with beautiful setpieces and an a great focus on storytelling. The story is simple: the mother drowns while her child watches helplessly and the father contracts an unknown illness that requires the brothers to go on a sort of suicide mission to retrieve the cure.  First, the negatives. You control the older brother with the Left Analog and Trigger and the younger one with the Right Analog and Trigger. You can already see that the controls will be awkward. It's almost impossible to control both brothers at the same time, so you'll spend a majority of your time just trying to control the two, rather than figuring out the puzzles. Thankfully, the puzzles are stupidly easily (it's difficult to classify them as puzzles, to be honest) and some of them seem to keep these awkward controls in mind.  Next up, the length. The game took me slightly more that 1.5 hours to beat and it seems pretty clear that either the money ran out or they didn't bother, since the game seemed to end at the apex of the story and the ending, despite being shocking (no spoilers!), didn't seem like it reached its potential.  Nevertheless, the visuals are impressive and there are strategically placed benches where you can sit and admire the gorgeous scenery. Brothers also excels in the emotional aspect: the relationship between the two brothers and their parents is not only demonstrated through very short cutscenes, but also through gameplay, an aspect that can rarely be seen in any other game.  Overall, despite the awkward controls and the disappointing length, the game is more than worth buying simplty for its breathtaking visuals and its incredible way of narrating the story and characters simply through gameplay without any dialogue or tedious cutscenes. It's a great example of showing without telling and I recommend every bit of it.

Now those are the reasons that I liked it, but as far as reasons the average gamer would enjoy, the game is a bit lacking. Combat is simplistic and feels lacking overall. Driving isn't tight and feels to chaotic to have an arcadey feel. The story is pretty ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ overall and just seems like random crap thrown in to get all these people in one place to do some voice acting. If you're a child of the 80-90's and love Metal and don't mind dropping a few bucks, get it. If you're looking for a new open world game to explore and have fun in, give it a miss.

Warframe- or as we call it, ninja-sliding simulator 2014, is a great game. Visually, its absolutely amazing, and the art style is very distinct, with very identified personality between the factions. The combat may be glitchy at times, but it flows and shows a degree of freedom seldomly seen in other games. Movement is also very in-depth, wall runs, slide-jumps, and they can all chain together so that you dont just run anymore, you run, slide, jump, land, slide, attack slide, wall boost, land with an almighty smash of your melee weapon. By this time you commit these moves to muslce memory, your left pinky would be substiantially strong than your right one, so if you were plan to thumbwars with your pinky, i'd highly recommend this game. Game play currently is very diverse, with several game modes: assisination or sabotage missions for those who enjoy Hitman; survival or defence missions for those who have nothing better to think about than SHOOT THOSE DUDES for 30 minutes+; boss fights for your inevitable grind for blueprints. Not to mention all the weapons and warframe you have, once you get to the 400hr+ mark, you'd have more suits and accessories than a spoiled-rotten 16 year old with her own creditcard. The bad- unfortunately, being any hardcore tenno require a lot of GRIND, as every weapon, warframe, needs to be grinded to level 30, and then repeat if you wish to forma(make it better). Not to mention all the grinding for material needed to craft the weapons and these awesome suits. Of course, it being a MMO, grinding is tolerated, and since I have 600 hrs in this game, certainly entertaining enough.  The really bad- MONEY. credits and orokin cells are easy enough to grind for, but to get to the real end game(OWN EVERYTHANG), you need PLATINUM. certainly, you can now sell blueprints and mods, but that's very inefficient way to get plat. So if you are willing to commit to the game, dunk 50 bucks in the game during a deal, its well worth your time.

Wall running, sliding and shooting, energy for specials. These are things I like seeing in games cause it shows that good games can be made like warframe. If more game creators could look at this game they could learn a thing or two. I even run this on all low at 1280x720 and this game still looks amazing. All I hope to see is some vs mode stuff like TDM, and Capture The Flag. These would make the game even better cause then everyone would be fighting people with a different play style due to what warframe they choose. Give this game a try and I garuntee you will like it.

But unfortunately for me I feel like the whole experience is dragged through the mud by a seriously uninspired and dull combat system. And there is a lot of combat in this game.  You might have a different experience if you enjoy strategy games but as someone who is not too fond of them the 'turn()'-system which the combat is more or less entierly based around just feels tedious to me, killing enemies doesn't feel as satisfying as it should, in fact it felt like a chore and worst of all it makes the game feel like it's progressing far slower than it actually does, which is weird for a game as short as this. Transistor is a work of art. Visuals, the soundtrack, the story.. it's a beautiful game with only one downfall; it's too short. I purchased it for the PS4, and then again on Steam because I wanted the soundtrack and the price difference was minimal, due to the winter sale. That's how much I adore this game. No other has pulled me into the story so completely.

Though whatever I can and will say about this game has already been said before and maybe better, I'll still offer my two cents on it. For starters, it's fantastic. This is a game dripping in emotion and atmosphere. This is a game that understands what art is. This is a game that soldifies the concept, shaky at best in the eyes of many slim eyed critics, that video games can be both be art and beautiful. Lately, this archaic idea is being crumbled and rightfully so, as indie games out to tell stories get the recognition they deserve.    I'll save my breath on describing the game plotwise. It's been covered. The characters are whole and rich and the voice actors great. The art style is, to understate, radiating out with severely serene, colorful and stylized buildings, backdrops, backgrounds, skies, streets, and everything in between. It's all flawless, as if one could ever hope to scrutinize something like this. The soundtrack is stunning and makes the game hit home. Music is important, very important to a medium like this. It's the same case with film. When films or games misuse their soundtrack, whether or not it had potential at all, it does more than damage the integrity; it creates monotony. Supergiant understands this. Supergiant did not fail. Supergiant, in fact, succeeded, a little bit too much almost.   The game also utilizes subtlelty well. Game mechanics are not shoved down your throat. The UI does not rip away one's attention. It never reminds you you're playing a videogame. Instead, it's convincing you that for these few short hours, you are in Cloudbank. And though it a love story and a revenge story, its take on both is unique for it is both noir and painting. The freedom with build creation was well done too, as choices matter here. Many diverse playstyles can be achieved and in my experience, they can all thrive.   And finally, the game's actualy gameplay is great. The planning phase, akin to V.A.T.S. in Fallout, makes this game authentic in its difficulty and neccesity for planning. Getting mileage out of your skills is the best way to succeed in Transistor. It's certainly not Dark Souls, but neither is it a Call of Duty campaign on easy.   And for all this, it even managed to give us a story with meaning. Transistor's worth your money, people. There's a lot of ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥t out there these days. And while the viscosity of this said ♥♥♥♥ hasn't changed, the volume has increased exponentially. Sometimes gems come out from the muck though. And Transistor is a diamond.

review_text: I grabbed this game during a sale for around 4 euro. I saw that other people have problem with uPlay, but I seriously do not understand why.  (TIP) Only problem which occoured only once for me is that it can start with some kind of 'slow-motion' effect, which will affect everything... I did a level with that, and it got me furious and quit after the cut, and that level was not saved due to my impatience. I played that level again, and took about 3 minutes, while with slow-motion it was 15... so be sure to restart the game if you experience the slowmotion effect.  All in all: 10/10

There are several types of enemies, but there's no difference (I could see, at least) in each one's play style. Since there are 4 bosses and you can pretty much beat the game before the first, the last 75% of the game is just repetitively navigating around a square map to the four corners to beat the 'legendary pirates'.   This game would be a looooooot better if there was more variability and the bosses weren't all all the same level, but rather you fought each one in a row and each was harder than the last. Resident Evil Remake Remastered is a survival horror game, created by Capcom and the director is the father of survival horror himself, Shinji Mikami (Creators of Resident Evil Zero - 4, The Evil Within and Vanquish). This game is a remastered or reworked, placing with high quality textures, volumetric lighting, shadows and other graphics fidelity related improvements (animation and the character details, more so). It's the same Resident Evil Remake from Gamecube. To be honest, I have never played the Gamecube version, I played the Playstation version and it's massive difference between Gamecube and Playstation version. Other than that, it is the same Gamecube version with better graphical changes. The gameplay is still the same old classic; item management, riddles to solve, exploration, heavy backtracking, fixed camera angles to create suspense at every corner and ammo conservative. The game is still scary, even I got jumped out of my seat when the dog came into the window (Oh, that classic jumpscare still got me XD). There are some technical issues, where the background music keeps cutting out for a second (it happens rarely, so it's not a big deal), the movement controls changed due to fix camera angle changes and the controls for PC are not recommended (I think you can change the controls if you can but aiming is annoying though). Other than that, it's a great survival horror game, I miss these kind of survival horror games. There are multiple endings to get an outcome based on your choice you have made.   + Pros

Enjoyable with some frustrating elements.  The game is interesting enough and has plenty of atmosphere. But it's a weird mix between a brutal unforgiving platformer, a story-driven puzzle platformer (think The Swapper) and a keyboard/mouse sidescrolling shooter in the vein of Abuse. All enjoyable in their own right, but the mix doesn't really work for me.  I believe a platformer should be entirely playable with a gamepad. This game doen't support one, but requires a keyboard for movement and a mouse to aim. Not to mention quick reflexes because some of the enemies pop out and are on top of you in no time, giving you precious little time to aim.  It also doesn't help that the mouse aim is not very predictable. In some screens you can traverse the entire screen, and your cursor will remain in exactly the same spot in absolute level-coordinates. Yet, other times you climb a ladder, the entire screen scrolls, and your mouse cursor travels along with you.I'd have preferred a more twin-stick friendly 'always relative aim' scheme, allowing for gamepad support in addition to keyboard/mouse. The game in its current form is not very couch friendly. You can make it work with a Steam Controller, but it's not ideal.  Despite the strange mix of genres and the slightly bizarre choice of control scheme, the game remains very enjoyable. Sometimes frustratingly so due to an overreliance on narrow platforms. Unless you're a seasoned platformer, you'll die a lot in this game.  Most people complain about the puzzles being too hard. I can't speak for the puzzles deeper into the game, but so far I found them all very logical, and some felt more like chores than puzzles. For me it's the more hardcore platform and combat elements that give rise to frustration.  The pixel art neither adds nor detracts from the gameplay, so it's unnecessary, merely a design choice by the developers. But it's well executed and gives the game a retro feel. From a gameplay and aesthetic point of view, the comparison to Another World is not undeserved.  Pros: * Atmosphere * Well done pixel art * Semi-challenging platform action * Homouristic pop culture references (Major Tom, PEANUT civilisation, Red Dwarf, ...)  Cons: * Requires keyboard/mouse, no couch platformer * Some parts rely too much on platform-spamming * Some required objects are easy to miss  Minor nitpicks: * Use of the cliché 'enemy that can be stunned but not killed' * Enemies that respawn every time you pass their spawn point

Wings of Vi, Where do I start with this one? This game certainly was an adventure!   First I have to say the most important thing is that 95%+ of the population has not beat this game as of a couple months of this game being released. The game is extremely difficult even on Angel Mode (Easy) for a majority of players, and Mortal Mode (Regular) is much more difficult. Take into account this game was made by the person who made 'I wanna be the Boshy' which is known as one of the most notoriously difficult games to date.  With that said, this game is absolutely stunning.    * The Controls are great, they are very responsive with both a keyboard and controller.  * The Music is fantastic, I ended up buying the Album and I rarely buy music, Ashton Morris definitely put his heart and soul into his tracks.  * The games length is quite long, and with the difficulty it will give you many of hours of fun (and agony). Every boss is very unique and most of them are very difficult so expect to die a lot until you get their attack patterns memorized.  * There are quite a lot of secrets in the game which all give you garments your character can wear, also defeating bosses and getting achievements earn you them too. There are also several hidden weapons to find in the game, though are not needed to complete it.  * The story is decent, nothing extremely special but I didnt jump into this game expecting a deep story but it gets the job done. Pretty much 'kill the bad guy save the world' kind of deal.   Here are my bad experiences with the game.  * There are a few parts in the game that will legitimately make you angry and want to quit unless you stay persistant and learn to perfect an obstacle until you get past it. There are a few parts that require perfect timing and execution so you will die hundreds of times on some parts of the game.  * Also, the last boss is one of the most frustrating experiences I have had in a game in a very long time. Every other boss you progress further as you learn their move sets and for the most part are very enjoyable, but not the final boss. You will bash your face on it for a long time, nearly everyone I have spoken too will agree that it is not a very enjoyable encounter at all; to which many have quit and not finish the game.    I seriously recommend this game to anyone who enjoys an extremely challenging platformer. If you do pick up Wings of Vi I truely hope you enjoy this game as much as I did!  Cheers!

Aero's Quest is one of these titles that the more you play it the more it gets better. The very fist impression might not be the best: everything looks fine but nothing really stands out. When you start playing it seems a very simple and generic platformer until the game starts to kick. The very first zone is just a tutorial and the second zone (a variation of the first forest) brings out more the platformer part rather than the puzzle part. When you get in the 3rd zone instead (the volcano) everything starts to change: you see new obstacles, the levels are not linear anymore and few more puzzles are coming out.  The game is a big 'crescendo' till the 5-6 zone (where I got so far) where puzzles and platforms are mixing in a great way. Aero's Quest is hard, very hard: one of the most challenging games of the year and it is long, very long: 101 levels to play in two different modes. I have to mention the sound: it's awesome! Every zone have its own music and they are all very good. If you pass the first 10 minutes and the first 20 deaths Aero's Quest can be really a big surprise.

review_text: I love this game. I hate this game.  This game will RANDOMLY crash to desktop. Hours after dealing with this POS you will goto 'subsim' forums. There the veterans have common knowledge of how to avoid crashes...ie, restart game 3-4 times during a patrol. Save all the time. Never save over a save. Never save with AI rendered. Never save near a sub. Don't get on the hydrophone with a sub near by. - STILL, you randomly crash to desktop. This games loading time is up there with CIV5...I kept on going though because I REALLY liked the game and put up with the random crash nonsense as well as minor in-game bugs...  Then you realize that there is a group of very dedicated modders (via subsim forums). You check out some pictures, you watch some videos, you get excited.  You realize that out of the multi-step process to mod the game YOU CANNOT MOD THE STEAM VERSION.  You buy a DL version or DVD version via ebay or amazon. LETS MOD! The game will be 9000 times more epic and not crash all the time weeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeeee!  WRONG.  After about 15 hours, I learned the ins and outs of modding SH5. I tried 2 different popular megamods. Random crashes STILL. Also it should be noted I have never had a worse modding experience. There is tons of information on subsim forums. I spent hours reading into the matter. I followed guides to the T. The game is a serious POS. It is still a POS modded. No offense to the modders of course. You can't make gold out of ♥♥♥♥ right?  ALSO NOTE: On steam you are buying the 'gold edition' - HOWEVER since I purchased via amazon there is OBVIOUSLY different/more content. Conclusion; STEAM GOLD EDITION IS NOT REALLY GOLD EDITION.   This game is cheap. Go ahead. You'll get your money out of it in gameplay hours.  The amount of time I have wasted though is not worth it in the end.   UBI sucks. The characters can be pretty strange, also the story, but it actually is part of the fun of the game. It has some dense atmosphere, most of the part, so you'll get into the game.  When the game finishes with the sixth you can buy it, and, well, the bundle is more recommended since it can be expensive to buy the chapters alone. If you buy the bundle, the sixth chapter will also be available for you. But, it still does not have any date to launch. So be on alert and only buy it if you don't mind waiting for the last chapter.

The base game is good and in the moment you'll find yourself caught up in the story. There is a very meaty challenege for anyone trying to 100% the game. I did it on PS3 and really enjoyed it.  Gameplay can be broken down into 4 parts:  Speed: You're Sonic/Shadow you run through levels. The best part of the game.  Shooting: Tails/Eggman are in mechs and you have a lock on lazer cannon the idea of each stage being how many enemies you can target at once before losing the lock, pretty neat once you get into it.  Treasure Hunting: Knuckles/Rouge fly around contained levels looking for 3 of something. You can climb, glide, dig, it's fun to just run around as them really.  There's also a few mods out that are pretty great and I highly recommend hitting up Sonic Retro for them.  Now for a controversial opinion: Chao Garden sucks. A lot of people will try to tell you the chao portion is something special. It was easily the poorest portion of the game by far. It's tedious, it's annoying, it's not deep or interesting it's a time waster through and through.  Essentially all the enemies in the game drop animals or energy cores that you can give to the chao leveling them up so they can better race or fight. The racing and fighting however are slow paced affairs you have no impact on other than cheering making the whole affair a massive time waster.   In short: Mechanically solid game with an excellent aesthetic, it has a tacked on time waster you need to 100% the game but you can ignore it really.

I have spent a large amount of time on this game (compared to the amount of extra time I could afford). This is the best large scale first person shooter you will find out there.  Pros: The game is a 1v1v1 with each faction able to have 1000 players at once over four different continents. This game is balanced enough that if you know what you're doing, you can have a lot of fun and enjoy yourself immensely. You usually can't do very much by yourself, it's a team game that requires team play. The game is gorgeous and will quench your thirst for graphics. Classes are balanced perfectly and they all deserve to be played.  Cons: It's hard to learn what to do and where to go when you first join the game. Sometimes I feel like there is no goal in the game, but then the goal of this game and like it should be for any other game is to enjoy yourself and have fun while crushing your enemies. You end up staying too late playing it.

Pros -Wide range of heists -Good selection of DLC if you wish to expand -Caters for the single player and multiplayer gamers -Caters different Playing styles, Loud and stealthy -Good range of weapons -Overall great game and will keep you busy for hours on end  Cons -'THE DRILL IS JAMMED SOMEBODY FIX IT !!!!'  10/10 from me  *Edit*  So after the most recent updates I have a few more cons to add to this review, Do I still enjoy the game?, Yes, but something doesn't seem right at all.  Why? well the recent updates have severely affected the whole feeling of the game, Through DLC I have gathered loads of weapons which at first gave some degree of an advantage, for example LMG's, these were a godsend as I a prefer to play this with a loud tactic, I enjoy that side of the game, Stealth is a fantastic option but every now and again its fun to pop off more rounds than you can physically count. Well recently it seems like an LMG is just on oversized BB gun and my .45 is a pretty much a pocket sized handcannon the balance is completley off and bulldozers? nearly takes a whole clip from a 50 cal to end them whats that all about ?.   It is not totally unplayable though, just a bit more excrutiating and painful.  If there was anything that could be changed it would be to revert the weapons back to the way they were before, I cannot speak for the whole community but I think this would make a lot of peoples day.  To sum up yes I am still going to play this game and I do enjoy it, but I may be less inclined to expand further in terms of DLC until there has been some changes made to these rather extreme nerfs and buffs.

You will never play a game that feels or looks anything like Half Life 2, it is that special.

10/10, this game is quite awesome and makes you feel like a battlewarrior fighting through group of enemies alone. More reviews @  TheVideogameBacklog.com   If you’ve bought Watch_Dogs and just have it sitting in your Steam or game disc collection, I’ll skip to the end for you: play the game.  I’m a fairly huge fan of open-world games where you can just go and do whatever you want. I guess I’ll specify that it’s really only when you can do what you want to do out of the list of things to do in any order. Watch_Dogs gave me a lot more freedom to be able to access my inner paladin more than almost any other game. When you get reports of a crime about to happen and you can u-turn and stop it. It was freaking sweet (even if it got a little played out near the end).  Watch_Dogs basically starts with a job gone bad and hit on you gone wrong. You end up on a full city run-a-muck to figure out what in the world is going on and who’s to really blame for the death of your niece that died on the hit gone wrong (basically). Watch_Dogs has a few twists and turns that kept me interested the whole way through. Well, enough to have me come back and finish the game. Granted, there were a lot of interrupts in my playthrough, but sometimes that spells a non-finished game for yours truly. This was obviously not the case here.  There are a ton of things to experience besides the main story (which is why it took me 72 hours to complete the game after I stopped myself from doing all the random stuff). You can investigate people smuggling, stop crimes, play AR games and find QR codes hidden at funky angles. One of the things I didn’t do too much of were the Fixer jobs where you help whoever has a problem that needs “fixing”. The issue I had with them is sometimes there are good causes, but more often than not you’re helping some less-than-savory type of person and that didn’t jive with my good-guy approach to gaming.  How you approach the action in Watch_Dogs is mostly up to you. You can go for the stealthy or full guns-blazing approach. The only issue here is the same issue I had with Deus Ex: Human Revolution before and after they patched the bosses. You could play through the whole game and not kill anyone, but as soon as you got to a boss you had to have a shootout which killed the completely stealthy approach they were going for. Even after they patched Human Revolution you still ended up killing the boss in a cut-scene. While Watch_Dogs dances on the same line, the situations that “force” you to use your guns that aren’t attached to your fists seem to be less forced and completely suited to the situations. Whether the situation is that you’re suddenly and completely out-numbered or something extremely horrible happens to a buddy. When you are put into the position of whipping out the pistol it feels exactly like the right thing to do. I was still reluctant to do so since I’ve spent hours sneaking around gang hang-outs taking everyone down with stealth, but it still didn’t really break the immersion.  I have only two gripes with my time with Watch_Dogs and one of them isn’t the graphical settings. When Watch_Dogs came out on the PC, it was pretty buggy if you played on the high-end settings so people had to set the specs down and that was a huge negative for some people. It didn’t affect me as much, but I really had to turn the settings down when I did my CToS challenge on Twitch. I didn’t have the processing power to stream and play the game at the same time. It’s probably time for an upgrade and that’s completely on me.  The driving was such a pain in the butt. I’m a traditionalist when it comes to PC gaming. I love my mouse and keyboard and will usually prefer them over any other option. Most of my shooters don’t really have driving in them or it’s a tank and then it’s fine. In actual driving simulations there is a gradual increase in speed that a keyboard cannot duplicate, but a controller is specifically designed for. I have to use a controller whenever the driving starts. I’ve been able to play some of the game with a controller since I usually just sneak and melee, but still having to jump back and forth since I am more comfortable with the mouse and keyboard was kind of a pain. I have no fix for this because I understand what they were going for and you can’t achieve it with a keyboard. I still lament because I suck at shooting/aiming with dual joysticks.  The other and biggest gripe was the hacking. Now, it’s cool that you could walk around and hack people and get more music and stuff, but stealing is where I draw the line most of the time. That is, of course, unless you’re stealing from some scumbag. What’s funky in Watch_Dogs is that while you have all this hacking power and can do all sorts of cool stuff, you can only hack 45-ish% of the people walking around and it’s completely random. What? Do so many of the people walking around have a way to beat me at my ability to hack their smartphone?!? Really? That’s just dumb… if I walk by a some jerk that uploads child porn and can’t hack and steal money from him/her, but then ten feet later walk by someone who gives money to homeless shelters if up for grabs? Why not everyone? That would make more sense. That’s some seriously messed up mechanics. Maybe they didn’t expect me to read all the little stuff, but… I did. Bad call Ubi, that’s just a dumb and crappy decision.  Score: Tasty!  My goodness, what a ride. Walking around, stopping crimes, racing people online and stealthy gameplay? Watch_Dogs was easily one of my favorite games at the time and when I finished it recently I have to say that I still enjoy my play time. That story was pretty good for one of those generic action movie flicks. So it wasn’t super deep, but it had some “twists” and while I may have seen them coming, I enjoyed taking part in it. I did feel a little cheated by some of the mechanics built into the game, but in the end that didn’t ruin the experience. Some even added to it. This was a game I would say shouldn’t be missed by almost anyone. Action/FPS/stealth fans are all welcome here. I bought it for the straight $60 when it launched and while I couldn’t play on ultra settings, it was money well spent.

Shadowgrounds is a game that belongs in the 1990s. I downloaded it thinking it'd provide a bit of local co-op entertainment, after an hour of configuration to get 2 xbox controllers to work in tandum I discovered that it is a complete waste of time and money.

At first I kept thinking, 'what am I supposed to do?' I just kept going up and up, expecting that at SOME point there would be more. So again, I kept going up, and up. What I hadn't realized until I finished the game was that all the questions I was asking 'Where am I going' 'What is going on' 'What is the point' are all the questions that the developers MEANT for me to ask. The fly protagonist just keeps flying up, runs into some trouble along the way (wind blowing it back, leaves falling on top of it), but the fly just keeps going. Then, finally, death.   This game (whether you want to call it that or not) is very beautifully made. It was designed to make the player understand that we as humans are just like the Fly; enduring the trials that life throws at us, but destined to push forward, until we eventually die.   It was relaxing and eye-opening at the same time. Great soundtrack, beautiful artwork, and it allows you to conclude the story with your own words. I would recommend spending the 10 minutes to experience it.

At this time - anno domini 2014 - my Shmup collection is starting to be pretty much complete.   So which game shines the brightest? Well that would have to be this old runt of a shooter game.   This game is the closest equivalent of giving a shotgun to a hobo with crooked teeth and thinking it's a great idea. It stinks, it's ugly but damn it's awesome at the same time.   What to expect when you fire this game up: Weird practice area where you can test gun setups. then a crappy looking shop that got a TV-Shop like selection of guns.  So grab some guns, test them if you like and go do some missions. Early missions will be hard as hell and the difficulty level will be kept quite high for quite some time - until you either read from guides what you should concentrate on - or buy and test guns until you find the ones that match your style. Difficulty actualy goes down as go further into the game and kit your new ships better and learn the ropes. New challenging bosses come to yank the difficulty up but you are prepared. Or should be. Did i mention this game is quite hard - but fair if you do your testing/homework on the testing arena.   Still have not completed the game but i do seem to be 2/3 trough at this time and it's been an awesome ride. I also expect to re-play this game at some point with different approach to loadouts.   Best things last: this game is dirt cheap, is often discounted or found in the bundles. If you like shumps you really do MUST have this game in your library. It really is that good that i will from now on use this game as a reference when comparing games.

Great little game. I played the heck out of it on Android, and decided to get this on Steam even though I basically completed the game on Android. Decent PC port I'd have to say, though it is a fairly basic game as far as PC games are concerned with a focus on gameplay.  I'd compare it most to Frozen Synapse, though this with a real-life visual interface makes it better for me and it is also much less complex to get into. Some elements of XCOM thrown in for good measure as well.   Lots of content, that's one thing for sure. To merely complete all the missions (7 locations, five maps per location, 3 scenarios per map), you have 105 missions to play. Which is a lot. Missions aren't super long. The earlier maps are smaller, while as you progress they get much bigger.  Lots of different weapons customization, and the 5-point difficulty scale adds quite a significant amount of depth to the game. There are six different classes, though I must admit I never fully utilized each class to its advantage in the mobile version so this is something I seek to correct on my current PC playthrough.  At the end of the day, Breach & Clear is a game that would delight tactical strategy gamers. It can be played in short bursts, rewards planning (and risks) and has a lot of content to offer. So much of content I am sure you'll never play all the levels in the game! $15 may be a tad too high an asking price, but the $10 it is on launch sale now seems like a good trade-off.   Recommended!

Play: It works like XCOM 1. If you liked XCOM 1 you probably will like XCOM 2.  Story: XCOM 1 was more interesting. XCOM 2 is boring.  Now time has past and the Aliens are actually building good cities, but XCOM is racist. Too much Xenophobia.   Human DNA, for real - is a mess. The common diseases and cancers prove that. If Aliens took the time and energy to cross the vastness of space - it probably is for their benefit, not ours. But, as upgraded and integrated humans into an expanded Galactic society, obviously they will join the Mutons, Sectoids, etc - all the other species. As long as people are free to marry and have human children, and - if they decide they don't want to be a GMO person - then no problem. If humans are integrated into the ships crew and also can travel and explore space, that is good.   If aliens invade and bring total genocide to our species - that would be very bad.  Humans of course would object to genocide. Fair enough.  XCOM 2 would have been more interesting - as the exact same story,  if and only if - you could also choose to play the ADVENT side, fighting against the human terrorists. Stopping the human attacks against the ADVENT cities, etc.  We already played the Human side in XCOM 1. Playing as the Alien team would be far more interesting in XCOM 2, with gathering resources, building cities, restoring utilities, food, water, etc. and construction of ship yards to manufacture new UFOs etc...   The goal of the Alien team would be to take over the planet and add it's species to the fleet: welcome to the Glactic Empire, humans ! :-)

This is a fantastically atmospheric, disturbing and thought-provoking game. The sotry is amazing, graphics crisply stylized, and gameplay is hide to survive horror. I will not lie, I played the second half of the game with the Wuss Mode mod on, in order to make the games enemies non-vioilent. I do not feel that this deterred the experience, rather it made me fall in love with the games ambiance, atmosphere and story telling all the more. Every note, computer screen or audio log is purposeful and drive the game forward. The world feels real, believable and lived-in.   If you are a horror game weakling like me, there are options to make them game bearable, which is perfect because the story is the best element in this package. It is extremely challenging to those who desire it, but I would recommend this game to anyone who likes high-concept sci-fi stories, the Alien film aesthtic, or horror games.
