My new rule is any game that involves jumping through firey hoops to get working automatically gets a bad review. This is one of those such cases. Kinda like GTA IV.  There really are no excuses. There's hundreds of games that come out all the time that work fine on all types of computers... There is no reason one should have to go on a hunt for the Holy Grail just to get the framerate normal and eliminate input lag. No reason at all.  The racing itself can be fun, albeit difficult. I was first turned onto this game from the PS4 demo (and should I have stuck with the console version?) then got it on here and the headache started.  And once you do get it running it's got some design flaws. I don't usually complain about loading screens but why is there the need to interupt the loading screen to ask you to confirm the bike you want to ride that you already confirmed earlier, just to go back to the same loading screen for the same amount of time again? And this is on top of the other loading screen that doesn't look like a loading screen that can make one wonder if their game is frozen up.  Anyway, just take a look at the reviews... Apparently there's also some save problems and other issues running rampant that haven't been fixed. This game's a mess and I would recommend avoid or stick with the console version or try a cracked version (I ain't afraid to say that in cases like this cause this is ridiculous).

Far too short, pretty poor performance and horrible cube throwing mini games. These 3 things completely spoil the game, which does have some pretty cool features/aspects. The visuals are overall pretty great, from the scenery to the robot(s) and their brilliant eyes. The story and voices are pretty good, some good puzzles too.   Framerate reugularly dips well below comfortable levels on a pretty decent rig (8 core cpu @ 5ghz, 16Gb DDR2400, 512Gb SSD, MSI GTX 980). Entire game took 4 hours for me to complete from start to finish and could easily be finished quicker, feels like the beggining of a game that was cut majorly short and had a slap-dash ending thrown in. The cube throwing mini games are atrocious! (3 or 4 in the game). Every one the same just with different colours and every one as bad as the last. After the first one you will find yourself simply falling into the water and jumping up and down, just to speed up the process of failing and then being offered the 'skip mini game' option.  Its a massive shame as i instantly fell in love with this game when i first seen it about a year ago, it hinted at a lot more of an open world exploration puzzle game when in reality you get a yurt(tent) and a very small abandoned fairground with a couple of hundred meters worth of empty grasslands in between.  Cannot reccomend this at its current price, feels like an unfinished game that had completely 'out of character' block throwing mini games to replace any meaningful exploration/puzzles. Might be worth checking out if you see it on sale at 60% off or more.

This is by far the best Total War game I have ever played. While I completely understand that people are mad about the DLC (too expensive, should have been in the game at launch, etc.), I do not feel like it justifies putting out so many negative reviews about the base game itself.   The factions that are currently in the game are a lot of fun to play with. I love the fact that each faction plays differently, which is something that was lacking in previous titles imo. Battles are much more interesting now that there are so many different types of units on the field (think monsters, flying cavalry and others). The only thing I don't like is the way sieges are handled now. They are heavily focused on getting up and over the walls as quickly as possible. You're also limited to attacking one side of the fortress and battles in small towns no longer exist as they are moved to a normal battlefield. Your lords and heroes (generals and agents in previous games) are actually forces to be reckoned with in this game. As usual you can level them and choose their skills, but the skill trees are much more in depth than before. You can also choose to level an agent as a fighter and embed him in your army, making him a very effective addittion to the battlefield. Lords absolutely kick ♥♥♥ on the field! In previous TW games I would keep my general back because he was useless in combat and losing him was too great a risk. In this game I actively put him forward and he can take on the world.  Are there annoyances? Of course there are. The game suffers from glitches such as the reinforcement bug, where your reinforcements appear in the most illogical places on the battlefield, pretty much destroying your tactics. the spreading Vampiric corrruption seems to have issues every now and then (though that could be just me) and I'm sure there's more. But after the massive dissappointment that was Rome II (never played Attila), this game has been a great comeback. Up until the latest patch I have had little issues with crashes, desyncs or other gamebreaking bugs. Currently I'm experiencing a few random crashes but I'm sure that will be fixed shortly.   I could go on about this game for a while longer but I believe my message is clear: this game is amazing and you shouldn't write it off because of some pricey DLC. I myself have not bought any DLC yet (barring Chaos Warrios which I got with the game) and the game is keeping me entertained still!

I think after spending 200+ hours on this game, it's time to write a review. You guys ok with this? If not, so sorry (sarcasm)  The PC version of FFVII! You start off as Cloud, an edgy, roided up, Ex-SOLDIER mercenary. You are hired by AVALANCHE to rig one of the mako reactors to explode. You do this. Easy stuff. Story continues (I'm not going to spoil anything. Just look at this review and determine if you want to buy it.)  You will find enemies that you will absolutely hate. I'm quite sure my least favorites were the parasites and Master Tonberry. The story will tug a few heartstrings. I choked up a bit when a couple things happened. There'll be some twists and turns that you might not expect (Unless you were played the original. Then enjoy this masterpiece again, friend.) There'll be some goofs and gaffs. The characters are unique and their dialogue is entertaining.     DON'T TOUCH THE RED THING IN THE DESERT.

Garry's Mod is without a doubt the best game on Steam I own. You can spend countless hours playing and still find new and interesting things to do. Almost anything can be created whether you build it in Sandbox, or code it with LUA, the possibilities are endless.  Garry's Mod ships with two default gamemodes, Sandbox and Trouble in Terrorist town. Sandbox is simply what you make of it. The main point to sandbox is to create new contraptions, and play around with them. Sandbox has no real goal to it unless you make one yourself. And the other gamemode, Trouble in Terrorist Town, actually has a point to it. In Trouble in Terrorist Town you spawn randomly as a Traitor, Innocent, or Detective. The Traitors goal is to try and eliminate the Innocent and Detectives, while the Innocent and Detectives goal are to find out who the Traitor(s) are, and try to eliminate them instead!  Garry's Mod also supports custom gamemodes. Every other gamemode, besides Sandbox and Trouble in Terrorist town have been made by the community. There are many different gamemodes that have been created, ranging from Sandbox and Trouble in Terrorist town spin-offs, to roleplay, death match, survival, and more! Finding a new gamemode to play is as easy as opening the in-game server browser and joining a server.  Every server in Garry's Mod is run by the community, there are no server's hosted by the games creators. Server's run any gamemode they choose, and how they choose. Servers are able to add addons, created by the community, to make the experience more enjoyable for everyone.  Addons are another thing that Garry's Mod shines at. Addons are made by the community and usually help to heighten your overall fun while playing. Thousands and thousands of addons of every sort have been created. There are addons ranging from roller coaster creators, new and different weapons and tools, new maps, and almost anything you can think of!  Overall, I believe that Garry's Mod can be an enjoyable game for anyone. What you get out of playing Garry's Mod is what you make it. Since Garry's Mod has no pre-define goals, you can really do just about anything, and then some. Garry's Mod is worth the amazingly cheap price tag.

This Zombie Apocalypse is a FPS shooter, which im happy to say has full controller support. You can wield a shotgun, machine gun, handgun or sniper rifle the first two have upgraded versions (the autoshotgun is the best) on top of the guns you get to carry throwables like molotovs and pipe bombs, all to destroy the endless hordes of zombies and anything else you may encounter. Incase the action gets to thick for you on expert, The game hosts 4 different difficultys. if its to hard just drop it down level, The game try’s to keep you on the edge of your seat by introducing new enemies (special infected) randomly through the stages, which can be the most annoying thing when playing on harder difficultys, but makes the game alot more fun because they always seem to jump out unexpectedly. Health is very important, and the game works it in well, each player starts with a medkit which should be used wisely, you can also carry pills (need to be scavenged) which give you a minor health boost temporarily for when your on your ropes, these things come heavily into play the higher you set the difficulty. Just briefly wanted to touch on the games players A.I = USELESS, its nigh on impossible to do a level on expert when your playing alone. The replay value is great. and the achievements can have you doing levels over and over, the best part without doubt is the co-op. playing a 4 player game with friends trying to shoot your way through levels on expert is great fun. even when you ruin the session by getting your friends and yourself killed. If you like FPS that are fast paced and relentless this could be for you. P.S dont startle the witch. 5 out of 5 from me.

First off, my credentials:   I first played Duke Nukem in the form of Duke Nukem 2 Shareware on my AST Advantage 386mhz computer running on MS-DOS and Windows 3.1, shortly after, I went to spend the night at a friends house - he had 'Duke Nukem 3D', It was a shooting game he said, like Wolfenstein 3D or Blake Stone, but better. With ♥♥♥♥ and explosions.   Well being that was just reaching puberty this sounded wonderful. I was hooked, like everyone who played DN3D I was a fan for life. To this day I can go back and enjoy it the same as I did on day one. But this isn't about DN3D, this is about Duke Nukem Forever. And the introduction is the base problem.   I just drowned in nostalgia over Duke, and expected a game in development hell for over a ♥♥♥♥ing decade was going to be anything similar. I didn't just buy Duke Nukem Forever, I pre-ordered. I spent a whopping amount of money on the initial release on console, (Xbox 360), only to be greeted with a two weapon limit, dodgy graphics and horrible load times that made Gen 1 PS1 games look fast. It was awful.   I sold Duke Nukem Forever soon after reaching the 'Octaking' boss, but was gifted the game and DLC by a long time friend who wanted to make sure 'I gave it a fair shot'   He was right.   The PC version of DNF is substantially better. Does this mean it's a great game? No. It's not. But it's something many games fail to be these days. Fun. It's a good game. Not great. Good. It's like 7 dollar 4 hour porno DVDs you buy from the gas station with a pack of jerky and a six pack. You know it's not going to be the prettiest or cleanest thing, but you get your fun out of it and you didn't have to spend a lot.   If Duke Nukem Forever had been released in it's current form with DLC for a lower price point no one would be complaining as much as they are. But first impressions are everything. And I can honestly say - yeah. I hated it when it came out. I hated the turret sections (Though, I like the monster truck, Gearbox driving is never a bad time) I hated the attempts to out-edge the original Duke Humor (The twins are just outright cringe worthy). Shadow Warrior is a much better example of how to update a classic FPS to the modern day. Same with Rise of the Triad. I just feel like Duke Nukem got too big for it's britches.   If I were to rate the original game, no DLC, no patches. It's a 1/10. Easily. It's up there with ♥♥♥♥ games like Cosmic Race or Bubsy 3D.   But the PC version, with improved textures and lighting, better weapon capacity, and overall a more pleasant experience? It's a 6/10.   I recommend Duke Nukem Forever for the sheer fact that it's a lesson in how not to develop a game. Passing it off to a bunch of people like a Thai ladyboy at a bachelor party to whoever wants to take a shot at it next. In the end we're all left a little dirtier and devoid a few bucks. But we had a good time in the process.

I have not played the previous game, The Longest Journey, so I can't really compare them to one another, how it may have evolved into a better, or even worse game.. However, I can very much still talk about it.   I found this game while searching across the internet a few years back, it looked fantastic.. And kinda 'exotic' to me, as I were one of those FPS only gamers at the time. After a few days thinking about it I decided to download it, unfortunately I had to turn to piracy, due to lack of money. My first impressions of it were quite solid, it was a new and interesting game, with a good story to it. At least what I could grasp at that time.. Seeing as my English was not very good. After about a third of the game done, it decided to die on me.. It would not start anymore and I quickly gave up on it.  Now, a few years later, during the annual Winter's sale, I picked it up, alongside Dreamfall Chapters, the new sprout in the series. And so I began playing it for a second time. Now with a more.. *Mature understanding..   The game does indeed feel a bit old, or at least older.. Probably due to how the controls work out. They are a bit clumsy in some scenarios. But it is something that can easily be overlooked. As for the gameplay itself.. It was very good. I felt that I got a deep enough bond to some of the characters, especially the MC, to actually care about them. Which is a plus in my book. The story was interesting and captivating, with much varitaion and exitment, though I believe I would have gotten a sligthly different.. Feel.. To it, if I had played its predecessor, The Longest Journey. To say the least I am very happy that I came back to this game, it was truly time well-spent..!

When you get down to it, this game is, mechanically speaking, more or less a clone of civilization 5 with a few modifications, none of which are earth shattering.   Of course, the same could be said of Sid Meier's Alpha Centauri, and while the developers of this game were quite insistent that it is not intended to be Alpha Centauri 2, it's quite clearly intended as a spiritual successor, and looking back, Alpha Centauri was, mechanically speaking, more or less a clone of civilization 2 with a few modifications.  Indeed, what made Alpha Centauri something special was the spectacular (and quite believable) worldbuilding, the interesting leaders, and the way the world and the characters were presented to the player. At first glance, Beyond Earth falls flat on its face in that regard: While most of the quotes accompanying the technologies are attributed to one of the game's leaders, they don't tell us much about the setting, the narrative, or the technologies you've discovered, nor do they do much to develop the leaders as characters. Just as bad is the fact that the quotes are all read by a single narrator, which divorces the quotes from the leader in queston; the fact that the quotes in Alpha Centauri were read by the character they was one of the big reasons those characters were memorable.  However, after going through the civilopedia entries on the various buildings, technologies, and wonders, I'm starting to warm up to the setting the developers have constructed. While the explanations given for many of the technologies, structures may sound like pure technobabble, most of the ones concerning my area of expertise (medicine) are plausible or at least theoretically possible, and the odd instance of improperly used medical/biological jargon seems more like honest mistakes rather than the result of a willful disregard for the use of the word 'science' in the term 'science fiction.' These entires flesh out the game quite nicely, and, for once, actually gives a coherent explanation for what exactly the unobtanium we're finding on the planet IS; Firaxite is a high temperature superconductor which functions at relatively mundane temperatures, floatstone contains magnetic monopoles, xenomoss is a fungus analogue with a unique biology, and so on.  The fact that this is not presented particularly well is the biggest problem with this game when compared with Alpha Centauri, and while I enjoyed the game and recommend buying it if it happens to go on sale, I would suggest people who didn't enjoy civilization 5's gameplay avoid this game for the time being.

It doesn't take a hater to realize that the past couple of years have been pretty bad to Sonic. Sonic The Headgehog 2006 was the zenith of these terrible games. And I mean terrible. Like, really bad.  Sonic Generations however is the complete opposite of those games. It feels like the 'Orcarina of Time' for Sega. The controls are tight, and combines the best of both the old school 2D Sonic games with the Next Gen 3D Sonic games. Theres a wonderful little story that ties it up, and the best part for many will be the fact that Sonic's friends take a backseat completely and only serve as a motivation for Sonic to run at the speed of sound.  I do not like Platformers. So take what I say with a grain of salt, but I really enjoyed Sonic Generations, mainly because of all the wonderful BGMs (City Escape and Rooftop Run). Sonic Generations hosts a collection of the best momments in the Sonic franchise sprinkled with alittle bit of Next Gen quality with old school care. No matter if your a fan, a hater, or someone who could careless about Sonic, you'll find enjoyment in this game.  GOTTA RUN FAST!

They fixed fatigue and a worth while DLC is on the way. Who died and took over? ----------------------------------- [Fatigue Update] This has ruined the game. I'm not even exagerating. Unless you only use vehicles 24/7 this update will have ruined the game for you, making you aim like a drunkard after jogging about all of 10m and forcing you to walk at a snails pace after a whole 20m. That's some ♥♥♥♥ing wonderful realism, isn't it guys. ----------------------------------- If I had to compare Arma 3 to another game, I'd say its the grandchild of Battlefield and Skyrim. With massive-scale combat, maps that dwarf any other shooter without question, and a variety of user-made game modes along with the vanilla series of Zeus game modes there is no shortage of content. In the recently added Zeus modes you can even turn this first-person shooter into an RTS game.  The game mechanics are mostly solid however some minor things such as falling damage, parachute mechanics, and cars/trucks turning to useless wrecks after a realtively minor bump into anything leave plenty of room for improvement. Bugs are also still a signifigant issue, even to the point of causing some destroyed buildings to randomly shoot someone into the air and have them fall to their death. And in general, the engine feels stiff.  Much like Skyrim—issues asside, if you find the right game mode for you, almost anyone should be able to find some enjoyment out of Arma 3. Whether you're some hardcore-roleplaying type, want to fight for survival all over an island, or simply want to vie for control over a city; there is plenty to do.

What I liked: - Attention to detail: Wildfires spread, crappy guns jam, foliage conceals you. So many things are unexpectedly realistic. - Novel setting: Very few games are set in an African landscape, and this one pretty much makes it a main character. - Total immersion: There are no forced third-person cutscenes, and the HUD only appears when needed or when you press the 'Reload' key.  What I didn't care for: - Buddies are fragile: You can only successfully rescue a near-death buddy three times. This means you have to metagame if you want to keep a buddy alive.  Recommendations: - Don't worry about having played the first  Far Cry  beforehand; there are no story ties between that game and this one. - Play it like  Fallout 3  or  Red Faction Guerrilla , not like  Call of Duty  or  Gears of War . Explore, take side missions, and approach fights tactically rather than recklessly. - Turn off the music; it's not bad music, but it causes you to miss subtle audio cues.

My Sains Row 4 review.  Simply put, so long as you can run it, it is one of the biggest laughs and to be perfectly honest, one of the funnest games ive played for a long loooooooooong time, it has sequences that made me burst out laughing, made me feel awesome, and generally is just one of those games where if all you really want from a game is some nice flashing lights and lots of fun, its absolutely perfect, in fact even if you dont like flashing lights and lots of fun.  Details wise, i guess ill go in the standard format of graphics, gameplay, performance etc etc.   First up, and to get it out of the way, graphics.  They're pretty good based on what ive seen on my office laptop (will touch on it in the performance section) it looks pretty good in places and it usually looks fairly good, though ive noticed some weird visual glitching (may be down to my system, and i cant tell if its intentional on the games front). a solid 7/10, would rate higher if i could actually max it, although im sure itll look acceptable to anyone.  Gameplay, the meat of any game. Its your standard GTA-esque third person sandbox world shooter/racer/thing, but with a slight, tiny addition..... SUPER POWERS, one of the things i adore about SR 4 is that its essentially the same as saints row the third, but with more content and more awesomeness, and a half decent story at the very least. I do need to mention that i used an Xbox 360 controller for the majority of my play time but only because i found it more comfortable (due to owning SR 3 and SR 2 on the Xbox 360) if you do use the keyboard and mouse i can say it felt ok and tolerable to use. Little sidetracked, i know, the super powers are one of the reasons this game feels so awesome to play, they literally let you run around faster than cars, blow everything up without using guns, pick up and throw objects, etc etc, its incredibley satisfying sometimes. The shooting also feels solid and very well done with a large arsenal to pick from, and customise to your hearts content. It also features the series staple, character customisation, and its so damn good, can customise practically anything, and everything you could want to. Finally i come to the vehicles and extras, Vehicle wise i dont see much use in them but they handle pretty well with a controller, but from past experience with driving around with a keyboard, its a little clunky. Extra's wise its got some fun activities and the like, and its free roam.....with awesome super powers... Gameplay is a 9/10 overall, the only thing i wish it did differently would be the top end of the heat meter, without spoilers, it just stops after a boss battle.....which kinda makes it feel pointless to rampage but its fun while it lasts.  Story: no spoilers, at all......rating is a good solid 8/10, reason with no spoilers: great writing, good voice acting, laugh out loud sections occasionally. There is no more on this section.  Performance: this one i undoubtedly will probably be heavily discredited on, but i can run SR 4 on an Acer office laptop that was never once intended for gaming, while this should not compel you to go buy it if you cant run it at all and its specs are way higher than your systems, chances are it wont run, obviously. I got extremely lucky as my graphics card is not suppose to do this kind of thing in this much detail, or on this scale. Actual in game performance is therefore hindered severely, but at worst my game dropped to about 2-5 frames per second when i spammed one of the powers in a mission, and this happened twice, due to my own combat needs, on average it hits 25-30 frames at the very least and is playable, again, at the very least. I cant stress enough that if you arent sure you can run it, dont try to. Performance rating: 8-9/10, it undoubtedly can run pretty well on a proper gaming system, but i did notice when i tried using steam broadcast the game slowed to a crawl, whether this is related to my system or anything is kind of out there in the realms of mystery, but i can say that no other game (even ones that are as needy, or needier than this one) has this broadcast problem.

app_name: DARK SOULS™: Prepare To Die Edition
review_text: Usually I like my single player games with good character development & an engaging story... that's not Dark Souls.  However, what it lacks in storytelling it more than makes up for in gameplay that's pitched perfectly - it's difficult, but no section seems impossible. You progress by learning how to handle each enemy or situation and there's a real sense of accomplishment when you finally clear an area.  I'd advise keeping a walkthru to hand when you play. The game doesn't hand hold you much & it's easy to get frustrated because you've skipped a huge chunk of the game & you're not ready for the spike in difficulty.  Overall - highly recommended for anyone looking for a challenge.

I cannot recommend this game enough. There is very little to complain about. Fun for short or extended play sessions. Progrssion is fair, and nothing is blocked behind a paywalls. No fun bucks required, just buy it, play it, the sky's the limit. It's everything that I wanted in Quest for Epic Loot, but didnt' get.  First off, I feel like this game is like if Zelda, Diablo, and Little Big Planet. And like LBP it has something for everyone. if you like to play top down rpg's; you have an ever expanding selection of adventures. If you like to be creative, the level creatore is robust yet easy to learn. And, within those two categories' it has much to offer for the level of complexity that you desire.  You could focus on being artistic or you can use it more like RPG maker. Designing complex events, quests, or puzzles. You could simply play a level or two and see the sights or you could could delve heavily into optimizing your stats. Play by yourself, with friends, or strangers.  The online component is very intuitive. You can group up with friends privately or publicly. I haven't had any serious problems with lag. And, since it's all cooperative I have seen the least ill will playing this than any other online game I've ever experienced.  I like thet there is some steam workshop support for character models. Instead of having to spend real money on fun bucks when I get bored with my appearence, I can just download a new one. It's touches like that that make me feel like I'm not being denied any content so the dev can weasel money out of me.  Also to the devs' credit. they have a suggestions forum that they actially take into acoount when releasing updates. So if you feel something is lacking, they may actually improve on it itf it's a good idea.  All in all, 9 out of 10. Worth every penny.

Oh look, I have finally written a serious review.   From someone with over 1700 hours on Borderlands 2*:  Short answer:  I bought this game at a little over half price thanks to some vouchers from my steam wallet, and I felt disgruntled. So I would feel absolutely cheated if I bought this game at full price.  If it is still in it's current state, buy the GOTY at a dirt cheap price a year from now, you thankfully don’t need to be in multiplayer lobby full of strangers which would dwindle away to zilch a year on so you won't be missing much.  Alternatively spend your money on a good book.  Long answer:-  End game:  I start off with this because I have a feeling that this game was marketed towards existing fans of the series.   End game value-there is very little so far.  Like Borderlands 2, DLC may come to *save* the day however I cannot recommend buying any of it at full price when the base game’s quality simply does not live up to similar expectations of Borderlands 2.   To be honest, I wasn’t expecting to be as good, but the constant fixing is getting on my nerves because most of it doesn’t address any of the popular issues with the game and they haven’t given a statement as to why:  Fast travel to other maps still do not exist  Respawnable unique enemies still do not exist  Some skills still disable once you get on and off a buggy despite having “fixed” the issue. The raid boss mechanics are exactly the same as the normal boss but with more health. The only reason you would do it repeatedly would be to keep a record on how fast you can kill it next time.   Gameplay:  Borderlands 2 went to a moon and a space station. Take what you want from that opening statement.  A number of additions peddled in the marketing are very good-lasers, the grinder, Oz kits, butt slams and low gravity. The problem is that some of it unbalanced adding to my frustration at the support the game receives. Do they seriously not acknowledge informal advice being voiced on videos, forum threads, social media…etc etc all echoing the same issues?  Character class are on the whole very good, improved from Borderlands 2. Though I have a problem with Wilhelm being underpowered by comparison to all the characters.  Mobs and scaling I do like, which was a major gripe about Borderlands 2.  Terrain traveling in the more open sections of the game is odd because there are invisible walls where there should logically be. I understand that in the previous game you could get to a number of places you shouldn’t be but seriously? Over a cliff face in the same section of map that is clearly scalable by legitimate means?  This brings me on to terrain glitches in internal areas of the game which are everywhere, nothing unusual about that from Borderlands 2 then. Quests all seem padded out and I spent a large portion of my time not doing anything interesting other than travelling passed low level mobs or admiring the scenery (Pandora, space station, star space station, Pandora…and look back again). Give us down time between actions but this is just a joke, it is a giant middle finger up at your free time! What is with the bloody unskippable cut scenes?   Loot:  Stingy as ♥♥♥♥ with a lot of weapons being totally part of the random loot pool and not part of farmable unique enemy that we know of (did I mention that some of the unique enemies do not respawn?). Are they planning to release these in DLCs? In which case ♥♥♥♥ off. Also the recent nerfing bonanza has made it harder to get good loot end game.  A number of legendary items have returned from the previous game with a few added extras. However on the whole there is a disproportionate balance of improved fan favourites (some of them haven’t at all) and new legendary and unique loot. If this is a new game I want more new loot!   This feels more and more like a dragged out expansion the more I write about it.  Storyline and humour:  Story is worse than Borderlands 2 (and that was pretty bad), better than Borderlands 1 (wtf was that about again?). Lilith commits more cardinal story sins just to set up the game for Borderlands 2 whilst setting up Borderlands 3 that it makes Michael Bay’s Transformers 4 look like a work of genius.**  Humour will always be like Marmite in this game, but anything involving censored obscenity just sucks (Although replacing ‘midget’ was funny). Screw you Australia’s censorship Law. Most of the NPCs are dry and forgettable, or they add nothing to story. In fact there is total overkill on side quests that do not aid in the telling of the story.    Final Conclusion   Do: Call it a padded out standalone expansion sold at full price.  Don't: Call it a DLC, its not short enough for that.  Recommended alternative: Eating tacos whilst attempting to train your cat to hula dance to Taylor Swift’s Shake It Off, while a friend shoots the whole show on Instagram. This would be a much better co-op exercise of your time.    *As of writing, probably a lot more by now **Okay, that was a bit harsh on the Borderlands writers but still, a dumb cool game should not have to be filed next a Michael Bay movie. The Banner Saga 2 is a terrific pickup... if you enjoyed the first one.  The Good: The story continues in an expectedly hopeless manner, but it throws a few curve balls and left me excited for the third installment. The artwork remains beautiful and even though you spend the majority of your time watching your caravan trek endlessly left across your screen, man does it look good. The characters are also much more compelling this go around, but that might just be that it is fresher in my mind. Seriously, Bolverk and Iver and Hakon, hell all the Varl are just amazing. If you really get in the head of the protagonists, instead of choosing what you believe are optimal paths, the decision making weighs heavily on you. At its heart, this game is a visual novel and to play it like Fire Emblem is just silly.  The Bad: The Combat. So many people like the Armor vs Strength combat thing, and its cool but it is so flawed. Turn order needs a serious rework for Banner Saga 3. I learned this in the first one, and slogged through it in the sequel, but the optimal tactics are not the realistic ones. Often it is advantageous to leave enemy combatants with 2 or 3 strength rather than killing them, because it is better to have a weak enemy in the turn order. I got it handed to me several times in Banner Saga 1 before I figured this out because I would one-shot all the weaker enemies and then the 2 or 3 remaining big dudes would alternate turns wrecking me. My biggest complaint with the game. But like I said, it is a visual novel at its core and there are only I think half a dozen fights in the game that have major story developments attached to them.  Neutral: Illusion of Choice. This could be good, depending on your outlook I suppose. But sometimes it feels like you are at the mercy of a bad DM. No matter what you do, bad things happen to you and no decision is correct. It really plays into that hopelessness but some of the 'Surprise!' bad things that happen to you are super ham-fisted. For a decision based, heavy narrative RPG sometimes you feel at the whim of the writers. Maybe it plays into the despair more than I am letting on and it does feel like things are out of your control, but to me that wasn't always a good thing.  The Verdict: Play it. If you haven't played the first one, its like five bucks on sale all the time. It isn't better or worse necessarily. It's definitely an improvement, but it doesn't quite have the wonder the first one gave you. It's like Pokemon sequels. They're better, but they aren't the same.

I'd like to start by saying: Learning curve.  That's it, the thing that will most likely prevent you from playing more than a few hours. It stopped me for a long time, because as soon as the basic movement and combat tutorial ended - you're on your own with zero direction.  At the same time though, that's exactly what makes this game such a blast to play. The core elements are exploration, trading, and to a lesser degree - combat. Your ultimate goal is that of your choosing, and how you reach it is entirely left up to you. The game has a large focus on economies, with the GoD Engine simulating various fluxuations in markets - and you can get a piece of the action by building your own factories and eventually create an empire that belongs solely to you.  It's a game that is hard to get into, but very rewarding once you do. I recommend it for those looking for a challenge aside from scaled up enemies, and those who might be interested in a game which is mostly about trading.

As someone with 1000+ hours in Civilization V, I believe I have atleast a shred of credibility when it comes to voicing my opinion on this game.  In short, the game is pretty good. The smallest things that have been changed are the things that irritate me the most, and really make me want to punch my screen. Some of these may just be my experience.  -For instance, in Civ. V, when you had 1 movement left and wanted to move onto a hill (which movement cost is 2), you could. In Civ 6, you cannot; if you have 1 movement and are surrounded by tiles that require 2 movement, you cannot move. -So far, I've learned scouts no longer have immunity over moving over rough terrains, such as hills or forests. -Perhaps due to the artstyle or some other factor, the beginning game seems very very slow, even when compared to Civ V. Compared to the other reasons, this is subjective. -Moving and attacking with units near enemies is really weird, in that sometimes it's right click to attack, and others it's left click. -Agenda system is cool, but AI's seem to vindictive even from the little bit I've interacted with them. Right from the start, I had a negative relationship with the first Civ. I met. I think it was like, 'disliked' or something, I forget. Agendas seem like it might make the game too predictable.  ***Update after an additional 2 hours and 20 minutes**** -Economy system appears VERY weak; every turn or so my GPT (Gold per Turn) goes from 18.8 to 31.5 to 1.6 to 19.5 and so on. I havent been able to get passed 40+ GPT despite being on turn like 350. -Religion needs work big time. I founded a religion but instantly another civ just used a missionary on my holy city. No big deal right? Well since the dominant religion in my holy city is another religion, not only was my religion not able to spread to my other cities, but I also wasn't able to get the other religion out at all. The game only lets you buy religious units (Inquisitors, apostles etc.) for the dominant religion in your city. So if your holy city is converted gg, you lost your religion.  -Another thing about religion is that the religious victory is garabge (subjective). I was playing out of nowhere a cinematic came on. I skipped it and said I was defeated because the same civ that overtook my religion got the religion in every other Civ's cities and won. That seems incredibly easy.  -I'm a little behind where humans actually were in the 1920's, but I must say the mid-ish game point that I'm in does not feel rewarding at all. Compared to Civ V that had lots of ongoing tasks every turn, Civ VI feels EXTREMELY redundant while you're playing. Make a builder>build 3 times>another builder>need amenities>need housing space....etc. It feels very dull.  The biggest thing that irks me is the Amenities (might be misspelled) feature. This is basically the new happiness system where for the more citizens you have, you need more Amenities or else they might rebel against you. The Amenities are luxury resources, which makes it very annoying and/or difficult to keep that balanced when in my case you spawn near just one luxury resource but need more to keep your citizens at bay.   If your a huge Civ V fan, you'll feel pretty at home with everything but will still notice the little changes they've made to many parts of the game. If you've not played Civ too much before, I would try to wait for one of the signature Game-changing DLC's in order to add more content.  After further playtime, reducing rating from 8 to 6.5 Overall: 6.5/10

Oh, boy. I really wanted to like this, because I've loved every other game from Wadjet Eye so far, but I really had a tough time getting into this one. I did finish it, more due to the completionist in me, but also in hopes that it would get better. Parts of it near the end did improve a little, but overall, the game felt very disjointed, and involved a LOT of 'To do this, first you have to get this person to do that, but to do THAT, you have to get these three items', rinse and repeat. A lot of seemingly-arbitrary tasks ahead of you, and not much in the way of an overarching storyline to tie it all together, other than Alfie (the main character) 'trying to make it big' and restore his family's name. That's about it as far as plot.  I looked up reviews to see if I was missing something, and a lot of reviewers said how they really identified with the main character, Alfie Banks, but I honestly felt he was pretty bland... a fairly generic stereotype of a 1920's real estate agent.  The one thing I did like about the game is that it's based on the real 'history' (with some artistic liberties taken, of course) of Coral Gables, FL. Many of the main characters have real life counterparts, with the exception of Alfie. But other than that, with a plot that couldn't figure out which way it was going and never really tied together, subpar voice acting, a step back in the way of retro-styled graphics that didn't just feel 'retro-styled' but more like it actually was made years ago (and not in a good way)... I just wasn't a big fan of the game. Not awful, but not that good. A big disappointment compared to Wadjet Eye's other fantastic productions.

app_name: Broken Sword 2 - the Smoking Mirror: Remastered
review_text: It was literally as if another team worked on the sequel. Everything that was done well in the first was just mangled up in this Smoking Mirrors. In BS1, George was an easy going guy with a ready smile - intensely likeable, in BS2 he was frowning and had this disgusted look on his face half the time. The character interaction and writing in BS1 was intensely witty and funny, the script of BS2 has its funny moments but was more cynical and mean spirited. Nico was voice acted by a different person and the new actress's voice over was terrible. Though George did a lot of the leg work in the first Broken Sword, you still felt that Nico was an active partner in the relationship. She was just completly useless in BS2. She was pranced around like a little princess waiting for George to do everything. Content wise: BS2 was a lot shorter then BS1 which was a godsend given the heavily reduced quality of the production, I just wanted to move along to 3 as quickly as possible. Unless you're a hardcore fan, I'd give number 2 a skip.

Bad Rats is a game about physics and killing cats because we are sick ♥♥♥♥s who need help. Because we let Bad Rats to happen, there is no reason to buy any other video game. Portal? Zelda? Half-Life? Citizen Kane the Video Game? No. Bad Rats legitimately changed my life.   You start out into a cutscene where Rats are being enslaved. Where we quickly cut to the HQ of the S.H.I.T. Finding out what those letters mean was the greatest gaming moment of my life and I can't spoil it.  The game is simple. You are shooting propaganda for the S.H.I.T in order to gain ground in this ever changing war of Cat and Mouse. The cat is trapped and no matter how hard the puzzle. You must get that kill on camera. You have your generic Call of Duty terrorist, rat with baseball bat and a soldier with a rocket (a classic callback to Toy Story).  Don't be misled by the gameplay footage. It was early beta when it was filmed. I think the creator was making a statement on how gameplay footage can be misleading.   The story is amazing, showcasing brilliant voice acting and mocap work. The graphics look amazing and every detail is on display here.   The pricing is impeccable. For $1 AUD dollars, you can experience the best gaming treasure since the Ocarina of Time and I think that's beautiful.   Remember this was before the advent of Greenlight. Where such classics as Air Control, The Jumper and Starship Tranquility are found.  I rate this game 10/10. It's a shame I couldn't handle more the 1 hour of it. It was the best game I have ever played. I stopped playing because it was too good. I want to enjoy other games. Unfortunately.  Thanks for reading,  -Cornchip

I wouldn't recommend this game, at least not if you are wanting to play it for awhile.   Going through the mission/story mode solo was alright, but once you hit level 30 and have completed the missions the game gets old fast.   It's a 3rd person shoot and loot, but the loot is all RNG and the drop rates for the high end equipment (yellows) are incredibly low. So, not only is it hard to get, but even if you do manage to get it to drop you'll probably just end up deconstructing it for materials to craft (Crafting has RNG stats, but at least has a guaranteed quality). So, that's what you have to look forward to at endgame. It's reminiscent of D2 Vanilla and D3 at Launch, in terms of loot.   If you go into the DZ, Darkzone, you will realize very quick that even if you ignore all the cheaters (running Cheat Engine Tables) teleporting and 1-hit PK'ing you, that the PvP system itself is terrible. It almost reminds me of TPPK in Diablo 2, if you want to call that PvP (I don't). It's essentially just a gankfest.   I am at the point where I just log in to do the dailies and then log out and do other things, just like a facebook game.

Masochisia is a deeply disturbing game, covering such happy topics as masochism, sadism, physical and emotional abuse, murder and madness. In it's brief runtime it managed to be more horrifying and memorable than many other games several times its length. Every piece of the game works together to create an atmosphere of growing dread and revulsion, not just at the game's dark subject matter but the warped lens it is viewed through. Hamilton, the game's viewpoint character, is a particularly warped individual. The player is encouraged not necessarily to justify his actions, but sympathize with the madness that drives them.   Here's what the game does right:    The art, soundtrack, and subject matter combine to make a unique and memorable psychological horror experience.    The game approaches madness and mental illness in a surprisingly nuanced way, neither glorifying nor entirely condemning the aberrant thought patterns and impulses of the afflicted. It's an interesting approach, given the relative rarity of horror portraying psychosis as more than people who want to stab anything within arm's reach.    The viewpoint perspective, as mentioned above, is well executed.   There are some missteps, however.    Without being told directly from another review, I would have never known the subject of the game was    the serial killer, cannibal, and child ♥♥♥♥♥♥ Albert Fish   and without that knowledge context for much of what occurs in the game is lost.    Every so often the game shows coded messages that are not required to progress but reveal additional information. Someone with little patience or preference for ciphers, such as myself, is consequently left with an incomplete picture of the game's portrayal of its subject matter.    The way the game portrays   the murderous nature of its protagonist as a matter of 'destiny'   could easily be misconstrued as   justifying murder in general   rather than   that being the self-rationalization of a deranged and murderous mind.     So do I recommend this game? If you can withstand it, yes. Will I every play it again? HAHAHAHAHAHA.... no.

This game is just terrible. First of all, the performance is so bad that on 'ultra', which still looks like an early PlayStation2 game, it chugs at an erratic and frustrating 25-30fps. Drop it down to medium and the game becomes the jaggiest polygonal game made after 1997, but at least the framerate jumps up to an erratic and annoying 50fps. It should probably be noted that my machine can do, for example, Witcher 3 on all ultra at 90fps, but apparently it can't handle a PS2-level action game with ninjas in it. At ultra low settings, the game finally stabilizes the framerate and it stops being erratic, but it hovers around 40fps (lower than medium?!) and it literally looks worse than a PS1 game. It looks like, I don't know, an N-Gage game?  But that can be forgiven if the game design is good enough. If it's fun to murder ninjas, it can be acceptable that the framerate is poor and the graphics are horrible, but that's not the case here. This is a dualstick shooter where they forgot about the second stick, so you can only toss shuriken and bombs in the direction your character is facing. I hate this design. It just forces the player to run in dumb little circles to keep recentering the character, or to simply stop moving and hurl as many shurikens as possible before dodging. It's such an inelegant design compared to just using one stick for movement and one stick for shooting (or keyboard and mouse for each if you prefer). On keyboard, you move with WASD and do all of your attacks with the other half of the keyboard, and the mouse does nothing. The worst part is that this reductive design doesn't even make any sense from a retro perspective, because the oldest dualstick ever made is Robotron: 2084. It came out in 1982 and it used two sticks, so why are people creating singlestick shooters in 2016?  It's too bad, because I like the idea. It's massive hordes of ninjas (wait, I don't think that's how ninjas worked...) storming at you and you kill dozens at a time, which is cool and sort of like a retro arcade version of Dynasty Warriors, but the horrid performance and uninteresting gameplay loop make this a hard pass. Even if they fix the performance, they'd have to redesign the whole gameplay core to make it fun. I've only played 12 minutes and that number is not going to rise.

Deadlight is a very middle of the road title. I just finished it after playing it on and off for a year (Steam Shame Pile is real life), and I got around 8.5 hours of gameplay out of it. I unlocked Nightmare Mode after I finished, but the truth is, I don't even think I'll bother to play it again.   While the graphics are slick and I appreciate the side-scrolling style, the controls get in the way of your fun on numerous occasions. I lost count of how many times I died because of sticky controls or unclear instructions.  Another annoyance was the game crashing repeatedly on Windows 10. This might be fixed in the future, as it worked fine before I upgraded, but it's something to be aware of. I was sometimes in the middle of trying to shoot a zombie and everything would just crash to the desktop. WTF?  The story is pretty stupid, as well. Trying to follow it is a giant waste of your time. Just skip all the cut scenes (they are crap anyway) and shoot stuff. That's way more fun.  Lastly, the checkpoint system stinks. If you stop playing and come back, you'll get booted back 3-4 checkpoints. It's really frustrating.   If you can get past those cons, the game can be pretty enjoyable. I wouldn't buy it unless it was on a Steam sale, though. $14.99 is too much for this considering the much better games available here with more substance and replayablity.

Good humor and interesting situations make this a good play through for even seasoned point and clickers and multiple endings add a reasonable amount of replay value.  My only gripe with this game is the lack of character movement speed in the settings when you are really stuck and need to move through scenes over and over to find a solution - waiting for your character to walk up and interact with something can get rather annoying.   Over all 8.5/10 Drogglejugs

Today's Review: Lost Planet 3  General Overview: A bit of story first. Lost Planet 3 takes us trough a journey of Peyton, a mechanic hired by NEVEC (the company that wants to save the world...for profit) that hires Peyton for a remarkable contract to work on E.D.N. III, there's a energy crisis on earth and the criatures on E.D.N. III (The Akrid) can generate it's on energy called T-Energy, NEVEC sees this as an opportunity and establish a base in the planet to extract T-Energy. As you can probably guess by the time played I didn't really play the game. Not at all. I merely remained in the character select for that long.  What I first noticed is that you cannot change the gender of any class, so you are stuck with whatever gender a specific class has, which is still okay considering the games system that every class can potentially learn anything. When I finally chose what character I wanted to play, given the already few choices I had, I then needed to pick a name and I could not find a single halfway decent name that was not taken. The names cannot include blanks which also cancels out appealing and senseful suffixes. I find that a system like that is very outdated and that other games of this genre handle it a lot better like account or character specific numbers that allow for the use of ANY desired name, the simple inclusion of blanks or a 'forename' 'surename' approach to name a few examples.  I at least do not feel like getting emersed in a game where I either run around as ICantFindAName, XxICantFindANamexX or the 1000th variation of typical names that include hundreds of nonsensical extra syllables.  Notice: This review does only concentrate on the character creation of this game, which is minimal. If you do not mind any of the mentioned things above, then this review does and should not further encourage or discourage you from downloading or playing this game.

Fighting games have never been an easy genre to get into for anyone other than the truly devoted. Most games require massive amounts of meta knowledge regarding frames, hitboxes, and matchup knowledge. Skullgirls is a unique game to me in that, it has all these requirements but, unlike most other games, is willing to offer you a hand in getting started. The game wins a massive number of points for having a well designed and implemented tutorial that explains everything from basic mechanics to specific character playstyles, allowing for players new to the genre or the game itself, to easily pick up the fundamentals easily.  Character design is vibrant and beautifully illustrated making the game just much fun to look at as it is to play. It also deserves points for being the first game to have a cast of mostly women, with the inital playable characters being exclusively female.  Another thing I appreciate is that the game is constantly being patched and evaluated in this PC port. Patching on consoles was a rare occurance and often lead to massive exploits becoming simple facts of the game that would alter the way the game was played for long stretches. With the PC port, I feel like the game is constantly evolving with the meta, allowing for the game to stay consistently fresh.

+ Many choices that can affect the story + Interesting story + Nice art that you'd expect from a Telltale game, combined with cinematic fight scenes + Good voice acting  - Some of the game's lore doesn't seem fully fleshed out  Although I enjoyed both The Walking Dead and The Wolf Among Us, I like the latter a little more. There are plenty of choices you can make in the game, and practically every choice affects the storyline in some way. In addition, the story is very compelling and had several unpredictable plot twists. Both of these factors make The Wolf Among Us a highly enjoyable experience; the fluid animations and the good voice acting is really just icing on the cake.  One thing that bothered me about plot was the fact that some parts of the lore seemed underdeveloped. For example, you're told that the Fables had to leave their storybook lands to come to New York. However, you are told very little outside of that. Why did the Fables have to leave in the first place? Why did a lot of Fables die making their way to New York? Why were some Fables able to maintain their wealth while others went from riches to rags? There are other examples like this, and perhaps if you read the graphic novels they address a lot of the questions I have. However, it does bother me that the story leaves many questions unanswered, and it doesn't look like a sequel will come along and answer them for me.  Overall, The Wolf Among Us is a highly enjoyable experience. I would recommend it to anyone looking for a game with a good plot.

First off, I'd like to say that TEW is NOT a terrible game, however, it has more issues and let downs that I like (or even should exist), therefore, I cannot recommend this game. At least right now with my current progress... I am a long-time survival horror fan (horror fan in general) and I love these types of games, movies and everything. It's hard to scare me or disturb me, but I know real horror when I see it. In all honesty TEW, in my opinion, is just a modern recreation of Resident Evil 4 and Silent Hill, with some additional survival elements added to the game and fantastic graphics. It has NOTHING new or innovative at all. I'll list the strengths and weaknesses I see and why I do not currently recommend this game.  NOTE: Review is subject to change as I experience any, and/or when I finally beat the game and have completed the experience entirely.  WEAKNESSES:  1) 30fps lock, forced widescreen, and aiming The 30fps lock is absolutely ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥... I know they created this game with the intent of making it feel, play, and even look old school, but you can never totally achieve that unless you and the gamers want to take a time machine back to 1990.... The fact of the matter is that you need to be modern at a basic level. That includes not only graphics, but design features of the game as well. fps is included in this. With the quality of graphics this game has, limiting fps to 30 creates issues for a lot of people (though some don't experience any). If you're going to have great graphics, you need a great fps, and that's just a basic fact.  Forced widescreen isn't all that bad of an idea, but the fact that it takes up between 30-35% of the ENTIRE screen IS bad! I know they're trying to achieve a movie feel, but it's a game and you're still controlling it. The forced widescreen is such a leap that it is uncomfortable on many levels for most players. On top of that, the aspect ratio isn't even spot on for the forced wide-screen... They need to really change this... The bars need to be cut by 1/3 (maybe even 1/2 or removed entirely), and the aspect ratio needs to be optimized for TRUE wide-screen if they're going to do it all... Right now, it's just a full screen aspect ratio stretched out between two random ♥♥♥ black bars to make it look like it's wide screen when it's actually not... it's awkward as hell... some people may be able to get used to it, but many can't.  Aiming.... the zoom for aiming is quite massive... this may be very intentional, but it makes aiming harder than is realistic... Anybody who is trained at using fire arms and knows how to aim would easily be able to cap a zomie in the head while it was only yards away from them... especially with how awkwardly and at times slowly they move. The fact that it's just awkward moving the aiming corsair around because of how far the zoom is makes the player (and Sebastian) shoot about as good as 2 year old. It also moves way too slowly. This may be a subjective downfall, but to me, it's a big enough one to point out.  2) Not Scary... At All For people who have played their share of horror games and seen their share of horror films, you will be disappointed to find out that the fear factor of this game (at least to me) is next to 0... most of the enemies are just ugly ♥♥♥ zomies, then there's one bad guy that is a total rip off of skin-face (chainsaw massacre dude). There also enemies that you can tell were directly influenced from Silent Hill... enemies are predictable... the AI is dumb... everything I've experienced so far is something I have already experienced 50 million times to the T, and is therefore no longer scary or disturbing.   The scariest part of the game is the environment... and that's a bad thing. The environment should only ever serve to setup the scares, immerse you into the world, and put you in the mood. Yet the developers seemed to fail entirely at establishing this beyond the strong environment. To me, it's just not scary and a survival horror kinda needs to be scary, not a regurgitation of something that everybody is already used to and no longer scared of... Whenever I play this game, I feel like I'm playing RE4 again, and that is a bad thing.... I shouldn't feel like that, I should feel like I'm playing The Evil Within: a 'new' game with it's 'own' identity.  Yes there's nothing new under the sun, and it's extremely rare and unlikely for anything truly original. I'm not saying that, I'm saying that it's impossible to play this game without feeling like you're actually playing a Resident Evil or Silent Hill game. That's why this is bad. It's The Evil Within, not Resident Evil or Silent Hill...  3) Bad Port.... I really shouldn't have to explain how obvious that is...   STRENGHTS   1) Graphics are pretty good if you have an up-to-date system. It's a pretty game to play.  2) The story is good (though not amazing). It certainly isn't a new idea, but at least it's well written with good voice acting and progression. The only bad part to this is that the chapters are super short.  3) The Name is awesome; metal as ♥♥♥♥.  4) Game-play is pretty decent, not very clunky or confusing (except for aiming). It's an easy game to play, mechanically.  CONCLUSION: TEW isn't a terrible game and it's good enough for me to keep playing it because I spent $60 on it and I don't want to waste my money. It's also good enough to at least play through 1 time. However, it's not good enough to make it my first choice, or make me stoked whenever turning it on. It's just another Resident Evil/Silent Hill game. Literally, that's it. The design flaws can be worked around with console commands or editing the .cfg file, and hopefully more stuff will be fixed with patches, but I just can't recomment this game because it fails to wow me in almost every way. Plus it's a bad port... wait for it to go on sale, but do not spend $60 on this game... You will likely slap or kick yourself for doing so.

TL:DR Do I recommend this Freaking Awesome game? Yes, yes, yes. It's like playing out your own space opera.   Season One:  Journey to the West.  The High Monkians, a race of spirtualists and individuals who aren't afraid to knock some heads in where needed. With the invention of warp drive, the Jade Emporer sought to bring The Order of The Monkey King to the stars.  From their home world Jade, a moon orbiting the gas giant known as the Mountain of Ice, in the Celestial Temple system, they expanded outward. Surveying systems looking for a new world to colonize. It needed to be the right kind of joungle world, not continental, nor oceanic.  Space Whales graced the skies, amebas and crystaline entities bared the path of exploration. But they found it, a new world filled with dangerous animals and thick jungle. They named it Emerald and soon a thriving colony arose.  Onward the Monkians progressed, spredding throughout the nearby stars in search of enlightenment. A distant world, Peridot, was next on the list. Local fauna proved... intoxicating. Work ethics fell, polititions got 'groovy' and the society became difficult to maintain. The Emporer considered purging the planet of the plants responsible, but that didn't seem very enlightened. So Peridot became the center of a new sector, governed by it's own leader and allowed to function at %10 under capacity as long as they handled their own problems and continued to support the Order.  New alien races were discovered one after another. Some friendly, some smelly. A particularly vile smelling creature formed an empire on borders of the Celestial worlds, sending frequent insults and resisting attempts to trade and improve relations.  A new race, a young race was discovered, in a system with half a dozen habitable worlds. They inhabited the only Jungle world. The Monkians were too enlightened to take it from them. Instead they studied them from afar. They gained an immense wealth of knowledge, and passed laws restricting the interaction with such races. They even saved the world from an impending asteroid collision, though the child race may never know.  Distant alies were made, as the Monkians ventured further out. Trading star charts became their biggest leverage in gaining allies, as the Monkians amassed a wealth of knoledge about the galaxy before they had even ventured through a tenth of it. An aliance was formed, and soon a federation.  The Order of The Monkey King thrived in this new galaxy. There were greater civilizations out there, threats unseen, wars looming. But together, with other enlightened races, they stand a chance to continue venturing where no monkey has gone before.  Will their civilization continue to thrive or unexpectedly fall? What secrets await them in western half of the galaxy? Next time on Journey to the West. What happened to the science ship that discovered a fallen civilization?? We've lost contact for months, who are these superior beings?

While Fable is a wondeful game, the PC port falls short in a lot of ways. I say supposedly because the feature to start the game in the editor for me is missing an executable file. The main problems with this game are the counter-intuitive UI and the embelishment that this is going to be modable. Don't get me wrong, wonderful game, fable was my first RPG, and I own this game for multiple consoles. I'm certain the modding community will be able to crack this one wide open, but don't hold your breath for it being any time soon.

What impressed me most is the background music. The music with FM synthesis creates the atmospheres of the video games from 1980s and early 1990s. What makes the music more unique is the use of absurd melodies and the unusual combinations of different timbres, and that absolutely makes the later parts of this game horrible, and somehow odd. Good job Miroslav Malesevic, you have inspired me! For me, this game is like a hommage to, yet a parody of the Super Mario Bros series. The theme of 'a hero beats numerous monsters and finally saved his heroine' is embodied in this game. However, don't you think this theme is somehow old-fashioned? Wait, after finishing the whole game, you will find out the reason why this game deviates from that hackneyed theme, and why I said this game is a parody. This is a great game with fantastic BGM and unusual atmospheres and endings. I hope everyone who loved the traditional ACT games will also enjoy in this game. :3

I had fond memories of this game from back in 2009. Unfortunately, it has not aged well at all. While the story and setting still holds up, the gameplay is very repetetive. As in 'punch the exact same ten dudes over and over' kind of repetetive. The gamplay ends up being an annoyance in the way of the interesting story.  Even if you can stand that drawback, there's more problems to come. When 'different' enemies are introduced (different as in 'takes more punches') they're basically just the first boss battle over and over. This escalates through the game until multible 'tough' enemies are introduced at the same time as ~10 regular ones. This would be fine if it didn't mess up the auto-aim in the combat. You try to hit the tougher enemies and Batman flies across the room to a regular dude instead.  No, as previously stated I would not recommend this game anymore. Watch the story bits on YouTube instead and save whatever they want for it. This is the opposite of entertaining.

As a die-hard Assassin's Creed fan, I really wanted to enjoy this. The level design is quite good and the backgrounds are nothing short of gorgeous. I would love to see this game in stereoscopic 3D because the art design really is amazing. Even the 2.5D format can play itself well to what Assassin's Creed is supposed to be. There really are a lot of reasons why this game could have been a good match for the Assassin's Creed line.  What killed this game for me, however, is the relatively large number of timed missions (which I hate, no matter what game it is) and the number of missions that judge you based on how quickly you play through the level, so they - more or less - are also timed missions. In some cases there were multiple timed or time-judged missions in a row! This is not what Assassin's Creed is all about. I don't care if the developers attempt to excuse this under the guise of a 'platformer'. The regular, non-timed missions are wonderful to play. But once you take away the ability to strategize how to approach an enemy in your own way, you take away the fundamental reasons that define why we love Assassin's Creed. The timed missions are thrown in there like those pathetic tower defense missions in 'Revelations', except the timed missions here aren't optional.  I don't remember if there were this many timed missions in 'Chronicles: China', but the large number and frequency of them in 'Chronices: India' killed my desire to continue playing or play in Plus mode after I finished it. If the levels in 'Chronicles: Japan' are like this, I definitely will not buy it and will instead go back and play 'Black Flag' for a fourth time. If I want to deal with timed platform levels, there are plenty of other games to scratch that itch, but that's not why I want to play anything related to 'Assassin's Creed'.

This is a short illustrated story that depicts a single night in one person's life. It takes about 10 minutes to read through.  The writing is good and the pacing is perfect. The visuals and audio work well to set the mood and convey emotion.  There is no twist, punchline or moral; no tragedy, triumph or deep philosophical revelation. Anyone can have a night like this, and many people do. Maybe you did too. This is only an opportunity to see the world through someone else's eyes and share their experience. It might lead you to think, feel and understand. And if not, hey, it's only 10 minutes.

My original (thumbs down) review was:  'If I ever had any love for this game, it was killed with the last boss fight.'  And somehow I still stand by this. If you don't think boss fights are fun (like me), you don't want to play the ending of this game. But there are many aspects in the game I like - for example, it's up to you how you tackle the levels: you can be sneaky, but you can also go on a rampage. A combination is wise, too. Each level is short enough to let you actually enjoy the lack of save/load - it gets intense this way, and challenging. ROOT focuses on this experience, and that's what makes it mostly cool experience.  But there are also many things I'd like to see changed - the boss fights are the best example. But also the fact that the levels all look quite the same. You can change the wall colors, which is a nice touch, but I wonder why the developers didn't adjust the colors for each level, to make them more distinct. (I also wonder about their choice of standard color. It's a bit ugly.) The controls felt awkward, at least at the beginning, and some keys seem to be superfluous. Enemies see you miles away, which makes sense, because you can shoot them at once, but especially with the small spiders it's awful to shoot them while they're only a pixel high.  I'm also quite sad that there is a lot of wasted potential; ROOT has a lot of objects in the levels, but interaction with them is either meaningless 'flush the toilet' or 'take and throw it'. There could have been more, like traps and bridges, etc. And then there are some scripted events, e.g. a creature climbs up a wall and escapes, and later is never mentioned again. (Or I missed something?)

As a kid I loved Gothic 2. And I'm pretty sure I still love it. But after purchasing the Gold Edition with Night of the Raven expansion i just HATE this expansion so much.   NR adds some cool parts to the story and surely makes the world filled with more characters and ideas what is much appreciated. (Vanilla Gothic 2 had some unused, empty areas which are basically in the game only to make use of them in the expansion - like the field of pyramids.) However the Gold Edition requires SO MUCH BORING GRINDING it's ridiculous. Most of the enemies are stronger than they were - to the point you have to spend dozens of additional hours to face them in a way you could do so in vanilla Gothic 2. The problem is the fighting gets really repetitive. You faced a hundred orks? Here is another 200. Farm them one by one.  I've played this game in a completionist style for over 60 hours and I am so, so tired of it. And it's just a couple of weaks after I had great time playing Gothic 1 - which I always considered inferior.   Another problem is the game was freezing for me once every 15-20 minutes. Thankfully saving and loading the games is superfast and you can get used to restarting the game, however this added to many, many bugs Gothic series always had completely shattered my immersion. For what I googled many people have the same problem on Windows 7, so I suggest trying a non-Steam version.

If you are a true Transformers fan, then you will love the game. A neat feature about the game is that there are 10 chapters; the chapters are LONG. The first 5 chapters you will play as the Decepticons and the last 5 chapters, you will play as the Autobots.  A cool thing about War for Cybertron is when you select a chapter, you can decide if you want to be Megatron, Barricade, Star Scream or even Sound Wave and a few others. My all time favorite Decepticon to play, would be Megatron. Megtron's main weapon is a huge cannon, which knocks an autobot down in one hit! Star Scream is also really sweet to play, he is quick and has a deadly weapon.   On the Autobot side, you can play as Optimus Prime! (like we didn't know already lol). He is really cool. His main weapon is a awesome assault weapon. A few characters you can play on the Autobot side, are Optimus, Bumblebee, Ironhide, Jetfire and a few more. Probably my two favorite are Optimus and Ironhide.  The campaign is pretty good. I read the book, 'War for Cybertron' a year before I played the actual game. I enjoyed the game because it explained things better than the book. There were only a few things I didn't like; one was when you played as the Decepticons, the Autobots sometimes acted like bad guys when they shouldn't. For exmaple - autobot 'I am going to destroy you!!'. That seems like something Megatron would say. lol.  The graphics are pretty decent. The only downfall to the game, which is biggest issue for me, is that it's capped at 30fps so you won't be able to go past that (unless you download programs which I don't recommend). Since it's capped, it's best to keep the settings on low so it will run smoothly. There are times that my PC stuttered because of the 30 cap and it's not because my PC isn't good. My PC can run alll CoD games on High-Extra.  Other than the 30 cap, my next issue is that the last chapter was a little 'lame' as for it was similar to the Autobot's campaign. I won't reveal any more info than this as I don't want to ruin it others. :\\  As a side note, when I first launched it on Windows 8.1, it wouldn't load. If you expenience this issue, move the Physx file to desktop and launch the game, (it will give an error, after that, put the Physx file back where you found it), then launch it again and it should run.  Overall, good story, fun Decepticons/Autobots characters, decent graphics, = Worth it!

I'm deeply shocked by how rough the FS community is man-handling this innocent $15 game.   I'm a 15+ year FS veteran, FS9, FSX, P3D, the latest, the greatest, addons, OC'd PCs and tweaked configs til the cows come home, I've done it all. But even coming from that background, spending an average of $250-1000 annually for addons, accersories and peripherals, watching an waiting with bated breath for over a decade for a true successor to pick up the mantle of flagship Flight Simulators... yet EVEN I CAN FIND SOMETHING NICE TO SAY ABOUT DTG Flight School.  I have to congratulate the hard working developers for picking up the torch and running the brutal gauntlet that the flight simming community has become, to take the abuse --undeserved-- of a selfish, entitled, over-critical and under-helpful society of miserly, withering, 'armchair pilot' wannabes with nothing better to do than COMPLAIN.  Is this today, as of this moment, the next best simulation platform? Is it FSeleven? FS2016? No. Of course not. It was never meant to be. IT'S ONLY $15 FOR HEAVENS SAKE! Have the whiners SEEN the prices of FS addons this last DECADE?  But does DTG Flight School show potential...? OH MY, YES. YES INDEED.  I just spent my first total hour of flight time, the first few training missions, a few Alaskan Bush pilot missions and a few touch n' gos around my local real life airport... I HAD A BLAST. In fact I was surprised to find it performing almost just as smoothly 'out-the-box' as my heavily tweaked FSX and P3Dv3 except with a slightly BETTER framerate! The amazing effect of the new lighting system (needs some adjustment, perhaps, but attractive) combined with Orbx scenery and higher, smoother framerate equals immersion, sweet immersion... Toss in the simple goal of racking up Flight hours that equate to real achivements and earning licenses, and now, all your flights matter, now getting back on the ground safely means something, now you care.   Dovetail Games still have lots of work to do until they release their full blown DTG Flight Simulator, that's a given, but shame on every poncy, 2 sentence reviewer who bails at the first glimpse of their unrealistic over-expectations dashed in disappointment, ending with, 'save your money, refund requested'.   Let them leave, they'll never know what their missing!
